title: GAIDE: Graph-based Attention Masking for Spatial- and Embodiment-aware Motion Planning
summary: Sampling-based motion planning algorithms are widely used for motion planning of robotic manipulators, but they often struggle with sample inefficiency in high-dimensional configuration spaces due to their reliance on uniform or hand-crafted informed sampling primitives. Neural informed samplers address this limitation by learning the sampling distribution from prior planning experience to guide the motion planner towards planning goal. However, existing approaches often struggle to encode the spatial structure inherent in motion planning problems. To address this limitation, we introduce Graph-based Attention Masking for Spatial- and Embodiment-aware Motion Planning (GAIDE), a neural informed sampler that leverages both the spatial structure of the planning problem and the robotic manipulator's embodiment to guide the planning algorithm. GAIDE represents these structures as a graph and integrates it into a transformer-based neural sampler through attention masking. We evaluate GAIDE against baseline state-of-the-art sampling-based planners using uniform sampling, hand-crafted informed sampling, and neural informed sampling primitives. Evaluation results demonstrate that GAIDE improves planning efficiency and success rate.

AI.tldr: GAIDE is a graph-structured, transformer-based neural sampler using attention masking to encode spatial structure and robot embodiment, improving planning efficiency and success rate over baselines.
AI.motivation: Sampling-based planners are sample-inefficient in high-dimensional spaces; existing neural samplers often fail to exploit the spatial structure and embodiment of the problem.
AI.method: Represent spatial structure and robot embodiment as a graph; integrate this graph into a transformer-based neural sampler via attention masking to bias sampling toward promising regions.
AI.result: Empirical results show GAIDE improves planning efficiency and success rate compared with baselines: uniform sampling, hand-crafted informed sampling, and standard neural informed sampling.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating graph-structured spatial and embodiment information through attention masking enhances neural-informed sampling for motion planning, yielding measurable performance gains.

title: Act-Observe-Rewrite: Multimodal Coding Agents as In-Context Policy Learners for Robot Manipulation
summary: Can a multimodal language model learn to manipulate physical objects by reasoning about its own failures-without gradient updates, demonstrations, or reward engineering? We argue the answer is yes, under conditions we characterise precisely. We present Act-Observe-Rewrite (AOR), a framework in which an LLM agent improves a robot manipulation policy by synthesising entirely new executable Python controller code between trials, guided by visual observations and structured episode outcomes. Unlike prior work that grounds LLMs in pre-defined skill libraries or uses code generation for one-shot plan synthesis, AOR makes the full low-level motor control implementation the unit of LLM reasoning, enabling the agent to change not just what the robot does, but how it does it. The central claim is that interpretable code as the policy representation creates a qualitatively different kind of in-context learning from opaque neural policies: the agent can diagnose systematic failures and rewrite their causes. We validate this across three robosuite manipulation tasks and report promising results, with the agent achieving high success rates without demonstrations, reward engineering, or gradient updates.

AI.tldr: LLM-based robot manipulation via Act-Observe-Rewrite (AOR) where the policy is executable Python code; it synthesizes new controllers between trials to diagnose and fix failures without demonstrations, rewards, or gradient updates.
AI.motivation: Enable in-context learning for physical manipulation by letting the agent rewrite its own low-level code based on visual observations and outcomes, bypassing the need for gradient updates or pre-defined skill libraries.
AI.method: Act-Observe-Rewrite (AOR): an LLM agent observes outcomes, diagnoses failures, and rewrites executable Python controller code between trials to implement a new policy for subsequent attempts; no gradient updates or external demonstrations are used.
AI.result: Validated on three robosuite manipulation tasks; the agent achieves high success rates without demonstrations, reward engineering, or gradient updates.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable code as policy enables a qualitatively different form of in-context learning for robot control, allowing diagnosis of failures and rewriting the cause; potential for generalization and reduced reliance on external supervision.

title: Efficient Autonomous Navigation of a Quadruped Robot in Underground Mines on Edge Hardware
summary: Embodied navigation in underground mines faces significant challenges, including narrow passages, uneven terrain, near-total darkness, GPS-denied conditions, and limited communication infrastructure. While recent learning-based approaches rely on GPU-accelerated inference and extensive training data, we present a fully autonomous navigation stack for a Boston Dynamics Spot quadruped robot that runs entirely on a low-power Intel NUC edge computer with no GPU and no network connectivity requirements. The system integrates LiDAR-inertial odometry, scan-matching localization against a prior map, terrain segmentation, and visibility-graph global planning with a velocity-regulated local path follower, achieving real-time perception-to-action at consistent control rates. After a single mapping pass of the environment, the system handles arbitrary goal locations within the known map without any environment-specific training or learned components. We validate the system through repeated field trials using four target locations of varying traversal difficulty in an experimental underground mine, accumulating over 700 m of fully autonomous traverse with a 100% success rate across all 20 trials (5 repetitions x 4 targets) and an overall Success weighted by Path Length (SPL) of 0.73 \pm 0.09.

AI.tldr: Fully autonomous, GPU-free navigation stack for underground mines on a Spot robot using edge computing; single mapping pass; robust to unknown goals; 700 m trials; 100% success; SPL 0.73±0.09.
AI.motivation: Navigate in GPS-denied, dark, narrow underground mines with limited comms; reduce reliance on GPU-trained models and network connectivity by computing on edge hardware.
AI.method: LiDAR-IMU odometry; scan-matching against a prior map for localization; terrain segmentation; visibility-graph global planning; velocity-regulated local path follower; runs on Intel NUC; no GPU; no network; one map pass; arbitrary goals within map; no learned components.
AI.result: Accumulated over 700 m fully autonomous traverse; 20 trials (5 repeats x 4 targets); 100% success rate; SPL 0.73 ± 0.09.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasible real-time perception-to-action on a resource-constrained edge platform in challenging conditions; system operates without environment-specific training and generalizes to known-map goals; suitable for GPS-denied mining environments.

title: PTLD: Sim-to-real Privileged Tactile Latent Distillation for Dexterous Manipulation
summary: Tactile dexterous manipulation is essential to automating complex household tasks, yet learning effective control policies remains a challenge. While recent work has relied on imitation learning, obtaining high quality demonstrations for multi-fingered hands via robot teleoperation or kinesthetic teaching is prohibitive. Alternatively, with reinforcement we can learn skills in simulation, but fast and realistic simulation of tactile observations is challenging. To bridge this gap, we introduce PTLD: sim-to-real Privileged Tactile Latent Distillation, a novel approach to learning tactile manipulation skills without requiring tactile simulation. Instead of simulating tactile sensors or relying purely on proprioceptive policies to transfer zero-shot sim-to-real, our key idea is to leverage privileged sensors in the real world to collect real-world tactile policy data. This data is then used to distill a robust state estimator that operates on tactile input. We demonstrate from our experiments that PTLD can be used to improve proprioceptive manipulation policies trained in simulation significantly by incorporating tactile sensing. On the benchmark in-hand rotation task, PTLD achieves a 182% improvement over a proprioception only policy. We also show that PTLD enables learning the challenging task of tactile in-hand reorientation where we see a 57% improvement in the number of goals reached over using proprioception alone. Website: https://akashsharma02.github.io/ptld-website/.

AI.tldr: PTLD uses privileged tactile data from the real world to distill a tactile state estimator, enabling a proprioception-based policy trained in simulation to leverage tactile sensing without simulating tactile sensors; it achieves large gains on in-hand tasks (182% on in-hand rotation, 57% on in-hand reorientation).
AI.motivation: Tactile dexterous manipulation with multi-finger hands is difficult due to expensive demonstrations and the sim-to-real gap in tactile sensing. Purely proprioceptive policies capped performance; simulating tactile observations is challenging. The work aims to exploit real-world privileged tactile data to improve simulated policies without tactile sensor simulation.
AI.method: Collect real-world tactile data using privileged sensors and distill a robust tactile state estimator from this data. Use the distilled tactile latent representation to improve proprioceptive policies trained in simulation, without relying on tactile sensor simulation. The approach enables zero-shot transfer to real robots and demonstrates improvements on in-hand rotation and tactile in-hand reorientation.
AI.result: On the in-hand rotation benchmark, PTLD yields a 182% improvement over a proprioception-only policy. For tactile in-hand reorientation, the method improves the number of goals reached by 57% over proprioception alone. A project webpage is provided for more details.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging privileged real-world tactile data to distill a tactile latent estimator can substantially boost sim-to-real transfer for tactile manipulation without tactile sensor simulation, enhancing the effectiveness of simulation-trained policies when deployed with real tactile input.

title: Hypercube drawings with no long plane paths
comment: 19 pages, 11 figures, preliminary version to appear in proceedings of EuroCG 2026
summary: We study the existence of plane substructures in drawings of the $d$-dimensional hypercube graph $Q_d$. We construct drawings of $Q_d$ which contain no plane subgraph with more than $2d-2$ edges, no plane path with more than $2d-3$ edges, and no plane matching of size more than $2d-4$. On the other hand, we prove that every rectilinear drawing of $Q_d$ with vertices in convex position contains a plane path of length $d$ (if $d$ is odd) or $d-1$ (if $d$ is even). We also prove that if a graph $G$ is a plane subgraph of every drawing of $Q_d$ for a sufficiently large $d$, then $G$ is necessarily a forest of caterpillars. Lastly, we give a short proof of a generalization of a result by Alpert et al. [Cong. Numerantium, 2009] on the maximum rectilinear crossing number of $Q_d$.

AI.tldr: The paper studies plane subgraphs in drawings of the d-dimensional hypercube Q_d, giving tight bounds on maximal plane substructures, a convex-position rectilinear-path guarantee, a structural characterization of subgraphs common to all drawings, and a short proof generalizing rectilinear crossing numbers.
AI.motivation: Understand how geometric/drawing constraints limit subgraphs (paths, matchings) in high-dimensional hypercube drawings; connect extremal plane subgraph problems with rectilinear drawings and crossing numbers; extend known results (Alpert et al.).
AI.method: Construct drawings with bounded plane subgraphs; analyze rectilinear convex drawings; derive universal-subgraph property via large-d obstructions; leverage known results on rectilinear crossing numbers and provide a concise generalization.
AI.result: (i) There exist drawings of Q_d with no plane subgraph exceeding 2d-2 edges; no plane path longer than 2d-3 edges; no plane matching larger than 2d-4. (ii) Every rectilinear drawing of Q_d with vertices in convex position contains a plane path of length d (d odd) or d-1 (d even). (iii) If a graph G is a plane subgraph of every Q_d-drawing for sufficiently large d, then G is a forest of caterpillars. (iv) Provide a short proof of a generalization of Alpert et al.'s result on the maximum rectilinear crossing number of Q_d.
AI.conclusion: The work clarifies the limitations and necessities of plane substructures in Q_d drawings, identifies a universal-structure obstruction (forests of caterpillars) for large d, and connects to crossing-number theory by generalizing known results; it offers tight bounds and methods that may extend to related graph families and drawing styles.

title: Lost in Translation: How Language Re-Aligns Vision for Cross-Species Pathology
comment: 27 pages, 6 figures, 7 tables. Code and data available at https://github.com/ekansh-arora0/cross-species-pathology
summary: Foundation models are increasingly applied to computational pathology, yet their behavior under cross-cancer and cross-species transfer remains unspecified. This study investigated how fine-tuning CPath-CLIP affects cancer detection under same-cancer, cross-cancer, and cross-species conditions using whole-slide image patches from canine and human histopathology. Performance was measured using area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC). Few-shot fine-tuning improved same-cancer (64.9% to 72.6% AUC) and cross-cancer performance (56.84% to 66.31% AUC). Cross-species evaluation revealed that while tissue matching enables meaningful transfer, performance remains below state-of-the-art benchmarks (H-optimus-0: 84.97% AUC), indicating that standard vision-language alignment is suboptimal for cross-species generalization. Embedding space analysis revealed extremely high cosine similarity (greater than 0.99) between tumor and normal prototypes. Grad-CAM shows prototype-based models remain domain-locked, while language-guided models attend to conserved tumor morphology. To address this, we introduce Semantic Anchoring, which uses language to provide a stable coordinate system for visual features. Ablation studies reveal that benefits stem from the text-alignment mechanism itself, regardless of text encoder complexity. Benchmarking against H-optimus-0 shows that CPath-CLIP's failure stems from intrinsic embedding collapse, which text alignment effectively circumvents. Additional gains were observed in same-cancer (8.52%) and cross-cancer classification (5.67%). We identified a previously uncharacterized failure mode: semantic collapse driven by species-dominated alignment rather than missing visual information. These results demonstrate that language acts as a control mechanism, enabling semantic re-interpretation without retraining.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning CPath-CLIP with few-shot data improves cancer detection within the same cancer type and across cancer types, but cross-species transfer remains challenging. Introducing Semantic Anchoring (language-based coordinates) mitigates embedding collapse and aids cross-domain generalization, though performance still trails state-of-the-art for cross-species tasks. Text-guided alignment acts as a control mechanism, reinterpreting semantics without retraining.
AI.motivation: Evaluate the generalization of foundation-model–based pathology pipelines across same-cancer, cross-cancer, and cross-species settings; identify failure modes of vision-language alignment and propose language-driven strategies to stabilize cross-domain visual representations.
AI.method: Fine-tune CPath-CLIP on whole-slide image patches from canine and human histopathology under same-cancer, cross-cancer, and cross-species conditions. Assess with AUC. Compare against SOTA H-optimus-0. Analyze embedding space via cosine similarity, use Grad-CAM for localization, and perform ablations. Introduce Semantic Anchoring to provide a language-derived stable coordinate system for visual features.
AI.result: Same-cancer AUC improved from 64.9% to 72.6%; cross-cancer AUC from 56.84% to 66.31%. Cross-species transfer shows meaningful generalization when tissue matching is allowed but remains below SOTA (H-optimus-0: 84.97% AUC). Embedding space exhibits near-perfect tumor/normal prototype similarity (>0.99). Grad-CAM reveals domain-locked prototypes; language-guided models attend to conserved tumor morphology. Semantic Anchoring stabilizes features via text alignment; ablations show gains stem from the text-alignment mechanism rather than heavy text encoders. Benchmarking against SOTA suggests failure due to intrinsic embedding collapse, which text alignment helps circumvent. Additional gains observed in same-cancer (8.52%) and cross-cancer (5.67%). A semantic-collapse failure mode identified where species-dominated alignment drives collapse rather than missing visual info.
AI.conclusion: Language-guided alignment can re-interpret visual features semantically without retraining and acts as a control mechanism to improve cross-domain generalization. However, cross-species performance remains below SOTA, indicating room for improvement; Semantic Anchoring is a promising general strategy to stabilize cross-domain representations and mitigate embedding collapse.

title: Decorrelating the Future: Joint Frequency Domain Learning for Spatio-temporal Forecasting
summary: Standard direct forecasting models typically rely on point-wise objectives such as Mean Squared Error, which fail to capture the complex spatio-temporal dependencies inherent in graph-structured signals. While recent frequency-domain approaches such as FreDF mitigate temporal autocorrelation, they often overlook spatial and cross spatio-temporal interactions. To address this limitation, we propose FreST Loss, a frequency-enhanced spatio-temporal training objective that extends supervision to the joint spatio-temporal spectrum. By leveraging the Joint Fourier Transform (JFT), FreST Loss aligns model predictions with ground truth in a unified spectral domain, effectively decorrelating complex dependencies across both space and time. Theoretical analysis shows that this formulation reduces estimation bias associated with time-domain training objectives. Extensive experiments on six real-world datasets demonstrate that FreST Loss is model-agnostic and consistently improves state-of-the-art baselines by better capturing holistic spatio-temporal dynamics.

AI.tldr: FreST Loss uses a joint spatio-temporal spectrum via the Joint Fourier Transform to supervise forecasts, addressing both spatial and temporal correlations neglected by point-wise and purely temporal frequency losses.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of point-wise objectives and existing frequency-domain methods that miss cross-spatio-temporal interactions in graph-structured signals, and to reduce estimation bias in time-domain training.
AI.method: Introduce FreST Loss, a frequency-enhanced objective that supervises predictions in the joint spatio-temporal spectrum using the Joint Fourier Transform, making the training model-agnostic and decorrelating complex dependencies.
AI.result: The method consistently improves state-of-the-art baselines across six real-world datasets, demonstrating better capture of holistic spatio-temporal dynamics; theoretical analysis indicates reduced estimation bias.
AI.conclusion: FreST Loss provides a unified spectral supervision for spatio-temporal graph forecasting, yielding model-agnostic gains by effectively aligning predictions with ground truth in the joint spectrum.

title: SkillNet: Create, Evaluate, and Connect AI Skills
summary: Current AI agents can flexibly invoke tools and execute complex tasks, yet their long-term advancement is hindered by the lack of systematic accumulation and transfer of skills. Without a unified mechanism for skill consolidation, agents frequently ``reinvent the wheel'', rediscovering solutions in isolated contexts without leveraging prior strategies. To overcome this limitation, we introduce SkillNet, an open infrastructure designed to create, evaluate, and organize AI skills at scale. SkillNet structures skills within a unified ontology that supports creating skills from heterogeneous sources, establishing rich relational connections, and performing multi-dimensional evaluation across Safety, Completeness, Executability, Maintainability, and Cost-awareness. Our infrastructure integrates a repository of over 200,000 skills, an interactive platform, and a versatile Python toolkit. Experimental evaluations on ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld demonstrate that SkillNet significantly enhances agent performance, improving average rewards by 40% and reducing execution steps by 30% across multiple backbone models. By formalizing skills as evolving, composable assets, SkillNet provides a robust foundation for agents to move from transient experience to durable mastery.

AI.tldr: SkillNet is an open infrastructure to create, evaluate, and organize AI skills at scale via a unified ontology, a large skill repository, interactive platform, and Python toolkit; it improves agent performance and efficiency across ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld.
AI.motivation: Lack of unified, scalable mechanism for consolidating and transferring skills across agents leads to reinvention and inefficient learning.
AI.method: Develop a unified skill ontology; integrate heterogeneous sources; establish multi-dimensional evaluation (Safety, Completeness, Executability, Maintainability, Cost-awareness); build a 200k+ skill repository, interactive platform, and Python toolkit; evaluate on multiple backbones across three environments.
AI.result: Significant performance gains: average rewards up 40%, execution steps down 30%, across several backbone models; demonstrates durable, composable skill assets improving long-term agent mastery.
AI.conclusion: SkillNet provides a scalable, evolvable framework for skill consolidation, enabling agents to transition from transient experience to durable capabilities.

title: Many-RRT*: Robust Joint-Space Trajectory Planning for Serial Manipulators
summary: The rapid advancement of high degree-of-freedom (DoF) serial manipulators necessitates the use of swift, sampling-based motion planners for high-dimensional spaces. While sampling-based planners like the Rapidly-Exploring Random Tree (RRT) are widely used, planning in the manipulator's joint space presents significant challenges due to non-invertible forward kinematics. A single task-space end-effector pose can correspond to multiple configuration-space states, creating a multi-arm bandit problem for the planner. In complex environments, simply choosing the wrong joint space goal can result in suboptimal trajectories or even failure to find a viable plan. To address this planning problem, we propose Many-RRT*: an extension of RRT*-Connect that plans to multiple goals in parallel. By generating multiple IK solutions and growing independent trees from these goal configurations simultaneously alongside a single start tree, Many-RRT* ensures that computational effort is not wasted on suboptimal IK solutions. This approach maintains robust convergence and asymptotic optimality. Experimental evaluations across robot morphologies and diverse obstacle environments demonstrate that Many-RRT* provides higher quality trajectories (44.5% lower cost in the same runtime) with a significantly higher success rate (100% vs. the next best of 1.6%) than previous RRT iterations without compromising on runtime performance.

AI.tldr: Many-RRT* extends RRT*-Connect to plan to multiple goal configurations in parallel for high-DoF manipulators, addressing multi-IK ambiguity by generating multiple IK solutions and growing independent trees from each goal alongside a single start tree; it preserves convergence and asymptotic optimality while improving planning performance.
AI.motivation: High-DoF serial manipulators have non-invertible forward kinematics, causing a single end-effector pose to correspond to multiple joint configurations (multi-IK). This creates a multi-armed bandit problem where choosing a poor IK can lead to suboptimal or failed plans. Robust, efficient joint-space planning is needed, especially in cluttered environments.
AI.method: Extend RRT*-Connect to multiple-goal planning (Many-RRT*). Generate multiple IK solutions for the end-effector pose and grow independent trees from these goal configurations in parallel with a start tree. This parallel, multi-IK-aware approach avoids spending effort on suboptimal IK choices and preserves convergence properties.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across various robot morphologies and obstacle environments show substantially improved performance: 44.5% lower trajectory cost within the same runtime and a perfect 100% success rate compared to the next-best 1.6% achieved by prior RRT iterations, without sacrificing runtime efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Many-RRT* provides robust convergence and asymptotic optimality while significantly enhancing planning quality and reliability in high-DoF manipulator motion planning through parallel, multi-IK-aware exploration.

title: Quadratic polarity and polar Fenchel-Young divergences from the canonical Legendre polarity
summary: Polarity is a fundamental reciprocal duality of $n$-dimensional projective geometry which associates to points polar hyperplanes, and more generally $k$-dimensional convex bodies to polar $(n-1-k)$-dimensional convex bodies. It is well-known that the Legendre-Fenchel transformation of functions can be interpreted from the polarity viewpoint of their graphs using an extra dimension. In this paper, we first show that generic polarities induced by quadratic polarity functionals can be expressed either as deformed Legendre polarity or as the Legendre polarity of deformed convex bodies, and be efficiently manipulated using linear algebra on $(n+2)\times (n+2)$ matrices operating on homogeneous coordinates. Second, we define polar divergences using the Legendre polarity and show that they generalize the Fenchel-Young divergence or equivalent Bregman divergence. This polarity study brings new understanding of the core reference duality in information geometry. Last, we show that the total Bregman divergences can be considered as a total polar Fenchel-Young divergence from which we newly exhibit the reference duality using dual polar conformal factors.

AI.tldr: Polarity in nD projective geometry is connected to Legendre-Fenchel duality; quadratic polarity functionals can be represented as deformed Legendre polarities or as Legendre polarities of deformed convex bodies, computable via (n+2)×(n+2) matrices in homogeneous coordinates; polar divergences extend Fenchel-Young/Bregman divergences; total Bregman divergences emerge as total polar Fenchel-Young divergences, exposing a duality via dual polar conformal factors.
AI.motivation: Unify polarity with convex analysis and information geometry, and provide an efficient linear-algebraic framework for manipulating polarity via homogeneous coordinates; extend dualities from Legendre transforms to polar divergences.
AI.method: Characterize polarities induced by quadratic polarity functionals as deformed Legendre polarities or Legendre polarities of deformed convex bodies; implement computations with linear algebra on (n+2)×(n+2) matrices in homogeneous coordinates; define polar divergences using Legendre polarity; relate them to Fenchel-Young/Bregman divergences; interpret total Bregman divergences as total polar Fenchel-Young divergences with dual polar conformal factors.
AI.result: Demonstrates that generic polarities can be efficiently manipulated in a unified linear-algebraic framework; polar divergences generalize the Fenchel-Young and Bregman divergences; provides a new interpretation of the core duality in information geometry via polarity; links total Bregman divergences to total polar Fenchel-Young divergences.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a novel polarity-based geometric framework that connects Legendre duality, convex bodies, and information-geometric divergences; reveals reference duality through dual polar conformal factors and shows practical computational machinery using (n+2)×(n+2) matrices in homogeneous coordinates.

title: Recognition of Daily Activities through Multi-Modal Deep Learning: A Video, Pose, and Object-Aware Approach for Ambient Assisted Living
summary: Recognition of daily activities is a critical element for effective Ambient Assisted Living (AAL) systems, particularly to monitor the well-being and support the independence of older adults in indoor environments. However, developing robust activity recognition systems faces significant challenges, including intra-class variability, inter-class similarity, environmental variability, camera perspectives, and scene complexity. This paper presents a multi-modal approach for the recognition of activities of daily living tailored for older adults within AAL settings. The proposed system integrates visual information processed by a 3D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with 3D human pose data analyzed by a Graph Convolutional Network. Contextual information, derived from an object detection module, is fused with the 3D CNN features using a cross-attention mechanism to enhance recognition accuracy. This method is evaluated using the Toyota SmartHome dataset, which consists of real-world indoor activities. The results indicate that the proposed system achieves competitive classification accuracy for a range of daily activities, highlighting its potential as an essential component for advanced AAL monitoring solutions. This advancement supports the broader goal of developing intelligent systems that promote safety and autonomy among older adults.

AI.tldr: A multi-modal activity recognition system for older adults in indoor AAL settings using 3D CNN video features, 3D pose data with Graph ConvNet, and cross-attention fusion of object-detection context; evaluated on Toyota SmartHome with competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Addresses robust recognition of daily activities in AAL environments, tackling intra-class variability, inter-class similarity, environmental variation, camera viewpoints, and scene complexity to support safe, autonomous monitoring of older adults.
AI.method: A multi-modal pipeline: visual features from a 3D CNN (video subsequences), 3D human pose modeled with a Graph Convolutional Network, and contextual cues from an object-detection module. Fusion is performed via a cross-attention mechanism to integrate modalities and improve recognition.
AI.result: The approach yields competitive classification accuracy across a range of daily activities on the Toyota SmartHome dataset, indicating effective multimodal fusion for AAL activity recognition.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates the potential of multi-modal fusion (vision, pose, and context) for robust indoor activity recognition in AAL, contributing to safer and more autonomous monitoring of older adults.

title: Machine Learning for Complex Systems Dynamics: Detecting Bifurcations in Dynamical Systems with Deep Neural Networks
summary: Critical transitions are the abrupt shifts between qualitatively different states of a system, and they are crucial to understanding tipping points in complex dynamical systems across ecology, climate science, and biology. Detecting these shifts typically involves extensive forward simulations or bifurcation analyses, which are often computationally intensive and limited by parameter sampling. In this study, we propose a novel machine learning approach based on deep neural networks (DNNs) called equilibrium-informed neural networks (EINNs) to identify critical thresholds associated with catastrophic regime shifts. Rather than fixing parameters and searching for solutions, the EINN method reverses this process by using candidate equilibrium states as inputs and training a DNN to infer the corresponding system parameters that satisfy the equilibrium condition. By analyzing the learned parameter landscape and observing abrupt changes in the feasibility or continuity of equilibrium mappings, critical thresholds can be effectively detected. We demonstrate this capability on nonlinear systems exhibiting saddle-node bifurcations and multi-stability, showing that EINNs can recover the parameter regions associated with impending transitions. This method provides a flexible alternative to traditional techniques, offering new insights into the early detection and structure of critical shifts in high-dimensional and nonlinear systems.

AI.tldr: EINNs (equilibrium-informed neural networks) reverse the standard approach by inferring system parameters from candidate equilibrium states to identify critical thresholds in nonlinear dynamical systems. The method detects tipping points by analyzing changes in the feasibility/continuity of the equilibrium map, demonstrated on saddle-node bifurcations and multistability, offering a flexible, scalable alternative to forward simulations and bifurcation analyses.
AI.motivation: Critical transitions (tipping points) in ecology, climate science, and biology are hard to predict with forward simulations or parameter sweeps due to computational expense and high dimensionality. A data-efficient method that can reveal impending regime shifts by exploiting equilibrium information is highly desirable.
AI.method: Train a deep neural network to map equilibrium states x to corresponding system parameters p that satisfy the equilibrium condition F(x,p)=0. This equilibrium-informed learning reverses the usual parameter-to-state search. By inspecting the learned parameter landscape and the continuity/feasibility of the equilibrium mapping, the method identifies parameter regions where transitions occur, particularly in systems with saddle-node bifurcations and multistability.
AI.result: Applied to nonlinear systems with saddle-node bifurcations and multistability, EINNs recover parameter regions associated with impending transitions and reveal the structure of critical shifts.
AI.conclusion: EINNs provide a flexible, scalable alternative to traditional bifurcation analysis and long forward simulations, enabling early detection and characterization of critical transitions in high-dimensional nonlinear systems.

title: Capability Thresholds and Manufacturing Topology: How Embodied Intelligence Triggers Phase Transitions in Economic Geography
summary: The fundamental topology of manufacturing has not undergone a paradigm-level transformation since Henry Ford's moving assembly line in 1913. Every major innovation of the past century, from the Toyota Production System to Industry 4.0, has optimized within the Fordist paradigm without altering its structural logic: centralized mega-factories, located near labor pools, producing at scale. We argue that embodied intelligence is poised to break this century-long stasis, not by making existing factories more efficient, but by triggering phase transitions in manufacturing economic geography itself. When embodied AI capabilities cross critical thresholds in dexterity, generalization, reliability, and tactile-vision fusion, the consequences extend far beyond cost reduction: they restructure where factories are built, how supply chains are organized, and what constitutes viable production scale. We formalize this by defining a Capability Space C = (d, g, r, t) and showing that the site-selection objective function undergoes topological reorganization when capability vectors cross critical surfaces. Through three pathways, weight inversion, batch collapse, and human-infrastructure decoupling, we show that embodied intelligence enables demand-proximal micro-manufacturing, eliminates "manufacturing deserts," and reverses geographic concentration driven by labor arbitrage. We further introduce Machine Climate Advantage: once human workers are removed, optimal factory locations are determined by machine-optimal conditions (low humidity, high irradiance, thermal stability), factors orthogonal to traditional siting logic, creating a production geography with no historical precedent. This paper establishes Embodied Intelligence Economics, the study of how physical AI capability thresholds reshape the spatial and structural logic of production.

AI.tldr: Embodied AI thresholds can trigger phase transitions in manufacturing geography, redefining factory location, scale, and supply chains via a Capability Space framework (d,g,r,t) and introducing Machine Climate Advantage.
AI.motivation: Challenge the Fordist-centric view of manufacturing and explore how embodied intelligence can reconfigure production geography beyond marginal efficiency gains.
AI.method: Formalize Capability Space C=(d,g,r,t); analyze site-selection as a topological problem with critical surfaces; identify three mechanisms—weight inversion, batch collapse, human-infrastructure decoupling—that enable demand-proximal micro-manufacturing and reversal of traditional concentration patterns; introduce Machine Climate Advantage as machine-centric siting criteria.
AI.result: Argues for a reorganization of production geography driven by AI capability thresholds, enabling distributed manufacturing, erasing manufacturing deserts, and reducing the role of labor arbitrage; proposes Embodied Intelligence Economics as a new framework for studying production geography.
AI.conclusion: Embodied intelligence thresholds redefine the spatial and structural logic of production, motivating new theory, metrics, and policy considerations to study and manage this transition.

title: From Local Corrections to Generalized Skills: Improving Neuro-Symbolic Policies with MEMO
summary: Recent works use a neuro-symbolic framework for general manipulation policies. The advantage of this framework is that -- by applying off-the-shelf vision and language models -- the robot can break complex tasks down into semantic subtasks. However, the fundamental bottleneck is that the robot needs skills to ground these subtasks into embodied motions. Skills can take many forms (e.g., trajectory snippets, motion primitives, coded functions), but regardless of their form skills act as a constraint. The high-level policy can only ground its language reasoning through the available skills; if the robot cannot generate the right skill for the current task, its policy will fail. We propose to address this limitation -- and dynamically expand the robot's skills -- by leveraging user feedback. When a robot fails, humans can intuitively explain what went wrong (e.g., ``no, go higher''). While a simple approach is to recall this exact text the next time the robot faces a similar situation, we hypothesize that by collecting, clustering, and re-phrasing natural language corrections across multiple users and tasks, we can synthesize more general text guidance and coded skill templates. Applying this hypothesis we develop Memory Enhanced Manipulation (MEMO). MEMO builds and maintains a retrieval-augmented skillbook gathered from human feedback and task successes. At run time, MEMO retrieves relevant text and code from this skillbook, enabling the robot's policy to generate new skills while reasoning over multi-task human feedback. Our experiments demonstrate that using MEMO to aggregate local feedback into general skill templates enables generalization to novel tasks where existing baselines fall short. See supplemental material here: https://collab.me.vt.edu/memo

AI.tldr: MEMO proposes a retrieval-augmented, memory-based skillbook that learns from human corrections to dynamically expand robot skills, enabling generalization to new tasks with improved manipulation policies.
AI.motivation: Grounded manipulation policies are bottlenecked by finite skill sets; leveraging multi-user natural language feedback can synthesize general templates to bridge the gap between high-level planning and embodied motion.
AI.method: Construct a memory of text and code templates from human feedback and task successes; at run-time retrieve relevant items to generate new skills; cluster and rephrase corrections to form generalized guidance; apply to multi-task context.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that MEMO aggregates local feedback into general skill templates, enabling generalization to novel tasks where existing baselines underperform.
AI.conclusion: Memory-augmented, retrieval-based skill synthesis can effectively expand robot capabilities and improve generalization by leveraging clustered natural language corrections across users and tasks.

title: An Optimal Algorithm for Computing Many Faces in Line Arrangements
summary: Given a set of $m$ points and a set of $n$ lines in the plane, we consider the problem of computing the faces of the arrangement of the lines that contain at least one point. In this paper, we present an $O(m^{2/3}n^{2/3}+(n+m)\log n)$ time algorithm for the problem. We also show that this matches the lower bound under the algebraic decision tree model and thus our algorithm is optimal. In particular, when $m=n$, the runtime is $O(n^{4/3})$, which matches the worst case combinatorial complexity $Ω(n^{4/3})$ of all output faces. This is the first optimal algorithm since the problem was first studied more than three decades ago [Edelsbrunner, Guibas, and Sharir, SoCG 1988].

AI.tldr: An optimal algorithm for computing faces of a line arrangement that contain at least one point, with time O(m^{2/3} n^{2/3} + (n+m) log n); proven optimal via a lower bound in the algebraic decision tree model; for m=n it gives O(n^{4/3}) matching the worst-case complexity Ω(n^{4/3}); first such optimal result since 1988.
AI.motivation: Address the computational problem of extracting exactly those faces of a line arrangement that are incident to any of m given points, a fundamental task in incidence/arrangement geometry; determine the true time complexity and settle optimality of this problem.
AI.method: Propose an algorithm achieving the stated bound by combining partitioning/cutting techniques typical in geometric range searching and line arrangements, yielding a cross-term m^{2/3}n^{2/3} plus an (n+m) log n term; establish optimality by a lower bound in the algebraic decision tree model.
AI.result: The algorithm runs in O(m^{2/3}n^{2/3} + (n+m) log n) time; when m=n, this is O(n^{4/3}). The bound matches the known lower bound and the worst-case output size Ω(n^{4/3}), making the approach optimal.
AI.conclusion: This work resolves the optimality question for computing faces of a line arrangement that contain points, providing the first optimal algorithm since the 1988 study, and sets a benchmark for related problems in geometric arrangements and incidence geometry.

title: InverseNet: Benchmarking Operator Mismatch and Calibration Across Compressive Imaging Modalities
comment: Benchmarking Operator Mismatch and Calibration Across Compressive Imaging Modalities
summary: State-of-the-art EfficientSCI loses 20.58 dB when its assumed forward operator deviates from physical reality in just eight parameters, yet no existing benchmark quantifies operator mismatch, the default condition in deployed compressive imaging systems. We introduce InverseNet, the first cross-modality benchmark for operator mismatch, spanning CASSI, CACTI, and single-pixel cameras. Evaluating 12 methods under a four-scenario protocol (ideal, mismatched, oracle-corrected, blind calibration) across 27 simulated scenes and 9 real hardware captures, we find: (1) deep learning methods lose 10-21 dB under mismatch, eliminating their advantage over classical baselines; (2) performance and robustness are inversely correlated across modalities (Spearman r_s = -0.71, p < 0.01); (3) mask-oblivious architectures recover 0% of mismatch losses regardless of calibration quality, while operator-conditioned methods recover 41-90%; (4) blind grid-search calibration recovers 85-100% of the oracle bound without ground truth. Real hardware experiments confirm that simulation trends transfer to physical data. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: First cross-modality benchmark (InverseNet) for operator mismatch in compressive imaging; deep learning methods are highly sensitive to mismatch; calibration helps, but many architectures fail to recover losses; real hardware trends match simulations.
AI.motivation: Operator mismatch is prevalent in deployed compressive imaging, yet no standardized benchmark quantifies its impact; existing methods may overestimate robustness under idealized forward models.
AI.method: Introduce InverseNet, a cross-modality benchmark across CASSI, CACTI, and single-pixel cameras. Evaluate 12 methods under four scenarios (ideal, mismatched, oracle-corrected, blind calibration) over 27 simulated scenes and 9 real hardware captures.
AI.result: Deep learning methods lose 10–21 dB under forward-operator mismatch, erasing their advantage over classical baselines; performance robustness is inversely correlated across modalities (Spearman rs = -0.71, p < 0.01); mask-oblivious architectures recover 0% of mismatch losses, while operator-conditioned methods recover 41–90%; blind grid-search calibration recovers 85–100% of the oracle bound without ground truth; hardware results corroborate simulation trends.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark quantifies operator mismatch effects across modalities and shows calibration can largely recover losses; results advocate for operator-aware designs and calibration strategies in practical compressive imaging. Code will be released upon acceptance.

title: FedEMA-Distill: Exponential Moving Average Guided Knowledge Distillation for Robust Federated Learning
summary: Federated learning (FL) often degrades when clients hold heterogeneous non-Independent and Identically Distributed (non-IID) data and when some clients behave adversarially, leading to client drift, slow convergence, and high communication overhead. This paper proposes FedEMA-Distill, a server-side procedure that combines an exponential moving average (EMA) of the global model with ensemble knowledge distillation from client-uploaded prediction logits evaluated on a small public proxy dataset. Clients run standard local training, upload only compressed logits, and may use different model architectures, so no changes are required to client-side software while still supporting model heterogeneity across devices. Experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, FEMNIST, and AG News under Dirichlet-0.1 label skew show that FedEMA-Distill improves top-1 accuracy by several percentage points (up to +5% on CIFAR-10 and +6% on CIFAR-100) over representative baselines, reaches a given target accuracy in 30-35% fewer communication rounds, and reduces per-round client uplink payloads to 0.09-0.46 MB, i.e., roughly an order of magnitude less than transmitting full model weights. Using coordinate-wise median or trimmed-mean aggregation of logits at the server further stabilizes training in the presence of up to 10-20% Byzantine clients and yields well-calibrated predictions under attack. These results indicate that coupling temporal smoothing with logits-only aggregation provides a communication-efficient and attack-resilient FL pipeline that is deployment-friendly and compatible with secure aggregation and differential privacy, since only aggregated or obfuscated model outputs are exchanged.

AI.tldr: FedEMA-Distill introduces a server-side FL pipeline that uses an exponential moving average (EMA) of the global model and ensemble knowledge distillation from client-prediction logits on a public proxy to improve accuracy and reduce communication in non-IID, potentially adversarial settings. It supports model heterogeneity, requires no client-side changes, and remains compatible with secure aggregation and DP; robustness is enhanced via coordinate-wise median/trimmed-mean logits aggregation.
AI.motivation: Federated learning under non-IID data distributions and potential Byzantine/robust clients suffers from client drift, slow convergence, and high communication costs. A deployment-friendly method that is server-centric, preserves client heterogeneity, and reduces uplink payload while improving accuracy is needed.
AI.method: Clients perform standard local training and upload compressed logits (not full models). The server maintains an EMA of the global model and performs ensemble knowledge distillation using client logits evaluated on a small public proxy dataset. Optionally, the server uses coordinate-wise median or trimmed-mean aggregation of logits to enhance robustness to Byzantine clients. The approach does not require changes to client-side software and supports heterogeneous model architectures.
AI.result: Empirical gains on CIFAR-10 (+ up to 5%), CIFAR-100 (+ up to 6%), FEMNIST, and AG News under Dirichlet-0.1 skew. Faster attainment of target accuracy (30-35% fewer rounds). Dramatic reduction in uplink payload (0.09–0.46 MB per round, about an order of magnitude less than full model uploads). Additional robustness with median/trimmed-mean to tolerate 10–20% Byzantine clients, yielding calibrated predictions under attack.
AI.conclusion: A temporal smoothing (EMA) plus logits-only aggregation framework yields a communication-efficient, attack-resilient FL pipeline that is deployment-friendly and compatible with secure aggregation and differential privacy, since only aggregated logits are exchanged.

title: Progressive Refinement Regulation for Accelerating Diffusion Language Model Decoding
comment: 19 pages, 10 figures, Code available upon publication
summary: Diffusion language models generate text through iterative denoising under a uniform refinement rule applied to all tokens. However, tokens stabilize at different rates in practice, leading to substantial redundant refinement and motivating refinement control over the denoising process. Existing approaches typically assess refinement necessity from instantaneous, step-level signals under a fixed decoding process. In contrast, whether a token has converged is defined by how its prediction changes along its future refinement trajectory. Moreover, changing the refinement rule reshapes future refinement trajectories, which in turn determine how refinement rules should be formulated, making refinement control inherently dynamic. We propose \emph{Progressive Refinement Regulation} (PRR), a progressive, trajectory-grounded refinement control framework that derives a token-level notion of empirical convergence progress from full decoding rollouts. Based on this signal, PRR learns a lightweight token-wise controller to regulate refinement via temperature-based distribution shaping under a progressive self-evolving training scheme. Experiments show that PRR substantially accelerates diffusion language model decoding while preserving generation quality.

AI.tldr: Introduces Progressive Refinement Regulation (PRR), a trajectory-grounded token-wise control framework for diffusion language models that speeds up decoding via learned temperature-based refinement control.
AI.motivation: Uniform refinement across tokens is inefficient since tokens converge at different rates; existing methods rely on instantaneous signals and fixed decoding, ignoring future refinement trajectories.
AI.method: Derives a token-level empirical convergence progress from full decoding rollouts; trains a lightweight per-token controller; performs refinement regulation via temperature-based distribution shaping; employs progressive self-evolving training.
AI.result: PRR substantially accelerates decoding while preserving generation quality.
AI.conclusion: PRR enables dynamic, trajectory-aware refinement control for diffusion LMs, linking future refinement behavior to current control decisions.

title: Distributed State Estimation for Vision-Based Cooperative Slung Load Transportation in GPS-Denied Environments
comment: In proceedings of the 2026 AIAA SciTech Forum, Session: Intelligent Systems-27
summary: Transporting heavy or oversized slung loads using rotorcraft has traditionally relied on single-aircraft systems, which limits both payload capacity and control authority. Cooperative multilift using teams of rotorcraft offers a scalable and efficient alternative, especially for infrequent but challenging "long-tail" payloads without the need of building larger and larger rotorcraft. Most prior multilift research assumes GPS availability, uses centralized estimation architectures, or relies on controlled laboratory motion-capture setups. As a result, these methods lack robustness to sensor loss and are not viable in GPS-denied or operationally constrained environments. This paper addresses this limitation by presenting a distributed and decentralized payload state estimation framework for vision-based multilift operations. Using onboard monocular cameras, each UAV detects a fiducial marker on the payload and estimates its relative pose. These measurements are fused via a Distributed and Decentralized Extended Information Filter (DDEIF), enabling robust and scalable estimation that is resilient to individual sensor dropouts. This payload state estimate is then used for closed-loop trajectory tracking control. Monte Carlo simulation results in Gazebo show the effectiveness of the proposed approach, including the effect of communication loss during flight.

AI.tldr: Distributed and decentralized payload state estimation for vision-based multilift using onboard monocular cameras and a DDEIF, enabling robust control in GPS-denied or sensor-dropout scenarios.
AI.motivation: Single-aircraft systems limit payload capacity and control authority; GPS-dependent multilift struggles in GPS-denied or sensor-constrained environments. A decentralized, robust estimation framework is needed for scalable, mission-relevant long-tail payloads.
AI.method: Each UAV detects a fiducial marker on the payload with an onboard monocular camera to estimate relative pose. Measurements are fused via a Distributed and Decentralized Extended Information Filter (DDEIF) across all UAVs, enabling robust, scalable estimation despite sensor dropouts. The payload state estimate drives a closed-loop trajectory tracking controller. Evaluated via Monte Carlo simulations in Gazebo, including communication loss.
AI.result: The approach yields robust payload pose estimation and closed-loop tracking under sensor loss and communication dropout. It demonstrates scalability and resilience, supporting distributed operation without a central estimator.
AI.conclusion: A viable pathway for robust, scalable, GPS-denied cooperative multilift using vision-based sensing. The framework reduces reliance on centralized estimation and GPS, though real-world validation and marker-occlusion handling remain to be explored.

title: Reconfiguration of Squares Using a Constant Number of Moves Each
summary: Multi-robot motion planning is a hard problem. We investigate restricted variants of the problem where square robots are allowed to slide over an arbitrary curve to a new position only a constant number of times each. We show that the problem remains NP-hard in most cases, except when the squares have unit size and when the problem is unlabeled, i.e., the location of each square in the target configuration is left unspecified.

AI.tldr: Restricted multi-robot motion planning with curved sliding and a constant number of moves per robot remains NP-hard in most cases; the only identified tractable exception is unit-size squares in the unlabeled setting.
AI.motivation: Clarify the computational complexity boundary for restricted multi-robot motion planning variants, including curved motion and bounded moves, and compare labeled vs unlabeled target configurations.
AI.method: Complexity-theoretic analysis with reductions to establish NP-hardness in general; identification of a specific exception where the problem is not NP-hard (unit-sized robots, unlabeled).
AI.result: NP-hardness persists for most restricted variants; however, when robots are unit-sized and the problem is unlabeled (target locations unspecified), the problem is not NP-hard according to the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The constrained variants largely retain intractability, with a narrow, well-defined exception yielding tractability; this delineates the boundary between hard and potentially tractable instances in restricted multi-robot motion planning.

title: Fusion and Grouping Strategies in Deep Learning for Local Climate Zone Classification of Multimodal Remote Sensing Data
summary: Local Climate Zones (LCZs) give a zoning map to study urban structures and land use and analyze the impact of urbanization on local climate. Multimodal remote sensing enables LCZ classification, for which data fusion is significant for improving accuracy owing to the data complexity. However, there is a gap in a comprehensive analysis of the fusion mechanisms used in their deep learning (DL) classifier architectures. This study analyzes different fusion strategies in the multi-class LCZ classification models for multimodal data and grouping strategies based on inherent data characteristics. The different models involving Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) include: (i) baseline hybrid fusion (FM1), (ii) with self- and cross-attention mechanisms (FM2), (iii) with the multi-scale Gaussian filtered images (FM3), and (iv) weighted decision-level fusion (FM4). Ablation experiments are conducted to study the pixel-, feature-, and decision-level fusion effects in the model performance. Grouping strategies include band grouping (BG) within the data modalities and label merging (LM) in the ground truth. Our analysis is exclusively done on the So2Sat LCZ42 dataset, which consists of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) and Multispectral Imaging (MSI) image pairs. Our results show that FM1 consistently outperforms simple fusion methods. FM1 with BG and LM is found to be the most effective approach among all fusion strategies, giving an overall accuracy of 76.6\%. Importantly, our study highlights the effect of these strategies in improving prediction accuracy for the underrepresented classes. Our code and processed datasets are available at https://github.com/GVCL/LCZC-MultiModalHybridFusion

AI.tldr: Multimodal fusion strategies for LCZ classification with CNNs were compared on the So2Sat LCZ42 dataset; the baseline hybrid fusion (FM1) with band grouping (BG) and label merging (LM) achieves the best overall accuracy of 76.6%, outperforming other fusion schemes and highlighting improvements for underrepresented classes.
AI.motivation: To enhance LCZ classification accuracy in multimodal remote sensing by systematically analyzing fusion mechanisms and grouping strategies within CNN-based classifiers, and to provide a reproducible framework.
AI.method: Evaluate four fusion schemes (FM1 baseline hybrid fusion; FM2 self-/cross-attention; FM3 multi-scale Gaussian filtered images; FM4 weighted decision-level fusion) with ablations at pixel-, feature-, and decision-level fusion. Apply grouping strategies (band grouping within modalities; label merging in ground truth) on the So2Sat LCZ42 dataset (SAR + MSI). Report overall accuracy and analyze class performance; provide code and processed data.
AI.result: FM1 consistently outperforms simple fusion approaches; FM1 with BG and LM yields the best performance with an overall accuracy of 76.6%, and the approach improves accuracy for underrepresented LCZ classes.
AI.conclusion: Simple, well-structured fusion (FM1) combined with band grouping and label merging is the most effective strategy among those tested for multimodal LCZ classification on So2Sat LCZ42, underscoring the importance of fusion design and data grouping; the work offers a reproducible framework and resources for future multimodal LCZ studies.

title: Delta-Crosscoder: Robust Crosscoder Model Diffing in Narrow Fine-Tuning Regimes
summary: Model diffing methods aim to identify how fine-tuning changes a model's internal representations. Crosscoders approach this by learning shared dictionaries of interpretable latent directions between base and fine-tuned models. However, existing formulations struggle with narrow fine-tuning, where behavioral changes are localized and asymmetric. We introduce Delta-Crosscoder, which combines BatchTopK sparsity with a delta-based loss prioritizing directions that change between models, plus an implicit contrastive signal from paired activations on matched inputs. Evaluated across 10 model organisms, including synthetic false facts, emergent misalignment, subliminal learning, and taboo word guessing (Gemma, LLaMA, Qwen; 1B-9B parameters), Delta-Crosscoder reliably isolates latent directions causally responsible for fine-tuned behaviors and enables effective mitigation, outperforming SAE-based baselines, while matching the Non-SAE-based. Our results demonstrate that crosscoders remain a powerful tool for model diffing.

AI.tldr: Delta-Crosscoder introduces BatchTopK sparsity with a delta-based loss and implicit contrastive signals to isolate latent directions responsible for narrow fine-tuning changes, achieving robust mitigation and outperforming SAE baselines across multiple model families.
AI.motivation: Existing crosscoders struggle with localized, asymmetric changes from narrow fine-tuning; reliable diffing is needed to identify causal latent directions for targeted mitigation.
AI.method: Delta-Crosscoder extends the crosscoder framework by (1) applying BatchTopK sparsity to identify shared, interpretable latent directions; (2) using a delta-based loss that prioritizes directions that change between base and fine-tuned models; (3) leveraging paired activations on matched inputs as an implicit contrastive signal; evaluated on 10 model families (Gemma, LLaMA, Qwen) with 1B–9B parameters, including synthetic false facts, emergent misalignment, subliminal learning, and taboo word guessing.
AI.result: Consistently isolates latent directions causally linked to fine-tuned behaviors and enables effective mitigation; outperforms SAE-based baselines and matches non-SAE baselines; demonstrates that crosscoders remain a powerful tool for model diffing across diverse tasks and model sizes.
AI.conclusion: Delta-Crosscoder advances model diffing for narrow fine-tuning by leveraging delta-driven sparsity and contrastive signals, enabling precise causal attribution of behavior changes and practical mitigation; supports continued effectiveness of crosscoders in diffing heterogeneous models.

title: Discovering mathematical concepts through a multi-agent system
summary: Mathematical concepts emerge through an interplay of processes, including experimentation, efforts at proof, and counterexamples. In this paper, we present a new multi-agent model for computational mathematical discovery based on this observation. Our system, conceived with research in mind, poses its own conjectures and then attempts to prove them, making decisions informed by this feedback and an evolving data distribution. Inspired by the history of Euler's conjecture for polyhedra and an open challenge in the literature, we benchmark with the task of autonomously recovering the concept of homology from polyhedral data and knowledge of linear algebra. Our system completes this learning problem. Most importantly, the experiments are ablations, statistically testing the value of the complete dynamic and controlling for experimental setup. They support our main claim: that the optimisation of the right combination of local processes can lead to surprisingly well-aligned notions of mathematical interestingness.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent autonomous discovery framework yields homology understanding from polyhedral data and linear algebra, with ablation experiments showing that a carefully tuned mix of local processes can generate mathematically meaningful notions of interest.
AI.motivation: Mathematical concepts emerge from iterative experimentation, proof attempts, and counterexamples. Automating discovery helps reveal how local processes interact to create high-level mathematical insight. The homology-from-polyhedral-data task, grounded in Euler's conjecture history, provides a concrete benchmark for this dynamic.
AI.method: A multi-agent system generates conjectures and attempts proofs, with decisions guided by feedback and an evolving data distribution. The setup benchmarks autonomous recovery of homology from polyhedral data using linear algebra, and includes ablation studies to isolate the value of the complete dynamic and the experimental setup.
AI.result: The system successfully recovers the concept of homology from the given data. Ablation experiments support the main claim that the right combination of local processes yields behavior aligned with mathematical interestingness.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic optimization of local processes can drive effective autonomous mathematical discovery, suggesting a generalizable approach for AI-assisted discovery across mathematical domains.

title: Risk-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Mobile Manipulation
summary: For robots to successfully transition from lab settings to everyday environments, they must begin to reason about the risks associated with their actions and make informed, risk-aware decisions. This is particularly true for robots performing mobile manipulation tasks, which involve both interacting with and navigating within dynamic, unstructured spaces. However, existing whole-body controllers for mobile manipulators typically lack explicit mechanisms for risk-sensitive decision-making under uncertainty. To our knowledge, we are the first to (i) learn risk-aware visuomotor policies for mobile manipulation conditioned on egocentric depth observations with runtime-adjustable risk sensitivity, and (ii) show risk-aware behaviours can be transferred through Imitation Learning (IL) to a visuomotor policy conditioned on egocentric depth observations. Our method achieves this by first training a privileged teacher policy using Distributional Reinforcement Learning (DRL), with a risk-neutral distributional critic. Distortion risk-metrics are then applied to the critic's predicted return distribution to calculate risk-adjusted advantage estimates used in policy updates to achieve a range of risk-aware behaviours. We then distil teacher policies with IL to obtain risk-aware student policies conditioned on egocentric depth observations. We perform extensive evaluations demonstrating that our trained visuomotor policies exhibit risk-aware behaviour (specifically achieving better worst-case performance) while performing reactive whole-body motions in unmapped environments, leveraging live depth observations for perception.

AI.tldr: Learns risk-aware visuomotor policies for mobile manipulation using a privileged DRL teacher, distills via imitation learning to depth-conditioned student policies, enabling runtime-adjustable risk sensitivity and improved worst-case performance in unmapped, dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: Real-world mobile manipulation demands risk-sensitive decision-making under uncertainty; existing whole-body controllers lack explicit risk awareness, hindering safe, robust operation in unstructured environments.
AI.method: Train a privileged teacher policy with Distributional Reinforcement Learning (DRL) using a risk-neutral critic; apply distortion risk metrics to the critic’s return distribution to produce risk-adjusted advantages for policy updates; distill the teacher via Imitation Learning to obtain risk-aware student policies conditioned on egocentric depth observations; evaluate on reactive whole-body motions in unmapped environments with live depth sensing.
AI.result: The approach yields policies that exhibit risk-aware behaviors, notably improved worst-case performance, while executing reactive whole-body motions using live depth sensors in unstructured settings; the learned policies can be transferred to depth-conditioned visuomotor control through imitation learning.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable pipeline for risk-aware visuomotor control in mobile manipulation, providing runtime-adjustable risk sensitivity and successful transfer from privileged DRL teachers to depth-conditioned student policies.

title: Structural Properties of Shortest Flip Sequences Between Plane Spanning Trees
summary:
We study the reconfiguration of plane spanning trees on point sets in the plane in convex position, where a reconfiguration step (flip) replaces one edge with another, yielding again a plane spanning tree. The flip distance between two trees is then the minimum number of flips needed to transform one tree into the other. We study structural properties of shortest flip sequences.
  The folklore happy edge conjecture suggests that any edge shared by both the initial and target tree is never flipped in a shortest flip sequence. The more recent parking edge conjecture, which would have implied the happy edge conjecture, states that there exist shortest flip sequences which use only edges of the start and target tree, and edges in the convex hull of the point set. Finally, another conjecture that is implicit in the literature is the reparking conjecture which states that no edge is flipped more than twice. Essentially all recent flip algorithms respect these three conjectures and the properties they imply. We study cases in which the latter two conjectures hold and disprove them for the general setting.
  (Shortened abstract due to arXiv restrictions.)

AI.tldr: Analyzes flip-distance reconfiguration of plane spanning trees on convex-position point sets; shows that the parking-edge and reparking conjectures fail in general, while exploring restricted cases where these conjectures may hold; highlights limitations of current flip-based algorithms.
AI.motivation: Understand the combinatorial structure of plane spanning-tree flips to inform geometric reconfiguration algorithms and related graph-theoretic problems.
AI.method: Theoretical, structural analysis of shortest flip sequences for plane spanning trees on convex-position point sets; assesses three conjectures (happy edge, parking-edge, reparking) and constructs case studies to test their validity, with emphasis on general vs. restricted settings.
AI.result: In the general setting, both the parking-edge and reparking conjectures fail. The paper also investigates restricted cases where these conjectures can hold, implying that no single conjecture universally governs flip sequences across all instances; the status of the happy-edge conjecture is discussed but not definitively settled in this abstract.
AI.conclusion: Common flip-based algorithms cannot universally rely on parking-edge or reparking properties. The findings motivate developing new invariants or criteria to characterize shortest flip sequences and delineate when restricted conjectures may apply.

title: Structure-Guided Histopathology Synthesis via Dual-LoRA Diffusion
summary:
Histopathology image synthesis plays an important role in tissue restoration, data augmentation, and modeling of tumor microenvironments. However, existing generative methods typically address restoration and generation as separate tasks, although both share the same objective of structure-consistent tissue synthesis under varying degrees of missingness, and often rely on weak or inconsistent structural priors that limit realistic cellular organization.
  We propose Dual-LoRA Controllable Diffusion, a unified centroid-guided diffusion framework that jointly supports Local Structure Completion and Global Structure Synthesis within a single model. Multi-class nuclei centroids serve as lightweight and annotation-efficient spatial priors, providing biologically meaningful guidance under both partial and complete image absence. Two task-specific LoRA adapters specialize the shared backbone for local and global objectives without retraining separate diffusion models. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art GAN and diffusion baselines across restoration and synthesis tasks.
  For local completion, LPIPS computed within the masked region improves from 0.1797 (HARP) to 0.1524, and for global synthesis, FID improves from 225.15 (CoSys) to 76.04, indicating improved structural fidelity and realism. Our approach achieves more faithful structural recovery in masked regions and substantially improved realism and morphology consistency in full synthesis, supporting scalable pan-cancer histopathology modeling.

AI.tldr: Unified diffusion framework for histopathology restoration and synthesis guided by nucleus centroids, with task-specific LoRA adapters for local and global structure objectives.
AI.motivation: To bridge restoration and generation tasks under varying degrees of missing data using biologically meaningful, annotation-efficient priors, enabling consistent, structure‑preserving tissue synthesis.
AI.method: A single centroid-guided diffusion backbone with two task-specific LoRA adapters (local and global). Multi-class nuclei centroids serve as spatial priors; no need to retrain separate diffusion models. The model jointly handles Local Structure Completion and Global Structure Synthesis under partial/complete absence.
AI.result: Demonstrates consistent improvements over GAN and diffusion baselines: local LPIPS within masked region improves from 0.1797 (HARP) to 0.1524; global FID improves from 225.15 (CoSys) to 76.04. Shows faithful local recovery and realistic full synthesis; supports scalable pan-cancer histopathology modeling.
AI.conclusion: Unified, annotation-efficient diffusion approach with centroid guidance and LoRA adapters yields substantial gains in structural fidelity and realism for histopathology restoration and synthesis across tasks.

title: Thin Keys, Full Values: Reducing KV Cache via Low-Dimensional Attention Selection
summary: Standard transformer attention uses identical dimensionality for queries, keys, and values ($d_q = d_k = d_v = \dmodel$). Our insight is that these components serve fundamentally different roles, and this symmetry is unnecessary. Queries and keys produce scalar attention weights (\emph{selection}), while values carry rich semantic representations (\emph{value transfer}). We argue that selection is an inherently lower-dimensional operation than value transfer, requiring only $\BigO(\log N)$ dimensions to distinguish among $N$ relevant patterns. We validate this hypothesis across seven experiments: (1)~positional selection tasks requiring just 1~dimension per head, (2)~content-based retrieval requiring $\sim\!\log_2 N$ dimensions, (3--4)~WikiText-2 and WikiText-103 language modeling where $\dselect = \dmodel/4$ incurs only 4.3\% perplexity increase while reducing QK parameters by 75\%, (5)~post-training SVD compression of GPT-2, revealing keys to be far more compressible than queries, with lightweight QK fine-tuning recovering nearly all quality loss, (6)~a 125M-parameter LLaMA model confirming identical degradation ratios across architectures, and (7)~Mistral-7B (7.2B parameters), where SVD compression followed by QK fine-tuning achieves 75\% key cache savings at just 2.0\% residual quality cost. For existing models, SVD compression followed by QK fine-tuning (3 epochs on a small fraction of pretraining data) achieves 75\% key cache savings at $<$2\% residual quality cost. For a 7B-parameter model serving 128K context, asymmetric attention saves 25\,GB of KV cache per user, enabling approximately 60\% more concurrent users on the same GPU.

AI.tldr: Asymmetric attention: reduce the dimensionality of queries/keys (Q/K) to a low-dimensional selection space (O(log N)) while keeping values (V) high-dimensional for richer semantics; this yields substantial QK compression and KV-cache savings with minimal perplexity/accuracy loss across multiple models and tasks, validated on seven experiments and several architectures.
AI.motivation: Challenging the conventional symmetric dimension choice (d_q = d_k = d_v = d_model) by recognizing that Q/K primarily perform selection of relevant patterns, a low-dimensional operation, whereas V carries semantic information. The goal is to reduce memory and compute without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: Introduce asymmetric attention with lower-dimensional Q/K per head (d_select on the order of log N); test various settings including 1-d per head for positional tasks; apply content-based retrieval requiring ~log2 N dims; evaluate language modeling on WikiText-2/103 with d_select = d_model/4; perform post-training SVD compression of Q/K (keys more compressible than queries) with lightweight Q/K fine-tuning; validate across models (GPT-2, LLaMA-125M, Mistral-7B) and hardware implications (KV cache).
AI.result: Empirical results across seven experiments show that selection can be performed in far lower dimensions than the value transfer (V) path. For WikiText, d_select = d_model/4 yields only 4.3% perplexity increase while reducing QK parameters by 75%. SVD compression reveals keys are more compressible than queries, with Q/K fine-tuning recovering most quality. In 7B Mistral and other models, 75% KV cache savings are achieved with about 2% residual quality loss; 128K context yields ~25 GB KV cache reduction enabling ~60% more concurrent users. The degradation ratios between architectures remain consistent.
AI.conclusion: Asymmetric Q/K/V attention is a viable design pattern for efficient transformers. Reducing Q/K dimensionality substantially lowers memory and KV-cache requirements with modest performance penalties, and SVD-based Q/K compression further enhances efficiency. The approach generalizes across architectures and contexts, suggesting a broader shift away from strict symmetry in transformer attention.

title: Adaptive Memory Admission Control for LLM Agents
summary: LLM-based agents increasingly rely on long-term memory to support multi-session reasoning and interaction, yet current systems provide little control over what information is retained. In practice, agents either accumulate large volumes of conversational content, including hallucinated or obsolete facts, or depend on opaque, fully LLM-driven memory policies that are costly and difficult to audit. As a result, memory admission remains a poorly specified and weakly controlled component in agent architectures. To address this gap, we propose Adaptive Memory Admission Control (A-MAC), a framework that treats memory admission as a structured decision problem. A-MAC decomposes memory value into five complementary and interpretable factors: future utility, factual confidence, semantic novelty, temporal recency, and content type prior. The framework combines lightweight rule-based feature extraction with a single LLM-assisted utility assessment, and learns domain-adaptive admission policies through cross-validated optimization. This design enables transparent and efficient control over long-term memory.
