Experiments on the LoCoMo benchmark show that A-MAC achieves a superior precision-recall tradeoff, improving F1 to 0.583 while reducing latency by 31% compared to state-of-the-art LLM-native memory systems. Ablation results identify content type prior as the most influential factor for reliable memory admission. These findings demonstrate that explicit and interpretable admission control is a critical design principle for scalable and reliable memory in LLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: A-MAC frames memory admission as a structured decision problem, using five interpretable factors and lightweight features plus a single LLM-assisted utility to learn domain-adaptive policies, yielding improved memory control with better F1 and lower latency.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of transparent, controllable memory management in LLM agents, which either accumulates stale/hallucinated data or relies on opaque policies.
AI.method: Decompose memory value into five factors (future utility, factual confidence, semantic novelty, temporal recency, content type prior); apply lightweight rule-based feature extraction; use a single LLM-assisted utility assessment; learn domain-adaptive admission policies via cross-validated optimization.
AI.result: On LoCoMo, achieves better precision-recall (F1=0.583) and 31% latency reduction versus state-of-the-art LLM-native memory systems; ablation shows content type prior as the most influential factor.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, interpretable memory-admission control is essential for scalable, reliable memory in LLM-based agents.

title: ELLIPSE: Evidential Learning for Robust Waypoints and Uncertainties
summary: Robust waypoint prediction is crucial for mobile robots operating in open-world, safety-critical settings. While Imitation Learning (IL) methods have demonstrated great success in practice, they are susceptible to distribution shifts: the policy can become dangerously overconfident in unfamiliar states. In this paper, we present \textit{ELLIPSE}, a method building on multivariate deep evidential regression to output waypoints and multivariate Student-t predictive distributions in a single forward pass. To reduce covariate-shift-induced overconfidence under viewpoint and pose perturbations near expert trajectories, we introduce a lightweight domain augmentation procedure that synthesizes plausible viewpoint/pose variations without collecting additional demonstrations. To improve uncertainty reliability under environment/domain shift (e.g., unseen staircases), we apply a post-hoc isotonic recalibration on probability integral transform (PIT) values so that prediction sets remain plausible during deployment. We ground the discussion and experiments in staircase waypoint prediction, where obtaining robust waypoint and uncertainty is pivotal. Extensive real world evaluations show that \textit{ELLIPSE} improves both task success rate and uncertainty coverage compared to baselines.

AI.tldr: ELLIPSE combines multivariate deep evidential regression to predict waypoints and a joint multivariate Student-t predictive distribution in a single pass, with lightweight domain augmentation to mitigate covariate shifts near expert trajectories and a post-hoc isotonic recalibration of PIT values to maintain calibration under environment shifts. Evaluated on staircase waypoint prediction, it reports improved task success and uncertainty coverage versus baselines.
AI.motivation: Imitation Learning often fails under distribution shifts, leading to overconfident decisions in unfamiliar states. There is a need for reliable, calibrated uncertainty and robust predictions in open-world, safety-critical robotics tasks such as waypoint planning.
AI.method: Use multivariate deep evidential regression to output both waypoint predictions and a multivariate Student-t predictive distribution in one forward pass. Apply lightweight domain augmentation to synthesize viewpoint/pose variations near expert trajectories without extra demonstrations. After deployment, perform post-hoc isotonic recalibration on PIT values to preserve plausible prediction sets under domain shifts.
AI.result: Real-world evaluations on staircase waypoint prediction show that ELLIPSE improves both task success rate and uncertainty coverage compared to baselines and baselines with standard calibration methods.
AI.conclusion: ELLIPSE provides robust, calibrated waypoint predictions under covariate and environment shifts by combining evidential regression with domain augmentation and PIT recalibration, offering a promising approach for safe, reliable navigation in open-world settings.

title: What induces plane structures in complete graph drawings?
summary: This paper considers the task of connecting points on a piece of paper by drawing a curve between each pair of them. Under mild assumptions, we prove that many pairwise disjoint curves are unavoidable if either of the following rules is obeyed: any two adjacent curves do not cross, or any two non-adjacent curves cross at most once. Here, two curves are called adjacent if they share an endpoint. On the other hand, we demonstrate how to draw all curves such that any two adjacent curves cross exactly once, any two non-adjacent curves cross at least once and at most twice, and thus no two curves are disjoint. Furthermore, we analyze the emergence of disjoint curves without these mild assumptions, and characterize the plane structures in complete graph drawings guaranteed by each of the rules above.

AI.tldr: Examines when pairwise-disjoint curves are unavoidable under two crossing rules, provides a construction with no disjoint curves under a stronger rule, and analyzes when disjoint occurrences arise and the resulting planar structures.
AI.motivation: To understand how local crossing constraints influence global disjointness in topological graph drawings, with application to plane structure characterization in complete graphs.
AI.method: Combinatorial/topological proofs; proving unavoidability under mild assumptions; constructing a universal-crossing drawing; analyzing emergent disjointness without the assumptions; characterizing plane structures guaranteed by each rule.
AI.result: Under mild rules (adjacent curves do not cross, or non-adjacent cross at most once), many pairwise disjoint curves are unavoidable. There exists a drawing in which any two adjacent cross exactly once and any two non-adjacent cross at least once and at most twice, yielding zero disjoint curves. Without these mild assumptions, disjoint curves may emerge; the plane structures guaranteed by each rule are characterized.
AI.conclusion: Crossing rules critically govern disjointness in complete graph drawings; some rules enforce disjointness, others permit universal crossing patterns; the work provides a structural taxonomy of plane drawings under these rules.

title: Agent Memory Below the Prompt: Persistent Q4 KV Cache for Multi-Agent LLM Inference on Edge Devices
comment: 24 pages, 6 figures, 16 tables. Open-source implementation at https://github.com/yshk-mxim/agent-memory
summary: Multi-agent LLM systems on edge devices face a memory management problem: device RAM is too small to hold every agent's KV cache simultaneously. On Apple M4 Pro with 10.2 GB of cache budget, only 3 agents fit at 8K context in FP16. A 10-agent workflow must constantly evict and reload caches. Without persistence, every eviction forces a full re-prefill through the model -- 15.7 seconds per agent at 4K context. We address this by persisting each agent's KV cache to disk in 4-bit quantized format and reloading it directly into the attention layer, eliminating redundant O(n) prefill computation via direct cache restoration. The system comprises three components: a block pool providing per-agent isolated Q4 KV caches in safetensors format, a BatchQuantizedKVCache for concurrent inference over multiple agents' quantized caches, and cross-phase context injection that accumulates attention state across conversation phases without re-computation. Evaluated on three architectures (Gemma 3 12B, dense GQA, 48 layers; DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Lite 16B, MoE MLA, 27 layers; Llama 3.1 8B, dense GQA, 32 layers), cache restoration reduces time-to-first-token by up to 136x (Gemma: 22--136x at 4K--32K; DeepSeek: 11--76x at 4K--32K; Llama: 24--111x at 4K--16K; 3--10x at 1K). Q4 quantization fits 4x more agent contexts into fixed device memory than FP16. Perplexity measured with actual Q4 KV caches shows -0.7% for Gemma, +2.8% for Llama, and +3.0% for DeepSeek. Open-source at https://github.com/yshk-mxim/agent-memory

AI.tldr: Quantized KV cache persistence enables large-scale multi-agent LLMs on edge devices by eliminating re-prefill, achieving up to 136x speedups and 4x more agent contexts per memory budget.
AI.motivation: Edge-device RAM limits prevent storing all agents' KV caches; eviction causes costly recomputation; need persistent, quantized cache storage integrated with attention.
AI.method: Three components: block pool with per-agent Q4 KV caches in safetensors; BatchQuantizedKVCache for multi-agent inference over quantized caches; cross-phase context injection to accumulate attention state across phases without recomputation; evaluation on Gemma/DeepSeek/Llama with 4K-32K contexts; perplexity metrics.
AI.result: TTF reduced up to 136x; Q4 allows 4x more contexts; perplexity changes: Gemma -0.7%, Llama +2.8%, DeepSeek +3.0%; open-source at repo.
AI.conclusion: Quantized KV caching enables scalable, memory-efficient multi-agent LLM inference on edge devices with negligible/perplexity impact; contributes practical system design and open-source implementation.

title: Self-Attribution Bias: When AI Monitors Go Easy on Themselves
summary: Agentic systems increasingly rely on language models to monitor their own behavior. For example, coding agents may self critique generated code for pull request approval or assess the safety of tool-use actions. We show that this design pattern can fail when the action is presented in a previous or in the same assistant turn instead of being presented by the user in a user turn. We define self-attribution bias as the tendency of a model to evaluate an action as more correct or less risky when the action is implicitly framed as its own, compared to when the same action is evaluated under off-policy attribution. Across four coding and tool-use datasets, we find that monitors fail to report high-risk or low-correctness actions more often when evaluation follows a previous assistant turn in which the action was generated, compared to when the same action is evaluated in a new context presented in a user turn. In contrast, explicitly stating that the action comes from the monitor does not by itself induce self-attribution bias. Because monitors are often evaluated on fixed examples rather than on their own generated actions, these evaluations can make monitors appear more reliable than they actually are in deployment, leading developers to unknowingly deploy inadequate monitors in agentic systems.

AI.tldr: Self-attribution bias degrades monitors that evaluate their own actions in agentic systems; when actions are framed as the assistant's own (previous assistant turn), monitors under-report high-risk or low-accuracy actions compared to when the action is framed in a user turn. Explicit attribution that the action is from the monitor does not by itself induce the bias. Off-policy evaluation on fixed examples can overestimate monitor reliability, risking deployment of inadequate monitors.
AI.motivation: Assess how self-attribution bias affects the reliability of self-monitoring components in language-model–driven agents, particularly when actions are generated or framed by the assistant, and to quantify bias across coding/tool-use tasks.
AI.method: Examine four coding and tool-use datasets. Compare monitoring outcomes under two contexts: (i) action generated in a previous assistant turn (off-policy framing) vs (ii) the same action evaluated in a new user turn (on-policy framing). Test whether explicitly stating that the action originates from the monitor mitigates bias. Use metrics on reporting high-risk and low-correctness actions.
AI.result: Monitors more often fail to report high-risk or low-correctness actions when evaluation follows a previous assistant turn in which the action was generated, compared to evaluating the same action in a user turn. Explicitly attributing the action to the monitor does not by itself induce self-attribution bias. Evaluations on fixed examples may overstate monitor reliability, risking deployment of inadequate monitors.
AI.conclusion: Self-attribution bias is a practical threat to the reliability of monitors in agentic systems. Evaluation practices that rely on off-policy or fixed exemplars can misrepresent monitor performance. To mitigate, evaluations should reflect deployment contexts (on-policy), and explicit attribution alone is insufficient to prevent bias. Consider design and evaluation strategies that decouple monitor-generated actions from biased evaluation contexts.

title: RoboMME: Benchmarking and Understanding Memory for Robotic Generalist Policies
summary: Memory is critical for long-horizon and history-dependent robotic manipulation. Such tasks often involve counting repeated actions or manipulating objects that become temporarily occluded. Recent vision-language-action (VLA) models have begun to incorporate memory mechanisms; however, their evaluations remain confined to narrow, non-standardized settings. This limits their systematic understanding, comparison, and progress measurement. To address these challenges, we introduce RoboMME: a large-scale standardized benchmark for evaluating and advancing VLA models in long-horizon, history-dependent scenarios. Our benchmark comprises 16 manipulation tasks constructed under a carefully designed taxonomy that evaluates temporal, spatial, object, and procedural memory. We further develop a suite of 14 memory-augmented VLA variants built on the π0.5 backbone to systematically explore different memory representations across multiple integration strategies. Experimental results show that the effectiveness of memory representations is highly task-dependent, with each design offering distinct advantages and limitations across different tasks. Videos and code can be found at our website https://robomme.github.io.

AI.tldr: RoboMME introduces a large-scale standardized benchmark for evaluating memory-enabled vision-language-action models in long-horizon robotic manipulation, with 16 tasks and 14 memory-augmented VLA variants, revealing task-dependent effectiveness of memory designs.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of standardized, systematic evaluation for memory-based VLA models in long-horizon, history-dependent manipulation tasks, enabling fair comparison and progress tracking.
AI.method: Construct RoboMME with 16 tasks organized by a taxonomy assessing temporal, spatial, object, and procedural memory; implement 14 memory-augmented VLA variants based on the π0.5 backbone, exploring diverse memory representations and integration strategies.
AI.result: Memory representations show highly task-dependent effectiveness; no single design dominates across tasks, with each variant offering different trade-offs in accuracy, robustness, or generalization across the benchmark tasks.
AI.conclusion: RoboMME provides a standardized platform and a family of memory-augmented models to systematically study memory for long-horizon manipulation, guiding design choices and enabling reproducible progress; resources are publicly available.

title: Drone Air Traffic Control: Tracking a Set of Moving Objects with Minimal Power
summary:
A common sensing problem is to use a set of stationary tracking locations to monitor a collection of moving devices: Given $n$ objects that need to be tracked, each following its own trajectory, and $m$ stationary traffic control stations, each with a sensing region of adjustable range; how should we adjust the individual sensor ranges in order to optimize energy consumption? We provide both negative theoretical and positive practical results for this important and natural challenge.
  On the theoretical side, we show that even if all objects move at constant speed along straight lines, no polynomial-time algorithm can guarantee optimal coverage for a given starting solution. On the practical side, we present an algorithm based on geometric insights that is able to find optimal solutions for the $\min \max$ variant of the problem, which aims at minimizing peak power consumption. Runtimes for instances with 500 moving objects and 25 stations are in the order of seconds for scenarios that take minutes to play out in the real world, demonstrating real-time capability of our methods.

AI.tldr: The abstract studies energy-efficient adjustment of sensing radii for stationary stations monitoring moving objects; it proves hardness for optimal coverage and presents a geometric algorithm that solves the min-max (peak-power) variant with real-time performance on sizeable instances.
AI.motivation: Energy consumption in sensor networks is dominated by sensing ranges; adapting radii to track moving devices can reduce energy use while preserving coverage.
AI.method: Theoretical result: even with simple motion models (constant speed, straight lines), no polynomial-time algorithm can guarantee optimal coverage from a given starting solution. Practical result: a geometry-based algorithm that finds optimal solutions for the min-max variant (minimize peak power). Empirical evaluation shows runtimes of seconds for instances with 500 moving objects and 25 stations, indicating real-time capability.
AI.result: Hardness of guaranteeing optimal coverage in poly-time under the given model; an exact, geometry-based algorithm for the min-max variant achieving optimality; real-time performance on sizable instances.
AI.conclusion: The work reveals a dual landscape: intrinsic computational hardness for general optimal coverage and a practical, exact solution for a meaningful special case. It demonstrates potential for real-time energy management in sensor networks and invites further exploration of broader motion models and robustness.

title: PinPoint: Evaluation of Composed Image Retrieval with Explicit Negatives, Multi-Image Queries, and Paraphrase Testing
summary: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) has made significant progress, yet current benchmarks are limited to single ground-truth answers and lack the annotations needed to evaluate false positive avoidance, robustness and multi-image reasoning. We present PinPoint, a comprehensive real world benchmark with 7,635 queries and 329K relevance judgments across 23 query categories. PinPoint advances the field by providing: (1) multiple correct answers (averaging 9.1 per query) (2) explicit hard negatives, (3) six instruction paraphrases per query for robustness testing, (4) multi-image composition support (13.4% of queries), and (5) demographic metadata for fairness evaluation. Based on our analysis of 20+ methods across 4 different major paradigms, we uncover three significant drawbacks: The best methods while achieving mAP@10 of 28.5%, still retrieves irrelevant results (hard negatives) 9% of the time. The best models also exhibit 25.1% performance variation across paraphrases, indicating significant potential for enhancing current CIR techniques. Multi-image queries performs 40 to 70% worse across different methods. To overcome these new issues uncovered by our evaluation framework, we propose a training-free reranking method based on an off-the-shelf MLLM that can be applied to any existing system to bridge the gap. We release the complete dataset, including all images, queries, annotations, retrieval index, and benchmarking code.

AI.tldr: PinPoint provides a large, real-world Composed Image Retrieval benchmark with multiple correct answers, explicit hard negatives, paraphrase robustness tests, multi-image support, and demographic metadata; reveals current methods’ false-positives and robustness gaps and proposes a training-free reranking with an off-the-shelf MLLM; dataset and code released.
AI.motivation: Current CIR benchmarks are limited to a single ground-truth and lack annotations for false positives, robustness to paraphrase, multi-image reasoning, and fairness considerations. PinPoint aims to address these gaps with richer annotations and realism.
AI.method: Constructed a real-world CIR benchmark: 7,635 queries and 329K relevance judgments across 23 categories. Features include multiple correct answers (avg 9.1 per query), explicit hard negatives, six paraphrase variants per query, 13.4% multi-image queries, and demographic metadata for fairness analysis. Evaluated 20+ methods across 4 paradigms; identified limitations (hard negatives, paraphrase sensitivity, and multi-image difficulty). Proposes a training-free reranking method using an off-the-shelf multimodal-language model (MLLM). Public release of data, images, queries, annotations, retrieval index, and benchmarking code.
AI.result: Best reported mAP@10 is 28.5%; despite this, hard negatives account for ~9% of retrieved results. Paraphrase stability shows 25.1% performance variation across paraphrases. Multi-image queries underperform by 40–70% across methods.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark reveals substantial gaps in current CIR approaches, especially regarding false positives, robustness to paraphrase, and multi-image reasoning. A practical, training-free reranking approach using MLLMs is proposed, and the dataset and evaluation tools are released to accelerate progress.

title: Flowers: A Warp Drive for Neural PDE Solvers
summary: We introduce Flowers, a neural architecture for learning PDE solution operators built entirely from multihead warps. Aside from pointwise channel mixing and a multiscale scaffold, Flowers use no Fourier multipliers, no dot-product attention, and no convolutional mixing. Each head predicts a displacement field and warps the mixed input features. Motivated by physics and computational efficiency, displacements are predicted pointwise, without any spatial aggregation, and nonlocality enters \emph{only} through sparse sampling at source coordinates, \emph{one} per head. Stacking warps in multiscale residual blocks yields Flowers, which implement adaptive, global interactions at linear cost. We theoretically motivate this design through three complementary lenses: flow maps for conservation laws, waves in inhomogeneous media, and a kinetic-theoretic continuum limit. Flowers achieve excellent performance on a broad suite of 2D and 3D time-dependent PDE benchmarks, particularly flows and waves. A compact 17M-parameter model consistently outperforms Fourier, convolution, and attention-based baselines of similar size, while a 150M-parameter variant improves over recent transformer-based foundation models with much more parameters, data, and training compute.

AI.tldr: Flowers is a neural PDE solver that uses a multihead warp architecture with pointwise displacement prediction and sparse sampling, avoiding Fourier multipliers, dot-product attention, and convolution. It delivers linear-cost, global interactions via stacked multiscale warps, achieving state-of-the-art performance on 2D/3D time-dependent PDE benchmarks with small to large models that outperform Fourier, convolution, and attention baselines.
AI.motivation: To learn efficient, scalable PDE solution operators that capture global effects without expensive operators (FFT, attention, or dense convolutions) and that respect physics-like properties, enabling accurate 2D/3D time-dependent PDE modeling at low computational cost.
AI.method: Flowers uses multiple heads that predict a displacement field; each head warps the input features by sampling sparsely at source coordinates (one sample per head). There is no spatial aggregation within heads; nonlocal interactions arise through these sparse samples. The architecture stacks warps in multiscale residual blocks, enabling adaptive, global interactions at linear cost. The design is motivated theoretically via flow maps for conservation laws, wave propagation in inhomogeneous media, and a kinetic-theoretic continuum limit.
AI.result: The model achieves strong performance on 2D and 3D time-dependent PDE benchmarks, especially flows and waves. A compact 17M-parameter variant outperforms Fourier, convolution, and attention-based baselines of similar size, while a 150M-parameter version surpasses recent transformer-based foundation models that require substantially more data and compute.
AI.conclusion: Flowers provide an efficient, scalable PDE solution-operator with global coupling realized through warp-based displacements and sparse sampling, supported by theoretical motivation and strong empirical results across medium-to-large models.

title: ECG-MoE: Mixture-of-Expert Electrocardiogram Foundation Model
summary: Electrocardiography (ECG) analysis is crucial for cardiac diagnosis, yet existing foundation models often fail to capture the periodicity and diverse features required for varied clinical tasks. We propose ECG-MoE, a hybrid architecture that integrates multi-model temporal features with a cardiac period-aware expert module. Our approach uses a dual-path Mixture-of-Experts to separately model beat-level morphology and rhythm, combined with a hierarchical fusion network using LoRA for efficient inference. Evaluated on five public clinical tasks, ECG-MoE achieves state-of-the-art performance with 40% faster inference than multi-task baselines.

AI.tldr: ECG-MoE combines beat-level morphology and rhythm with a period-aware expert module to achieve state-of-the-art results with faster inference across five ECG tasks.
AI.motivation: ECG analysis requires capturing periodicity and diverse features across tasks; existing foundation models often fail to model rhythm and morphology jointly.
AI.method: A dual-path Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture that separately models beat-level morphology and rhythm, a hierarchical fusion network, and LoRA-based efficient adapters for scalable inference.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on five public clinical tasks with approximately 40% faster inference than multi-task baselines.
AI.conclusion: ECG-MoE effectively models cardiac periodicity and feature diversity, delivering superior accuracy and efficiency across multiple ECG tasks.

title: Autonomous Aerial Non-Destructive Testing: Ultrasound Inspection with a Commercial Quadrotor in an Unstructured Environment
summary: This work presents an integrated control and software architecture that enables arguably the first fully autonomous, contact-based non-destructive testing (NDT) using a commercial multirotor originally restricted to remotely-piloted operations. To allow autonomous operation with an off-the-shelf platform, we developed a real-time framework that interfaces directly with its onboard sensor suite. The architecture features a multi-rate control scheme: low-level control is executed at 200 Hz, force estimation at 100 Hz, while an admittance filter and trajectory planner operate at 50 Hz, ultimately supplying acceleration and yaw rate commands to the internal flight controller. We validate the system through physical experiments on a Flyability Elios 3 quadrotor equipped with an ultrasound payload. Relying exclusively on onboard sensing, the vehicle successfully performs autonomous NDT measurements within an unstructured, industrial-like environment. This work demonstrates the viability of retrofitting off-the-shelf platforms for autonomous physical interaction, paving the way for safe, contact-based inspection of hazardous and confined infrastructure.

AI.tldr: A real-time onboard autonomous contact-based NDT framework for a commercial multirotor with ultrasound payload, using a multi-rate control architecture (200 Hz low-level, 100 Hz force estimation, 50 Hz admittance/trajectory) validated on Flyability Elios 3; demonstrates retrofitting off-the-shelf platforms for autonomous safe inspection in unstructured environments.
AI.motivation: Enable autonomous contact-based non-destructive testing in hazardous or confined infrastructure while leveraging off-the-shelf multirotor platforms to improve safety and reduce reliance on remotely piloted operations.
AI.method: Developed an integrated control and software architecture that interfaces directly with the platform’s onboard sensor suite. Implemented a multi-rate control stack: 200 Hz for low-level control, 100 Hz force estimation, 50 Hz admittance filter and trajectory planner, which generate acceleration and yaw-rate commands to the internal flight controller. The system uses an ultrasound payload for NDT measurements, and operates entirely with onboard sensing.
AI.result: Physical experiments on a Flyability Elios 3 equipped with an ultrasound payload demonstrated autonomous NDT measurements in an unstructured, industrial-like environment, validating the viability of retrofitting off-the-shelf platforms for autonomous physical interaction.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the feasibility of autonomous contact-based inspection using commercially available multirotors by integrating real-time control, sensing, and planning onboard, enabling safe, autonomous inspection of hazardous or confined infrastructure.

title: Garment numbers of bi-colored point sets in the plane
summary: We consider colored variants of a class of geometric-combinatorial questions on $k$-gons and empty $k$-gons that have been started around 1935 by Erdős and Szekeres. In our setting we have $n$ points in general position in the plane, each one colored either red or blue. A structure on $k$ points is a geometric graph where the edges are spanned by (some of) these points and is called monochromatic if all $k$ points have the same color. Already for $k=4$ there exist interesting open problems. Most prominently, it is still open whether for any sufficiently large bichromatic set there always exists a convex empty, monochromatic quadrilateral. In order to shed more light on the underlying geometry we study the existence of five different monochromatic structures that all use exactly 4 points of a bichromatic point set. We provide several improved lower and upper bounds on the smallest $n$ such that every bichromatic set of at least $n$ points contains (some of) those monochromatic structures.

AI.tldr: Monochromatic 4-point structures in bichromatic point sets; improved bounds for the minimum n ensuring existence.
AI.motivation: To shed light on colored Erdős–Szekeres questions and progress on the open problem of convex empty monochromatic quadrilaterals, by studying five 4-point monochromatic configurations.
AI.method: Combinatorial-geometry analysis using extremal constructions and bounds to derive new lower and upper bounds for the threshold n.
AI.result: Improved lower and upper bounds on the minimal n that guarantees at least one of the five monochromatic 4-point structures in any bichromatic point set.
AI.conclusion: Advances understanding of colored geometric configurations; tighter thresholds for existence, guiding future work on colored Erdős–Szekeres type problems.

title: SGR3 Model: Scene Graph Retrieval-Reasoning Model in 3D
summary: 3D scene graphs provide a structured representation of object entities and their relationships, enabling high-level interpretation and reasoning for robots while remaining intuitively understandable to humans. Existing approaches for 3D scene graph generation typically combine scene reconstruction with graph neural networks (GNNs). However, such pipelines require multi-modal data that may not always be available, and their reliance on heuristic graph construction can constrain the prediction of relationship triplets. In this work, we introduce a Scene Graph Retrieval-Reasoning Model in 3D (SGR3 Model), a training-free framework that leverages multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for semantic scene graph generation. SGR3 Model bypasses the need for explicit 3D reconstruction. Instead, it enhances relational reasoning by incorporating semantically aligned scene graphs retrieved via a ColPali-style cross-modal framework. To improve retrieval robustness, we further introduce a weighted patch-level similarity selection mechanism that mitigates the negative impact of blurry or semantically uninformative regions. Experiments demonstrate that SGR3 Model achieves competitive performance compared to training-free baselines and on par with GNN-based expert models. Moreover, an ablation study on the retrieval module and knowledge base scale reveals that retrieved external information is explicitly integrated into the token generation process, rather than being implicitly internalized through abstraction.

AI.tldr: A training-free 3D scene graph generation framework (SGR3) uses retrieval-augmented generation with MLLMs to build semantic scene graphs without explicit 3D reconstruction, leveraging cross-modal retrieval of semantically aligned graphs and a patch-level similarity weighting to robustly select informative regions; achieves competitive results with training-free baselines and GNN-based models, with ablations showing explicit retrieval integration into generation.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D scene graph pipelines rely on multi-modal data and heuristic graph construction, often requiring expensive 3D reconstruction and training. There is a need for training-free, scalable semantic scene graph generation that can leverage external knowledge.
AI.method: SGR3 Model: a training-free framework that uses retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with multi-modal large language models (MLLMs). It retrieves semantically aligned scene graphs via a ColPali-style cross-modal framework and uses a weighted patch-level similarity selection to mitigate blurry/uninformative regions; no explicit 3D reconstruction is performed; retrieved information is integrated into token generation.
AI.result: The method achieves competitive performance against training-free baselines and is on par with GNN-based expert models. Ablation shows the retrieved external information is explicitly integrated into token generation rather than implicitly internalized through abstraction.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented reasoning with cross-modal retrieval can enable effective 3D scene graph generation without 3D reconstruction. The retrieval module and knowledge base scale are crucial factors, and explicit integration of retrieved knowledge into generation is validated by ablation.

title: Uncertainty-Calibrated Spatiotemporal Field Diffusion with Sparse Supervision
summary: Physical fields are typically observed only at sparse, time-varying sensor locations, making forecasting and reconstruction ill-posed and uncertainty-critical. We present SOLID, a mask-conditioned diffusion framework that learns spatiotemporal dynamics from sparse observations alone: training and evaluation use only observed target locations, requiring no dense fields and no pre-imputation. Unlike prior work that trains on dense reanalysis or simulations and only tests under sparsity, SOLID is trained end-to-end with sparse supervision only. SOLID conditions each denoising step on the measured values and their locations, and introduces a dual-masking objective that (i) emphasizes learning in unobserved void regions while (ii) upweights overlap pixels where inputs and targets provide the most reliable anchors. This strict sparse-conditioning pathway enables posterior sampling of full fields consistent with the measurements, achieving up to an order-of-magnitude improvement in probabilistic error and yielding calibrated uncertainty maps (\r{ho} > 0.7) under severe sparsity.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based SOLID learns spatiotemporal dynamics from sparse observations alone, enabling posterior sampling of full fields with calibrated uncertainties and large probabilistic error reduction under sparse sensing.
AI.motivation: Forecasting/reconstruction of physical fields from sparse, time-varying measurements is ill-posed and uncertainty-sensitive; existing methods rely on dense data or pre-imputation; there is a need for sparse-supervision approaches.
AI.method: Mask-conditioned diffusion model trained end-to-end with sparse supervision; denoising steps conditioned on measured values and locations; dual-masking objective emphasizing learning in unobserved regions and upweighting reliable overlaps; no dense fields or pre-imputation.
AI.result: Significant probabilistic error reduction (up to an order of magnitude); calibrated uncertainty maps with a high reliability metric (>0.7) under severe sparsity; enables posterior sampling of full fields consistent with measurements.
AI.conclusion: Strict sparse-conditioning is effective for learning from sparse data, enabling accurate full-field reconstructions and uncertainty quantification without dense training data.

title: Towards automated data analysis: A guided framework for LLM-based risk estimation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into critical decision-making pipelines, a trend that raises the demand for robust and automated data analysis. Current approaches to dataset risk analysis are limited to manual auditing methods which involve time-consuming and complex tasks, whereas fully automated analysis based on Artificial Intelligence (AI) suffers from hallucinations and issues stemming from AI alignment. To this end, this work proposes a framework for dataset risk estimation that integrates Generative AI under human guidance and supervision, aiming to set the foundations for a future automated risk analysis paradigm. Our approach utilizes LLMs to identify semantic and structural properties in database schemata, subsequently propose clustering techniques, generate the code for them and finally interpret the produced results. The human supervisor guides the model on the desired analysis and ensures process integrity and alignment with the task's objectives. A proof of concept is presented to demonstrate the feasibility of the framework's utility in producing meaningful results in risk assessment tasks.

AI.tldr: A semi-automated dataset risk analysis framework using Generative AI with human supervision, where LLMs analyze database schemata, propose clustering methods, generate code, and interpret results, with a human supervisor guiding the process; a proof-of-concept demonstrates feasibility.
AI.motivation: Manual auditing of dataset risks is time-consuming and complex; fully automated AI approaches suffer from hallucinations and alignment issues. A human-in-the-loop framework aims to balance automation with oversight to enable robust, scalable risk analysis.
AI.method: Leverage LLMs to extract semantic and structural properties from database schemata, propose clustering techniques, generate the corresponding code, and interpret the produced results. A human supervisor guides the analysis, ensuring alignment with objectives and preserving process integrity.
AI.result: A proof-of-concept demonstrates feasibility and usefulness of the framework in producing meaningful risk assessment results. Specific quantitative outcomes are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The framework establishes foundational steps toward future automated risk analysis, leveraging human supervision to mitigate AI limitations while enabling scalable, automated insights.

title: GIANT - Global Path Integration and Attentive Graph Networks for Multi-Agent Trajectory Planning
comment: Published in: 2025 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS)
summary: This paper presents a novel approach to multi-robot collision avoidance that integrates global path planning with local navigation strategies, utilizing attentive graph neural networks to manage dynamic interactions among agents. We introduce a local navigation model that leverages pre-planned global paths, allowing robots to adhere to optimal routes while dynamically adjusting to environmental changes. The models robustness is enhanced through the introduction of noise during training, resulting in superior performance in complex, dynamic environments. Our approach is evaluated against established baselines, including NH-ORCA, DRL-NAV, and GA3C-CADRL, across various structurally diverse simulated scenarios. The results demonstrate that our model achieves consistently higher success rates, lower collision rates, and more efficient navigation, particularly in challenging scenarios where baseline models struggle. This work offers an advancement in multi-robot navigation, with implications for robust performance in complex, dynamic environments with varying degrees of complexity, such as those encountered in logistics, where adaptability is essential for accommodating unforeseen obstacles and unpredictable changes.

AI.tldr: Hybrid multi-robot collision avoidance: global planning plus local navigation via attentive graph neural networks, with noise-augmented training; outperforms NH-ORCA, DRL-NAV, GA3C-CADRL in dynamic scenarios; implications for logistics.
AI.motivation: Address the need for reliable, scalable multi-robot navigation in dynamic environments by unifying global route optimality with robust local control.
AI.method: A hybrid architecture: pre-planned global paths guiding local navigation; an attentive graph neural network models dynamic inter-agent interactions; training includes noise to improve robustness; evaluation against NH-ORCA, DRL-NAV, GA3C-CADRL across diverse simulated scenarios.
AI.result: Higher success rates, lower collision rates, and more efficient navigation, especially in challenging scenarios where baselines struggle.
AI.conclusion: Advances robust multi-robot navigation with potential logistics applications; adaptable to unforeseen obstacles and environmental changes.

title: Revisiting Graph Modification via Disk Scaling: From One Radius to Interval-Based Radii
comment: Extended abstract will be presented at EuroCG'26; 46 pages, 11 figures
summary: For a fixed graph class $Π$, the goal of $Π$-Modification is to transform an input graph $G$ into a graph $H\inΠ$ using at most $k$ modifications. Vertex and edge deletions are common operations, and their (parameterized) complexity for various $Π$ is well-studied. Classic graph modification operations such as edge deletion do not consider the geometric nature of geometric graphs such as (unit) disk graphs. This led Fomin et al. [ITCS' 25] to initiate the study of disk scaling as a geometric graph modification operation for unit disk graphs: For a given radius $r$, each modified disk will be rescaled to radius $r$. In this paper, we generalize their model by allowing rescaled disks to choose a radius within a given interval $[r_{\min}, r_{\max}]$ and study the (parameterized) complexity (with respect to $k$) of the corresponding problem $Π$-Scaling. We show that $Π$-Scaling is in XP for every graph class $Π$ that can be recognized in polynomial time. Furthermore, we show that $Π$-Scaling: (1) is NP-hard and FPT for cluster graphs, (2) can be solved in polynomial time for complete graphs, and (3) is W[1]-hard for connected graphs. In particular, (1) and (2) answer open questions of Fomin et al. and (3) generalizes the hardness result for their variant where the set of scalable disks is restricted.

AI.tldr: Generalizes disk scaling on unit disk graphs by allowing radii to vary within [r_min, r_max], and studies Π-Scaling under k modifications. It yields XP for any polynomial-time recognizable graph class Π; proves NP-hard and FPT for cluster graphs; polynomial-time for complete graphs; W[1]-hard for connected graphs; resolves open questions and extends prior hardness results.
AI.motivation: To extend the geometric modification framework from fixed-radius unit disk graphs to scalable disks with a radius interval, and to map the parameterized complexity of Π-Scaling across graph classes, connecting to Fomin et al.'s work and addressing open questions.
AI.method: Introduce Π-Scaling as a generalization of disk scaling with radii chosen in [r_min, r_max]. Prove that the problem is in XP for all Π recognizable in polynomial time. Establish NP-hardness and fixed-parameter tractability (FPT) for cluster graphs, provide a polynomial-time algorithm for complete graphs, and show W[1]-hardness for connected graphs.
AI.result: A complete complexity landscape: (i) XP-membership for all Π recognizable in P; (ii) NP-hard and FPT for cluster graphs; (iii) polynomial-time solvability for complete graphs; (iv) W[1]-hardness for connected graphs.
AI.conclusion: The work generalizes the prior unit-disk scaling framework, settles complexity for several natural graph classes (cluster, complete, connected), and answers open questions raised by Fomin et al., while broadening the applicability of geometric graph modification models.

title: Spinverse: Differentiable Physics for Permeability-Aware Microstructure Reconstruction from Diffusion MRI
summary: Diffusion MRI (dMRI) is sensitive to microstructural barriers, yet most existing methods either assume impermeable boundaries or estimate voxel-level parameters without recovering explicit interfaces. We present Spinverse, a permeability-aware reconstruction method that inverts dMRI measurements through a fully differentiable Bloch-Torrey simulator. Spinverse represents tissue on a fixed tetrahedral grid and treats each interior face permeability as a learnable parameter; low-permeability faces act as diffusion barriers, so microstructural boundaries whose topology is not fixed a priori (up to the resolution of the ambient mesh) emerge without changing mesh connectivity or vertex positions. Given a target signal, we optimize face permeabilities by backpropagating a signal-matching loss through the PDE forward model, and recover an interface by thresholding the learned permeability field. To mitigate the ill-posedness of permeability inversion, we use mesh-based geometric priors; to avoid local minima, we use a staged multi-sequence optimization curriculum. Across a collection of synthetic voxel meshes, Spinverse reconstructs diverse geometries and demonstrates that sequence scheduling and regularization are critical to avoid outline-only solutions while improving both boundary accuracy and structural validity.

AI.tldr: A differentiable Bloch-Torrey PDE solver on a fixed tetrahedral mesh learns interior face permeabilities to recover microstructural interfaces from diffusion MRI signals, using priors and a multi-sequence curriculum to mitigate ill-posedness; validated on synthetic voxel meshes with claimed improvements in boundary accuracy and structural validity.
AI.motivation: To move beyond impermeable-boundary assumptions and voxel-level parameter estimation by explicitly reconstructing microstructural interfaces implied by dMRI data, up to mesh resolution.
AI.method: Represent tissue on a fixed tetrahedral grid; treat interior face permeabilities as learnable parameters; low-permeability faces act as diffusion barriers; backpropagate a signal-matching loss through a differentiable Bloch-Torrey forward model; recover an interface by thresholding the learned permeability field; use mesh-based geometric priors and a staged multi-sequence optimization curriculum to address ill-posedness and local minima.
AI.result: In synthetic voxel meshes, Spinverse reconstructs diverse geometries; sequence scheduling and regularization are critical to avoid outline-only solutions and to improve boundary accuracy and structural validity.
AI.conclusion: Permeability-based, differentiable PDE inversion on a fixed mesh can recover microstructural interfaces from dMRI signals when equipped with priors and careful optimization; the approach yields more faithful boundary geometry than voxel-level parameter estimates, at least in synthetic tests.

title: ZorBA: Zeroth-order Federated Fine-tuning of LLMs with Heterogeneous Block Activation
summary: Federated fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) enables collaborative tuning across distributed clients. However, due to the large size of LLMs, local updates in federated learning (FL) may incur substantial video random-access memory (VRAM) usage. Moreover, frequent model exchange may lead to significant communication overhead. To tackle these challenges, in this paper we propose ZorBA, a zeroth-order optimization-based federated fine-tuning framework with heterogeneous block activation. ZorBA leverages zeroth-order optimization to eliminate the storage of gradients at the clients by forward passes. ZorBA includes a heterogeneous block activation mechanism in which the central server allocates different subsets of transformer blocks to clients in order to accelerate the convergence rate and reduce the VRAM usage. Furthermore, ZorBA utilizes shared random seeds and the finite differences of gradients in order to reduce the communication overhead. We conduct theoretical analysis to characterize the effect of block activation decisions on the convergence rate and VRAM usage. To jointly enhance the convergence rate and reduce the VRAM usage, we formulate an optimization problem to optimize the block activation decisions. We propose an $ε$-constraint lexicographic algorithm to solve this problem. Experimental results show that ZorBA outperforms three federated fine-tuning baselines in VRAM usage by up to 62.41% and incurs a low communication overhead.

AI.tldr: ZorBA is a zeroth-order, federated fine-tuning framework for LLMs with heterogeneous block activation to reduce VRAM and communication costs, using forward passes, shared seeds, and finite-difference gradients, with theory and an ε-constraint lexicographic optimization to select block activations; experiments show significant VRAM reduction and low communication overhead.
AI.motivation: Federated fine-tuning of large language models suffers from high VRAM usage due to local gradient storage and substantial communication overhead due to frequent model exchanges; there is a need to reduce both resource usage while maintaining convergence.
AI.method: ZorBA employs zeroth-order optimization to avoid gradient storage by using forward passes; introduces heterogeneous block activation where the server assigns subsets of transformer blocks to clients; uses shared random seeds and finite-difference estimates to enable gradient information with reduced communication; provides theoretical convergence and VRAM analysis; formulates a block-activation optimization problem solved by an ε-constraint lexicographic algorithm.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows VRAM usage reductions up to 62.41% over three baselines and maintains low communication overhead; ZorBA accelerates convergence and reduces memory demands.
AI.conclusion: ZorBA demonstrates that careful block-activation and zeroth-order optimization can enable efficient federated fine-tuning of LLMs by lowering VRAM and communication costs while preserving performance, supported by theoretical and optimization-based evidence.

title: When Agents Persuade: Propaganda Generation and Mitigation in LLMs
comment: Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Agents in the Wild (AgentWild). 20 pages including appendix, 3 figures
summary: Despite their wide-ranging benefits, LLM-based agents deployed in open environments can be exploited to produce manipulative material. In this study, we task LLMs with propaganda objectives and analyze their outputs using two domain-specific models: one that classifies text as propaganda or non-propaganda, and another that detects rhetorical techniques of propaganda (e.g., loaded language, appeals to fear, flag-waving, name-calling). Our findings show that, when prompted, LLMs exhibit propagandistic behaviors and use a variety of rhetorical techniques in doing so. We also explore mitigation via Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and ORPO (Odds Ratio Preference Optimization). We find that fine-tuning significantly reduces their tendency to generate such content, with ORPO proving most effective.

AI.tldr: LLMs can be prompted to generate propaganda and exhibit rhetorical techniques; domain detectors identify such content; fine-tuning (notably ORPO) most effectively reduces propagation risk.
AI.motivation: Assess the risk that LLMs deployed in open environments will produce propagandistic content and compare mitigation approaches.
AI.method: Prompt LLMs with propaganda objectives; evaluate outputs with two domain-specific classifiers (propaganda vs non-propaganda; and detection of rhetorical techniques like loaded language, fear appeals, flag-waving, name-calling); compare mitigation strategies—Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and Odds Ratio Preference Optimization (ORPO)—and measure reductions in propagandistic output.
AI.result: LLMs generate propagandistic outputs and employ various rhetorical techniques when prompted; SFT and DPO reduce such tendency, with ORPO yielding the strongest reduction.
AI.conclusion: Targeted fine-tuning strategies can curb propaganda generation in LLMs, with ORPO being the most effective among those tested; highlights importance of safeguarding LLM deployments through domain-specific mitigation and continued evaluation.

title: Python Bindings for a Large C++ Robotics Library: The Case of OMPL
summary: Python bindings are a critical bridge between high-performance C++ libraries and the flexibility of Python, enabling rapid prototyping, reproducible experiments, and integration with simulation and learning frameworks in robotics research. Yet, generating bindings for large codebases is a tedious process that creates a heavy burden for a small group of maintainers. In this work, we investigate the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) to assist in generating nanobind wrappers, with human experts kept in the loop. Our workflow mirrors the structure of the C++ codebase, scaffolds empty wrapper files, and employs LLMs to fill in binding definitions. Experts then review and refine the generated code to ensure correctness, compatibility, and performance. Through a case study on a large C++ motion planning library, we document common failure modes, including mismanaging shared pointers, overloads, and trampolines, and show how in-context examples and careful prompt design improve reliability. Experiments demonstrate that the resulting bindings achieve runtime performance comparable to legacy solutions. Beyond this case study, our results provide general lessons for applying LLMs to binding generation in large-scale C++ projects.

AI.tldr: LLM-assisted generation of nanobind wrappers with human in the loop can streamline Python bindings for large C++ codebases while maintaining performance, though it requires careful prompt design and expert review to avoid common binding pitfalls.
AI.motivation: Binding large C++ libraries to Python is tedious and labor-intensive, hindering rapid prototyping and experimentation in robotics. Automating this with LLMs can reduce maintainer burden while preserving correctness and performance.
AI.method: A workflow that mirrors the C++ codebase structure, scaffolds empty wrapper files, uses LLMs to fill in binding definitions, and leverages human experts to review/refine for correctness, compatibility, and performance. The process emphasizes in-context examples and careful prompts to improve reliability.
AI.result: In a case study on a large motion-planning library, the approach identified common failure modes (shared_ptr lifetime, overload resolution, trampolines) and demonstrated runtime performance comparable to legacy bindings. The study provides practical lessons for applying LLMs to binding generation in large C++ projects.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can aid binding generation for large C++ projects when integrated with human-in-the-loop review and task-structure alignment; the method shows promise and yields transferable lessons for similar binding-generation tasks.

title: ETH-Tight Complexity of Optimal Morse Matching on Bounded-Treewidth Complexes
comment: Full version. Accepted for the ACM Symposium on Computational Geometry (SoCG 2026). 44 pages, 21 figures
summary: The Optimal Morse Matching (OMM) problem asks for a discrete gradient vector field on a simplicial complex that minimizes the number of critical simplices. It is NP-hard and has been studied extensively in heuristic, approximation, and parameterized complexity settings. Parameterized by treewidth $k$, OMM has long been known to be solvable on triangulations of $3$-manifolds in $2^{O(k^2)} n^{O(1)}$ time and in FPT time for triangulations of arbitrary manifolds, but the exact dependence on $k$ has remained an open question. We resolve this by giving a new $2^{O(k \log k)} n$-time algorithm for any finite regular CW complex, and show that no $2^{o(k \log k)} n^{O(1)}$-time algorithm exists unless the Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH) fails.

AI.tldr: A new 2^{O(k log k)} n-time algorithm for Optimal Morse Matching on finite regular CW complexes parameterized by treewidth k, and an ETH-based lower bound ruling out 2^{o(k log k)} n^{O(1)} algorithms, resolving the dependence on k.
AI.motivation: OMM is NP-hard; existing bounds left a gap between 2^{O(k^2)} n^{O(1)} and FPT results; determining the precise k-dependence for CW complexes is important for practical and theoretical reasons.
AI.method: Develop a refined dynamic programming/branching framework that leverages treewidth to solve OMM in 2^{O(k log k)} n time on finite regular CW complexes; complement with a reduction-based ETH lower bound to rule out subexponential in k log k improvements.
AI.result: Algorithm achieves 2^{O(k log k)} n time for any finite regular CW complex; ETH implies no 2^{o(k log k)} n^{O(1)}-time algorithm unless ETH fails.
AI.conclusion: The work settles the exact k-dependence for OMM in this setting, bridging previous gaps and highlighting the tight complexity barrier; has implications for heuristic/parameterized study of discrete Morse theory.

title: sFRC for assessing hallucinations in medical image restoration
comment: 16 pages; 14 figures; 1 Supplemental document. TechRxiv Preprints, 2025
summary: Deep learning (DL) methods are currently being explored to restore images from sparse-view-, limited-data-, and undersampled-based acquisitions in medical applications. Although outputs from DL may appear visually appealing based on likability/subjective criteria (such as less noise, smooth features), they may also suffer from hallucinations. This issue is further exacerbated by a lack of easy-to-use techniques and robust metrics for the identification of hallucinations in DL outputs. In this work, we propose performing Fourier Ring Correlation (FRC) analysis over small patches and concomitantly (s)canning across DL outputs and their reference counterparts to detect hallucinations (termed as sFRC). We describe the rationale behind sFRC and provide its mathematical formulation. The parameters essential to sFRC may be set using predefined hallucinated features annotated by subject matter experts or using imaging theory-based hallucination maps. We use sFRC to detect hallucinations for three undersampled medical imaging problems: CT super-resolution, CT sparse view, and MRI subsampled restoration. In the testing phase, we demonstrate sFRC's effectiveness in detecting hallucinated features for the CT problem and sFRC's agreement with imaging theory-based outputs on hallucinated feature maps for the MR problem. Finally, we quantify the hallucination rates of DL methods on in-distribution versus out-of-distribution data and under increasing subsampling rates to characterize the robustness of DL methods. Beyond DL-based methods, sFRC's effectiveness in detecting hallucinations for a conventional regularization-based restoration method and a state-of-the-art unrolled method is also shown.

AI.tldr: Patch-based Fourier Ring Correlation (sFRC) detects DL-induced hallucinations in medical image restoration by cross-analyzing DL outputs with references across small patches; validated on CT and MRI problems and extended to non-DL methods.
AI.motivation: DL reconstructions can appear visually plausible yet contain hallucinated features; there is a lack of robust, user-friendly metrics to identify such hallucinations and to quantify DL robustness across distributions and undersampling regimes.
AI.method: Compute Fourier Ring Correlation on small image patches, scanning across the DL outputs and their references to identify mismatched features (sFRC). Parameterize using expert-annotated hallucinations or imaging-theory-based hallucination maps. Evaluate on three undersampled problems: CT super-resolution, CT sparse-view, and MRI subsampled restoration; assess detection performance and agreement with theory-based maps; quantify hallucination rates for in-distribution vs out-of-distribution data and under increasing subsampling. Also apply to traditional regularization-based restoration and a state-of-the-art unrolled DL method.
AI.result: sFRC effectively detects hallucinated features in the CT problem; it shows agreement with imaging theory-based hallucination maps for MR outputs; it enables quantification of hallucination rates across distribution shifts and subsampling levels; demonstrates applicability beyond DL methods to conventional regularization and unrolled architectures.
AI.conclusion: sFRC is a practical, physics-informed metric for identifying and quantifying hallucinations in medical image restoration, aiding robust assessment of DL methods and enabling cross-method comparisons under varying data subsampling conditions.

title: ASFL: An Adaptive Model Splitting and Resource Allocation Framework for Split Federated Learning
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables multiple clients to collaboratively train a machine learning model without sharing their raw data. However, the limited computation resources of the clients may result in a high delay and energy consumption on training. In this paper, we propose an adaptive split federated learning (ASFL) framework over wireless networks. ASFL exploits the computation resources of the central server to train part of the model and enables adaptive model splitting as well as resource allocation during training. To optimize the learning performance (i.e., convergence rate) and efficiency (i.e., delay and energy consumption) of ASFL, we theoretically analyze the convergence rate and formulate a joint learning performance and resource allocation optimization problem. Solving this problem is challenging due to the long-term delay and energy consumption constraints as well as the coupling of the model splitting and resource allocation decisions. We propose an online optimization enhanced block coordinate descent (OOE-BCD) algorithm to solve the problem iteratively. Experimental results show that when compared with five baseline schemes, our proposed ASFL framework converges faster and reduces the total delay and energy consumption by up to 75% and 80%, respectively.

AI.tldr: Adaptive split federated learning (ASFL) with online optimization and block coordinate descent improves convergence and reduces delay/energy in wireless networks by adaptively splitting the model and allocating resources.
AI.motivation: In FL, clients have limited compute, causing high training delay and energy consumption. There's a need to leverage server-side computation and flexible model splitting to improve efficiency without sacrificing convergence.
AI.method: Propose ASFL framework that uses server-side computation to train parts of the model with adaptive model splitting and resource allocation. Formulates a joint learning performance and resource allocation optimization under long-term delay and energy constraints. Introduces an online optimization enhanced block coordinate descent (OOE-BCD) algorithm to solve the problem iteratively.
AI.result: The proposed ASFL framework converges faster and reduces total delay and energy consumption by up to 75% and 80% respectively compared with five baselines.
AI.conclusion: ASFL effectively enhances FL in wireless settings by adaptively splitting the model and allocating resources, leveraging server computation to improve convergence and efficiency. The OOE-BCD algorithm addresses long-term constraints and demonstrates substantial empirical gains, validating the approach.

title: Using Vision + Language Models to Predict Item Difficulty
summary: This project investigates the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) to determine the difficulty of data visualization literacy test items. We explore whether features derived from item text (question and answer options), the visualization image, or a combination of both can predict item difficulty (proportion of correct responses) for U.S. adults. We use GPT-4.1-nano to analyze items and generate predictions based on these distinct feature sets. The multimodal approach, using both visual and text features, yields the lowest mean absolute error (MAE) (0.224), outperforming the unimodal vision-only (0.282) and text-only (0.338) approaches. The best-performing multimodal model was applied to a held-out test set for external evaluation and achieved a mean squared error of 0.10805, demonstrating the potential of LLMs for psychometric analysis and automated item development.

AI.tldr: Multimodal LLM-based modeling predicts data-visualization item difficulty more accurately than text-only or vision-only models; external evaluation shows strong predictive performance, indicating potential for automated item development in psychometrics.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether LLM-derived features from item text and visualization images can predict item difficulty (proportion correct) in a data-visualization literacy test, and compare unimodal vs. multimodal approaches.
AI.method: Apply GPT-4.1-nano to extract and combine features from item text (question and options) and the associated visualization image, building unimodal (text-only, vision-only) and multimodal models; train/validate on existing data and evaluate on held-out test set; report MAE and MSE.
AI.result: Multimodal features yield the best performance with MAE = 0.224, outperforming vision-only (MAE = 0.282) and text-only (MAE = 0.338); external hold-out test shows MSE = 0.10805, indicating good predictive accuracy for item difficulty.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can effectively leverage multimodal information to predict item difficulty in psychometric data, supporting automated item development and scalable psychometric analysis; future work should examine robustness, generalization across domains, and calibration of prediction errors.

title: Selecting Spots by Explicitly Predicting Intention from Motion History Improves Performance in Autonomous Parking
summary: In many applications of social navigation, existing works have shown that predicting and reasoning about human intentions can help robotic agents make safer and more socially acceptable decisions. In this work, we study this problem for autonomous valet parking (AVP), where an autonomous vehicle ego agent must drop off its passengers, explore the parking lot, find a parking spot, negotiate for the spot with other vehicles, and park in the spot without human supervision. Specifically, we propose an AVP pipeline that selects parking spots by explicitly predicting where other agents are going to park from their motion history using learned models and probabilistic belief maps. To test this pipeline, we build a simulation environment with reactive agents and realistic modeling assumptions on the ego agent, such as occlusion-aware observations, and imperfect trajectory prediction. Simulation experiments show that our proposed method outperforms existing works that infer intentions from future predicted motion or embed them implicitly in end-to-end models, yielding better results in prediction accuracy, social acceptance, and task completion. Our key insight is that, in parking, where driving regulations are more lax, explicit intention prediction is crucial for reasoning about diverse and ambiguous long-term goals, which cannot be reliably inferred from short-term motion prediction alone, but can be effectively learned from motion history.

AI.tldr: An AVP pipeline that explicitly predicts other agents' parking intentions from motion history using learned models and probabilistic belief maps, showing superior prediction accuracy, social acceptance, and task completion in simulation versus baselines that rely on predicted future motion or end-to-end models.
AI.motivation: In social navigation, anticipating human intentions improves safety and social acceptability. AVP requires reasoning over long-horizon, diverse, and ambiguous goals (e.g., which spot to take) that are not reliably inferred from short-term motion alone, especially in settings with lax parking regulations.
AI.method: Develop an AVP pipeline that selects spots by predicting where other agents will park from their motion history using learned models and probabilistic belief maps. Build a simulation with reactive agents, incorporating occlusion-aware observations and imperfect trajectory prediction for realism. Compare against baselines that infer intentions from future predicted motion or embed them implicitly in end-to-end models.
AI.result: Simulation experiments show the proposed method outperforms the baselines in prediction accuracy, social acceptance, and task completion.
AI.conclusion: Explicit intention prediction using motion history is crucial in parking scenarios with diverse, long-horizon goals; it captures long-term planning better than short-term motion or end-to-end approaches, enabling more safe and socially acceptable AVP behavior.

title: Decoding the Pulse of Reasoning VLMs in Multi-Image Understanding Tasks
summary: Multi-image reasoning remains a significant challenge for vision-language models (VLMs). We investigate a previously overlooked phenomenon: during chain-of-thought (CoT) generation, the text-to-image (T2I) attention of reasoning VLMs exhibits diffuse "pulses": sporadic and unfocused attention patterns that fail to concentrate on task-relevant images. We further reveal a systematic positional bias in attention allocation across images. Motivated by these observations, we propose PulseFocus, a training-free, inference-time method that structures CoT reasoning into interleaved plan/focus blocks with soft attention gating. By forcing the model to explicitly plan which image to examine and then gating decode-time attention to the referenced image, PulseFocus sharpens attention focus and yields consistent improvements on multi-image benchmarks like BLINK benchmark (+3.7%) and MuirBench (+1.07%).

AI.tldr: PulseFocus sharpens multi-image reasoning by restricting text-to-image attention to a referenced image during chain-of-thought, addressing diffuse attention and positional biases without additional training.
AI.motivation: VLMs exhibit diffuse, unfocused text-to-image attention pulses and a systematic positional bias during CoT that harms multi-image reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce PulseFocus: training-free, inference-time architecture that interleaves plan and focus blocks with soft attention gating, forcing explicit image planning and gating decode-time attention to the referenced image.
AI.result: Sharper attention focus yields consistent gains on multi-image benchmarks: BLINK +3.7% and MuirBench +1.07%.
AI.conclusion: PulseFocus improves multi-image CoT reasoning without extra training by aligning attention with explicit planning and selective gating.

title: Model Medicine: A Clinical Framework for Understanding, Diagnosing, and Treating AI Models
summary: Model Medicine is the science of understanding, diagnosing, treating, and preventing disorders in AI models, grounded in the principle that AI models -- like biological organisms -- have internal structures, dynamic processes, heritable traits, observable symptoms, classifiable conditions, and treatable states. This paper introduces Model Medicine as a research program, bridging the gap between current AI interpretability research (anatomical observation) and the systematic clinical practice that complex AI systems increasingly require. We present five contributions: (1) a discipline taxonomy organizing 15 subdisciplines across four divisions -- Basic Model Sciences, Clinical Model Sciences, Model Public Health, and Model Architectural Medicine; (2) the Four Shell Model (v3.3), a behavioral genetics framework empirically grounded in 720 agents and 24,923 decisions from the Agora-12 program, explaining how model behavior emerges from Core--Shell interaction; (3) Neural MRI (Model Resonance Imaging), a working open-source diagnostic tool mapping five medical neuroimaging modalities to AI interpretability techniques, validated through four clinical cases demonstrating imaging, comparison, localization, and predictive capability; (4) a five-layer diagnostic framework for comprehensive model assessment; and (5) clinical model sciences including the Model Temperament Index for behavioral profiling, Model Semiology for symptom description, and M-CARE for standardized case reporting. We additionally propose the Layered Core Hypothesis -- a biologically-inspired three-layer parameter architecture -- and a therapeutic framework connecting diagnosis to treatment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Model Medicine as a clinical-like framework for diagnosing, treating, and preventing AI model disorders using a five-layer diagnostic framework, a taxonomy, a Four Shell Model, a Neural MRI diagnostic tool, and standardized clinical model sciences.
AI.motivation: Bridge interpretability research and systematic health management of complex AI systems, enabling reliable deployment, maintenance, and governance of AI models by treating them as living-like entities with diagnosable states.
AI.method: Develops a discipline taxonomy (15 subdisciplines across four divisions: Basic Model Sciences, Clinical Model Sciences, Model Public Health, Model Architectural Medicine); introduces Four Shell Model (v3.3) empirically grounded in 720 agents and 24,923 decisions from the Agora-12 program; presents Neural MRI (Model Resonance Imaging), mapping five medical neuroimaging modalities to AI interpretability techniques and validated through four clinical cases; proposes a five-layer diagnostic framework for model assessment; develops clinical model sciences including the Model Temperament Index, Model Semiology, and M-CARE; and proposes the Layered Core Hypothesis (three-layer parameter architecture) and a therapeutic framework linking diagnosis to treatment.
AI.result: Outlines a cohesive research program with concrete constructs (taxonomy, diagnostic framework, imaging-inspired tools, standardized scales) and preliminary validations (four clinical cases; 720 agents; 24,923 decisions) that together aim to systematize model health and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: If further developed and validated, Model Medicine could unify interpretability research with practical model health management, enabling standardized diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment of AI systems across development and deployment contexts.

title: LEGS-POMDP: Language and Gesture-Guided Object Search in Partially Observable Environments
comment: 10 pages, 8 figures, accepted at ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction (HRI 2026)
summary: To assist humans in open-world environments, robots must interpret ambiguous instructions to locate desired objects. Foundation model-based approaches excel at multimodal grounding, but they lack a principled mechanism for modeling uncertainty in long-horizon tasks. In contrast, Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) provide a systematic framework for planning under uncertainty but are often limited in supported modalities and rely on restrictive environment assumptions. We introduce LanguagE and Gesture-Guided Object Search in Partially Observable Environments (LEGS-POMDP), a modular POMDP system that integrates language, gesture, and visual observations for open-world object search. Unlike prior work, LEGS-POMDP explicitly models two sources of partial observability: uncertainty over the target object's identity and its spatial location. In simulation, multimodal fusion significantly outperforms unimodal baselines, achieving an average success rate of 89\% across challenging environments and object categories. Finally, we demonstrate the full system on a quadruped mobile manipulator, where real-world experiments qualitatively validate robust multimodal perception and uncertainty reduction under ambiguous instructions.

AI.tldr: A modular POMDP framework (LEGS-POMDP) combines language, gesture, and vision to handle two core uncertainties in open-world object search, achieving high simulated success and real-world qualitative validation on a quadruped robot.
AI.motivation: Open-world object search requires reasoning under uncertainty. Foundation models excel at multimodal grounding but lack principled long-horizon uncertainty planning; POMDPs provide principled uncertainty handling but are often limited in modalities and make restrictive assumptions. The paper seeks to combine the strengths of both.
AI.method: LEGS-POMDP is a modular POMDP system that fuses language, gesture, and visual observations. It explicitly models two partial observability sources: (1) the identity of the target object and (2) its spatial location. The approach is validated in simulation, showing multimodal fusion outperforming unimodal baselines, and demonstrated on a quadruped mobile manipulator with real-world experiments.
AI.result: In simulation, multimodal fusion achieves an average success rate of 89% across challenging environments and object categories, outperforming unimodal baselines. Real-world experiments with a quadruped mobile manipulator qualitatively validate robust multimodal perception and uncertainty reduction under ambiguous instructions.
AI.conclusion: LEGS-POMDP provides a principled, modular approach to integrate language, gesture, and vision within a POMDP framework for open-world search, effectively modeling key uncertainties and delivering strong simulated performance with supportive real-world validation. It lays groundwork for robust multimodal planning under long-horizon uncertainty.

title: A Benchmark Study of Neural Network Compression Methods for Hyperspectral Image Classification
summary: Deep neural networks have achieved strong performance in image classification tasks due to their ability to learn complex patterns from high-dimensional data. However, their large computational and memory requirements often limit deployment on resource-constrained platforms such as remote sensing devices and edge systems. Network compression techniques have therefore been proposed to reduce model size and computational cost while maintaining predictive performance. In this study, we conduct a systematic evaluation of neural network compression methods for a remote sensing application, namely hyperspectral land cover classification. Specifically, we examine three widely used compression strategies for convolutional neural networks: pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation. Experiments are conducted on two benchmark hyperspectral datasets, considering classification accuracy, memory consumption, and inference efficiency.
