AI.tldr: Open-set corrective assistance for embodied foundation models evaluated in a synthetic Overcooked domain: fine-tuning a LLaMA-based multimodal model on diverse interactive data to assess generalization to unseen user behaviors and novel configurations, with findings that diverse, multimodal datasets improve generalization but open-set challenges persist.
AI.motivation: Need for data-efficient generalization in interactive assistive models that can handle unseen user behavior and tasks without relying on closed-set assumptions or external planners; open-set corrective assistance provides a rigorous testbed for such capabilities.
AI.method: Generate synthetic assistive datasets in a simulated domain (Overcooked) and fine-tune a LLaMA-based multimodal model on this data. Evaluate generalization along two axes: (a) unseen user behavior categories and (b) new task configurations not seen during training. Investigate open-set corrective assistance requiring inspection of lengthy user behavior and corrective actions or language feedback, emphasizing dataset properties such as multimodal grounding, defect inference, and diverse scenarios.
AI.result: The study suggests that models benefit from assistive datasets spanning multiple aspects (multimodal grounding, defect inference, scenario diversity). The open-set corrective task remains challenging, indicating limits of current datasets and training approaches, but synthetic data can enable generalization to novel tasks and user behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Insights into how assistive dataset design supports open-set generalization for embodied foundation models; highlights the importance of covering diverse modalities, failure modes (defects), and broad scenarios to enable open-set corrective intelligence, while acknowledging outstanding challenges in fully solving open-set correction.

title: MADCrowner: Margin Aware Dental Crown Design with Template Deformation and Refinement
summary: Dental crown restoration is one of the most common treatment modalities for tooth defect, where personalized dental crown design is critical. While computer-aided design (CAD) systems have notably enhanced the efficiency of dental crown design, extensive manual adjustments are still required in the clinic workflow. Recent studies have explored the application of learning-based methods for the automated generation of restorative dental crowns. Nevertheless, these approaches were challenged by inadequate spatial resolution, noisy outputs, and overextension of surface reconstruction. To address these limitations, we propose \totalframework, a margin-aware mesh generation framework comprising CrownDeformR and CrownSegger. Inspired by the clinic manual workflow of dental crown design, we designed CrownDeformR to deform an initial template to the target crown based on anatomical context, which is extracted by a multi-scale intraoral scan encoder. Additionally, we introduced \marginseg, a novel margin segmentation network, to extract the cervical margin of the target tooth. The performance of CrownDeformR improved with the cervical margin as an extra constraint. And it was also utilized as the boundary condition for the tailored postprocessing method, which removed the overextended area of the reconstructed surface. We constructed a large-scale intraoral scan dataset and performed extensive experiments. The proposed method significantly outperformed existing approaches in both geometric accuracy and clinical feasibility.

AI.tldr: Per a margin-aware mesh generation framework for dental crowns, combining CrownDeformR (template deformation guided by multi-scale intraoral scans) and CrownSegger (margin segmentation) to enforce cervical margins, reduce overextension, and improve geometric accuracy and clinical feasibility on large intraoral scan data.
AI.motivation: Existing learning-based crown generation suffers from limited spatial resolution, noisy outputs, and surface overextension; there is a need for a clinically feasible automated design workflow that respects anatomical margins.
AI.method: Introduce CrownDeformR to deform an initial crown template toward the target using anatomical context from a multi-scale intraoral scan encoder. Develop MarginSegger to segment the cervical margin; use the cervical margin as an extra constraint and as a boundary condition for a postprocessing step to remove overextended surfaces. Build a large intraoral scan dataset and perform extensive experiments.
AI.result: The proposed margin-aware framework significantly outperforms existing approaches in geometric accuracy and clinical feasibility.
AI.conclusion: Margin-aware crown design with explicit margin segmentation and constrained deformation yields improved accuracy and practicality, validated on a large intraoral scan dataset.

title: Standing on the Shoulders of Giants: Rethinking EEG Foundation Model Pretraining via Multi-Teacher Distillation
summary: Pretraining for electroencephalogram (EEG) foundation models has predominantly relied on self-supervised masked reconstruction, a paradigm largely adapted from and inspired by the success of vision and language foundation models. However, unlike images and text, EEG datasets are notoriously expensive to collect and characterized by low signal-to-noise ratio. These challenges introduce difficulties in scaling the EEG foundation models and capturing the underlying neural semantics through reconstruction. In this work, we ask the question: can we stand on the shoulders of well-established foundation models from well-represented modalities to bootstrap the pretraining of EEG foundation models? We first demonstrate that mainstream foundation models, such as those from vision and time series, transfer surprisingly well to EEG domain. To this end, we propose the Multi-Teacher Distillation Pretraining (MTDP) framework for pretraining EEG foundation models via a two-stage multi-teacher distillation. In the first stage, we introduce a learnable gating network to fuse representations from diverse teachers (e.g., DINOv3 and Chronos) via a masked latent denoising objective. In the second stage, we distill the fused representation into an EEG foundation model. Extensive evaluations across 9 downstream tasks and 12 datasets demonstrate that our MTDP-based EEG foundation model outperforms its self-supervised counterparts while requiring only 25% of the pretraining data.

AI.tldr: Cross-modal transfer enables data-efficient EEG foundation model pretraining via a two-stage multi-teacher distillation framework, achieving superior performance with only 25% of the usual pretraining data.
AI.motivation: EEG datasets are expensive to collect and noisy (low SNR), making scaling EEG foundation models challenging. Existing self-supervised masked reconstruction, borrowed from vision/language, may not capture neural semantics efficiently. The work explores leveraging established foundation models from vision and time-series to bootstrap EEG pretraining.
AI.method: Stage 1: Learnable gating network fuses representations from diverse teachers (e.g., DINOv3, Chronos) using a masked latent denoising objective. Stage 2: Distill the fused representation into an EEG foundation model. The approach constitutes a two-stage multi-teacher distillation (MTDP) framework. Pretraining data efficiency is highlighted (only 25% of standard data).
AI.result: MTDP-based EEG foundation model outperforms self-supervised counterparts across 9 downstream tasks and 12 datasets, demonstrating the transferability of vision/time-series foundation models to EEG domains and achieving data efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that mainstream foundation models from well-represented modalities can effectively bootstrap EEG foundation model pretraining. A two-stage multi-teacher distillation framework yields data-efficient EEG models that surpass self-supervised baselines.

title: Evaluating the Search Agent in a Parallel World
summary: Integrating web search tools has significantly extended the capability of LLMs to address open-world, real-time, and long-tail problems. However, evaluating these Search Agents presents formidable challenges. First, constructing high-quality deep search benchmarks is prohibitively expensive, while unverified synthetic data often suffers from unreliable sources. Second, static benchmarks face dynamic obsolescence: as internet information evolves, complex queries requiring deep research often degrade into simple retrieval tasks due to increased popularity, and ground truths become outdated due to temporal shifts. Third, attribution ambiguity confounds evaluation, as an agent's performance is often dominated by its parametric memory rather than its actual search and reasoning capabilities. Finally, reliance on specific commercial search engines introduces variability that hampers reproducibility. To address these issues, we propose a novel framework, Mind-ParaWorld, for evaluating Search Agents in a Parallel World. Specifically, MPW samples real-world entity names to synthesize future scenarios and questions situated beyond the model's knowledge cutoff. A ParaWorld Law Model then constructs a set of indivisible Atomic Facts and a unique ground-truth for each question. During evaluation, instead of retrieving real-world results, the agent interacts with a ParaWorld Engine Model that dynamically generates SERPs grounded in these inviolable Atomic Facts. We release MPW-Bench, an interactive benchmark spanning 19 domains with 1,608 instances. Experiments across three evaluation settings show that, while search agents are strong at evidence synthesis given complete information, their performance is limited not only by evidence collection and coverage in unfamiliar search environments, but also by unreliable evidence sufficiency judgment and when-to-stop decisions-bottlenecks.

AI.tldr: Mind-ParaWorld (MPW) provides a parallel-world benchmark framework to evaluate Search Agents, addressing high-quality benchmarks, dynamic obsolescence, attribution, and reproducibility. MPW-Bench uses a ParaWorld Engine grounded in Atomic Facts for deterministic, ground-truth SERP generation. Results show agents excel at evidence synthesis with complete information but struggle with evidence collection, coverage in unfamiliar environments, evidence sufficiency judgments, and stopping decisions.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of web-search-enabled agents suffers from expensive, unverifiable synthetic data, rapid information obsolescence, attribution ambiguity, and irreproducibility due to reliance on real search engines. A controlled, ground-truth framework is needed to decouple search and reasoning from memory and to enable robust, reproducible evaluation.
AI.method: Sample real-world entity names to synthesize future scenarios; a ParaWorld Law Model constructs indivisible Atomic Facts and a unique ground-truth for each question. An evaluation-time ParaWorld Engine Model generates SERPs grounded on those Atomic Facts rather than querying real-time sources. MPW-Bench offers 19 domains with 1,608 instances and evaluates across three settings.
AI.result: Agents perform well at evidence synthesis given complete information, but are limited by evidence collection and coverage in unfamiliar search environments, unreliable evidence sufficiency judgments, and when-to-stop decisions bottlenecks.
AI.conclusion: MPW-Bench provides a principled means to evaluate Search Agents by simulating a deterministic, ground-truth world, highlighting critical bottlenecks in evidence gathering and stop-criteria, and enabling reproducible cross-domain benchmarking.

title: Task-Relevant and Irrelevant Region-Aware Augmentation for Generalizable Vision-Based Imitation Learning in Agricultural Manipulation
summary: Vision-based imitation learning has shown promise for robotic manipulation; however, its generalization remains limited in practical agricultural tasks. This limitation stems from scarce demonstration data and substantial visual domain gaps caused by i) crop-specific appearance diversity and ii) background variations. To address this limitation, we propose Dual-Region Augmentation for Imitation Learning (DRAIL), a region-aware augmentation framework designed for generalizable vision-based imitation learning in agricultural manipulation. DRAIL explicitly separates visual observations into task-relevant and task-irrelevant regions. The task-relevant region is augmented in a domain-knowledge-driven manner to preserve essential visual characteristics, while the task-irrelevant region is aggressively randomized to suppress spurious background correlations. By jointly handling both sources of visual variation, DRAIL promotes learning policies that rely on task-essential features rather than incidental visual cues. We evaluate DRAIL on diffusion policy-based visuomotor controllers through robot experiments on artificial vegetable harvesting and real lettuce defective leaf picking preparation tasks. The results show consistent improvements in success rates under unseen visual conditions compared to baseline methods. Further attention analysis and representation generalization metrics indicate that the learned policies rely more on task-essential visual features, resulting in enhanced robustness and generalization.

AI.tldr: DRAIL is a region-aware augmentation framework for vision-based imitation learning in agriculture that improves generalization by separating observations into task-relevant and task-irrelevant regions; task-relevant visuals are augmented with domain knowledge to preserve essential cues, while background regions are randomized to suppress spurious correlations, leading to more robust visuomotor policies. Evaluated on diffusion-policy controllers for artificial vegetable harvesting and lettuce leaf picking, it shows improved success under unseen visuals and reports attention/feature analyses indicating reliance on task-essential cues.
AI.motivation: Vision-based imitation learning in agriculture suffers from limited demonstrations and large visual domain gaps due to crop appearance diversity and background variability. The goal is to enhance generalization by reducing reliance on incidental visual cues and focusing on task-critical features.
AI.method: Introduce Dual-Region Augmentation for Imitation Learning (DRAIL) that segments observations into task-relevant and task-irrelevant regions. Apply domain-knowledge-driven augmentation to the task-relevant region to preserve essential cues, while aggressively randomizing the task-irrelevant background. Train diffusion-policy-based visuomotor controllers with this augmentation strategy and evaluate on real robot tasks.
AI.result: DRAIL yields consistent improvements in success rates under unseen visual conditions compared to baselines. Attention analysis and representation metrics indicate learned policies emphasize task-essential features, improving robustness and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Region-aware augmentation that isolates and preserves task-relevant visual cues while perturbing background cues enhances generalization in vision-based imitation learning for agricultural manipulation.

title: Privacy-Aware Camera 2.0 Technical Report
summary: With the increasing deployment of intelligent sensing technologies in highly sensitive environments such as restrooms and locker rooms, visual surveillance systems face a profound privacy-security paradox. Existing privacy-preserving approaches, including physical desensitization, encryption, and obfuscation, often compromise semantic understanding or fail to ensure mathematically provable irreversibility. Although Privacy Camera 1.0 eliminated visual data at the source to prevent leakage, it provided only textual judgments, leading to evidentiary blind spots in disputes. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a novel privacy-preserving perception framework based on the AI Flow paradigm and a collaborative edge-cloud architecture. By deploying a visual desensitizer at the edge, raw images are transformed in real time into abstract feature vectors through nonlinear mapping and stochastic noise injection under the Information Bottleneck principle, ensuring identity-sensitive information is stripped and original images are mathematically unreconstructable. The abstract representations are transmitted to the cloud for behavior recognition and semantic reconstruction via a "dynamic contour" visual language, achieving a critical balance between perception and privacy while enabling illustrative visual reference without exposing raw images.

AI.tldr: A privacy-preserving perception framework that processes raw video at the edge to produce irreversible abstract features for cloud-based recognition, using Information Bottleneck and stochastic noise, enabling visual references via a dynamic contour language.
AI.motivation: Addresses the privacy-security paradox in sensitive environments by moving beyond desensitization, encryption, and obfuscation; aims for mathematically irreversible representations with preserved semantic utility, overcoming evidentiary gaps of prior approaches like Privacy Camera 1.0.
AI.method: Deploy an edge-side visual desensitizer that maps raw images into abstract feature vectors through nonlinear transformations and stochastic noise under the Information Bottleneck principle; transmit abstractions to the cloud for behavior recognition and semantic reconstruction via a 'dynamic contour' visual language; leverage AI Flow paradigm and a collaborative edge–cloud architecture to balance privacy and perception.
AI.result: Proposes a framework that purportedly achieves a balance between perceptual usefulness and privacy, enabling illustrative visual references without exposing raw images and with mathematically irreversible representations.
AI.conclusion: If validated, this approach offers a practical path to privacy-preserving perception in sensitive environments by combining edge processing with controllable abstract representations and an interpretable reconstruction language; however, empirical validation, formal irreversibility guarantees, and robustness evaluations remain essential next steps.

title: Augmenting representations with scientific papers
comment: Accepted at the 2nd Workshop on Foundation Models for Science (ICLR 2026)
summary: Astronomers have acquired vast repositories of multimodal data, including images, spectra, and time series, complemented by decades of literature that analyzes astrophysical sources. Still, these data sources are rarely systematically integrated. This work introduces a contrastive learning framework designed to align X-ray spectra with domain knowledge extracted from scientific literature, facilitating the development of shared multimodal representations. Establishing this connection is inherently complex, as scientific texts encompass a broader and more diverse physical context than spectra. We propose a contrastive pipeline that achieves a 20% Recall@1% when retrieving texts from spectra, proving that a meaningful alignment between these modalities is not only possible but capable of accelerating the interpretation of rare or poorly understood sources. Furthermore, the resulting shared latent space effectively encodes physically significant information. By fusing spectral and textual data, we improve the estimation of 20 physical variables by 16-18% over unimodal spectral baselines. Our results indicate that a Mixture of Experts (MoE) strategy, which leverages both unimodal and shared representations, yields superior performance. Finally, outlier analysis within the multimodal latent space identifies high-priority targets for follow-up investigation, including a candidate pulsating ULX (PULX) and a gravitational lens system. Importantly, this framework can be extended to other scientific domains where aligning observational data with existing literature is possible.

AI.tldr: A contrastive learning framework aligns X-ray spectra with scientific literature to create a shared multimodal representation, enabling retrieval of texts from spectra and vice versa, and improving multi-variable physical estimates; a Mixture of Experts (MoE) fusion further boosts performance and multimodal outlier analysis highlights high-priority targets like a candidate pulsating ULX and a gravitational lens system.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of systematic integration between heterogeneous astrophysical data (images, spectra, time series) and decades of literature. By bridging spectra with textual domain knowledge, the work aims to accelerate interpretation of rare or poorly understood sources and enable richer, physically meaningful multimodal representations.
AI.method: A contrastive learning pipeline that aligns X-ray spectra with domain knowledge extracted from scientific literature to form a shared latent space. The approach uses a Mixture of Experts (MoE) to combine unimodal and multimodal representations and conducts outlier analysis to flag high-priority targets.
AI.result: Achieves 20% Recall@1% retrieval when linking spectra to texts, demonstrating meaningful cross-modal alignment. The shared latent space encodes physically significant information, and fusing spectral and textual data improves estimation of 20 physical variables by 16–18% over unimodal spectral baselines. MoE fusion yields superior performance, and outlier analysis identifies targets such as a candidate pulsating ULX and a gravitational lens system.
AI.conclusion: The framework proves that aligning observational spectra with literature-derived knowledge is feasible and beneficial, yielding actionable multimodal representations and improved physical inference. It is extensible to other scientific domains where observational data can be connected with existing literature.

title: MOOSEnger -- a Domain-Specific AI Agent for the MOOSE Ecosystem
summary: MOOSEnger is a tool-enabled AI agent tailored to the Multiphysics Object-Oriented Simulation Environment (MOOSE). MOOSE cases are specified in HIT ".i" input files; the large object catalog and strict syntax make initial setup and debugging slow. MOOSEnger offers a conversational workflow that turns natural-language intent into runnable inputs by combining retrieval-augmented generation over curated docs/examples with deterministic, MOOSE-aware parsing, validation, and execution tools. A core-plus-domain architecture separates reusable agent infrastructure (configuration, registries, tool dispatch, retrieval services, persistence, and evaluation) from a MOOSE plugin that adds HIT-based parsing, syntax-preserving ingestion of input files, and domain-specific utilities for input repair and checking. An input precheck pipeline removes hidden formatting artifacts, fixes malformed HIT structure with a bounded grammar-constrained loop, and resolves invalid object types via similarity search over an application syntax registry. Inputs are then validated and optionally smoke-tested with the MOOSE runtime in the loop via an MCP-backed execution backend (with local fallback), translating solver diagnostics into iterative verify-and-correct updates. Built-in evaluation reports RAG metrics (faithfulness, relevancy, context precision/recall) and end-to-end success by actual execution. On a 125-prompt benchmark spanning diffusion, transient heat conduction, solid mechanics, porous flow, and incompressible Navier--Stokes, MOOSEnger achieves a 0.93 execution pass rate versus 0.08 for an LLM-only baseline.

AI.tldr: A tool-enabled AI agent, MOOSEnger, converts natural-language intents into runnable MOOSE HIT inputs via retrieval-augmented generation and deterministic parsing, achieving 0.93 execution pass rate on a 125-prompt benchmark across multiple physics domains, far outperforming an LLM-only baseline.
AI.motivation: MOOSE input setup and debugging are slow due to large object catalogs and strict HIT syntax; there is a need for end-to-end reliability that converts intent into valid, executable inputs with automatic diagnostics.
AI.method: Core-plus-domain architecture separating reusable agent infrastructure from a MOOSE plugin; input precheck removing artifacts and repairing malformed HIT through grammar-constrained loops; similarity search over a syntax registry for invalid types; validation and optional runtime smoke-testing via MCP-backed execution loop; iterative verify-and-correct based on solver diagnostics; evaluation reports include RAG metrics and end-to-end success via actual execution.
AI.result: 0.93 execution pass rate on a 125-prompt benchmark spanning diffusion, transient heat conduction, solid mechanics, porous flow, and incompressible Navier–Stokes; LLM-only baseline achieves 0.08.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that combining retrieval-augmented generation with domain-aware validation and execution yields robust, end-to-end tooling for generating runnable MOOSE inputs, with strong end-to-end performance and potential applicability to similar domain-specific configuration languages.

title: Hyperbolic Multiview Pretraining for Robotic Manipulation
comment: This paper was submitted to CVPR 2026 and was recommended for Findings, but the authors have withdrawn it and are currently adding more content to submit it elsewhere
summary: 3D-aware visual pretraining has proven effective in improving the performance of downstream robotic manipulation tasks. However, existing methods are constrained to Euclidean embedding spaces, whose flat geometry limits their ability to model structural relations among embeddings. As a result, they struggle to learn structured embeddings that are essential for robust spatial perception in robotic applications. To this end, we propose HyperMVP, a self-supervised framework for \underline{Hyper}bolic \underline{M}ulti\underline{V}iew \underline{P}retraining. Hyperbolic space offers geometric properties well suited for capturing structural relations. Methodologically, we extend the masked autoencoder paradigm and design a GeoLink encoder to learn multiview hyperbolic representations. The pretrained encoder is then finetuned with visuomotor policies on manipulation tasks. In addition, we introduce 3D-MOV, a large-scale dataset comprising multiple types of 3D point clouds to support pretraining. We evaluate HyperMVP on COLOSSEUM, RLBench, and real-world scenarios, where it consistently outperforms strong baselines across diverse tasks and perturbation settings. Our results highlight the potential of 3D-aware pretraining in a non-Euclidean space for learning robust and generalizable robotic manipulation policies.

AI.tldr: HyperMVP introduces hyperbolic multi-view pretraining for 3D-aware robotic manipulation, using a GeoLink encoder within a masked autoencoder framework, supported by a new 3D-MOV dataset; it improves performance on COLOSSEUM, RLBench, and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Euclidean embedding spaces are flat and struggle to capture complex structural relations in 3D representations; hyperbolic geometry naturally encodes hierarchical and relational structure, potentially improving robustness and generalization in robotic perception.
AI.method: Extend the masked autoencoder paradigm to multiview hyperbolic representations with a GeoLink encoder; pretrain on a large-scale 3D-MOV dataset; finetune the pretrained encoder with visuomotor policies for manipulation tasks; evaluate on COLOSSEUM, RLBench, and real-world scenarios.
AI.result: HyperMVP consistently outperforms strong baselines across diverse tasks and perturbations, demonstrating improved robustness and generalization; the 3D-MOV dataset supports scalable hyperbolic pretraining.
AI.conclusion: Non-Euclidean, specifically hyperbolic, 3D-aware pretraining is a promising direction for robust and generalizable robotic manipulation policies; future work could explore broader task settings and efficiency of hyperbolic representations.

title: RMK RetinaNet: Rotated Multi-Kernel RetinaNet for Robust Oriented Object Detection in Remote Sensing Imagery
summary: Rotated object detection in remote sensing imagery is hindered by three major bottlenecks: non-adaptive receptive field utilization, inadequate long-range multi-scale feature fusion, and discontinuities in angle regression. To address these issues, we propose Rotated Multi-Kernel RetinaNet (RMK RetinaNet). First, we design a Multi-Scale Kernel (MSK) Block to strengthen adaptive multi-scale feature extraction. Second, we incorporate a Multi-Directional Contextual Anchor Attention (MDCAA) mechanism into the feature pyramid to enhance contextual modeling across scales and orientations. Third, we introduce a Bottom-up Path to preserve fine-grained spatial details that are often degraded during downsampling. Finally, we develop an Euler Angle Encoding Module (EAEM) to enable continuous and stable angle regression. Extensive experiments on DOTA-v1.0, HRSC2016, and UCAS-AOD show that RMK RetinaNet achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art rotated object detectors while improving robustness in multi-scale and multi-orientation scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RMK RetinaNet for rotated object detection in remote sensing, featuring MSK blocks, MDCAA, a bottom-up path, and EAEM, achieving state-of-the-art–like performance with improved robustness to scale and orientation on DOTA-v1.0, HRSC2016, and UCAS-AOD.
AI.motivation: Rotated object detection in remote sensing suffers from (1) non-adaptive receptive field usage, (2) insufficient long-range multi-scale feature fusion, and (3) discontinuities in angle regression. Addressing these bottlenecks is essential for robust, multi-scale, multi-orientation detection.
AI.method: Propose four key components integrated into RetinaNet: (1) Multi-Scale Kernel (MSK) Block for adaptive multi-scale feature extraction; (2) Multi-Directional Contextual Anchor Attention (MDCAA) for cross-scale/orientation contextual modeling in the feature pyramid; (3) Bottom-up Path to preserve fine-grained spatial details during downsampling; (4) Euler Angle Encoding Module (EAEM) to enable continuous and stable angle regression.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on DOTA-v1.0, HRSC2016, and UCAS-AOD show RMK RetinaNet achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art rotated object detectors, with improved robustness under multi-scale and multi-orientation conditions.
AI.conclusion: The RMK RetinaNet framework effectively mitigates the three bottlenecks in rotated remote-sensing detection by combining adaptive multi-scale feature extraction, cross-scale/orientation contextual attention, preserved spatial details, and stable angle encoding, yielding a robust detector that matches SOTA performance on multiple benchmarks.

title: Invariant Causal Routing for Governing Social Norms in Online Market Economies
summary: Social norms are stable behavioral patterns that emerge endogenously within economic systems through repeated interactions among agents. In online market economies, such norms -- like fair exposure, sustained participation, and balanced reinvestment -- are critical for long-term stability. We aim to understand the causal mechanisms driving these emergent norms and to design principled interventions that can steer them toward desired outcomes. This is challenging because norms arise from countless micro-level interactions that aggregate into macro-level regularities, making causal attribution and policy transferability difficult. To address this, we propose \textbf{Invariant Causal Routing (ICR)}, a causal governance framework that identifies policy-norm relations stable across heterogeneous environments. ICR integrates counterfactual reasoning with invariant causal discovery to separate genuine causal effects from spurious correlations and to construct interpretable, auditable policy rules that remain effective under distribution shift. In heterogeneous agent simulations calibrated with real data, ICR yields more stable norms, smaller generalization gaps, and more concise rules than correlation or coverage baselines, demonstrating that causal invariance offers a principled and interpretable foundation for governance.

AI.tldr: Proposes Invariant Causal Routing (ICR), a causal governance framework that identifies stable policy-norm relations across environments using invariant causal discovery and counterfactuals; reports improved stability, generalization, and conciseness in agent simulations with real data.
AI.motivation: Norms emerge endogenously in online economies and are hard to attribute causally or transfer across contexts; a robust, interpretable method is needed to steer norms toward desired outcomes under distribution shifts.
AI.method: Combines counterfactual reasoning with invariant causal discovery to separate causal effects from spurious correlations and to derive interpretable, auditable policy rules; evaluated in heterogeneous agent simulations calibrated with real data.
AI.result: ICR yields more stable norms, smaller generalization gaps, and more concise rules than correlation or coverage baselines.
AI.conclusion: Causal invariance provides a principled, interpretable foundation for governance of emergent norms, enabling policy rules that remain effective under distribution shift.

title: Breaking Contextual Inertia: Reinforcement Learning with Single-Turn Anchors for Stable Multi-Turn Interaction
summary: While LLMs demonstrate strong reasoning capabilities when provided with full information in a single turn, they exhibit substantial vulnerability in multi-turn interactions. Specifically, when information is revealed incrementally or requires updates, models frequently fail to integrate new constraints, leading to a collapse in performance compared to their single-turn baselines. We term the root cause as \emph{Contextual Inertia}: a phenomenon where models rigidly adhere to previous reasoning traces. Even when users explicitly provide corrections or new data in later turns, the model ignores them, preferring to maintain consistency with its previous (incorrect) reasoning path. To address this, we introduce \textbf{R}einforcement \textbf{L}earning with \textbf{S}ingle-\textbf{T}urn \textbf{A}nchors (\textbf{RLSTA}), a generalizable training approach designed to stabilize multi-turn interaction across diverse scenarios and domains. RLSTA leverages the model's superior single-turn capabilities as stable internal anchors to provide reward signals. By aligning multi-turn responses with these anchors, RLSTA empowers models to break contextual inertia and self-calibrate their reasoning based on the latest information. Experiments show that RLSTA significantly outperforms standard fine-tuning and abstention-based methods. Notably, our method exhibits strong cross-domain generalization (e.g., math to code) and proves effective even without external verifiers, highlighting its potential for general-domain applications.

AI.tldr: Proposes RLSTA to mitigate Contextual Inertia in multi-turn LLM interactions by using single-turn anchors as stable rewards, improving adaptation to new constraints across domains without external verifiers.
AI.motivation: LLMs show strong single-turn reasoning but falter in multi-turn settings due to Contextual Inertia; need methods to update model reasoning with new information.
AI.method: Reinforcement Learning with Single-Turn Anchors (RLSTA): uses the model's strong single-turn behavior as anchors to shape rewards for multi-turn responses, aligning with latest information and correcting past mistakes; domain-agnostic training; cross-domain evaluation; no external verifiers required.
AI.result: RLSTA outperforms standard fine-tuning and abstention-based baselines; shows cross-domain generalization (math to code); effective without external verifiers; robust multi-turn performance.
AI.conclusion: RLSTA stabilizes multi-turn reasoning by breaking Contextual Inertia, enabling models to self-calibrate with updated information; holds promise for general-domain, cross-domain applications.

title: VPWEM: Non-Markovian Visuomotor Policy with Working and Episodic Memory
summary: Imitation learning from human demonstrations has achieved significant success in robotic control, yet most visuomotor policies still condition on single-step observations or short-context histories, making them struggle with non-Markovian tasks that require long-term memory. Simply enlarging the context window incurs substantial computational and memory costs and encourages overfitting to spurious correlations, leading to catastrophic failures under distribution shift and violating real-time constraints in robotic systems. By contrast, humans can compress important past experiences into long-term memories and exploit them to solve tasks throughout their lifetime. In this paper, we propose VPWEM, a non-Markovian visuomotor policy equipped with working and episodic memories. VPWEM retains a sliding window of recent observation tokens as short-term working memory, and introduces a Transformer-based contextual memory compressor that recursively converts out-of-window observations into a fixed number of episodic memory tokens. The compressor uses self-attention over a cache of past summary tokens and cross-attention over a cache of historical observations, and is trained jointly with the policy. We instantiate VPWEM on diffusion policies to exploit both short-term and episode-wide information for action generation with nearly constant memory and computation per step. Experiments demonstrate that VPWEM outperforms state-of-the-art baselines including diffusion policies and vision-language-action (VLA) models by more than 20% on the memory-intensive manipulation tasks in MIKASA and achieves an average 5% improvement on the mobile manipulation benchmark MoMaRT. Code is available at https://github.com/HarryLui98/code_vpwem.

AI.tldr: VPWEM introduces a non-Markovian visuomotor policy with working and episodic memories, using a Transformer-based memory compressor to convert past observations into fixed episodic tokens. It achieves substantial gains on memory-intensive robotic manipulation benchmarks with near-constant per-step cost and provides code for replication.
AI.motivation: To address non-Markovian control tasks in visuomotor policies that suffer from long-context demands, high compute/memory, and vulnerability to distribution shift when using large context windows. The work mirrors human memory strategies by compressing past experiences into episodic tokens.
AI.method: A VPWEM architecture that maintains a short-term sliding window as working memory and employs a Transformer-based contextual memory compressor to convert out-of-window observations into a fixed number of episodic memory tokens. The compressor uses self-attention over past summary tokens and cross-attention over historical observations, trained jointly with the policy. Implemented on diffusion-policy frameworks to leverage both short-term and episodic information during action generation, aiming for near-constant memory and computation per step.
AI.result: Empirical gains exceeding 20% over SOTA baselines (diffusion policies and VLA models) on memory-intensive manipulation tasks in the MIKASA suite, and about a 5% average improvement on the MoMaRT mobile manipulation benchmark. Code is released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: VPWEM demonstrates that combining short-term working memory with a compact, Transformer-based episodic memory compressor enables effective non-Markovian visuomotor control with limited memory footprint, improving performance under memory-intensive tasks while maintaining real-time feasibility.

title: LAW & ORDER: Adaptive Spatial Weighting for Medical Diffusion and Segmentation
summary: Medical image analysis relies on accurate segmentation, and benefits from controllable synthesis (of new training images). Yet both tasks of the cyclical pipeline face spatial imbalance: lesions occupy small regions against vast backgrounds. In particular, diffusion models have been shown to drift from prescribed lesion layouts, while efficient segmenters struggle on spatially uncertain regions. Adaptive spatial weighting addresses this by learning where to allocate computational resources. This paper introduces a pair of network adapters: 1) Learnable Adaptive Weighter (LAW) which predicts per-pixel loss modulation from features and masks for diffusion training, stabilized via a mix of normalization, clamping, and regularization to prevent degenerate solutions; and 2) Optimal Region Detection with Efficient Resolution (ORDER) which applies selective bidirectional skip attention at late decoder stages for efficient segmentation. Experiments on polyp and kidney tumor datasets demonstrate that LAW achieves 20% FID generative improvement over a uniform baseline (52.28 vs. 65.60), with synthetic data then improving downstream segmentation by 4.9% Dice coefficient (83.2% vs. 78.3%). ORDER reaches 6.0% Dice improvement on MK-UNet (81.3% vs. 75.3%) with 0.56 GFLOPs and just 42K parameters, remaining 730x smaller than the standard nnUNet.

AI.tldr: A pair of adapters, LAW and ORDER, tackle spatial imbalance in diffusion-based training and segmentation for medical images; LAW modulates per-pixel losses during diffusion training via learned weights from features/masks; ORDER uses selective bidirectional skip attention at late decoder stages for efficient segmentation. Together, they improve generative quality (FID) and segmentation accuracy (Dice) with low computational cost.
AI.motivation: Lesion regions are small relative to backgrounds, causing diffusion drift and poor segmentation in spatially uncertain regions. Adaptive spatial weighting aims to allocate computation where needed.
AI.method: LAW predicts per-pixel loss modulation from features and masks; stabilized by normalization, clamping, regularization to avoid degenerate solutions. ORDER applies selective bidirectional skip attention in late decoder stages for efficiency during segmentation.
AI.result: LAW improves FID by ~20% (65.60 -> 52.28) and downstream Dice by 4.9% (78.3% -> 83.2%). ORDER improves Dice on MK-UNet by 6.0% (75.3% -> 81.3%), with 0.56 GFLOPs and 42k parameters, ~730x smaller than nnUNet.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive spatial weighting via these adapters can enhance both generative modeling and segmentation in spatially imbalanced medical imaging tasks while maintaining efficiency; further validation across more datasets and anatomies is needed.

title: An LLM-Guided Query-Aware Inference System for GNN Models on Large Knowledge Graphs
summary: Efficient inference for graph neural networks (GNNs) on large knowledge graphs (KGs) is essential for many real-world applications. GNN inference queries are computationally expensive and vary in complexity, as each involves a different number of target nodes linked to subgraphs of diverse densities and structures. Existing acceleration methods, such as pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation, instantiate smaller models but do not adapt them to the structure or semantics of individual queries. They also store models as monolithic files that must be fully loaded, and miss the opportunity to retrieve only the neighboring nodes and corresponding model components that are semantically relevant to the target nodes. These limitations lead to excessive data loading and redundant computation on large KGs. This paper presents KG-WISE, a task-driven inference paradigm for large KGs. KG-WISE decomposes trained GNN models into fine-grained components that can be partially loaded based on the structure of the queried subgraph. It employs large language models (LLMs) to generate reusable query templates that extract semantically relevant subgraphs for each task, enabling query-aware and compact model instantiation. We evaluate KG-WISE on six large KGs with up to 42 million nodes and 166 million edges. KG-WISE achieves up to 28x faster inference and 98% lower memory usage than state-of-the-art systems while maintaining or improving accuracy across both commercial and open-weight LLMs.

AI.tldr: KG-WISE introduces a task-driven, modular GNN inference approach for large knowledge graphs by decomposing models into fine-grained components and using LLM-generated query templates to fetch semantically relevant subgraphs, enabling query-aware and compact model instantiation. It delivers substantial speed and memory benefits while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Efficient GNN inference on very large knowledge graphs is challenging due to varying query complexities and subgraph structures. Existing acceleration methods (pruning, quantization, distillation) are monolithic and do not adapt to specific query structures, leading to excessive data loading and wasted computation.
AI.method: KG-WISE decomposes trained GNNs into fine-grained components that can be selectively loaded according to the queried subgraph structure. It uses large language models to generate reusable query templates that identify semantically relevant subgraphs for each task, enabling query-aware and compact model instantiation.
AI.result: Evaluated on six large knowledge graphs with up to 42 million nodes and 166 million edges, KG-WISE achieves up to 28x faster inference and 98% lower memory usage than state-of-the-art systems, while maintaining or improving accuracy across both commercial and open-weight LLMs.
AI.conclusion: KG-WISE enables efficient, query-aware GNN inference on large knowledge graphs by modularizing models and leveraging LLM-guided subgraph extraction, offering substantial speedups and memory reductions without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Timer-S1: A Billion-Scale Time Series Foundation Model with Serial Scaling
summary: We introduce Timer-S1, a strong Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) time series foundation model with 8.3B total parameters, 0.75B activated parameters for each token, and a context length of 11.5K. To overcome the scalability bottleneck in existing pre-trained time series foundation models, we perform Serial Scaling in three dimensions: model architecture, dataset, and training pipeline. Timer-S1 integrates sparse TimeMoE blocks and generic TimeSTP blocks for Serial-Token Prediction (STP), a generic training objective that adheres to the serial nature of forecasting. The proposed paradigm introduces serial computations to improve long-term predictions while avoiding costly rolling-style inference and pronounced error accumulation in the standard next-token prediction. Pursuing a high-quality and unbiased training dataset, we curate TimeBench, a corpus with one trillion time points, and apply meticulous data augmentation to mitigate predictive bias. We further pioneer a post-training stage, including continued pre-training and long-context extension, to enhance short-term and long-context performance. Evaluated on the large-scale GIFT-Eval leaderboard, Timer-S1 achieves state-of-the-art forecasting performance, attaining the best MASE and CRPS scores as a pre-trained model. Timer-S1 will be released to facilitate further research.

AI.tldr: Timer-S1 is an 8.3B-parameter MoE time-series foundation model with 11.5K context, using Serial Scaling and Serial-Token Prediction to achieve state-of-the-art long-horizon forecasting on GIFT-Eval, aided by a 1e12-point TimeBench and post-training.
AI.motivation: Address scalability bottlenecks in pre-trained time-series models and mitigate error accumulation and costly rolling-inference in long-horizon forecasting.
AI.method: Serial Scaling across model architecture, dataset, and training pipeline; incorporation of sparse TimeMoE blocks and TimeSTP blocks for Serial-Token Prediction; a serial forecasting objective; construction of TimeBench (1e12 time points) with data augmentation; post-training (continued pre-training and long-context extension).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art forecasting on GIFT-Eval, with best MASE and CRPS scores among pre-trained models.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of serial computation and large-scale MoE/time-series foundation models, with a large curated dataset and post-training strategy enabling improved long-horizon accuracy.

title: Beyond the Patch: Exploring Vulnerabilities of Visuomotor Policies via Viewpoint-Consistent 3D Adversarial Object
comment: 8 pages, 10 figures, Accepted to ICRA 2026. Project page: https://chan-mi-lee.github.io/3DAdvObj/
summary: Neural network-based visuomotor policies enable robots to perform manipulation tasks but remain susceptible to perceptual attacks. For example, conventional 2D adversarial patches are effective under fixed-camera setups, where appearance is relatively consistent; however, their efficacy often diminishes under dynamic viewpoints from moving cameras, such as wrist-mounted setups, due to perspective distortions. To proactively investigate potential vulnerabilities beyond 2D patches, this work proposes a viewpoint-consistent adversarial texture optimization method for 3D objects through differentiable rendering. As optimization strategies, we employ Expectation over Transformation (EOT) with a Coarse-to-Fine (C2F) curriculum, exploiting distance-dependent frequency characteristics to induce textures effective across varying camera-object distances. We further integrate saliency-guided perturbations to redirect policy attention and design a targeted loss that persistently drives robots toward adversarial objects. Our comprehensive experiments show that the proposed method is effective under various environmental conditions, while confirming its black-box transferability and real-world applicability.

AI.tldr: This work introduces a viewpoint-consistent adversarial texture optimization for 3D objects using differentiable rendering, combining EOT with a coarse-to-fine curriculum and saliency-guided perturbations to craft textures that deceive visuomotor policies across varying viewpoints and distances, with demonstrated black-box transferability and real-world applicability.
AI.motivation: Perceptual attacks on neural visuomotor policies are effective with fixed 2D patches but degrade with changing viewpoints; there is a need to study vulnerabilities in 3D, viewpoint-consistent attacks.
AI.method: Use differentiable rendering to optimize textures on 3D objects under transformations via EOT with a coarse-to-fine curriculum; exploit distance-dependent texture frequencies; apply saliency-guided perturbations; use a targeted loss to drive robots toward adversarial objects; test black-box transferability and real-world viability.
AI.result: Proposed method is effective under diverse environmental conditions, transfers to black-box settings, and works in real-world scenarios, validated by comprehensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: The approach proactively reveals vulnerabilities beyond traditional 2D patches, enabling evaluation of visuomotor policies in more realistic 3D, viewpoint-changing contexts and highlighting implications for defense and robustness.

title: Comparative Evaluation of Traditional Methods and Deep Learning for Brain Glioma Imaging. Review Paper
summary: Segmentation is crucial for brain gliomas as it delineates the glioma s extent and location, aiding in precise treatment planning and monitoring, thus improving patient outcomes. Accurate segmentation ensures proper identification of the glioma s size and position, transforming images into applicable data for analysis. Classification of brain gliomas is also essential because different types require different treatment approaches. Accurately classifying brain gliomas by size, location, and aggressiveness is essential for personalized prognosis prediction, follow-up care, and monitoring disease progression, ensuring effective diagnosis, treatment, and management. In glioma research, irregular tissues are often observable, but error free and reproducible segmentation is challenging. Many researchers have surveyed brain glioma segmentation, proposing both fully automatic and semi-automatic techniques. The adoption of these methods by radiologists depends on ease of use and supervision, with semi-automatic techniques preferred due to the need for accurate evaluations. This review evaluates effective segmentation and classification techniques post magnetic resonance imaging acquisition, highlighting that convolutional neural network architectures outperform traditional techniques in these tasks.

AI.tldr: A review of brain glioma segmentation/classification on MRI shows CNN-based methods outperform traditional techniques, with semi-automatic approaches favored for clinical adoption due to usability and supervision; accuracy and reproducibility remain critical for improved patient management.
AI.motivation: Glioma segmentation and classification are essential for precise treatment planning, prognosis, and monitoring, yet irregular tumor morphology and the need for reproducible results pose challenges; radiologists favor methods that balance accuracy with usability.
AI.method: Survey of segmentation techniques (fully automatic and semi-automatic) and classification approaches applied to post-MRI glioma data, emphasizing convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and comparative performance against traditional methods.
AI.result: CNN architectures outperform traditional techniques in segmentation and classification tasks; semi-automatic methods are preferred in clinical settings for ensuring accurate evaluations; MRI-based workflows are central to these advances.
AI.conclusion: CNN-based segmentation and classification are superior for glioma analysis on MRI, but clinical adoption hinges on usability, transparency, and reliable evaluation procedures; continued research is needed to improve accuracy, reproducibility, and integration into radiology workflows.

title: Oracle-efficient Hybrid Learning with Constrained Adversaries
summary:
The Hybrid Online Learning Problem, where features are drawn i.i.d. from an unknown distribution but labels are generated adversarially, is a well-motivated setting positioned between statistical and fully-adversarial online learning. Prior work has presented a dichotomy: algorithms that are statistically-optimal, but computationally intractable (Wu et al., 2023), and algorithms that are computationally-efficient (given an ERM oracle), but statistically-suboptimal (Wu et al., 2024).
  This paper takes a significant step towards achieving statistical optimality and computational efficiency simultaneously in the Hybrid Learning setting. To do so, we consider a structured setting, where the Adversary is constrained to pick labels from an expressive, but fixed, class of functions $R$. Our main result is a new learning algorithm, which runs efficiently given an ERM oracle and obtains regret scaling with the Rademacher complexity of a class derived from the Learner's hypothesis class $H$ and the Adversary's label class $R$. As a key corollary, we give an oracle-efficient algorithm for computing equilibria in stochastic zero-sum games when action sets may be high-dimensional but the payoff function exhibits a type of low-dimensional structure. Technically, we develop a number of tools for the design and analysis of our learning algorithm, including a novel Frank-Wolfe reduction with "truncated entropy regularizer" and a new tail bound for sums of "hybrid" martingale difference sequences.

AI.tldr: New oracle-efficient algorithm for Hybrid Online Learning with adversarial labels, achieving regret tied to the joint Rademacher complexity of H and R, plus an FW-based reduction and new tail bounds; also yields an oracle-efficient equilibrium computation in structured stochastic zero-sum games.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between statistically-optimal but intractable and computationally-efficient but suboptimal results in Hybrid Online Learning by constraining the adversary's label class and leveraging ERM oracles.
AI.method: Designs an ERM-oracle-efficient learning algorithm with regret scaling as the Rademacher complexity of a class derived from the learner's hypothesis class H and the adversary's label class R; introduces a Frank-Wolfe reduction with a truncated entropy regularizer; develops a tail bound for sums of hybrid martingale difference sequences.
AI.result: Provides an oracle-efficient algorithm for computing equilibria in stochastic zero-sum games with high-dimensional action sets but low-dimensional payoff structure; establishes regret bounds in terms of the Rademacher complexity of the derived class; presents novel analytical tools.
AI.conclusion: Represents a significant advance toward simultaneous statistical optimality and computational efficiency in Hybrid Learning; the Frank-Wolfe reduction with truncated entropy and the hybrid martingale tail bounds are potentially useful beyond this setting.

title: EchoGuard: An Agentic Framework with Knowledge-Graph Memory for Detecting Manipulative Communication in Longitudinal Dialogue
summary: Manipulative communication, such as gaslighting, guilt-tripping, and emotional coercion, is often difficult for individuals to recognize. Existing agentic AI systems lack the structured, longitudinal memory to track these subtle, context-dependent tactics, often failing due to limited context windows and catastrophic forgetting. We introduce EchoGuard, an agentic AI framework that addresses this gap by using a Knowledge Graph (KG) as the agent's core episodic and semantic memory. EchoGuard employs a structured Log-Analyze-Reflect loop: (1) users log interactions, which the agent structures as nodes and edges in a personal, episodic KG (capturing events, emotions, and speakers); (2) the system executes complex graph queries to detect six psychologically-grounded manipulation patterns (stored as a semantic KG); and (3) an LLM generates targeted Socratic prompts grounded by the subgraph of detected patterns, guiding users toward self-discovery. This framework demonstrates how the interplay between agentic architectures and Knowledge Graphs can empower individuals in recognizing manipulative communication while maintaining personal autonomy and safety. We present the theoretical foundation, framework design, a comprehensive evaluation strategy, and a vision to validate this approach.

AI.tldr: EchoGuard uses a knowledge-graph-based episodic/semantic memory with a Log-Analyze-Reflect loop to detect six manipulation patterns and generate Socratic prompts for user self-discovery.
AI.motivation: Addresses the difficulty of recognizing manipulative communication due to subtle, context-dependent tactics and limited context windows in current agentic AIs; aims to provide long-term, structured memory and reasoning to empower users while maintaining autonomy and safety.
AI.method: Build a personal episodic KG of interactions; store six psychologically-grounded manipulation patterns in a semantic KG; perform graph queries to detect these patterns; an LLM generates Socratic prompts grounded in the detected subgraph to guide self-discovery; loop: Log-Analyze-Reflect.
AI.result: Claims theoretical foundation, framework design, a comprehensive evaluation strategy, and a vision to validate the approach; emphasizes the interplay between agentic architectures and knowledge graphs for user empowerment and safety.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a pathway to empower individuals to recognize manipulative communication through structured memory and guided self-reflection, while preserving autonomy and safety; future validation and refinement expected.

title: U-OBCA: Uncertainty-Aware Optimization-Based Collision Avoidance via Wasserstein Distributionally Robust Chance Constraints
summary: Uncertainties arising from localization error, trajectory prediction errors of the moving obstacles and environmental disturbances pose significant challenges to robot's safe navigation. Existing uncertainty-aware planners often approximate polygon-shaped robots and obstacles using simple geometric primitives such as circles or ellipses. Though computationally convenient, these approximations substantially shrink the feasible space, leading to overly conservative trajectories and even planning failure in narrow environments. In addition, many such methods rely on specific assumptions about noise distributions, which may not hold in practice and thus limit their performance guarantees. To address these limitations, we extend the Optimization-Based Collision Avoidance (OBCA) framework to an uncertainty-aware formulation, termed \emph{U-OBCA}. The proposed method explicitly accounts for the collision risk between polygon-shaped robots and obstacles by formulating OBCA-based chance constraints, and hence avoiding geometric simplifications and reducing unnecessary conservatism. These probabilistic constraints are further tightened into deterministic nonlinear constraints under mild distributional assumptions, which can be solved efficiently by standard numerical optimization solvers. The proposed approach is validated through theoretical analysis, numerical simulations and real-world experiments. The results demonstrate that U-OBCA significantly mitigates the conservatism in trajectory planning and achieves higher navigation efficiency compared to existing baseline methods, particularly in narrow and cluttered environments.

AI.tldr: Proposes U-OBCA, an uncertainty-aware OBCA extension that uses chance constraints to account for polygon-shaped robot-obstacle interactions, converting to deterministic nonlinear constraints for efficient optimization; reduces conservatism and improves navigation in narrow/cluttered environments, validated theoretically, numerically, and in real experiments.
AI.motivation: Uncertainties from localization, obstacle prediction errors, and disturbances; polygon simplifications (circles/ellipses) cause conservatism and narrow feasibility; noise distribution assumptions may not hold, limiting guarantees.
AI.method: Extend OBCA to U-OBCA; formulate collision risk as OBCA-based chance constraints for polygon shapes; tighten into deterministic nonlinear constraints under mild distributional assumptions; solvable by standard nonlinear optimization solvers.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis, simulations, and real-world experiments show reduced conservatism and higher navigation efficiency, especially in narrow/cluttered environments.
AI.conclusion: U-OBCA effectively handles polygon-shaped geometries without simplifications, offering less conservative and more efficient planning under uncertainty, with mild distributional assumptions providing guarantees.

title: MASQuant: Modality-Aware Smoothing Quantization for Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) with computational invariance for Large Language Models~(LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable advances, however, their application to Multimodal Large Language Models~(MLLMs) presents substantial challenges. In this paper, we analyze SmoothQuant as a case study and identify two critical issues: Smoothing Misalignment and Cross-Modal Computational Invariance. To address these issues, we propose Modality-Aware Smoothing Quantization (MASQuant), a novel framework that introduces (1) Modality-Aware Smoothing (MAS), which learns separate, modality-specific smoothing factors to prevent Smoothing Misalignment, and (2) Cross-Modal Compensation (CMC), which addresses Cross-modal Computational Invariance by using SVD whitening to transform multi-modal activation differences into low-rank forms, enabling unified quantization across modalities. MASQuant demonstrates stable quantization performance across both dual-modal and tri-modal MLLMs. Experimental results show that MASQuant is competitive among the state-of-the-art PTQ algorithms. Source code: https://github.com/alibaba/EfficientAI.

AI.tldr: MASQuant offers modality-aware post-training quantization for multimodal LLMs by learning modality-specific smoothing factors and applying SVD-based cross-modal compensation to enable unified quantization; it yields stable performance and competitive results on dual- and tri-modal models.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of SmoothQuant in MLLMs, specifically smoothing misalignment and cross-modal computational invariance that hinder PTQ.
AI.method: 1) Modality-Aware Smoothing (MAS): learn separate smoothing factors per modality to prevent misalignment; 2) Cross-Modal Compensation (CMC): apply SVD whitening to activation differences across modalities to convert them into low-rank representations enabling a single quantization scheme.
AI.result: MASQuant achieves stable PTQ performance across dual- and tri-modal MLLMs and is competitive with state-of-the-art PTQ methods; code provided.
AI.conclusion: MASQuant provides a robust PTQ framework for MLLMs, mitigating smoothing misalignment and cross-modal invariance, with practical applicability and open-source implementation.

title: Latent Particle World Models: Self-supervised Object-centric Stochastic Dynamics Modeling
summary: We introduce Latent Particle World Model (LPWM), a self-supervised object-centric world model scaled to real-world multi-object datasets and applicable in decision-making. LPWM autonomously discovers keypoints, bounding boxes, and object masks directly from video data, enabling it to learn rich scene decompositions without supervision. Our architecture is trained end-to-end purely from videos and supports flexible conditioning on actions, language, and image goals. LPWM models stochastic particle dynamics via a novel latent action module and achieves state-of-the-art results on diverse real-world and synthetic datasets. Beyond stochastic video modeling, LPWM is readily applicable to decision-making, including goal-conditioned imitation learning, as we demonstrate in the paper. Code, data, pre-trained models and video rollouts are available: https://taldatech.github.io/lpwm-web

AI.tldr: Self-supervised, object-centric world model (LPWM) trained on real-world multi-object videos that discovers keypoints, bounding boxes, and masks; uses a latent action module for stochastic particle dynamics; supports action, language, and image-goal conditioning; achieves state-of-the-art on diverse datasets and enables decision-making.
AI.motivation: Learn rich, structured scene representations from raw video data to enable scalable reasoning and planning in multi-object environments without supervision.
AI.method: End-to-end training from videos with an object-centric architecture that discovers and tracks keypoints, bounding boxes, and masks; introduces a latent action module to model stochastic particle dynamics; supports conditioning on actions, language, and image goals; applicable to decision-making and goal-conditioned imitation learning.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on a range of real-world and synthetic datasets; demonstrates applicability to decision-making tasks, including goal-conditioned imitation learning; provides code, data, and pretrained models for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: LPWM offers a scalable, self-supervised approach to learning object-centric world models capable of handling stochastic dynamics and guiding decision-making in real-world multi-object settings; broad applicability and strong empirical results suggest promising directions for future work.

title: LLM-Grounded Explainability for Port Congestion Prediction via Temporal Graph Attention Networks
summary: Port congestion at major maritime hubs disrupts global supply chains, yet existing prediction systems typically prioritize forecasting accuracy without providing operationally interpretable explanations. This paper proposes AIS-TGNN, an evidence-grounded framework that jointly performs congestion-escalation prediction and faithful natural-language explanation by coupling a Temporal Graph Attention Network (TGAT) with a structured large language model (LLM) reasoning module. Daily spatial graphs are constructed from Automatic Identification System (AIS) broadcasts, where each grid cell represents localized vessel activity and inter-cell interactions are modeled through attention-based message passing. The TGAT predictor captures spatiotemporal congestion dynamics, while model-internal evidence, including feature z-scores and attention-derived neighbor influence, is transformed into structured prompts that constrain LLM reasoning to verifiable model outputs. To evaluate explanatory reliability, we introduce a directional-consistency validation protocol that quantitatively measures agreement between generated narratives and underlying statistical evidence. Experiments on six months of AIS data from the Port of Los Angeles and Long Beach demonstrate that the proposed framework outperforms both LR and GCN baselines, achieving a test AUC of 0.761, AP of 0.344, and recall of 0.504 under a strict chronological split while producing explanations with 99.6% directional consistency. Results show that grounding LLM generation in graph-model evidence enables interpretable and auditable risk reporting without sacrificing predictive performance. The framework provides a practical pathway toward operationally deployable explainable AI for maritime congestion monitoring and supply-chain risk management.

AI.tldr: AIS-TGNN combines a Temporal Graph Attention Network with a structured LLM reasoning module to predict port congestion and generate evidence-grounded explanations; it achieves competitive predictive performance and high directional-consistency in explanations.
AI.motivation: Operational port congestion forecasting needs interpretable explanations; existing systems emphasize accuracy but lack verifiable, evidence-based reasoning; provide auditable risk narratives for decision-makers.
AI.method: Construct daily AIS-derived spatial graphs; use TGAT to capture spatiotemporal congestion; extract model-internal evidence (feature z-scores, neighbor influence) and convert into structured prompts to constrain an LLM; validate explanations with a directional-consistency protocol.
AI.result: Outperforms LR and GCN baselines on Port of LA/LB data with AUC 0.761, AP 0.344, recall 0.504 under chronological split; explanations show 99.6% directional consistency.
AI.conclusion: Grounding LLM narratives in graph-model evidence yields interpretable, auditable risk reporting without sacrificing predictive performance; supports deployment of explainable AI for maritime congestion and supply-chain risk management.

title: Integrated cooperative localization of heterogeneous measurement swarm: A unified data-driven method
summary: The cooperative localization (CL) problem in heterogeneous robotic systems with different measurement capabilities is investigated in this work. In practice, heterogeneous sensors lead to directed and sparse measurement topologies, whereas most existing CL approaches rely on multilateral localization with restrictive multi-neighbor geometric requirements. To overcome this limitation, we enable pairwise relative localization (RL) between neighboring robots using only mutual measurement and odometry information. A unified data-driven adaptive RL estimator is first developed to handle heterogeneous and unidirectional measurements. Based on the convergent RL estimates, a distributed pose-coupling CL strategy is then designed, which guarantees CL under a weakly connected directed measurement topology, representing the least restrictive condition among existing results. The proposed method is independent of specific control tasks and is validated through a formation control application and real-world experiments.

AI.tldr: A data-driven adaptive relative localization (RL) estimator enables robust cooperative localization (CL) in heterogeneous, directed sensing graphs by leveraging pairwise RL and odometry; a distributed pose-coupling CL strategy ensures localization under weak connectivity, validated by formation control and real-world experiments.
AI.motivation: Heterogeneous sensor suites create directed, sparse measurement topologies that challenge traditional multilateral localization; a flexible pairwise RL approach can relax geometric requirements and expand applicability.
AI.method: Develop a unified data-driven adaptive RL estimator using only mutual measurements and odometry to obtain pairwise relative poses; then design a distributed pose-coupling CL framework that fuses RL estimates to achieve global CL under weakly connected directed graphs; the method is task-agnostic and validated on formation control and real experiments.
AI.result: Convergent RL estimates enable distributed CL under weak connectivity; success in formation control and real-world experiments demonstrates practicality in heterogeneous, directed measurement topologies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pairwise RL-based CL approach relaxes measurement connectivity constraints, accommodates sensor heterogeneity, and provides a practical framework for distributed cooperative localization in real-world robotic networks.

title: Guiding Diffusion-based Reconstruction with Contrastive Signals for Balanced Visual Representation
summary: The limited understanding capacity of the visual encoder in Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) has become a key bottleneck for downstream performance. This capacity includes both Discriminative Ability (D-Ability), which reflects class separability, and Detail Perceptual Ability (P-Ability), which focuses on fine-grained visual cues. Recent solutions use diffusion models to enhance representations by conditioning image reconstruction on CLIP visual tokens. We argue that such paradigms may compromise D-Ability and therefore fail to effectively address CLIP's representation limitations. To address this, we integrate contrastive signals into diffusion-based reconstruction to pursue more comprehensive visual representations. We begin with a straightforward design that augments the diffusion process with contrastive learning on input images. However, empirical results show that the naive combination suffers from gradient conflict and yields suboptimal performance. To balance the optimization, we introduce the Diffusion Contrastive Reconstruction (DCR), which unifies the learning objective. The key idea is to inject contrastive signals derived from each reconstructed image, rather than from the original input, into the diffusion process. Our theoretical analysis shows that the DCR loss can jointly optimize D-Ability and P-Ability. Extensive experiments across various benchmarks and multi-modal large language models validate the effectiveness of our method. The code is available at https://github.com/boyuh/DCR.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based reconstruction method (DCR) for CLIP that combines diffusion modeling with contrastive learning to jointly enhance discriminative and detailed perceptual abilities, addressing gradient conflicts seen with naive combinations and improving multi-modal representations.
AI.motivation: CLIP's visual encoder has limited capacity in both discriminative ability (class separability) and detail perception; diffusion-based reconstructions conditioned on CLIP tokens can harm discriminative power. A method is needed to integrate contrastive signals without gradient conflicts to improve both D-Ability and P-Ability.
AI.method: Introduce Diffusion Contrastive Reconstruction (DCR) that unifies diffusion-based image reconstruction and contrastive learning by injecting contrastive signals from each reconstructed image into the diffusion process; first attempt augmented diffusion with input-image contrastive loss leads to gradient conflict; DCR resolves this by joint objective focusing on reconstructed-image-based contrastive signals.
AI.result: Naive combination suffers gradient conflicts and suboptimal performance; DCR enables joint optimization of D-Ability and P-Ability; extensive experiments across benchmarks and multi-modal LMs validate effectiveness; code released.
AI.conclusion: DCR is an effective framework to balance class separability and fine-grained visual cues in CLIP representations, mitigating limitations of prior diffusion-based methods and improving downstream performance.

title: Why Do Neural Networks Forget: A Study of Collapse in Continual Learning
summary: Catastrophic forgetting is a major problem in continual learning, and lots of approaches arise to reduce it. However, most of them are evaluated through task accuracy, which ignores the internal model structure. Recent research suggests that structural collapse leads to loss of plasticity, as evidenced by changes in effective rank (eRank). This indicates a link to forgetting, since the networks lose the ability to expand their feature space to learn new tasks, which forces the network to overwrite existing representations. Therefore, in this study, we investigate the correlation between forgetting and collapse through the measurement of both weight and activation eRank. To be more specific, we evaluated four architectures, including MLP, ConvGRU, ResNet-18, and Bi-ConvGRU, in the split MNIST and Split CIFAR-100 benchmarks. Those models are trained through the SGD, Learning-without-Forgetting (LwF), and Experience Replay (ER) strategies separately. The results demonstrate that forgetting and collapse are strongly related, and different continual learning strategies help models preserve both capacity and performance in different efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper links catastrophic forgetting in continual learning to structural collapse measured by activation/weight effective rank (eRank). It analyzes four architectures on split MNIST and CIFAR-100 under SGD, LwF, and ER, finding a strong correlation between forgetting and eRank collapse and that different CL strategies balance capacity preservation and performance with varying efficiency.
AI.motivation: To go beyond task-accuracy as the sole metric for continual learning by inspecting internal model structure (eRank) to understand plasticity loss and its relation to forgetting.
AI.method: Compute weight and activation eRank during continual learning. Evaluate four architectures (MLP, ConvGRU, ResNet-18, Bi-ConvGRU) on split MNIST and Split CIFAR-100. Train with SGD, Learning-without-Forgetting (LwF), and Experience Replay (ER) separately. Analyze correlations between forgetting (likely measured by accuracy drop or forgetting metric) and eRank changes.
AI.result: Forgetting and structural collapse (as measured by eRank) are strongly related. Different continual learning strategies help preserve both network capacity (as captured by eRank) and task performance, with trade-offs in efficiency across methods.
AI.conclusion: eRank serves as a meaningful proxy for plasticity and capacity in continual learning. Addressing structural collapse alongside accuracy can improve forgetting mitigation, and the effectiveness of CL strategies depends on the efficiency–capacity trade-off. Further investigation into eRank dynamics across layers and tasks could deepen understanding of plasticity preservation.

title: VISA: Value Injection via Shielded Adaptation for Personalized LLM Alignment
summary: Aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with nuanced human values remains a critical challenge, as existing methods like Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) often handle only coarse-grained attributes. In practice, fine-tuning LLMs on task-specific datasets to optimize value alignment inevitably incurs an alignment tax: the model's pre-calibrated value system drifts significantly due to latent bias absorption from training data, while the fine-tuning process also causes severe hallucinations and semantic information loss in generated responses.
