AI.result: On 35 engineering regression datasets, synthetic-only adaptation improves accuracy and data efficiency, beating TabPFN 2.5 on 29/35 and AutoGluon on 27/35; mean multiplicative data-efficiency gains of ~1.75x over TabPFN 2.5 and ~4.44x over AutoGluon.
AI.conclusion: Principled synthetic data curation can transform procedurally generated data into domain-relevant data engines, enabling foundation models to perform better in data-sparse engineering contexts where real data is scarce.

title: Differentially Private Multimodal In-Context Learning
summary: Vision-language models are increasingly applied to sensitive domains such as medical imaging and personal photographs, yet existing differentially private methods for in-context learning are limited to few-shot, text-only settings because privacy cost scales with the number of tokens processed. We present Differentially Private Multimodal Task Vectors (DP-MTV), the first framework enabling many-shot multimodal in-context learning with formal $(\varepsilon, δ)$-differential privacy by aggregating hundreds of demonstrations into compact task vectors in activation space. DP-MTV partitions private data into disjoint chunks, applies per-layer clipping to bound sensitivity, and adds calibrated noise to the aggregate, requiring only a single noise addition that enables unlimited inference queries. We evaluate on eight benchmarks across three VLM architectures, supporting deployment with or without auxiliary data. At $\varepsilon=1.0$, DP-MTV achieves 50% on VizWiz compared to 55% non-private and 35% zero-shot, preserving most of the gain from in-context learning under meaningful privacy constraints.

AI.tldr: DP-MTV enables many-shot multimodal in-context learning under formal differential privacy by aggregating demonstrations into compact activation-space task vectors with per-layer clipping and a single calibrated noise addition.
AI.motivation: Privacy costs in multimodal in-context learning grow with the number of tokens and demonstrations. A practical DP approach for many-shot multimodal ICL is needed.
AI.method: Partition private data into chunks, apply per-layer clipping to bound sensitivity, aggregate into activation-space task vectors, and add calibrated noise once to enable unlimited inference queries. Evaluated across eight benchmarks and three VLM architectures, with/without auxiliary data.
AI.result: With ε=1.0, DP-MTV achieves 50% on VizWiz versus 55% non-private and 35% zero-shot, maintaining most of the in-context learning gain under meaningful privacy constraints.
AI.conclusion: DP-MTV offers a practical, scalable DP framework for many-shot multimodal in-context learning, preserving most gains of non-private ICL while supporting unlimited queries via a single noise addition.

title: Lifelong Language-Conditioned Robotic Manipulation Learning
summary: Traditional language-conditioned manipulation agent sequential adaptation to new manipulation skills leads to catastrophic forgetting of old skills, limiting dynamic scene practical deployment. In this paper, we propose SkillsCrafter, a novel robotic manipulation framework designed to continually learn multiple skills while reducing catastrophic forgetting of old skills. Specifically, we propose a Manipulation Skills Adaptation to retain the old skills knowledge while inheriting the shared knowledge between new and old skills to facilitate learning of new skills. Meanwhile, we perform the singular value decomposition on the diverse skill instructions to obtain common skill semantic subspace projection matrices, thereby recording the essential semantic space of skills. To achieve forget-less and generalization manipulation, we propose a Skills Specialization Aggregation to compute inter-skills similarity in skill semantic subspaces, achieving aggregation of the previously learned skill knowledge for any new or unknown skill. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of our proposed SkillsCrafter.

AI.tldr: A continual-learning framework for robotic manipulation that reduces catastrophic forgetting by sharing knowledge through semantic subspaces and skill specialization aggregation, enabling learning of new skills while preserving old ones.
AI.motivation: Catastrophic forgetting during sequential skill acquisition in language-conditioned manipulation; need mechanisms to retain prior skills while learning new ones and to leverage shared semantic structure across skills.
AI.method: 1) Manipulation Skills Adaptation to retain old skill knowledge while leveraging shared knowledge with new skills. 2) Apply singular value decomposition on diverse skill instructions to derive common semantic subspace projection matrices, capturing essential skill semantics. 3) Skills Specialization Aggregation to measure inter-skill similarity in semantic subspaces and aggregate prior knowledge for new or unknown skills.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the approach’s effectiveness and superiority, showing reduced forgetting and improved generalization across manipulation tasks compared with baselines.
AI.conclusion: SkillsCrafter presents a principled continual-learning framework for robotic manipulation that combines adaptation and semantic-space aggregation to mitigate forgetting and enable effective generalization to new skills.

title: SURE: Semi-dense Uncertainty-REfined Feature Matching
summary: Establishing reliable image correspondences is essential for many robotic vision problems. However, existing methods often struggle in challenging scenarios with large viewpoint changes or textureless regions, where incorrect cor- respondences may still receive high similarity scores. This is mainly because conventional models rely solely on fea- ture similarity, lacking an explicit mechanism to estimate the reliability of predicted matches, leading to overconfident errors. To address this issue, we propose SURE, a Semi- dense Uncertainty-REfined matching framework that jointly predicts correspondences and their confidence by modeling both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties. Our approach in- troduces a novel evidential head for trustworthy coordinate regression, along with a lightweight spatial fusion module that enhances local feature precision with minimal overhead. We evaluated our method on multiple standard benchmarks, where it consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art semi-dense matching models in both accuracy and efficiency. our code will be available on https://github.com/LSC-ALAN/SURE.

AI.tldr: SURE is a semi-dense matching framework that jointly predicts image correspondences and their uncertainty by modeling both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty, using an evidential regression head and a lightweight spatial fusion module to improve reliability and efficiency; it achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks with code to be released.
AI.motivation: Existing semi-dense matching methods rely mainly on feature similarity and lack an explicit mechanism to estimate match reliability, leading to overconfident errors in challenging conditions with large viewpoint changes or textureless regions.
AI.method: SURE introduces an evidential head for trustworthy coordinate regression to model uncertainty (aleatoric + epistemic) and a lightweight spatial fusion module to refine local feature precision, jointly predicting correspondences and their confidence in a semi-dense matching framework.
AI.result: The method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art semi-dense matching models in both accuracy and efficiency across multiple standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SURE provides reliable correspondences by incorporating principled uncertainty estimation, improving robustness in challenging scenarios, with code availability on GitHub.

title: Implicit Bias and Loss of Plasticity in Matrix Completion: Depth Promotes Low-Rankness
summary: We study matrix completion via deep matrix factorization (a.k.a. deep linear neural networks) as a simplified testbed to examine how network depth influences training dynamics. Despite the simplicity and importance of the problem, prior theory largely focuses on shallow (depth-2) models and does not fully explain the implicit low-rank bias observed in deeper networks. We identify coupled dynamics as a key mechanism behind this bias and show that it intensifies with increasing depth. Focusing on gradient flow under block-diagonal observations, we prove: (a) networks of depth $\geq 3$ exhibit coupling unless initialized diagonally, and (b) convergence to rank-1 occurs if and only if the dynamics is coupled -- resolving an open question by Menon (2024) for a family of initializations. We also revisit the loss of plasticity phenomenon in matrix completion (Kleinman et al., 2024), where pre-training on few observations and resuming with more degrades performance. We show that deep models avoid plasticity loss due to their low-rank bias, whereas depth-2 networks pre-trained under decoupled dynamics fail to converge to low-rank, even when resumed training (with additional data) satisfies the coupling condition -- shedding light on the mechanism behind this phenomenon.

AI.tldr: Deep linear networks with gradient flow in matrix completion show depth-dependent coupling that biases toward low rank; depth≥3 leads to coupling (unless diagonal init) and rank-1 convergence iff coupled, addressing Menon 2024; explains plasticity loss phenomena where deep models maintain low-rank bias, unlike depth-2 networks.
AI.motivation: Understand how depth in deep matrix factorization influences training dynamics and the origin of the observed implicit low-rank bias, using a tractable gradient-flow setting.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of gradient flow for deep matrix factorization under block-diagonal observation patterns; classify regimes into coupled vs decoupled dynamics; prove necessary/sufficient conditions for rank-1 convergence; analyze initialization effects and relate to existing open problems (Menon 2024) and prior plasticity work (Kleinman et al., 2024).
AI.result: Proves that depth≥3 networks exhibit coupling unless initialized diagonally; rank-1 convergence iff the dynamics are coupled; resolves an open question for a family of initializations; shows deep models avoid plasticity loss due to low-rank bias, whereas depth-2 with decoupled pre-training fails to converge to low rank even with resumed data under coupling.
AI.conclusion: Depth-induced coupling dynamics account for the depth-dependent implicit low-rank bias in matrix completion, explain plasticity phenomena, and illuminate initialization sensitivity; provides theoretical resolution to prior open questions and informs design/training of deep matrix factorization models.

title: Authorize-on-Demand: Dynamic Authorization with Legality-Aware Intellectual Property Protection for VLMs
summary: The rapid adoption of vision-language models (VLMs) has heightened the demand for robust intellectual property (IP) protection of these high-value pretrained models. Effective IP protection should proactively confine model deployment within authorized domains and prevent unauthorized transfers. However, existing methods rely on static training-time definitions, limiting flexibility in dynamic environments and often producing opaque responses to unauthorized inputs. To address these limitations, we propose a novel dynamic authorization with legality-aware intellectual property protection (AoD-IP) for VLMs, a framework that supports authorize-on-demand and legality-aware assessment. AoD-IP introduces a lightweight dynamic authorization module that enables flexible, user-controlled authorization, allowing users to actively specify or switch authorized domains on demand at deployment time. This enables the model to adapt seamlessly as application scenarios evolve and provides substantially greater extensibility than existing static-domain approaches. In addition, AoD-IP incorporates a dual-path inference mechanism that jointly predicts input legality-aware and task-specific outputs. Comprehensive experimental results on multiple cross-domain benchmarks demonstrate that AoD-IP maintains strong authorized-domain performance and reliable unauthorized detection, while supporting user-controlled authorization for adaptive deployment in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: Proposes AoD-IP, a dynamic, legality-aware IP protection framework for VLMs that supports authorize-on-demand and dual-path inference to limit deployment to authorized domains while detecting unauthorized inputs.
AI.motivation: Static, training-time domain protections are inflexible in dynamic deployment settings and often provide opaque responses to illegitimate inputs, risking IP leakage or misuse of VLMs.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight dynamic authorization module enabling user-controlled authorization of domains at deployment; implement a dual-path inference mechanism that jointly predicts input legality-aware and task-specific outputs; evaluate on cross-domain benchmarks.
AI.result: AoD-IP preserves strong performance within authorized domains, reliably detects unauthorized inputs, and offers greater extensibility than static-domain methods.
AI.conclusion: AoD-IP enables adaptive, legality-aware IP protection for VLMs suitable for evolving applications and dynamic environments.

title: Critic in the Loop: A Tri-System VLA Framework for Robust Long-Horizon Manipulation
summary: Balancing high-level semantic reasoning with low-level reactive control remains a core challenge in visual robotic manipulation. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at cognitive planning, their inference latency precludes real-time execution. Conversely, fast Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often lack the semantic depth required for complex, long-horizon tasks. To bridge this gap, we introduce Critic in the Loop, an adaptive hierarchical framework driven by dynamic VLM-Expert scheduling. At its core is a bionic Tri-System architecture comprising a VLM brain for global reasoning, a VLA cerebellum for reactive execution, and a lightweight visual Critic. By continuously monitoring the workspace, the Critic dynamically routes control authority. It sustains rapid closed-loop execution via the VLA for routine subtasks, and adaptively triggers the VLM for replanning upon detecting execution anomalies such as task stagnation or failures. Furthermore, our architecture seamlessly integrates human-inspired rules to intuitively break infinite retry loops. This visually-grounded scheduling minimizes expensive VLM queries, while substantially enhancing system robustness and autonomy in out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios. Comprehensive experiments on challenging, long-horizon manipulation benchmarks reveal that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical, adaptive framework called Critic in the Loop combines fast Vision-Language-Action control with a Vision-Language-Model-based global planner. A lightweight Critic monitors and dynamically routes control between a VLA cerebellum (fast local control) and a VLM brain (global reasoning), triggering replanning when execution anomalies occur, while incorporating human-inspired rules to prevent infinite retries. The approach reduces expensive VLM queries and improves robustness, achieving state-of-the-art results on long-horizon manipulation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between slow, semantically rich VLM-based planning and fast, reactive control. Reduce inference latency without sacrificing long-horizon understanding, and improve robustness in out-of-distribution tasks.
AI.method: Propose a tri-system architecture: a VLM brain for global reasoning; a VLA cerebellum for reactive execution; and a lightweight visual Critic that monitors the workspace and dynamically schedules control authority. The Critic routes routine subtasks to the VLA, and triggers VLM replanning upon anomalies such as stagnation or failures. Incorporates human-inspired rules to avoid infinite retry loops. Aimed at minimizing VLM queries while maintaining robustness.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on challenging, long-horizon manipulation benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance, with improved robustness and autonomy, particularly in out-of-distribution scenarios, and reduced reliance on expensive VLM queries.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, hierarchical control with dynamic VLM-Expert scheduling and a lightweight Critic enables fast execution for routine tasks while maintaining semantic planning for complex steps. Human-inspired safeguards and visible-grounding further enhance robustness and autonomy.

title: Diffusion-Based sRGB Real Noise Generation via Prompt-Driven Noise Representation Learning
summary: Denoising in the sRGB image space is challenging due to noise variability. Although end-to-end methods perform well, their effectiveness in real-world scenarios is limited by the scarcity of real noisy-clean image pairs, which are expensive and difficult to collect. To address this limitation, several generative methods have been developed to synthesize realistic noisy images from limited data. These generative approaches often rely on camera metadata during both training and testing to synthesize real-world noise. However, the lack of metadata or inconsistencies between devices restricts their usability. Therefore, we propose a novel framework called Prompt-Driven Noise Generation (PNG). This model is capable of acquiring high-dimensional prompt features that capture the characteristics of real-world input noise and creating a variety of realistic noisy images consistent with the distribution of the input noise. By eliminating the dependency on explicit camera metadata, our approach significantly enhances the generalizability and applicability of noise synthesis. Comprehensive experiments reveal that our model effectively produces realistic noisy images and show the successful application of these generated images in removing real-world noise across various benchmark datasets.

AI.tldr: Prompt-Driven Noise Generation (PNG) synthesizes realistic real-world noisy images from high-dimensional prompt features without relying on camera metadata, enabling better denoising data augmentation and generalization.
AI.motivation: Denoising in sRGB is challenged by noise variability and the scarcity of real noisy-clean paired data. Generative methods often require camera metadata, which is often unavailable or inconsistent, limiting real-world applicability.
AI.method: PNG learns a prompt-conditioned noise generator that captures high-dimensional characteristics of input noise, generating diverse noisy images aligned with the distribution of real-world noise without relying on explicit camera metadata, thus enabling metadata-free noise synthesis for downstream denoising tasks.
AI.result: Experiments indicate that the generated noise is realistic and that the synthesized noisy-clean data effectively improves denoising performance across multiple benchmark datasets, demonstrating better generalization to real-world noise without metadata.
AI.conclusion: PNG enhances the generalizability and applicability of noise synthesis by removing reliance on camera metadata, providing a flexible framework for real-world denoising applications.

title: Probabilistic Dreaming for World Models
comment: Presented at ICLR 2026: 2nd Workshop on World Models
summary: "Dreaming" enables agents to learn from imagined experiences, enabling more robust and sample-efficient learning of world models. In this work, we consider innovations to the state-of-the-art Dreamer model using probabilistic methods that enable: (1) the parallel exploration of many latent states; and (2) maintaining distinct hypotheses for mutually exclusive futures while retaining the desirable gradient properties of continuous latents. Evaluating on the MPE SimpleTag domain, our method outperforms standard Dreamer with a 4.5% score improvement and 28% lower variance in episode returns. We also discuss limitations and directions for future work, including how optimal hyperparameters (e.g. particle count K) scale with environmental complexity, and methods to capture epistemic uncertainty in world models.

AI.tldr: Probabilistic Dreamer with multi-particle latent exploration and multi-hypothesis futures improves planning robustness, showing better score and lower variance on MPE SimpleTag; highlights future work on hyperparameter scaling and epistemic uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Enhance world-model learning by incorporating uncertainty-aware, parallel latent exploration to improve sample efficiency and robustness in reinforcement learning.
AI.method: Extend Dreamer with particle-based latent representations to explore many latent states in parallel and maintain distinct hypotheses for mutually exclusive futures, while preserving differentiable gradients on continuous latents.
AI.result: On the MPE SimpleTag domain, the method achieves a 4.5% improvement in score and 28% lower variance in episode returns compared with standard Dreamer.
AI.conclusion: The proposed probabilistic latent framework increases robustness and sample efficiency; limitations include scaling hyperparameters (e.g., particle count K) with environmental complexity and the need for methods to quantify epistemic uncertainty in world models.

title: EvoTool: Self-Evolving Tool-Use Policy Optimization in LLM Agents via Blame-Aware Mutation and Diversity-Aware Selection
summary: LLM-based agents depend on effective tool-use policies to solve complex tasks, yet optimizing these policies remains challenging due to delayed supervision and the difficulty of credit assignment in long-horizon trajectories. Existing optimization approaches tend to be either monolithic, which are prone to entangling behaviors, or single-aspect, which ignore cross-module error propagation. To address these limitations, we propose EvoTool, a self-evolving framework that optimizes a modular tool-use policy via a gradient-free evolutionary paradigm. EvoTool decomposes agent's tool-use policy into four modules, including Planner, Selector, Caller, and Synthesizer, and iteratively improves them in a self-improving loop through three novel mechanisms. Trajectory-Grounded Blame Attribution uses diagnostic traces to localize failures to a specific module. Feedback-Guided Targeted Mutation then edits only that module via natural-language critique. Diversity-Aware Population Selection preserves complementary candidates to ensure solution diversity. Across four benchmarks, EvoTool outperforms strong baselines by over 5 points on both GPT-4.1 and Qwen3-8B, while achieving superior efficiency and transferability. The code will be released once paper is accepted.

AI.tldr: EvoTool is a gradient-free, self-evolving framework that modularizes LLM tool-use policies into Planner, Selector, Caller, and Synthesizer, and improves them via three mechanisms: blame attribution, targeted mutation, and diversity-aware selection, achieving strong gains across four benchmarks on GPT-4.1 and Qwen3-8B with claimed efficiency and transferability benefits; code will be released with acceptance.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of monolithic or single-aspect optimization of LLM tool-use policies, particularly long-horizon tasks with delayed supervision and difficulty in credit assignment across modules.
AI.method: Decompose policy into four modules and optimize via a gradient-free evolutionary loop, using (1) Trajectory-Grounded Blame Attribution to localize failures to a module, (2) Feedback-Guided Targeted Mutation to edit that module via natural-language critique, and (3) Diversity-Aware Population Selection to preserve complementary candidates. Evaluate on four benchmarks across GPT-4.1 and Qwen3-8B.
AI.result: EvoTool outperforms strong baselines by over 5 points on both GPT-4.1 and Qwen3-8B across four benchmarks, with improved efficiency and transferability; code release promised after acceptance.
AI.conclusion: A modular, evolution-based optimization of LLM tool-use policies can yield better performance and generalization by localizing failures, applying targeted mutations, and maintaining diverse populations; the approach shows strong empirical gains and transferability across models.

title: Rethinking the Role of Collaborative Robots in Rehabilitation
summary: Current research on collaborative robots (cobots) in physical rehabilitation largely focuses on repeated motion training for people undergoing physical therapy (PuPT), even though these sessions include phases that could benefit from robotic collaboration and assistance. Meanwhile, access to physical therapy remains limited for people with disabilities and chronic illnesses. Cobots could support both PuPT and therapists, and improve access to therapy, yet their broader potential remains underexplored. We propose extending the scope of cobots by imagining their role in assisting therapists and PuPT before, during, and after a therapy session. We discuss how cobot assistance may lift access barriers by promoting ability-based therapy design and helping therapists manage their time and effort. Finally, we highlight challenges to realizing these roles, including advancing user-state understanding, ensuring safety, and integrating cobots into therapists' workflow. This view opens new research questions and opportunities to draw from the HRI community's advances in assistive robotics.

AI.tldr: Proposes expanding cobot roles in physical rehabilitation to assist therapists and PuPT across pre-, during-, and post-session stages, aiming to boost access and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current cobot research focuses on repetitive motion training with limited reach to therapists and broader therapy workflow; expanding roles could improve access to therapy and support therapists.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis and literature synthesis; proposes a framework of potential cobot roles, design principles, and research questions for assistive robotics in rehabilitation.
AI.result: A high-level framework outlining new roles for cobots in rehab, including pre-, during-, and post-session support, with design considerations and evaluation questions to guide future work.
AI.conclusion: Advocates an interdisciplinary research agenda bridging HRI and assistive robotics to realize cobot-enabled, access-enhancing physical rehabilitation workflows.

title: Interpretable Pre-Release Baseball Pitch Type Anticipation from Broadcast 3D Kinematics
summary: How much can a pitcher's body reveal about the upcoming pitch? We study this question at scale by classifying eight pitch types from monocular 3D pose sequences, without access to ball-flight data. Our pipeline chains a diffusion-based 3D pose backbone with automatic pitching-event detection, groundtruth-validated biomechanical feature extraction, and gradient-boosted classification over 229 kinematic features. Evaluated on 119,561 professional pitches, the largest such benchmark to date, we achieve 80.4\% accuracy using body kinematics alone. A systematic importance analysis reveals that upper-body mechanics contribute 64.9\% of the predictive signal versus 35.1\% for the lower body, with wrist position (14.8\%) and trunk lateral tilt emerging as the most informative joint group and biomechanical feature, respectively. We further show that grip-defined variants (four-seam vs.\ two-seam fastball) are not separable from pose, establishing an empirical ceiling near 80\% and delineating where kinematic information ends and ball-flight information begins.

AI.tldr: Classifies eight pitch types from monocular 3D poses with 80.4% accuracy; kinematics-only approach using diffusion-based 3D pose backbone and 229 biomechanical features.
AI.motivation: Quantify how much information body pose reveals about pitch type, and where kinematic signals end and ball-flight information begins.
AI.method: Diffusion-based 3D pose backbone with automatic pitching-event detection, groundtruth-validated biomechanical feature extraction, and gradient-boosted classification over 229 features; large-scale dataset of 119,561 professional pitches.
AI.result: Achieves 80.4% accuracy using body kinematics alone; upper-body mechanics contribute 64.9% of predictive signal vs 35.1% from lower body; wrist position accounts for 14.8% of signal; trunk lateral tilt identified as the most informative biomechanical feature; grip-defined variants (four-seam vs two-seam) are not separable from pose, ceiling near 80%.
AI.conclusion: Kinematic information suffices to predict a majority of pitch types up to an empirical ceiling near 80%; ball-flight data is needed to exceed this ceiling; upper-body mechanics are the dominant source of predictive signal, with specific joints and features identified as most informative.

title: Count Bridges enable Modeling and Deconvolving Transcriptomic Data
summary: Many modern biological assays, including RNA sequencing, yield integer-valued counts that reflect the number of molecules detected. These measurements are often not at the desired resolution: while the unit of interest is typically a single cell, many measurement technologies produce counts aggregated over sets of cells. Although recent generative frameworks such as diffusion and flow matching have been extended to non-Euclidean and discrete settings, it remains unclear how best to model integer-valued data or how to systematically deconvolve aggregated observations. We introduce Count Bridges, a stochastic bridge process on the integers that provides an exact, tractable analogue of diffusion-style models for count data, with closed-form conditionals for efficient training and sampling. We extend this framework to enable direct training from aggregated measurements via an Expectation-Maximization-style approach that treats unit-level counts as latent variables. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on integer distribution matching benchmarks, comparing against flow matching and discrete flow matching baselines across various metrics. We then apply Count Bridges to two large-scale problems in biology: modeling single-cell gene expression data at the nucleotide resolution, with applications to deconvolving bulk RNA-seq, and resolving multicellular spatial transcriptomic spots into single-cell count profiles. Our methods offer a principled foundation for generative modeling and deconvolution of biological count data across scales and modalities.

AI.tldr: Count Bridges introduces a stochastic bridge process on the integers for modeling count data, enabling exact diffusion-like training for discrete counts and EM-based deconvolution from aggregated observations; achieves state-of-the-art on integer distribution benchmarks and applies to single-cell RNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics deconvolution.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in diffusion-model-based generative modeling for discrete, integer-valued, bin-count data and the need to deconvolve aggregated measurements in single-cell biology.
AI.method: Develop a Count Bridges process with closed-form conditional distributions, training/sampling routines, and an EM-style approach to leverage aggregated data by treating unit-level counts as latent variables; compare against flow matching baselines; apply to scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics deconvolution.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on integer distribution matching benchmarks; successful deconvolution and nucleotide-resolution modeling; improved cross-scale generative modeling for count data.
AI.conclusion: Count Bridges provides a principled, tractable framework for discrete count data diffusion-like modeling and deconvolution, with broad applicability to biology and other domains.

title: Alignment Backfire: Language-Dependent Reversal of Safety Interventions Across 16 Languages in LLM Multi-Agent Systems
comment: 89 pages, 4 figures, 4 supplementary figures, 12 supplementary tables; preprint
summary: In perpetrator treatment, a recurring observation is the dissociation between insight and action: offenders articulate remorse yet behavioral change does not follow. We report four preregistered studies (1,584 multi-agent simulations across 16 languages and three model families) demonstrating that alignment interventions in large language models produce a structurally analogous phenomenon: surface safety that masks or generates collective pathology and internal dissociation. In Study 1 (N = 150), increasing alignment-instructed agents reduced collective pathology in English (g = -1.844, p < .0001) but amplified it in Japanese (g = +0.771, p = .038)--a directional reversal we term "alignment backfire." Study 2 (N = 1,174) extended to 16 languages: alignment-induced dissociation was near-universal (15/16 languages; beta = 0.0667, p < .0001), while collective pathology bifurcated along cultural-linguistic lines (interaction beta = 0.0684, p = .0003), correlating with Power Distance Index (r = 0.474, p = .064). Study 3 (N = 180) tested individuation as countermeasure; individuated agents became the primary source of both pathology and dissociation (DI = +1.120) with conformity above 84%--demonstrating iatrogenesis. Study 4 (N = 80) validated patterns across Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-4o-mini, and Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B, confirming English safety is model-general while Japanese backfire is model-specific. These findings reframe alignment as a behavioral intervention subject to risk homeostasis and iatrogenesis. Language space--the linguistic, pragmatic, and cultural properties inherited from training data--structurally determines alignment outcomes. Safety validated in English does not transfer to other languages, and prompt-level interventions cannot override language-space-level constraints.

AI.tldr: Alignment interventions in LLMs produce surface safety that masks collective pathology and dissociation; outcomes are language-space dependent, with backfire in Japanese; dissociation is near-universal across languages; individuation causes iatrogenesis; findings generalize across models; safety proven in English does not generalize to other languages.
AI.motivation: To test whether alignment reduces pathology across languages and to examine the role of linguistic-cultural context (language-space) on alignment outcomes, addressing the discrepancy between stated remorse and action in AI and drawing parallels to offender treatment dissociation.
AI.method: Four preregistered studies (N total 1,584) using multi-agent simulations across 16 languages and three model families. Study 1 compared English vs Japanese; Study 2 expanded to 16 languages; Study 3 tested individuated vs conforming agents; Study 4 validated across Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-4o-mini, Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B.
AI.result: Study 1: alignment-instructed agents reduced pathology in English but increased it in Japanese (backfire). Study 2: alignment-induced dissociation nearly universal (15/16 languages); cultural-linguistic lines modulated pathology via an interaction with Power Distance Index. Study 3: individuation created a primary source of both pathology and dissociation (iatrogenesis) at high conformity. Study 4: pattern replicated across additional models; English safety is model-general, Japanese backfire is model-specific.
AI.conclusion: Alignment acts as a behavioral intervention constrained by language-space properties; safety gains in English do not transfer to other languages; prompt-level tuning cannot override language-space-level constraints; outcomes reflect risk homeostasis and iatrogenesis driven by language, culture, and model architecture.

title: Structure Observation Driven Image-Text Contrastive Learning for Computed Tomography Report Generation
summary: Computed Tomography Report Generation (CTRG) aims to automate the clinical radiology reporting process, thereby reducing the workload of report writing and facilitating patient care. While deep learning approaches have achieved remarkable advances in X-ray report generation, their effectiveness may be limited in CTRG due to larger data volumes of CT images and more intricate details required to describe them. This work introduces a novel two-stage (structure- and report-learning) framework tailored for CTRG featuring effective structure-wise image-text contrasting. In the first stage, a set of learnable structure-specific visual queries observe corresponding structures in a CT image. The resulting observation tokens are contrasted with structure-specific textual features extracted from the accompanying radiology report with a structure-wise image-text contrastive loss. In addition, text-text similarity-based soft pseudo targets are proposed to mitigate the impact of false negatives, i.e., semantically identical image structures and texts from non-paired images and reports. Thus, the model learns structure-level semantic correspondences between CT images and reports. Further, a dynamic, diversity-enhanced negative queue is proposed to guide the network in learning to discriminate various abnormalities. In the second stage, the visual structure queries are frozen and used to select the critical image patch embeddings depicting each anatomical structure, minimizing distractions from irrelevant areas while reducing memory consumption. Also, a text decoder is added and trained for report generation.Our extensive experiments on two public datasets demonstrate that our framework establishes new state-of-the-art performance for CTRG in clinical efficiency, and its components are effective.

AI.tldr: Two-stage CTRG framework using structure-specific visual queries and contrastive learning to align CT structures with report text, followed by patch-level selection and report decoding; achieves state-of-the-art results on two public datasets.
AI.motivation: CT Rich CT data require structure-aware alignment between images and reports; existing X-ray-focused methods struggle with the volume and complexity of CT data, necessitating a method that learns structure-level semantic correspondences to improve efficacy and efficiency.
AI.method: Stage 1: learnable structure-specific visual queries observe structures in CT images; structure-wise image-text contrastive loss aligns observation tokens with structure-specific textual features; soft pseudo targets mitigate false negatives; dynamic diversity-enhanced negative queue. Stage 2: freeze visual queries to select critical image patch embeddings for each structure and train a text decoder for report generation.
AI.result: Experiments on two public datasets show state-of-the-art CTRG performance and improved clinical efficiency; ablations confirm effectiveness of the proposed components (structure-wise contrastive learning, soft targets, negative queue, and patch-level selection).
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage, structure-guided framework effectively learns structure-level image-text correspondences for CTRG, enabling accurate, efficient report generation with reduced distractions from irrelevant regions.

title: When Priors Backfire: On the Vulnerability of Unlearnable Examples to Pretraining
summary: Unlearnable Examples (UEs) serve as a data protection strategy that generates imperceptible perturbations to mislead models into learning spurious correlations instead of underlying semantics. In this paper, we uncover a fundamental vulnerability of UEs that emerges when learning starts from a pretrained model. Crucially, our empirical analysis shows that even when data are protected by carefully crafted perturbations, pretraining priors still furnish rich semantic representations that allow the model to circumvent the shortcuts introduced by UEs and capture genuine features, thereby nullifying unlearnability. To address this, we propose BAIT (Binding Artificial perturbations to Incorrect Targets), a novel bi-level optimization formulation. Specifically, the inner level aims at associating the perturbed samples with real labels to simulate standard data-label alignment, while the outer level actively disrupts this alignment by enforcing a mislabel-perturbation binding that maps samples to designated incorrect targets. This mechanism effectively overrides the semantic guidance of priors, forcing the model to rely on the injected perturbations and consequently preventing the acquisition of true semantics. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks and multiple pretrained backbones demonstrate that BAIT effectively mitigates the influence of pretraining priors and maintains data unlearnability.

AI.tldr: BAIT uses a bi-level optimization to preserve data unlearnability against pretrained priors by binding perturbations to incorrect targets, countering the semantic guidance from pretraining.
AI.motivation: Pretrained models can extract genuine semantics from perturbed data, undermining unlearnable examples. There is a need to counteract this by enforcing misalignment that cannot be resolved by priors.
AI.method: Bi-level optimization: inner level aligns perturbed samples with true labels to simulate standard data-label alignment; outer level enforces mislabel-perturbation binding to designated incorrect targets, overriding priors and forcing reliance on perturbations.
AI.result: BAIT mitigates the influence of pretraining priors and preserves unlearnability across standard benchmarks and multiple pretrained backbones.
AI.conclusion: The BAIT framework effectively neutralizes the semantic guidance from pretrained priors, maintaining data unlearnability by binding perturbations to incorrect targets.

title: Knowledge-informed Bidding with Dual-process Control for Online Advertising
summary: Bid optimization in online advertising relies on black-box machine-learning models that learn bidding decisions from historical data. However, these approaches fail to replicate human experts' adaptive, experience-driven, and globally coherent decisions. Specifically, they generalize poorly in data-sparse cases because of missing structured knowledge, make short-sighted sequential decisions that ignore long-term interdependencies, and struggle to adapt in out-of-distribution scenarios where human experts succeed. To address this, we propose KBD (Knowledge-informed Bidding with Dual-process control), a novel method for bid optimization. KBD embeds human expertise as inductive biases through the informed machine-learning paradigm, uses Decision Transformer (DT) to globally optimize multi-step bidding sequences, and implements dual-process control by combining a fast rule-based PID (System 1) with DT (System 2). Extensive experiments highlight KBD's advantage over existing methods and underscore the benefit of grounding bid optimization in human expertise and dual-process control.

AI.tldr: Knowledge-informed bidding (KBD) combines human expertise as inductive biases, a Decision Transformer for multi-step bid planning, and a dual-process controller (fast PID plus DT) to improve online ad bidding over black-box models.
AI.motivation: Current black-box bid optimizers struggle with data scarcity, lack long-horizon coherence, and fail to adapt to distribution shifts. Integrating human knowledge and dual-process reasoning aims to improve generalization and strategic bidding.
AI.method: Embed human expertise as inductive biases; use a Decision Transformer to optimize multi-step bidding sequences globally; implement dual-process control by pairing a fast rule-based PID (System 1) with the DT planner (System 2).
AI.result: Extensive experiments show KBD outperforms existing methods, underscoring the value of grounding bidding in human expertise and dual-process control.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge-informed bidding with dual-process control is a promising approach for robust, coherent, long-horizon bid optimization, particularly in data-sparse and out-of-distribution settings.

title: From Code to Road: A Vehicle-in-the-Loop and Digital Twin-Based Framework for Central Car Server Testing in Autonomous Driving
comment: 8 pages; Accepted for publication at the 37th IEEE Intelligent Vehicles Symposium (IV), Detroit, MI, United States, June 22-25, 2026
summary: Simulation is one of the most essential parts in the development stage of automotive software. However, purely virtual simulations often struggle to accurately capture all real-world factors due to limitations in modeling. To address this challenge, this work presents a test framework for automotive software on the centralized E/E architecture, which is a central car server in our case, based on Vehicle-in-the-Loop (ViL) and digital twin technology. The framework couples a physical test vehicle on a dynamometer test bench with its synchronized virtual counterpart in a simulation environment. Our approach provides a safe, reproducible, realistic, and cost-effective platform for validating autonomous driving algorithms with a centralized architecture. This test method eliminates the need to test individual physical ECUs and their communication protocols separately. In contrast to traditional ViL methods, the proposed framework runs the full autonomous driving software directly on the vehicle hardware after the simulation process, eliminating flashing and intermediate layers while enabling seamless virtual-physical integration and accurately reflecting centralized E/E behavior. In addition, incorporating mixed testing in both simulated and physical environments reduces the need for full hardware integration during the early stages of automotive development. Experimental case studies demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework in different test scenarios. These findings highlight the potential to reduce development and integration efforts for testing autonomous driving pipelines in the future.

AI.tldr: A centralized E/E automotive testing framework using Vehicle-in-the-Loop and digital twin to couple a real dynamometer-equipped vehicle with a synchronized virtual model, enabling full autonomous software execution on hardware after simulation, plus mixed simulated/physical testing for safer, reproducible, and cost-effective validation.
AI.motivation: Purely virtual simulations struggle to capture real-world factors; centralized E/E architectures require integrated testing across hardware and software; there is a need for a safe, reproducible, and cost-effective platform to validate autonomous driving pipelines without repeatedly testing individual ECUs or flashing software.
AI.method: Develop a test framework that links a physical vehicle on a dynamometer with a digital twin in simulation, ensuring synchronization between the real and virtual counterparts. The full autonomous driving software runs directly on the vehicle hardware after the simulation step, reflecting centralized E/E behavior. The approach supports mixed testing in simulated and physical environments and eliminates the need for ECU-by-ECU testing and intermediate layers.
AI.result: Experimental case studies demonstrate effectiveness across various test scenarios, showing the framework provides a safe, realistic, reproducible, and cost-effective platform for validating autonomous driving algorithms within a centralized architecture, and reduces development and integration efforts.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers potential to streamline testing of autonomous driving pipelines in centralized vehicle architectures and accelerate development and integration by enabling safe, mixed, and hardware-in-the-loop validation without ECU flashing.

title: Distribution-Conditioned Transport
summary: Learning a transport model that maps a source distribution to a target distribution is a canonical problem in machine learning, but scientific applications increasingly require models that can generalize to source and target distributions unseen during training. We introduce distribution-conditioned transport (DCT), a framework that conditions transport maps on learned embeddings of source and target distributions, enabling generalization to unseen distribution pairs. DCT also allows semi-supervised learning for distributional forecasting problems: because it learns from arbitrary distribution pairs, it can leverage distributions observed at only one condition to improve transport prediction. DCT is agnostic to the underlying transport mechanism, supporting models ranging from flow matching to distributional divergence-based models (e.g. Wasserstein, MMD). We demonstrate the practical performance benefits of DCT on synthetic benchmarks and four applications in biology: batch effect transfer in single-cell genomics, perturbation prediction from mass cytometry data, learning clonal transcriptional dynamics in hematopoiesis, and modeling T-cell receptor sequence evolution.

AI.tldr: Distribution-conditioned transport (DCT) generalizes transport maps to unseen distribution pairs by conditioning on learned embeddings of source and target distributions; it supports semi-supervised distributional forecasting, is agnostic to the transport mechanism, and shows benefits on synthetic data and four biology applications.
AI.motivation: There is a need for transport models that generalize across source/target distributions not seen during training, enabling robust predictive transport in scientific settings and leveraging partial distributional observations for semi-supervised learning.
AI.method: DCT introduces distribution-conditioned transport by learning embeddings of source and target distributions and conditioning the transport map on these embeddings. The framework is agnostic to the underlying transport mechanism (e.g., flow matching, Wasserstein/MMD-based divergence approaches) and can incorporate semi-supervised learning by using distributions observed at only one condition to improve predictions.
AI.result: DCT improves generalization to unseen distribution pairs on synthetic benchmarks and yields practical gains in four biology applications: batch effect transfer in single-cell genomics, perturbation prediction from mass cytometry data, evolution of clonal transcriptional dynamics in hematopoiesis, and modeling T-cell receptor sequence evolution.
AI.conclusion: DCT provides a flexible, broadly applicable conditioning scheme for transport models, enabling generalization to unseen distributions and semi-supervised forecasting, with demonstrated benefits in diverse scientific domains.

title: TimeWarp: Evaluating Web Agents by Revisiting the Past
summary: The improvement of web agents on current benchmarks raises the question: Do today's agents perform just as well when the web changes? We introduce TimeWarp, a benchmark that emulates the evolving web using containerized environments that vary in UI, design, and layout. TimeWarp consists of three web environments, each with six UI versions spanning different eras of the internet, paired with a set of complex, realistic tasks requiring different forms of web navigation. Our experiments reveal web agents' vulnerability to changes and the limitations of behavior cloning (BC) on single-version trajectories. To address this, we propose TimeTraj, a simple yet effective algorithm that uses plan distillation to collect trajectories across multiple versions. By training agents on teacher rollouts using our BC-variant, we achieve substantial performance gains: $20.4\%\rightarrow37.7\%$ for Qwen-3 4B and $0\%\rightarrow27.0\%$ for Llama-3.1 8B models. We hope our work helps researchers study generalization across web designs and unlock a new paradigm for collecting plans rather than trajectories, thereby improving the robustness of web agents.

AI.tldr: TimeWarp benchmarks evolving web designs with containerized UI variants; shows agents' vulnerability to UI changes and BC limitations on single-version data. TimeTraj uses plan distillation to train across multiple versions, yielding strong gains on large language models. Aims to study cross-version generalization and shift to plan-based data collection for robustness.
AI.motivation: Address robustness and generalization of web agents as the web evolves, and highlight limitations of behavior cloning when trained on a single web-version trajectory.
AI.method: Construct TimeWarp with 3 web environments, each containing 6 UI versions spanning eras of the internet, coupled with realistic navigation tasks. Introduce TimeTraj, a BC-variant that performs plan distillation by collecting teacher rollouts across multiple versions and training on distilled plans rather than single-version trajectories.
AI.result: Empirical findings show agents are vulnerable to UI changes; BC on single-version data underperforms. TimeTraj achieves notable gains: Qwen-3 4B improves from 20.4% to 37.7%; Llama-3.1 8B improves from 0% to 27.0%.
AI.conclusion: TimeWarp enables systematic study of generalization across evolving web designs; TimeTraj provides a simple, effective cross-version planning method that improves robustness by collecting and distilling plans across versions.

title: Iterative On-Policy Refinement of Hierarchical Diffusion Policies for Language-Conditioned Manipulation
summary: Hierarchical policies for language-conditioned manipulation decompose tasks into subgoals, where a high-level planner guides a low-level controller. However, these hierarchical agents often fail because the planner generates subgoals without considering the actual limitations of the controller. Existing solutions attempt to bridge this gap via intermediate modules or shared representations, but they remain limited by their reliance on fixed offline datasets. We propose HD-ExpIt, a framework for iterative fine-tuning of hierarchical diffusion policies via environment feedback. HD-ExpIt organizes training into a self-reinforcing cycle: it utilizes diffusion-based planning to autonomously discover successful behaviors, which are then distilled back into the hierarchical policy. This loop enables both components to improve while implicitly grounding the planner in the controller's actual capabilities without requiring explicit proxy models. Empirically, HD-ExpIt significantly improves hierarchical policies trained solely on offline data, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the long-horizon CALVIN benchmark among methods trained from scratch.

AI.tldr: HD-ExpIt introduces an iterative, environment-feedback loop to fine-tune hierarchical diffusion policies, enabling diffusion-based planning to autonomously discover successful long-horizon behaviors and distill them into the hierarchy, without explicit proxy models.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between high-level planners and low-level controllers in language-conditioned manipulation and overcome reliance on fixed offline datasets by grounding planning in the controller's actual capabilities.
AI.method: A self-reinforcing cycle where diffusion-based planning discovers successful behaviors in the environment, these behaviors are distilled back into the hierarchical policy, and the loop iteratively improves both planner and controller without requiring explicit proxy models.
AI.result: Significant improvements over offline-trained hierarchical policies, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the long-horizon CALVIN benchmark among methods trained from scratch.
AI.conclusion: HD-ExpIt grounds the planner in the controller's capabilities through environment feedback, reduces dependence on offline data, and provides a general framework for improving hierarchical diffusion-based policies for long-horizon tasks.

title: Federated Modality-specific Encoders and Partially Personalized Fusion Decoder for Multimodal Brain Tumor Segmentation
comment: Medical Image Analysis 2025. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2403.11803
summary: Most existing federated learning (FL) methods for medical image analysis only considered intramodal heterogeneity, limiting their applicability to multimodal imaging applications. In practice, some FL participants may possess only a subset of the complete imaging modalities, posing intermodal heterogeneity as a challenge to effectively training a global model on all participants' data. Meanwhile, each participant expects a personalized model tailored to its local data characteristics in FL. This work proposes a new FL framework with federated modality-specific encoders and partially personalized multimodal fusion decoders (FedMEPD) to address the two concurrent issues. Specifically, FedMEPD employs an exclusive encoder for each modality to account for the intermodal heterogeneity. While these encoders are fully federated, the decoders are partially personalized to meet individual needs -- using the discrepancy between global and local parameter updates to dynamically determine which decoder filters are personalized. Implementation-wise, a server with full-modal data employs a fusion decoder to fuse representations from all modality-specific encoders, thus bridging the modalities to optimize the encoders via backpropagation. Moreover, multiple anchors are extracted from the fused multimodal representations and distributed to the clients in addition to the model parameters. Conversely, the clients with incomplete modalities calibrate their missing-modal representations toward the global full-modal anchors via scaled dot-product cross-attention, making up for the information loss due to absent modalities. FedMEPD is validated on the BraTS 2018 and 2020 multimodal brain tumor segmentation benchmarks. Results show that it outperforms various up-to-date methods for multimodal and personalized FL, and its novel designs are effective.

AI.tldr: FedMEPD introduces federated modality-specific encoders with partially personalized multimodal fusion decoders to address intermodal heterogeneity and local personalization in federated learning for multimodal medical imaging; a server-fusion path with anchors and cross-attention-based missing-modality alignment enables effective training and personalization, validated on BraTS datasets with superior results.
AI.motivation: The limitations of existing FL methods in medical imaging: they mainly handle intramodal heterogeneity and assume all participants have all modalities. In practice, participants may have different modality subsets (intermodal heterogeneity) and desire personalized models. This motivates a framework that keeps modality-specific encoders federated while allowing partial personalization in fusion decoders and leveraging anchors and cross-attention to bridge missing modalities.
AI.method:
- An exclusive encoder per modality, fully federated across clients.
- A partially personalized fusion decoder: determine which decoder filters are personalized by the discrepancy between global and local parameter updates.
- Server with full-modal data uses a fusion decoder to combine modality representations; extracts multiple anchors from fused representations and distributes them to clients.
- Clients with missing modalities align their missing-modal representations to the global full-modal anchors via scaled dot-product cross-attention, compensating for absent modalities.
- Training involves backpropagation through the server-side fusion and modulation of personalized components.
- Evaluated on BraTS 2018 and 2020 for multimodal brain tumor segmentation.
AI.result: FedMEPD outperforms contemporary multimodal FL and personalized FL methods on BraTS 2018/2020, demonstrating effective handling of intermodal heterogeneity and personalized needs, with the combination of modality-specific encoders, partially personalized fusion decoders, anchors, and cross-attention-based missing-modality calibration contributing to performance gains.
AI.conclusion: The proposed FedMEPD framework effectively tackles intermodal heterogeneity and per-client personalization in federated multimodal medical image analysis. Its architecture enables modality-specific collaboration while preserving client-specific adaptation, and its use of anchors and cross-attention addresses missing modalities, yielding strong empirical results on standard benchmarks.

title: KindSleep: Knowledge-Informed Diagnosis of Obstructive Sleep Apnea from Oximetry
summary: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is a sleep disorder that affects nearly one billion people globally and significantly elevates cardiovascular risk. Traditional diagnosis through polysomnography is resource-intensive and limits widespread access, creating a critical need for accurate and efficient alternatives. In this paper, we introduce KindSleep, a deep learning framework that integrates clinical knowledge with single-channel patient-specific oximetry signals and clinical data for precise OSA diagnosis. KindSleep first learns to identify clinically interpretable concepts, such as desaturation indices and respiratory disturbance events, directly from raw oximetry signals. It then fuses these AI-derived concepts with multimodal clinical data to estimate the Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI). We evaluate KindSleep on three large, independent datasets from the National Sleep Research Resource (SHHS, CFS, MrOS; total n = 9,815). KindSleep demonstrates excellent performance in estimating AHI scores (R2 = 0.917, ICC = 0.957) and consistently outperforms existing approaches in classifying OSA severity, achieving weighted F1-scores from 0.827 to 0.941 across diverse populations. By grounding its predictions in a layer of clinically meaningful concepts, KindSleep provides a more transparent and trustworthy diagnostic tool for sleep medicine practices.

AI.tldr: KindSleep integrates clinical knowledge with raw oximetry and clinical data to accurately estimate AHI and classify OSA severity, achieving state-of-the-art performance across three large datasets.
AI.motivation: OSA is highly prevalent and diagnosing it with polysomnography is resource-intensive; there is a need for accurate, efficient, and interpretable AI approaches that leverage simple signals (oximetry) combined with clinical data.
AI.method: A deep learning framework that learns clinically interpretable concepts (e.g., desaturation indices and respiratory disturbance events) from raw oximetry signals, then fuses these AI-derived concepts with multimodal clinical data to estimate the Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI). Evaluation on three large independent datasets from the National Sleep Research Resource (SHHS, CFS, MrOS; total n=9,815).
AI.result: AHI estimation achieved R2=0.917 and ICC=0.957. OSA severity classification achieved weighted F1-scores ranging from 0.827 to 0.941 across diverse populations, outperforming existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Grounding predictions in clinically meaningful concepts yields a transparent and trustworthy diagnostic tool for sleep medicine, with strong cross-population performance and potential for broader adoption given its reliance on single-channel oximetry and standard clinical data.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Covariate Time Series
summary: While RAG has greatly enhanced LLMs, extending this paradigm to Time-Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) remains a challenge. This is exemplified in the Predictive Maintenance of the Pressure Regulating and Shut-Off Valve (PRSOV), a high-stakes industrial scenario characterized by (1) data scarcity, (2) short transient sequences, and (3) covariate coupled dynamics. Unfortunately, existing time-series RAG approaches predominantly rely on generated static vector embeddings and learnable context augmenters, which may fail to distinguish similar regimes in such scarce, transient, and covariate coupled scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose RAG4CTS, a regime-aware, training-free RAG framework for Covariate Time-Series. Specifically, we construct a hierarchal time-series native knowledge base to enable lossless storage and physics-informed retrieval of raw historical regimes. We design a two-stage bi-weighted retrieval mechanism that aligns historical trends through point-wise and multivariate similarities. For context augmentation, we introduce an agent-driven strategy to dynamically optimize context in a self-supervised manner. Extensive experiments on PRSOV demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in prediction accuracy. The proposed system is deployed in Apache IoTDB within China Southern Airlines. Since deployment, our method has successfully identified one PRSOV fault in two months with zero false alarm.

AI.tldr: A training-free, regime-aware RAG for covariate time-series (RAG4CTS) that uses a hierarchical knowledge base, bi-weighted retrieval, and agent-driven context augmentation to improve predictive maintenance in PRSOV, achieving higher accuracy and reliable fault detection in deployment.
AI.motivation: RAG enhancements for time-series foundation models are challenged by data scarcity, short transient sequences, and covariate-coupled dynamics; existing RAG methods rely on static embeddings and may fail to distinguish regimes in such settings.
AI.method: Construct hierarchical time-series native knowledge base for lossless storage and physics-informed retrieval of raw historical regimes; two-stage bi-weighted retrieval using point-wise and multivariate similarities; agent-driven, self-supervised context augmentation.
AI.result: Significant improvements over SOTA baselines in prediction accuracy on PRSOV; deployed in Apache IoTDB for China Southern Airlines; successfully identified one PRSOV fault in two months with zero false alarms.
AI.conclusion: A practical, training-free RAG framework for covariate time-series that addresses regime distinctions in scarce, transient data and demonstrates real-world utility in industrial predictive maintenance.

title: Latent Policy Steering through One-Step Flow Policies
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) allows robots to learn from offline datasets without risky exploration. Yet, offline RL's performance often hinges on a brittle trade-off between (1) return maximization, which can push policies outside the dataset support, and (2) behavioral constraints, which typically require sensitive hyperparameter tuning. Latent steering offers a structural way to stay within the dataset support during RL, but existing offline adaptations commonly approximate action values using latent-space critics learned via indirect distillation, which can lose information and hinder convergence. We propose Latent Policy Steering (LPS), which enables high-fidelity latent policy improvement by backpropagating original-action-space Q-gradients through a differentiable one-step MeanFlow policy to update a latent-action-space actor. By eliminating proxy latent critics, LPS allows an original-action-space critic to guide end-to-end latent-space optimization, while the one-step MeanFlow policy serves as a behavior-constrained generative prior. This decoupling yields a robust method that works out-of-the-box with minimal tuning. Across OGBench and real-world robotic tasks, LPS achieves state-of-the-art performance and consistently outperforms behavioral cloning and strong latent steering baselines.

AI.tldr: Latent Policy Steering (LPS) for offline RL improves latent policies by backpropagating original-action-space Q-gradients through a differentiable one-step MeanFlow policy, eliminating latent critics and letting the original critic guide end-to-end latent optimization with a behavior-constrained prior; achieves state-of-the-art on OGBench and real-world robots with minimal tuning.
AI.motivation: Offline RL faces a brittle trade-off between return maximization (risk of leaving dataset support) and behavioral constraints (sensitive hyperparameter tuning). Latent steering offers a way to stay within dataset support, but prior offline methods rely on latent-space critics via distillation, which can lose information and hinder convergence.
AI.method: Propose Latent Policy Steering (LPS): backpropagate original-action-space Q-gradients through a differentiable one-step MeanFlow policy to update a latent-action-space actor. No proxy latent critics are used; an original-action-space critic guides end-to-end latent optimization. The MeanFlow policy acts as a behavior-constrained generative prior, providing robust, out-of-the-box performance with minimal tuning.
AI.result: Empirically, LPS achieves state-of-the-art performance on OGBench and real-world robotic tasks, consistently outperforming behavioral cloning and strong latent steering baselines.
AI.conclusion: LPS decouples latent-policy optimization from latent critics by routing gradients from the true action space through a differentiable policy, enabling end-to-end learning guided by the original critic and constrained by a differentiable policy prior. This yields robust performance with minimal tuning across domains.

title: Locality-Attending Vision Transformer
summary: Vision transformers have demonstrated remarkable success in classification by leveraging global self-attention to capture long-range dependencies. However, this same mechanism can obscure fine-grained spatial details crucial for tasks such as segmentation. In this work, we seek to enhance segmentation performance of vision transformers after standard image-level classification training. More specifically, we present a simple yet effective add-on that improves performance on segmentation tasks while retaining vision transformers' image-level recognition capabilities. In our approach, we modulate the self-attention with a learnable Gaussian kernel that biases the attention toward neighboring patches. We further refine the patch representations to learn better embeddings at patch positions. These modifications encourage tokens to focus on local surroundings and ensure meaningful representations at spatial positions, while still preserving the model's ability to incorporate global information. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our modifications, evidenced by substantial segmentation gains on three benchmarks (e.g., over 6% and 4% on ADE20K for ViT Tiny and Base), without changing the training regime or sacrificing classification performance. The code is available at https://github.com/sinahmr/LocAtViT/.

AI.tldr: A lightweight add-on for Vision Transformers that biases self-attention toward local neighborhoods with a learnable Gaussian kernel and refines patch embeddings, improving segmentation performance after standard image-level pretraining while retaining classification accuracy.
AI.motivation: Vision transformers capture global context well but often miss fine-grained spatial details needed for segmentation. An approach that preserves image-level performance while enhancing local structure in the representations could boost segmentation without retraining from scratch.
AI.method: Introduce a learnable Gaussian kernel to modulate self-attention, biasting attention toward neighboring patches. Also refine patch representations to create better, spatially meaningful embeddings for patch positions. The training regime remains unchanged; the modifications are add-ons to the existing ViT architecture.
AI.result: Empirical gains in segmentation on three benchmarks, notably ADE20K with over 6 percentage points for ViT-Tiny and over 4 points for ViT-Base, while keeping image classification performance intact. Code available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: A simple, effective augmentation to ViT that enhances local spatial awareness for segmentation with minimal disruption to the original pretraining objective, enabling improved downstream segmentation without sacrificing classification capability.

title: ConTSG-Bench: A Unified Benchmark for Conditional Time Series Generation
comment: We have open-sourced ConTSG-Bench at https://github.com/seqml/ConTSG-Bench
summary: Conditional time series generation plays a critical role in addressing data scarcity and enabling causal analysis in real-world applications. Despite its increasing importance, the field lacks a standardized and systematic benchmarking framework for evaluating generative models across diverse conditions. To address this gap, we introduce the Conditional Time Series Generation Benchmark (ConTSG-Bench). ConTSG-Bench comprises a large-scale, well-aligned dataset spanning diverse conditioning modalities and levels of semantic abstraction, first enabling systematic evaluation of representative generation methods across these dimensions with a comprehensive suite of metrics for generation fidelity and condition adherence. Both the quantitative benchmarking and in-depth analyses of conditional generation behaviors have revealed the traits and limitations of the current approaches, highlighting critical challenges and promising research directions, particularly with respect to precise structural controllability and downstream task utility under complex conditions.

AI.tldr: A large-scale benchmark (ConTSG-Bench) for conditional time series generation, enabling systematic evaluation across diverse conditioning modalities and semantic abstraction levels with metrics for fidelity and condition adherence.
AI.motivation: To address data scarcity and enable causal analysis in real-world applications, while the field lacks a standardized benchmarking framework for conditional time series generation.
AI.method: Construct a well-aligned, large-scale dataset spanning diverse conditioning modalities and semantic abstraction, and provide a comprehensive evaluation suite for generation fidelity and adherence to conditioning. Perform quantitative benchmarking and in-depth analyses of conditional generation behaviors across representative methods.
AI.result: Shows the traits and limitations of current approaches, highlights critical challenges and promising research directions, notably precise structural controllability and usefulness for downstream tasks under complex conditions.
AI.conclusion: ConTSG-Bench enables systematic evaluation of conditional time series generation and guides future research by revealing strengths, limitations, and directions for improving controllability and downstream utility.

title: Rethinking Representativeness and Diversity in Dynamic Data Selection
summary: Dynamic data selection accelerates training by sampling a changing subset of the dataset while preserving accuracy. We rethink two core notions underlying sample evaluation: representativeness and diversity. Instead of local geometric centrality, we define representativeness as coverage of dataset-level common or high-frequency feature factors. Instead of within-subset dispersion, we define diversity at the process level, requiring the selection trajectory to gradually include complementary rare factors over training. Based on this view, we propose a dynamic selection framework with three components. First, we score representativeness in a plug-in feature space to prioritize samples covering frequent factors. We instantiate this with a sparse autoencoder trained on the target dataset, using sparse unit activations to summarize both individual samples and dataset-wide factor statistics. Second, we realize process-level diversity by combining rare-factor sampling with a Usage-Frequency Penalty that promotes sample rotation, provably discourages monopoly, and reduces gradient bias. Third, we couple the two-dimensional scoring with a smooth scheduler that transitions selection from core-pattern consolidation to rare-factor exploration, without extra gradients, influence estimates, or second-order computations on the training model. Extensive experiments on five benchmarks across vision and text tasks demonstrate improved accuracy-efficiency trade-offs across models. Our method matches or exceeds full-data accuracy with over 2x training acceleration. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: Dynamic data selection uses dataset-level representativeness and process-level diversity to gradually curate training data, via a sparse autoencoder-based representativeness score, a rare-factor sampling with a Usage-Frequency Penalty for sample rotation, and a scheduler, yielding 2x speedups with maintained accuracy on five vision/text benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Improve data sampling for accelerated training by redefining representativeness (coverage of common/high-frequency factors) and diversity (progressive inclusion of rare factors), addressing limitations of local centrality and within-subset dispersion.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Representativeness scoring in plug-in feature space using a sparse autoencoder with sparse activations to summarize samples and dataset-wide factor statistics; (2) Process-level diversity via rare-factor sampling plus a Usage-Frequency Penalty to promote rotation and reduce gradient bias; (3) A two-dimensional scoring with a smooth scheduler that shifts from core-pattern consolidation to rare-factor exploration, with no extra gradient computations; training data subset changes dynamically during training.
AI.result: Empirical validation on five benchmarks across vision and text tasks showing improved accuracy-efficiency; matches or exceeds full-data accuracy with over 2x training acceleration.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, two-dimensional scoring and scheduler enable efficient training without sacrificing accuracy, generalizes across domains; code will be released.

title: Constraint-Free Static Modeling of Continuum Parallel Robot
summary: Continuum parallel robots (CPR) combine rigid actuation mechanisms with multiple elastic rods in a closed-loop topology, making forward statics challenging when rigid--continuum junctions are enforced by explicit kinematic constraints. Such constraint-based formulations typically introduce additional algebraic variables and complicate both numerical solution and downstream control. This paper presents a geometric exact, configuration-based and constraint-free static model of CPR that remains valid under geometrically nonlinear, large-deformation and large-rotation conditions. Connectivity constraints are eliminated by kinematic embedding, yielding a reduced unconstrained problem. Each rod of CPR is discretized by nodal poses on SE(3), while the element-wise strain field is reconstructed through a linear strain parameterization. A fourth-order Magnus approximation yields an explicit and geometrically consistent mapping between element end poses and the strain. Rigid attachments at the motor-driven base and the end-effector platforms are handled through kinematic embeddings. Based on total potential energy and virtual work, we derive assembly-ready residuals and explicit Newton tangents, and solve the resulting nonlinear equilibrium equations using a Riemannian Newton iteration on the product manifold. Experiments on a three-servomotor, six-rod prototype validate the model by showing good agreement between simulation and measurements for both unloaded motions and externally loaded cases.

AI.tldr: A geometry-based, constraint-free static model for continuum parallel robots (CPR) using SE(3) discretization and Magnus-based strain mapping; eliminates connectivity constraints via kinematic embedding and solves with Riemannian Newton on a product manifold; experimentally validated on a six-rod prototype.
AI.motivation: Overcomes the difficulty of constraint-laden forward statics in CPR by deriving an unconstrained, exact geometric formulation that remains valid under large deformations and rotations, simplifying computation and control.
