title: WebFactory: Automated Compression of Foundational Language Intelligence into Grounded Web Agents
summary: Current paradigms for training GUI agents are fundamentally limited by a reliance on either unsafe, non-reproducible live web interactions or costly, scarce human-crafted data and environments. We argue this focus on data volume overlooks a more critical factor: the efficiency of compressing a large language model's (LLM) latent knowledge into actionable agent behavior. We introduce WebFactory, a novel, fully automated closed-loop reinforcement learning pipeline for GUI agents, systematically compressing LLM-encoded internet intelligence into efficient, grounded actions. Our pipeline features a process of scalable environment synthesis, knowledge-aware task generation, LLM-powered trajectory collection, decomposed reward RL training, and systematic agent evaluation. Remarkably, our agent demonstrates exceptional data efficiency and generalization. Trained on synthetic data from only 10 websites within WebFactory, it achieves performance comparable to GUI agents trained on the same amount of human-annotated data from a much larger set of environments. This superior performance is consistent across our internal offline and online transfer benchmarks, where our agent also significantly outperforms the base foundation model. We further provide critical insights into the "embodiment potential" of different LLM foundations, offering a new axis for model evaluation. This work presents a scalable and cost-effective paradigm for transforming passive internet knowledge into active, grounded intelligence, marking a critical step towards general-purpose interactive agents.

AI.tldr: A scalable, automated RL pipeline (WebFactory) distills LLM internet knowledge into grounded GUI agent behavior, achieving data-efficient generalization using synthetic data from a small set of websites, and outperforming baseline models on offline/online benchmarks. It also introduces an embodiment-based evaluation axis for LLMs.
AI.motivation: Current GUI agent training relies on unsafe live web interactions or costly human data; compressing latent LLM knowledge into actionable agent behavior could enable efficient, scalable agents.
AI.method: A fully automated closed-loop RL pipeline: scalable environment synthesis, knowledge-aware task generation, LLM-powered trajectory collection, decomposed reward RL training, and systematic agent evaluation. Trained on synthetic data from 10 websites; evaluated against larger environment sets; benchmarks include offline and online transfer; analyses include embodiment potential across LLM foundations.
AI.result: The agent exhibits exceptional data efficiency and generalization, achieving performance comparable to GUI agents trained with far more human-annotated data from larger environment sets. It outperforms the base foundation model on internal offline and online transfer benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: WebFactory presents a scalable, cost-effective paradigm for transforming passive internet knowledge into active, grounded intelligence, advancing toward general-purpose interactive agents. It also offers a new axis—embodiment potential—for evaluating LLM foundations.

title: PhysiFlow: Physics-Aware Humanoid Whole-Body VLA via Multi-Brain Latent Flow Matching and Robust Tracking
summary: In the domain of humanoid robot control, the fusion of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) with whole-body control is essential for semantically guided execution of real-world tasks. However, existing methods encounter challenges in terms of low VLA inference efficiency or an absence of effective semantic guidance for whole-body control, resulting in instability in dynamic limb-coordinated tasks. To bridge this gap, we present a semantic-motion intent guided, physics-aware multi-brain VLA framework for humanoid whole-body control. A series of experiments was conducted to evaluate the performance of the proposed framework. The experimental results demonstrated that the framework enabled reliable vision-language-guided full-body coordination for humanoid robots.

AI.tldr: Proposes a semantic-motion intent guided, physics-aware multi-brain VLA framework to enable reliable vision-language-guided whole-body control in humanoid robots.
AI.motivation: Addresses low VLA inference efficiency and lack of semantic guidance for whole-body control, which can cause instability in dynamic limb-coordinated tasks; aims to bridge perception-language understanding with physics-aware control for robust humanoid behavior.
AI.method: Introduces a semantic-motion intent guided, physics-aware multi-brain Vision-Language-Action framework and evaluates it through a series of experiments to demonstrate full-body coordination.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate the framework enables reliable vision-language-guided full-body coordination for humanoid robots.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates a viable approach to semantically guided, physics-aware whole-body control in humanoids, improving coordination and stability in real-world tasks.

title: 3D-RFT: Reinforcement Fine-Tuning for Video-based 3D Scene Understanding
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards ( RLVR ) has emerged as a transformative paradigm for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models ( LLMs), yet its potential in 3D scene understanding remains under-explored. Existing approaches largely rely on Supervised Fine-Tuning ( SFT), where the token-level cross-entropy loss acts as an indirect proxy for optimization, leading to a misalignment between training objectives and task performances. To bridge this gap, we present Reinforcement Fine-Tuning for Video-based 3D Scene Understanding (3D-RFT ), the first framework to extend RLVR to video-based 3D perception and reasoning. 3D-RFT shifts the paradigm by directly optimizing the model towards evaluation metrics. 3D-RFT first activates 3D-aware Multi-modal Large Language Models ( MLLM s) via SFT, followed by reinforcement fine-tuning using Group Relative Policy Optimization ( GRPO) with strictly verifiable reward functions. We design task-specific reward functions directly from metrics like 3D IoU and F1-Score to provide more effective signals to guide model training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that 3D-RFT-4B achieves state-of-the-art performance on various video-based 3D scene understanding tasks. Notably, 3D-RFT-4B significantly outperforms larger models (e.g., VG LLM-8B) on 3D video detection, 3D visual grounding, and spatial reasoning benchmarks. We further reveal good properties of 3D-RFT such as robust efficacy, and valuable insights into training strategies and data impact. We hope 3D-RFT can serve as a robust and promising paradigm for future development of 3D scene understanding.

AI.tldr: Extends reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards to video-based 3D scene understanding by introducing 3D-RFT; achieves SOTA with a 4B-parameter model, outperforming larger LLMs on 3D video tasks.
AI.motivation: Address misalignment between token-level cross-entropy training in supervised fine-tuning and actual task performance in 3D scene understanding; leverage verifiable rewards based on 3D metrics to directly optimize for task objectives.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: initial SFT to activate 3D-aware multimodal LLMs, followed by reinforcement fine-tuning using Group Relative Policy Optimization with verifiable rewards derived from metrics like 3D IoU and F1-Score; task-specific reward design.
AI.result: 3D-RFT-4B achieves state-of-the-art on multiple video-based 3D tasks; outperforms larger models (e.g., VG LLM-8B) on 3D video detection, 3D visual grounding, and spatial reasoning; demonstrates robust efficacy and insights into training strategies and data impact.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards for 3D scene understanding; 3D-RFT offers a robust paradigm for future development, shedding light on reward design, data impact, and training strategies.

title: U-Parking: Distributed UWB-Assisted Autonomous Parking System with Robust Localization and Intelligent Planning
comment: This paper has been accepted by infocom. The source code has been released at: https://github.com/qiongwu86/U-Parking
summary: This demonstration presents U-Parking, a distributed Ultra-Wideband (UWB)-assisted autonomous parking system. By integrating Large Language Models (LLMs)-assisted planning with robust fusion localization and trajectory tracking, it enables reliable automated parking in challenging indoor environments, as validated through real-vehicle demonstrations.

AI.tldr: U-Parking combines UWB-assisted distributed localization with LLM-guided planning and trajectory tracking to enable robust autonomous parking in challenging indoor environments, demonstrated on real vehicles.
AI.motivation: Autonomous parking indoors is notoriously difficult due to GPS denial, multipath, and dynamic obstacles. There is a need for reliable localization, planning, and control that can operate with scalable, distributed sensing and human-friendly planning via LLMs.
AI.method: The system fuses UWB-based distributed localization with robust sensor fusion, employs LLMs to assist planning, and integrates trajectory tracking for automated parking. Validation is performed on real vehicles in challenging indoor scenarios.
AI.result: The approach achieves reliable automated parking in difficult indoor environments, with validation through real-vehicle demonstrations, indicating practical feasibility.
AI.conclusion: LLM-assisted planning combined with UWB-enabled localization and robust fusion/control can enable practical, reliable indoor autonomous parking and guide future work on distributed sensing and human-friendly planning for autonomous parking systems.

title: Jagarin: A Three-Layer Architecture for Hibernating Personal Duty Agents on Mobile
summary: Personal AI agents face a fundamental deployment paradox on mobile: persistent background execution drains battery and violates platform sandboxing policies, yet purely reactive agents miss time-sensitive obligations until the user remembers to ask. We present Jagarin, a three-layer architecture that resolves this paradox through structured hibernation and demand-driven wake. The first layer, DAWN (Duty-Aware Wake Network), is an on-device heuristic engine that computes a composite urgency score from four signals: duty-typed optimal action windows, user behavioral engagement prediction, opportunity cost of inaction, and cross-duty batch resonance. It uses adaptive per-user thresholds to decide when a sleeping agent should nudge or escalate. The second layer, ARIA (Agent Relay Identity Architecture), is a commercial email identity proxy that routes the full commercial inbox -- obligations, promotional offers, loyalty rewards, and platform updates -- to appropriate DAWN handlers by message category, eliminating cold-start and removing manual data entry. The third layer, ACE (Agent-Centric Exchange), is a protocol framework for direct machine-readable communication from institutions to personal agents, replacing human-targeted email as the canonical channel. Together, these three layers form a complete stack from institutional signal to on-device action, without persistent cloud state, continuous background execution, or privacy compromise. A working Flutter prototype is demonstrated on Android, combining all three layers with an ephemeral cloud agent invoked only on user-initiated escalation.

AI.tldr: A three-layer on-device architecture (Jagarin) resolves mobile agent deployment by combining a duty-aware wake network, an inbox routing layer, and an exchange protocol to enable proactive actions without continuous cloud-state.
AI.motivation: Mobile persistent agents conflict with battery life and sandboxing; purely reactive agents miss time-sensitive obligations; a privacy-preserving, on-device decision-making stack with selective cloud use is needed.
AI.method: Three-layer stack: DAWN computes a composite urgency score from four signals with per-user adaptive thresholds; ARIA routes the full commercial inbox to DAWN handlers by category to avoid cold-start and manual data entry; ACE provides a machine-readable protocol for direct institution-to-agent communication, replacing human-targeted email. A Flutter-based Android prototype demonstrates all three layers with an ephemeral cloud agent invoked only on user-initiated escalation.
AI.result: Demonstrated a working Flutter prototype on Android; end-to-end stack functioning without persistent cloud state or continuous background execution; cloud interaction is limited to an ephemeral agent upon escalation, preserving privacy.
AI.conclusion: The architecture delivers a complete signal-to-action stack that enables proactive personal agents while meeting platform constraints and privacy requirements, without ongoing cloud state.

title: Residual RL--MPC for Robust Microrobotic Cell Pushing Under Time-Varying Flow
summary: Contact-rich micromanipulation in microfluidic flow is challenging because small disturbances can break pushing contact and induce large lateral drift. We study planar cell pushing with a magnetic rolling microrobot that tracks a waypoint-sampled reference curve under time-varying Poiseuille flow. We propose a hybrid controller that augments a nominal MPC with a learned residual policy trained by SAC. The policy outputs a bounded 2D velocity correction that is contact-gated, so residual actions are applied only during robot--cell contact, preserving reliable approach behavior and stabilizing learning. All methods share the same actuation interface and speed envelope for fair comparisons. Experiments show improved robustness and tracking accuracy over pure MPC and PID under nonstationary flow, with generalization from a clover training curve to unseen circle and square trajectories. A residual-bound sweep identifies an intermediate correction limit as the best trade-off, which we use in all benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Hybrid MPC with a bounded, contact-gated SAC residual improves robustness and tracking for contact-rich planar micromanipulation under time-varying Poiseuille flow.
AI.motivation: To address disturbance sensitivity and large lateral drift in contact-rich micromanipulation within microfluidic flow; integrate model-based MPC with a learned residual policy to improve robustness without sacrificing stable approach.
AI.method: Planar cell pushing using a magnetic rolling microrobot tracking a waypoint-sampled reference curve under time-varying Poiseuille flow. A nominal MPC is augmented with a learned residual policy (SAC). The policy outputs a bounded 2D velocity correction that is applied only during robot–cell contact (contact-gated). All controllers use the same actuation interface and speed envelope for fair comparison.
AI.result: Experiments show improved robustness and tracking accuracy over pure MPC and PID under nonstationary flow. The learned residual generalizes from a clover training curve to unseen circle and square trajectories. A residual-bound sweep identifies an intermediate correction limit as the best trade-off, used across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A contact-aware residual learning framework, gated to act only during contact and constrained by a bound, enhances performance and generalization of MPC-based micromanipulation in variable flow conditions while preserving stable approach behavior.

title: Think, Then Verify: A Hypothesis-Verification Multi-Agent Framework for Long Video Understanding
summary: Long video understanding is challenging due to dense visual redundancy, long-range temporal dependencies, and the tendency of chain-of-thought and retrieval-based agents to accumulate semantic drift and correlation-driven errors. We argue that long-video reasoning should begin not with reactive retrieval, but with deliberate task formulation: the model must first articulate what must be true in the video for each candidate answer to hold. This thinking-before-finding principle motivates VideoHV-Agent, a framework that reformulates video question answering as a structured hypothesis-verification process. Based on video summaries, a Thinker rewrites answer candidates into testable hypotheses, a Judge derives a discriminative clue specifying what evidence must be checked, a Verifier grounds and tests the clue using localized, fine-grained video content, and an Answer agent integrates validated evidence to produce the final answer. Experiments on three long-video understanding benchmarks show that VideoHV-Agent achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while providing enhanced interpretability, improved logical soundness, and lower computational cost. We make our code publicly available at: https://github.com/Haorane/VideoHV-Agent.

AI.tldr: VideoHV-Agent reframes long-video QA as a hypothesis-verification task, improving interpretability and efficiency while achieving state-of-the-art accuracy.
AI.motivation: Long videos suffer from dense redundancy, long-range dependencies, and error accumulation in reactive, retrieval-based approaches; proactive task formulation is needed to verify candidate answers.
AI.method: Thinker rewrites answer options into testable hypotheses; Judge derives a discriminative clue indicating what evidence must be checked; Verifier grounds and tests the clue with localized, fine-grained video content; Answer agent integrates validated evidence to produce the final answer.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on three long-video understanding benchmarks; offers enhanced interpretability, improved logical soundness, and lower computational cost; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: A thinking-before-finding paradigm for long-video reasoning yields more reliable, interpretable QA with efficient computation.

title: EVMbench: Evaluating AI Agents on Smart Contract Security
summary: Smart contracts on public blockchains now manage large amounts of value, and vulnerabilities in these systems can lead to substantial losses. As AI agents become more capable at reading, writing, and running code, it is natural to ask how well they can already navigate this landscape, both in ways that improve security and in ways that might increase risk. We introduce EVMbench, an evaluation that measures the ability of agents to detect, patch, and exploit smart contract vulnerabilities. EVMbench draws on 117 curated vulnerabilities from 40 repositories and, in the most realistic setting, uses programmatic grading based on tests and blockchain state under a local Ethereum execution environment. We evaluate a range of frontier agents and find that they are capable of discovering and exploiting vulnerabilities end-to-end against live blockchain instances. We release code, tasks, and tooling to support continued measurement of these capabilities and future work on security.

AI.tldr: EVMbench evaluates AI agents' ability to detect, patch, and exploit smart-contract vulnerabilities in Ethereum using 117 curated vulnerabilities from 40 repositories; programmatic grading in a local Ethereum execution environment; frontier agents can discover and exploit vulnerabilities end-to-end on live blockchain instances; code and tooling released.
AI.motivation: Public blockchains manage large value; need to assess AI capabilities for security—both defensive improvements and risk amplification—by measuring vulnerability detection, patching, and exploitation in realistic settings.
AI.method: Assemble EVMbench dataset with 117 curated vulnerabilities from 40 repositories. Use programmatic grading based on tests and blockchain state within a local Ethereum execution environment to evaluate frontier AI agents on end-to-end tasks for detecting, patching, and exploiting vulnerabilities.
AI.result: Frontier agents are capable of discovering and exploiting vulnerabilities end-to-end against live blockchain instances, indicating practical security-relevant capabilities of current AI systems; authors release code, tasks, and tooling for ongoing benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: Shows that AI-enabled agents can perform realistic security tasks on smart contracts, underscoring the need for robust defense measures and continuous benchmarking; sets up a framework for future work in security-oriented evaluation and tooling.

title: Bidirectional Curriculum Generation: A Multi-Agent Framework for Data-Efficient Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Enhancing mathematical reasoning in Large Language Models typically demands massive datasets, yet data efficiency remains a critical bottleneck. While Curriculum Learning attempts to structure this process, standard unidirectional approaches (simple-to-complex) suffer from inefficient sample utilization: they blindly escalate complexity even when foundational gaps persist, leading to wasted computation on unsolvable problems. To maximize the instructional value of every training sample, we introduce a novel Bidirectional Curriculum Generation framework. Unlike rigid trajectories, our multi-agent ecosystem mimics adaptive pedagogy to establish a closed feedback loop. It dynamically generates data by either complicating problems to challenge the model or, crucially, simplying them to repair specific reasoning failures. This mechanism ensures that the model consumes only the most effective data at any given stage. Grounded in the Optimal Pacing Theorem, our approach optimizes the learning trajectory, significantly outperforming baselines while achieving superior reasoning performance with substantially fewer instruction samples.

AI.tldr: Proposes bidirectional curriculum generation for data-efficient mathematical reasoning in LLMs, using adaptive problem simplification or complication via a multi-agent loop, grounded in an Optimal Pacing Theorem; claims superior performance with far fewer instruction samples compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: To overcome data inefficiency in teaching LLMs to perform mathematical reasoning, addressing inefficiencies of unidirectional (simple-to-complex) curricula that waste computational resources.
AI.method: A multi-agent framework that dynamically generates data by either increasing or decreasing problem difficulty in response to the model's reasoning state, creating a closed feedback loop that prioritizes the most informative samples; guided by the Optimal Pacing Theorem to optimize the learning trajectory.
AI.result: Reportedly significantly outperforms baselines and achieves superior reasoning with substantially fewer instruction samples.
AI.conclusion: Bidirectional curriculum generation offers a data-efficient, adaptive pedagogy for complex reasoning in LLMs, with theoretical grounding and empirical gains over traditional curricula.

title: Observing and Controlling Features in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action Models (VLAs) have shown remarkable progress towards embodied intelligence. While their architecture partially resembles that of Large Language Models (LLMs), VLAs exhibit higher complexity due to their multi-modal inputs/outputs and often hybrid nature of transformer and diffusion heads. This is part of the reason why insights from mechanistic interpretability in LLMs, which explain how the internal model representations relate to their output behavior, do not trivially transfer to VLA counterparts. In this work, we propose to close this gap by introducing and analyzing two main concepts: feature-observability and feature-controllability. In particular, we first study features that are linearly encoded in representation space, and show how they can be observed by means of a linear classifier. Then, we use a minimal linear intervention grounded in optimal control to accurately place internal representations and steer the VLA's output towards a desired region. Our results show that targeted, lightweight interventions can reliably steer a robot's behavior while preserving closed-loop capabilities. We demonstrate on different VLA architectures ($π_{0.5}$ and OpenVLA) through simulation experiments that VLAs possess interpretable internal structure amenable to online adaptation without fine-tuning, enabling real-time alignment with user preferences and task requirements.

AI.tldr: VLAs exhibit interpretable internal structure and two main concepts—feature-observability and feature-controllability. The paper shows features linearly encoded in representations can be observed with a linear classifier and steered via minimal linear interventions (optimal control) to guide outputs. Demonstrations on π_{0.5} and OpenVLA in simulation suggest real-time alignment with user/task requirements without fine-tuning, preserving closed-loop control.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between mechanistic interpretability in LLMs and Vision-Language-Action Models, which are multi-modal and hybrid in architecture, by enabling observation and control of internal representations to align behavior with goals.
AI.method: 1) Identify features that are linearly encoded in the representation space and observable via a linear classifier. 2) Apply a minimal linear intervention grounded in optimal control to reposition internal representations and steer outputs toward a target region.
AI.result: Lightweight, targeted interventions can reliably steer a robot's behavior while maintaining closed-loop capabilities. The approach demonstrates interpretable internal structure across VLAs (π_{0.5} and OpenVLA) and supports online adaptation without fine-tuning, enabling real-time alignment with preferences and tasks in simulation.
AI.conclusion: VLAs possess an interpretable internal structure that can be observed and controlled linearly. The proposed framework—feature-observability and feature-controllability—facilitates online adaptation and real-time alignment with user and task requirements without full fine-tuning.

title: A Simple Baseline for Unifying Understanding, Generation, and Editing via Vanilla Next-token Prediction
comment: Technical report. This work serves as a straightforward autoregressive baseline for unifying understanding, generation, and editing
summary: In this work, we introduce Wallaroo, a simple autoregressive baseline that leverages next-token prediction to unify multi-modal understanding, image generation, and editing at the same time. Moreover, Wallaroo supports multi-resolution image input and output, as well as bilingual support for both Chinese and English. We decouple the visual encoding into separate pathways and apply a four-stage training strategy to reshape the model's capabilities. Experiments are conducted on various benchmarks where Wallaroo produces competitive performance or exceeds other unified models, suggesting the great potential of autoregressive models in unifying multi-modality understanding and generation. Our code is available at https://github.com/JiePKU/Wallaroo.

AI.tldr: Wallaroo is a simple autoregressive baseline that unifies multi-modal understanding, image generation, and editing via next-token prediction, with multi-resolution image input/output and bilingual (Chinese/English) support; it uses decoupled visual encodings and a four-stage training strategy, achieving competitive or superior results on benchmarks and is open-sourced.
AI.motivation: To create a unified, autoregressive framework capable of processing and generating across modalities (text, images) and languages, while handling varying image resolutions and enabling editing tasks.
AI.method: Autoregressive model predicting next token; separate visual encoding pathways; four-stage training; supports multi-resolution input/output; bilingual training.
AI.result: On multiple benchmarks, Wallaroo matches or surpasses existing unified models, indicating strong potential of autoregressive approaches for multi-modal understanding and generation.
AI.conclusion: Autoregressive, multi-path visual encoding with staged training is a viable pathway to unified multi-modal AI; Wallaroo's results and open-source code demonstrate practicality and potential for future work.

title: BandPO: Bridging Trust Regions and Ratio Clipping via Probability-Aware Bounds for LLM Reinforcement Learning
summary: Proximal constraints are fundamental to the stability of the Large Language Model reinforcement learning. While the canonical clipping mechanism in PPO serves as an efficient surrogate for trust regions, we identify a critical bottleneck: fixed bounds strictly constrain the upward update margin of low-probability actions, disproportionately suppressing high-advantage tail strategies and inducing rapid entropy collapse. To address this, we introduce Band-constrained Policy Optimization (BandPO). BandPO replaces canonical clipping with Band, a unified theoretical operator that projects trust regions defined by f-divergences into dynamic, probability-aware clipping intervals. Theoretical analysis confirms that Band effectively resolves this exploration bottleneck. We formulate this mapping as a convex optimization problem, guaranteeing a globally optimal numerical solution while deriving closed-form solutions for specific divergences. Extensive experiments across diverse models and datasets demonstrate that BandPO consistently outperforms canonical clipping and Clip-Higher, while robustly mitigating entropy collapse.

AI.tldr: BandPO replaces fixed PPO clipping with Band, a convex-optimization-based operator that maps f-divergence trust regions into adaptive, probability-aware clipping intervals, enabling better exploration and preventing entropy collapse with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical gains.
AI.motivation: Fixed clipping bounds in PPO limit upward updates for low-probability actions, suppress high-advantage tail strategies, and induce entropy collapse; a dynamic, divergence-based projection is needed to maintain stable exploration.
AI.method: Introduce Band as the projection operator of trust regions defined by f-divergences into dynamic clipping intervals. Formulate the projection as a convex optimization problem with globally optimal solutions, and derive closed-form solutions for common divergences. Provide theoretical analysis showing Band resolves the exploration bottleneck.
AI.result: BandPO consistently outperforms canonical clipping and Clip-Higher across diverse models and datasets, while robustly mitigating entropy collapse and maintaining better exploration.
AI.conclusion: BandPO offers a principled, globally optimal alternative to PPO clipping, yielding dynamic, probability-aware updates that improve exploration stability in large-scale RL for language models.

title: MedCoRAG: Interpretable Hepatology Diagnosis via Hybrid Evidence Retrieval and Multispecialty Consensus
summary: Diagnosing hepatic diseases accurately and interpretably is critical, yet it remains challenging in real-world clinical settings. Existing AI approaches for clinical diagnosis often lack transparency, structured reasoning, and deployability. Recent efforts have leveraged large language models (LLMs), retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and multi-agent collaboration. However, these approaches typically retrieve evidence from a single source and fail to support iterative, role-specialized deliberation grounded in structured clinical data. To address this, we propose MedCoRAG (i.e., Medical Collaborative RAG), an end-to-end framework that generates diagnostic hypotheses from standardized abnormal findings and constructs a patient-specific evidence package by jointly retrieving and pruning UMLS knowledge graph paths and clinical guidelines. It then performs Multi-Agent Collaborative Reasoning: a Router Agent dynamically dispatches Specialist Agents based on case complexity; these agents iteratively reason over the evidence and trigger targeted re-retrievals when needed, while a Generalist Agent synthesizes all deliberations into a traceable consensus diagnosis that emulates multidisciplinary consultation. Experimental results on hepatic disease cases from MIMIC-IV show that MedCoRAG outperforms existing methods and closed-source models in both diagnostic performance and reasoning interpretability.

AI.tldr: MedCoRAG is a multi-agent RAG framework for hepatic disease diagnosis that jointly retrieves and prunes UMLS knowledge paths and guidelines, enabling iterative, role-specialized reasoning and a generalist synthesis that yields a traceable, multidisciplinary-consultation-like diagnosis. It shows improved diagnostic accuracy and interpretability on MIMIC-IV hepatic cases compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: Medical AI for hepatic diagnosis lacks transparency, structured reasoning, and reusable deployable evidence. Existing LLM/RAG approaches often rely on single-source evidence and do not support iterative, role-specific deliberation grounded in structured clinical data.
AI.method: An end-to-end pipeline: (1) generate diagnostic hypotheses from standardized abnormal findings; (2) build patient-specific evidence by jointly retrieving and pruning UMLS knowledge graph paths and clinical guidelines; (3) employ Multi-Agent Collaborative Reasoning with a Router Agent dispatching Specialist Agents to iteratively reason over evidence and trigger re-retrievals; (4) a Generalist Agent synthesizes deliberations into a traceable consensus diagnosis that mimics multidisciplinary consultation.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on hepatic disease cases from MIMIC-IV shows that MedCoRAG surpasses existing methods and closed-source models in diagnostic performance and reasoning interpretability.
AI.conclusion: MedCoRAG delivers accurate, transparent, and deployable hepatic disease diagnosis through structured evidence and collaborative, role-specialized reasoning, with potential to improve clinical decision support.

title: cuRoboV2: Dynamics-Aware Motion Generation with Depth-Fused Distance Fields for High-DoF Robots
summary: Effective robot autonomy requires motion generation that is safe, feasible, and reactive. Current methods are fragmented: fast planners output physically unexecutable trajectories, reactive controllers struggle with high-fidelity perception, and existing solvers fail on high-DoF systems. We present cuRoboV2, a unified framework with three key innovations: (1) B-spline trajectory optimization that enforces smoothness and torque limits; (2) a GPU-native TSDF/ESDF perception pipeline that generates dense signed distance fields covering the full workspace, unlike existing methods that only provide distances within sparsely allocated blocks, up to 10x faster and in 8x less memory than the state-of-the-art at manipulation scale, with up to 99% collision recall; and (3) scalable GPU-native whole-body computation, namely topology-aware kinematics, differentiable inverse dynamics, and map-reduce self-collision, that achieves up to 61x speedup while also extending to high-DoF humanoids (where previous GPU implementations fail). On benchmarks, cuRoboV2 achieves 99.7% success under 3kg payload (where baselines achieve only 72--77%), 99.6% collision-free IK on a 48-DoF humanoid (where prior methods fail entirely), and 89.5% retargeting constraint satisfaction (vs. 61% for PyRoki); these collision-free motions yield locomotion policies with 21% lower tracking error than PyRoki and 12x lower cross-seed variance than mink. A ground-up codebase redesign for discoverability enabled LLM coding assistants to author up to 73% of new modules, including hand-optimized CUDA kernels, demonstrating that well-structured robotics code can unlock productive human--LLM collaboration. Together, these advances provide a unified, dynamics-aware motion generation stack that scales from single-arm manipulators to full humanoids.

AI.tldr: cuRoboV2 delivers a unified, GPU-accelerated motion generation stack with B-spline trajectory optimization, dense GPU TSDF/ESDF perception, and scalable whole-body computation, achieving state-of-the-art performance from single-arm manipulators to humanoids and enabling human–LLM collaboration in development.
AI.motivation: Address fragmentation in robotics pipelines (infeasible fast-planned trajectories, perception bottlenecks, and high-DoF solver limitations) by unifying motion generation with dynamics-aware planning, perception, and control on GPU hardware.
AI.method: Three innovations: (1) B-spline trajectory optimization with torque limits; (2) GPU-native TSDF/ESDF perception producing dense fields across the full workspace with high speed and low memory; (3) scalable GPU-native whole-body computation (topology-aware kinematics, differentiable inverse dynamics, map-reduce self-collision) plus a ground-up codebase redesign enabling large-language-model (LLM) assisted development.
AI.result: Empirical gains: up to 10x faster and 8x less memory for perception; up to 99% collision recall; 61x speedup for whole-body computations; successful experiments include 99.7% success under 3 kg payload (vs 72–77% baselines), 99.6% collision-free IK on a 48-DoF humanoid (prior methods fail), and 89.5% retargeting constraint satisfaction (vs 61% for PyRoki). Collision-free motions yield locomotion with 21% lower tracking error than PyRoki and 12x lower cross-seed variance than Mink. Plus, 73% of new modules authored by LL-assisted development under a redesigned codebase.
AI.conclusion: cuRoboV2 provides a unified, dynamics-aware motion generation stack scalable from single-arm manipulators to full humanoids, achieving substantial performance gains and enabling productive human–LLM collaboration in robotics software development.

title: TAPFormer: Robust Arbitrary Point Tracking via Transient Asynchronous Fusion of Frames and Events
summary: Tracking any point (TAP) is a fundamental yet challenging task in computer vision, requiring high precision and long-term motion reasoning. Recent attempts to combine RGB frames and event streams have shown promise, yet they typically rely on synchronous or non-adaptive fusion, leading to temporal misalignment and severe degradation when one modality fails. We introduce TAPFormer, a transformer-based framework that performs asynchronous temporal-consistent fusion of frames and events for robust and high-frequency arbitrary point tracking. Our key innovation is a Transient Asynchronous Fusion (TAF) mechanism, which explicitly models the temporal evolution between discrete frames through continuous event updates, bridging the gap between low-rate frames and high-rate events. In addition, a Cross-modal Locally Weighted Fusion (CLWF) module adaptively adjusts spatial attention according to modality reliability, yielding stable and discriminative features even under blur or low light. To evaluate our approach under realistic conditions, we construct a novel real-world frame-event TAP dataset under diverse illumination and motion conditions. Our method outperforms existing point trackers, achieving a 28.2% improvement in average pixel error within threshold. Moreover, on standard point tracking benchmarks, our tracker consistently achieves the best performance. Project website: tapformer.github.io

AI.tldr: A transformer-based framework (TAPFormer) for asynchronous fusion of RGB frames and event streams to achieve robust and high-frequency arbitrary point tracking; introduces Transient Asynchronous Fusion (TAF) and Cross-modal Locally Weighted Fusion (CLWF); demonstrates superior performance and provides a real-world TAP dataset.
AI.motivation: To overcome temporal misalignment and robustness issues in frame-event tracking due to reliance on synchronous fusion and modality failures; leverage high-rate events with low-rate frames for stable, high-frequency tracking.
AI.method: Proposes TAPFormer with TAF to model temporal evolution between frames via continuous event updates; CLWF to adapt spatial attention by modality reliability; collects real-world frame-event TAP dataset under diverse conditions.
AI.result: Outperforms existing trackers, 28.2% improvement in average pixel error within threshold; achieves best performance on standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: TAPFormer enables robust, high-frequency point tracking under challenging illumination and motion, validating asynchronous frame-event fusion as a viable approach; opens avenues for real-world deployment.

title: KARL: Knowledge Agents via Reinforcement Learning
summary: We present a system for training enterprise search agents via reinforcement learning that achieves state-of-the-art performance across a diverse suite of hard-to-verify agentic search tasks. Our work makes four core contributions. First, we introduce KARLBench, a multi-capability evaluation suite spanning six distinct search regimes, including constraint-driven entity search, cross-document report synthesis, tabular numerical reasoning, exhaustive entity retrieval, procedural reasoning over technical documentation, and fact aggregation over internal enterprise notes. Second, we show that models trained across heterogeneous search behaviors generalize substantially better than those optimized for any single benchmark. Third, we develop an agentic synthesis pipeline that employs long-horizon reasoning and tool use to generate diverse, grounded, and high-quality training data, with iterative bootstrapping from increasingly capable models. Fourth, we propose a new post-training paradigm based on iterative large-batch off-policy RL that is sample efficient, robust to train-inference engine discrepancies, and naturally extends to multi-task training with out-of-distribution generalization. Compared to Claude 4.6 and GPT 5.2, KARL is Pareto-optimal on KARLBench across cost-quality and latency-quality trade-offs, including tasks that were out-of-distribution during training. With sufficient test-time compute, it surpasses the strongest closed models. These results show that tailored synthetic data in combination with multi-task reinforcement learning enables cost-efficient and high-performing knowledge agents for grounded reasoning.

AI.tldr: KARLBench and a reinforcement-learning pipeline yield state-of-the-art, multi-task enterprise search agents, with strong generalization across diverse tasks and Pareto-optimal trade-offs against baselines.
AI.motivation: Enterprise search requires robust performance across varied tasks (constraint-driven, synthesis, numerical reasoning, exhaustive retrieval, procedural reasoning, and internal notes) and out-of-distribution generalization. The work proposes a comprehensive benchmark and learning framework to address generalization, data efficiency, and grounded reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce KARLBench, a six-regime multi-capability evaluation suite. Train agents across heterogeneous search behaviors. Develop an agentic synthesis pipeline with long-horizon reasoning and tool use, using iterative bootstrapping from progressively capable models. Propose a post-training paradigm of large-batch off-policy RL, enabling sample efficiency and robustness to train-inference mismatch, with multi-task training for OOD generalization.
AI.result: KARL achieves state-of-the-art performance on KARLBench across six regimes. Models trained on heterogeneous tasks generalize better than single-benchmark-optimized models. Pareto-optimal on cost-quality and latency-quality trade-offs versus Claude 4.6 and GPT 5.2; with ample test-time compute, it surpasses the strongest closed models.
AI.conclusion: Tailored synthetic data combined with multi-task reinforcement learning enables cost-efficient, high-performing knowledge agents capable of grounded, long-horizon reasoning for enterprise search.

title: Safe-SAGE: Social-Semantic Adaptive Guidance for Safe Engagement through Laplace-Modulated Poisson Safety Functions
summary: Traditional safety-critical control methods, such as control barrier functions, suffer from semantic blindness, exhibiting the same behavior around obstacles regardless of contextual significance. This limitation leads to the uniform treatment of all obstacles, despite their differing semantic meanings. We present Safe-SAGE (Social-Semantic Adaptive Guidance for Safe Engagement), a unified framework that bridges the gap between high-level semantic understanding and low-level safety-critical control through a Poisson safety function (PSF) modulated using a Laplace guidance field. Our approach perceives the environment by fusing multi-sensor point clouds with vision-based instance segmentation and persistent object tracking to maintain up-to-date semantics beyond the camera's field of view. A multi-layer safety filter is then used to modulate system inputs to achieve safe navigation using this semantic understanding of the environment. This safety filter consists of both a model predictive control layer and a control barrier function layer. Both layers utilize the PSF and flux modulation of the guidance field to introduce varying levels of conservatism and multi-agent passing norms for different obstacles in the environment. Our framework enables legged robots to navigate semantically rich, dynamic environments with context-dependent safety margins while maintaining rigorous safety guarantees.

AI.tldr: A semantic-aware safety framework (Safe-SAGE) that integrates a Poisson safety function with a Laplace guidance field to modulate safety controllers (MPC and CBF) using semantic perception for dynamic, multi-agent legged robot navigation, yielding context-dependent safety margins.
AI.motivation: Conventional safety controllers (e.g., control barrier functions) are semantically blind and treat all obstacles uniformly, risking overly conservative or unsafe behavior; need context-aware safety that leverages semantic understanding and persistent perception.
AI.method: Fuse multi-sensor data (point clouds, vision-based instance segmentation, persistent tracking) to maintain semantic awareness beyond the camera field of view. Introduce a Poisson safety function (PSF) modulated by a Laplace guidance field. Implement a two-layer safety filter (MPC and CBF) that use PSF and flux modulation to adjust conservatism and passing norms based on obstacle semantics.
AI.result: The framework is presented as enabling legged robots to navigate semantically rich, dynamic environments with context-dependent safety margins while maintaining formal safety guarantees; concrete experimental validation is not detailed in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Safe-SAGE provides a unified bridge between high-level semantic understanding and low-level safety control, enabling context-aware navigation with rigorous safety guarantees.

title: MultiGO++: Monocular 3D Clothed Human Reconstruction via Geometry-Texture Collaboration
summary: Monocular 3D clothed human reconstruction aims to generate a complete and realistic textured 3D avatar from a single image. Existing methods are commonly trained under multi-view supervision with annotated geometric priors, and during inference, these priors are estimated by the pre-trained network from the monocular input. These methods are constrained by three key limitations: texturally by unavailability of training data, geometrically by inaccurate external priors, and systematically by biased single-modality supervision, all leading to suboptimal reconstruction. To address these issues, we propose a novel reconstruction framework, named MultiGO++, which achieves effective systematic geometry-texture collaboration. It consists of three core parts: (1) A multi-source texture synthesis strategy that constructs 15,000+ 3D textured human scans to improve the performance on texture quality estimation in challenge scenarios; (2) A region-aware shape extraction module that extracts and interacts features of each body region to obtain geometry information and a Fourier geometry encoder that mitigates the modality gap to achieve effective geometry learning; (3) A dual reconstruction U-Net that leverages geometry-texture collaborative features to refine and generate high-fidelity textured 3D human meshes. Extensive experiments on two benchmarks and many in-the-wild cases show the superiority of our method over state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: MultiGO++ is a monocular 3D clothed human reconstruction framework that jointly improves texture realism and geometric accuracy via (i) large-scale multi-source texture synthesis, (ii) region-aware geometry extraction with a Fourier-aware encoder, and (iii) a dual U-Net that fuses geometry-texture features for high-fidelity textured meshes.
AI.motivation: To overcome three core limitations of existing monocular 3D clothed human reconstruction methods: (i) texture data scarcity for reliable texture estimation, (ii) reliance on inaccurate external priors for geometry, and (iii) biased single-modality supervision that hinders systematic geometry-texture collaboration.
AI.method: 1) Multi-source texture synthesis: builds a repository of 15,000+ 3D textured human scans to enhance texture quality estimation in challenging scenarios. 2) Region-aware shape extraction with a Fourier geometry encoder: extracts and fuses features per body region to capture geometry, and uses Fourier-encoded representations to bridge modality gaps between texture and geometry. 3) Dual reconstruction U-Net: fuses geometry-texture collaborative features to refine and generate high-fidelity textured 3D meshes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two benchmarks and several in-the-wild cases demonstrate that MultiGO++ outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in textured 3D human reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework establishes effective geometry-texture collaboration in monocular 3D clothed human reconstruction, achieving superior texture quality and geometric fidelity across challenging data, with robust performance in real-world scenarios.

title: $\nabla$-Reasoner: LLM Reasoning via Test-Time Gradient Descent in Latent Space
summary:
Scaling inference-time compute for Large Language Models (LLMs) has unlocked unprecedented reasoning capabilities. However, existing inference-time scaling methods typically rely on inefficient and suboptimal discrete search algorithms or trial-and-error prompting to improve the online policy. In this paper, we propose $
abla$-Reasoner, an iterative generation framework that integrates differentiable optimization over token logits into the decoding loop to refine the policy on the fly. Our core component, Differentiable Textual Optimization (DTO), leverages gradient signals from both the LLM's likelihood and a reward model to refine textual representations. $
abla$-Reasoner further incorporates rejection sampling and acceleration design to robustify and speed up decoding. Theoretically, we show that performing inference-time gradient descent in the sample space to maximize reward is dual to aligning an LLM policy via KL-regularized reinforcement learning. Empirically, $
abla$-Reasoner achieves over 20% accuracy improvement on a challenging mathematical reasoning benchmark, while reducing number of model calls by approximately 10-40% compared to strong baselines. Overall, our work introduces a paradigm shift from zeroth-order search to first-order optimization at test time, offering a cost-effective path to amplify LLM reasoning.

AI.tldr: Introduces ∇-Reasoner, an inference-time differentiable optimization framework that updates token logits via differentiable optimization (DTO) within the decoding loop, yielding better reasoning accuracy and fewer calls than baselines.
AI.motivation: Current inference-time scaling relies on discrete search or trial-and-error prompting, which is inefficient and suboptimal. A principled optimization of the decoding policy at test time could improve performance without大量 model calls.
AI.method: An iterative generation framework that embeds differentiable optimization over token logits (DTO) into the decoding loop. DTO uses gradient signals from the LLM likelihood and a reward model to refine textual representations. Adds rejection sampling and acceleration to robustify/speed up decoding. The authors show a theoretical duality: test-time gradient descent to maximize reward equals KL-regularized RL alignment of the LLM policy.
AI.result: Empirically achieves >20% accuracy improvement on a challenging mathematical reasoning benchmark; reduces the number of model calls by ~10–40% compared with strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Signals a paradigm shift from zeroth-order search to first-order optimization at test time, offering a cost-effective path to amplify LLM reasoning.

title: AI+HW 2035: Shaping the Next Decade
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) and hardware (HW) are advancing at unprecedented rates, yet their trajectories have become inseparably intertwined. The global research community lacks a cohesive, long-term vision to strategically coordinate the development of AI and HW. This fragmentation constrains progress toward holistic, sustainable, and adaptive AI systems capable of learning, reasoning, and operating efficiently across cloud, edge, and physical environments. The future of AI depends not only on scaling intelligence, but on scaling efficiency, achieving exponential gains in intelligence per joule, rather than unbounded compute consumption. Addressing this grand challenge requires rethinking the entire computing stack. This vision paper lays out a 10-year roadmap for AI+HW co-design and co-development, spanning algorithms, architectures, systems, and sustainability. We articulate key insights that redefine scaling around energy efficiency, system-level integration, and cross-layer optimization. We identify key challenges and opportunities, candidly assess potential obstacles and pitfalls, and propose integrated solutions grounded in algorithmic innovation, hardware advances, and software abstraction. Looking ahead, we define what success means in 10 years: achieving a 1000x improvement in efficiency for AI training and inference; enabling energy-aware, self-optimizing systems that seamlessly span cloud, edge, and physical AI; democratizing access to advanced AI infrastructure; and embedding human-centric principles into the design of intelligent systems. Finally, we outline concrete action items for academia, industry, government, and the broader community, calling for coordinated national initiatives, shared infrastructure, workforce development, cross-agency collaboration, and sustained public-private partnerships to ensure that AI+HW co-design becomes a unifying long-term mission.

AI.tldr: A 10-year roadmap for AI+HW co-design to dramatically improve efficiency (aiming for ~1000x gains) by integrating algorithms, architectures, systems, and sustainability, across cloud, edge, and physical AI, with coordinated actions from academia, industry, government and society to realize a unified mission.
AI.motivation: The field faces fragmentation between AI and hardware research, lacking a cohesive, long-term vision. Progress is constrained by inefficiencies; the future of AI depends on energy-efficient scaling rather than unchecked compute growth. A holistic, cross-layer approach is needed to enable adaptive, sustainable AI systems across diverse environments.
AI.method: Propose a comprehensive roadmap spanning a decade, addressing algorithms, hardware architectures, system integration, and sustainability. Identify challenges and opportunities, assess potential obstacles, and offer integrated solutions grounded in algorithmic innovation, hardware advances, and software abstractions. Define success metrics for 10 years and outline concrete actions for academia, industry, government, and the broader community, including coordinated national initiatives, shared infrastructure, workforce development, cross-agency collaboration, and public-private partnerships.
AI.result: A concrete vision of 10-year success: a 1000x improvement in efficiency for AI training and inference; energy-aware, self-optimizing systems spanning cloud, edge, and physical AI; democratized access to advanced AI infrastructure; and embedding human-centric design principles in intelligent systems.
AI.conclusion: Calls for sustained, multi-sector collaboration to implement AI+HW co-design as a unified, long-term mission. Proposes specific action items: coordinated national initiatives, shared infrastructure, workforce development, cross-agency collaboration, and ongoing public-private partnerships to coordinate progress and funding across academia, industry, government, and society.

title: RoboPocket: Improve Robot Policies Instantly with Your Phone
summary: Scaling imitation learning is fundamentally constrained by the efficiency of data collection. While handheld interfaces have emerged as a scalable solution for in-the-wild data acquisition, they predominantly operate in an open-loop manner: operators blindly collect demonstrations without knowing the underlying policy's weaknesses, leading to inefficient coverage of critical state distributions. Conversely, interactive methods like DAgger effectively address covariate shift but rely on physical robot execution, which is costly and difficult to scale. To reconcile this trade-off, we introduce RoboPocket, a portable system that enables Robot-Free Instant Policy Iteration using single consumer smartphones. Its core innovation is a Remote Inference framework that visualizes the policy's predicted trajectory via Augmented Reality (AR) Visual Foresight. This immersive feedback allows collectors to proactively identify potential failures and focus data collection on the policy's weak regions without requiring a physical robot. Furthermore, we implement an asynchronous Online Finetuning pipeline that continuously updates the policy with incoming data, effectively closing the learning loop in minutes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RoboPocket adheres to data scaling laws and doubles the data efficiency compared to offline scaling strategies, overcoming their long-standing efficiency bottleneck. Moreover, our instant iteration loop also boosts sample efficiency by up to 2$\times$ in distributed environments a small number of interactive corrections per person. Project page and videos: https://robo-pocket.github.io.

AI.tldr: RoboPocket enables robot-free, data-efficient imitation learning using smartphones, using AR Visual Foresight to guide data collection and an online finetuning loop for rapid policy improvement.
AI.motivation: Imitation-learning data collection is bottlenecked by open-loop handheld demos and costly robot-based methods; scalable, low-cost data collection with responsive policy refinement is needed.
AI.method: Remote Inference framework visualizes the policy trajectory via AR to show weaknesses and guide data collection; online asynchronous finetuning updates the policy with new data; operates on consumer smartphones without physical robots.
AI.result: Claims include adherence to data scaling laws, 2x improvement in data efficiency over offline scaling strategies, and up to 2x gains in sample efficiency in distributed settings with minimal interactive corrections per person.
AI.conclusion: Robot-free, AR-guided data collection plus online finetuning can achieve scalable, efficient imitation learning, bridging the gap between open-loop demos and robot-based methods.

title: Physics-consistent deep learning for blind aberration recovery in mobile optics
summary: Mobile photography is often limited by complex, lens-specific optical aberrations. While recent deep learning methods approach this as an end-to-end deblurring task, these "black-box" models lack explicit optical modeling and can hallucinate details. Conversely, classical blind deconvolution remains highly unstable. To bridge this gap, we present Lens2Zernike, a deep learning framework that blindly recovers physical optical parameters from a single blurred image. To the best of our knowledge, no prior work has simultaneously integrated supervision across three distinct optical domains. We introduce a novel physics-consistent strategy that explicitly minimizes errors via direct Zernike coefficient regression (z), differentiable physics constraints encompassing both wavefront and point spread function derivations (p), and auxiliary multi-task spatial map predictions (m). Through an ablation study on a ResNet-18 backbone, we demonstrate that our full multi-task framework (z+p+m) yields a 35% improvement over coefficient-only baselines. Crucially, comparative analysis reveals that our approach outperforms two established deep learning methods from previous literature, achieving significantly lower regression errors. Ultimately, we demonstrate that these recovered physical parameters enable stable non-blind deconvolution, providing substantial in-domain improvement on the patented Institute for Digital Molecular Analytics and Science (IDMxS) Mobile Camera Lens Database for restoring diffraction-limited details from severely aberrated mobile captures.

AI.tldr: A multi-task deep learning framework (Lens2Zernike) recovers physical optical parameters from a single blurred image to enable stable non-blind deconvolution, bridging end-to-end DL deblurring and classical blind deconvolution.
AI.motivation: To address the instability and artifact risk of pure blind deconvolution and the lack of explicit optical modeling in end-to-end networks, by directly regressing Zernike coefficients and enforcing physics-based constraints.
AI.method: A ResNet-18 based architecture that jointly predicts Zernike coefficients (z), enforces differentiable physics constraints for wavefront/PSF (p), and predicts auxiliary spatial maps (m). The framework includes a novel physics-consistent training strategy and an ablation study comparing z, p, m components against coefficient-only baselines and two prior DL models.
AI.result: The full z+p+m model achieves ~35% improvement over coefficient-only baselines. It yields lower regression errors than two established DL methods and enables stable non-blind deconvolution, improving in-domain restoration of diffraction-limited mobile lens details on the IDMxS mobile camera lens database.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates that recovering physical optical parameters from a single blurred image, with explicit physics constraints and multi-task supervision, can outperform prior DL methods and support stable non-blind deconvolution in mobile photography.

title: Uncertainty-aware Blood Glucose Prediction from Continuous Glucose Monitoring Data
summary: In this work, we investigate uncertainty-aware neural network models for blood glucose prediction and adverse glycemic event identification in Type 1 diabetes. We consider three families of sequence models based on LSTM, GRU, and Transformer architectures, with uncertainty quantification enabled by either Monte Carlo dropout or through evidential output layers compatible with Deep Evidential Regression. Using the HUPA-UCM diabetes dataset for validation, we find that Transformer-based models equipped with evidential output heads provide the most effective uncertainty-aware framework, achieving consistently higher predictive accuracies and better-calibrated uncertainty estimates whose magnitudes significantly correlate with prediction errors. We further evaluate the clinical risk of each model using the recently proposed Diabetes Technology Society error grid, with risk categories defined by international expert consensus. Our results demonstrate the value of integrating principled uncertainty quantification into real-time machine-learning-based blood glucose prediction systems.

AI.tldr: Transformer models with evidential uncertainty heads provide the strongest performance and best-calibrated uncertainty for blood glucose prediction and adverse event risk, outperforming LSTM/GRU with MC dropout on the HUPA-UCM dataset.
AI.motivation: Address the safety-critical nature of diabetes forecasting by incorporating principled, calibrated uncertainty estimates to improve decision-making in real-time glucose prediction and risk assessment.
AI.method: Comparative evaluation of three sequence architectures (LSTM, GRU, Transformer) with two uncertainty quantification approaches (Monte Carlo dropout and Deep Evidential Regression). Validation on the HUPA-UCM diabetes dataset; assessments include predictive accuracy, calibration of uncertainties, and clinical risk via the Diabetes Technology Society error grid.
AI.result: Transformer models with evidential output heads achieved the highest accuracy and best-calibrated uncertainty estimates, with uncertainty magnitudes correlating with prediction errors; clinical risk assessment via the error grid supports the practical value of the approach.
AI.conclusion: Integrating principled uncertainty quantification, especially evidential heads with Transformer architectures, enhances real-time blood glucose prediction systems and their clinical risk interpretability.

title: Reclaiming Lost Text Layers for Source-Free Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning
summary: Source-Free Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (SF-CDFSL) focuses on fine-tuning with limited training data from target domains (e.g., medical or satellite images), where CLIP has recently shown promising results due to its generalizability to downstream tasks. Current works indicate CLIP's text encoder is more suitable for cross-domain tasks, however, we find that \textbf{removing certain middle layers of the text encoder can effectively improve performance in SF-CDFSL}, which we call the Lost Layers. In this paper, we delve into this phenomenon for a deeper understanding. We discover that instead of being harmful for the SF-CDFSL task, the information in these layers is actually beneficial, but visual gaps prevent this useful information from being fully utilized, making these layers seem redundant. Based on this understanding, unlike current works that simply remove these layers, we propose a method to teachs the model to \textbf{re-utilize} information in these lost layers at both the layer and encoder levels, guiding the re-learning of the visual branch under domain shifts. Our approach effectively addresses the issue of underutilized information in the text encoder. Extensive experiments across various settings, backbones (CLIP, SigLip, PE-Core), and tasks (4 CDFSL datasets and 10 Meta-dataset datasets) demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. Code is available at https://github.com/zhenyuZ-HUST/CVPR26-VtT.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates why removing middle layers of CLIP's text encoder can help in Source-Free Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (SF-CDFSL) and introduces a method to re-utilize information from those 'lost' layers at both layer and encoder levels, improving cross-domain few-shot performance across multiple backbones and datasets.
AI.motivation: SF-CDFSL involves adapting to target domains with very limited data; CLIP's text encoder shows strong cross-domain transfer, but the authors observe that selectively removing middle layers can boost performance. This questions the role of intermediate representations and aims to leverage the information these layers contain rather than discard it.
AI.method: Diagnose the role of middle encoder layers, identify 'Lost Layers' whose information is underutilized due to visual gaps, and propose a re-utilization training strategy that propagates and leverages these layers' information at both the layer and encoder levels to guide re-learning of the visual branch under domain shifts. Evaluates across backbones (CLIP, SigLip, PE-Core) and datasets (4 CDFSL + 10 Meta-dataset). Code is released.
AI.result: The method effectively reuses information from the previously discarded layers, addressing underutilization and yielding consistent performance gains across diverse backbones and datasets, as demonstrated by extensive experiments; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Understanding the informative content of mid-text-encoder layers and actively re-utilizing it during training improves SF-CDFSL performance and generalizes across multiple backbones and tasks, offering a new perspective on layer-wise information flow in cross-domain adaptation.

title: How far have we gone in Generative Image Restoration? A study on its capability, limitations and evaluation practices
summary: Generative Image Restoration (GIR) has achieved impressive perceptual realism, but how far have its practical capabilities truly advanced compared with previous methods? To answer this, we present a large-scale study grounded in a new multi-dimensional evaluation pipeline that assesses models on detail, sharpness, semantic correctness, and overall quality. Our analysis covers diverse architectures, including diffusion-based, GAN-based, PSNR-oriented, and general-purpose generation models, revealing critical performance disparities. Furthermore, our analysis uncovers a key evolution in failure modes that signifies a paradigm shift for the perception-oriented low-level vision field. The central challenge is evolving from the previous problem of detail scarcity (under-generation) to the new frontier of detail quality and semantic control (preventing over-generation). We also leverage our benchmark to train a new IQA model that better aligns with human perceptual judgments. Ultimately, this work provides a systematic study of modern generative image restoration models, offering crucial insights that redefine our understanding of their true state and chart a course for future development.

AI.tldr: A large-scale, multi-dimensional evaluation of Generative Image Restoration (GIR) models reveals performance disparities across architectures and a shift in failure modes from under-generation to over-generation; introduces an IQA model aligned with human judgments and provides a systematic assessment guiding future development.
AI.motivation: To quantify progress in GIR beyond perceptual realism by establishing a multi-dimensional benchmark that captures detail, sharpness, semantic correctness, and overall quality, and to understand how different architectures perform and fail.
AI.method: Construct and apply a new multi-dimensional evaluation pipeline; evaluate diffusion-based, GAN-based, PSNR-oriented, and general-purpose generation models across dimensions; analyze failure modes; train a new image quality assessment (IQA) model based on benchmark data.
AI.result: Identification of critical performance gaps between architectures; observation of a paradigm shift in failure modes from under-generation to quality-controlled over-generation; creation of a benchmark-aligned IQA model; systematic characterization of modern GIR models.
AI.conclusion: Provides a comprehensive framework and insights that redefine the state of GIR, guiding future development toward balanced, semantically accurate, high-quality restoration without over-generation.

title: WaterSIC: information-theoretically (near) optimal linear layer quantization
summary: This paper considers the problem of converting a given dense linear layer to low precision. The tradeoff between compressed length and output discrepancy is analyzed information theoretically (IT). It is shown that a popular GPTQ algorithm may have an arbitrarily large gap to the IT limit. To alleviate this problem, a novel algorithm, termed ''WaterSIC'', is proposed and is shown to be within a rate gap of 0.255 bits to the IT limit, uniformly over all possible covariance matrices of input activations. The key innovation of WaterSIC's is to allocate different quantization rates to different columns (in-features) of the weight matrix, mimicking the classical IT solution known as ''waterfilling''. Applying WaterSIC to the Llama and Qwen family of LLMs establishes new state-of-the-art performance for all quantization rates from 1 to 4 bits.

AI.tldr: WaterSIC is a per-column rate-allocation quantization method for dense layers that closely approaches the information-theoretic limit, outperforming GPTQ and achieving state-of-the-art low-precision quantization (1–4 bits) on Llama/Qwen models.
AI.motivation: To minimize the tradeoff between compressed weight size and output fidelity in low-precision neural networks, by addressing the potential large gap between practical quantizers (e.g., GPTQ) and the information-theoretic limit, in a way that is robust to input activation statistics.
AI.method: Introduce WaterSIC, which allocates different quantization rates to the weight matrix columns (in-features) following a waterfilling-like principle derived from information theory; show a uniform 0.255-bit gap to the IT limit across all input covariance matrices; apply it to Llama and Qwen models for 1–4 bit quantization.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantee: WaterSIC is within 0.255 bits of the IT limit for all input covariances. Empirical demonstration: achieves new state-of-the-art quantization performance across 1–4 bits on Llama and Qwen family.
AI.conclusion: WaterSIC provides near-information-theoretic-optimal low-precision quantization for dense layers, substantially narrowing the gap to the IT limit and delivering strong practical gains on large language models.

title: GCAgent: Enhancing Group Chat Communication through Dialogue Agents System
summary: As a key form in online social platforms, group chat is a popular space for interest exchange or problem-solving, but its effectiveness is often hindered by inactivity and management challenges. While recent large language models (LLMs) have powered impressive one-to-one conversational agents, their seamlessly integration into multi-participant conversations remains unexplored. To address this gap, we introduce GCAgent, an LLM-driven system for enhancing group chats communication with both entertainment- and utility-oriented dialogue agents. The system comprises three tightly integrated modules: Agent Builder, which customizes agents to align with users' interests; Dialogue Manager, which coordinates dialogue states and manage agent invocations; and Interface Plugins, which reduce interaction barriers by three distinct tools. Through extensive experiment, GCAgent achieved an average score of 4.68 across various criteria and was preferred in 51.04\% of cases compared to its base model. Additionally, in real-world deployments over 350 days, it increased message volume by 28.80\%, significantly improving group activity and engagement. Overall, this work presents a practical blueprint for extending LLM-based dialogue agent from one-party chats to multi-party group scenarios.

AI.tldr: GCAgent is an LLM-based group-chat agent system with three modules (Agent Builder, Dialogue Manager, Interface Plugins) enabling customized, coordinated multi-party conversations; shows strong subjective scores and engagement gains in experiments and a 350-day real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in applying LLM-driven agents to multi-party group chats, where inactivity and management hinder effectiveness; extend one-to-one oriented agents to group settings with tooling to coordinate agents and reduce interaction barriers.
AI.method: Three integrated modules and tools: Agent Builder for customization, Dialogue Manager for state coordination and agent invocation, Interface Plugins for user-friendly tools; evaluation via extensive experiments and a real-world deployment.
AI.result: Average score 4.68; 51.04% preference vs base model; 28.80% increase in message volume over 350 days deployment; improved group activity and engagement.
AI.conclusion: Presents a practical blueprint to extend LLM-based dialogue agents from one-party chats to multi-party group scenarios, showing feasibility and effectiveness.

title: Tell2Adapt: A Unified Framework for Source Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation via Vision Foundation Model
comment: Accepted by IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR 2026)
summary: Source Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (SFUDA) is critical for deploying deep learning models across diverse clinical settings. However, existing methods are typically designed for low-gap, specific domain shifts and cannot generalize into a unified, multi-modalities, and multi-target framework, which presents a major barrier to real-world application. To overcome this issue, we introduce Tell2Adapt, a novel SFUDA framework that harnesses the vast, generalizable knowledge of the Vision Foundation Model (VFM). Our approach ensures high-fidelity VFM prompts through Context-Aware Prompts Regularization (CAPR), which robustly translates varied text prompts into canonical instructions. This enables the generation of high-quality pseudo-labels for efficiently adapting the lightweight student model to target domain. To guarantee clinical reliability, the framework incorporates Visual Plausibility Refinement (VPR), which leverages the VFM's anatomical knowledge to re-ground the adapted model's predictions in target image's low-level visual features, effectively removing noise and false positives. We conduct one of the most extensive SFUDA evaluations to date, validating our framework across 10 domain adaptation directions and 22 anatomical targets, including brain, cardiac, polyp, and abdominal targets. Our results demonstrate that Tell2Adapt consistently outperforms existing approaches, achieving SOTA for a unified SFUDA framework in medical image segmentation. Code are avaliable at https://github.com/derekshiii/Tell2Adapt.

AI.tldr: A unified SFUDA framework for medical image segmentation (Tell2Adapt) leverages Vision Foundation Models with Context-Aware Prompts Regularization (CAPR) and Visual Plausibility Refinement (VPR) to generate reliable pseudo-labels and re-ground predictions, achieving state-of-the-art performance across 10 domain directions and 22 anatomical targets in a broad evaluation.
AI.motivation: To enable robust, scalable SFUDA across multi-modal medical imaging domains (brain, cardiac, polyp, abdomen) where domain shifts are large, and previous methods struggle to generalize beyond low-gap or single-domain adaptations.
AI.method: Introduce Tell2Adapt: a SFUDA framework that uses Vision Foundation Model prompts translated via Context-Aware Prompts Regularization (CAPR) to produce high-quality pseudo-labels for a lightweight student model. Incorporates Visual Plausibility Refinement (VPR) to re-ground predictions using anatomical knowledge and target image low-level features, reducing noise and false positives.
AI.result: Evaluated across 10 domain adaptation directions and 22 anatomical targets, including brain, cardiac, polyp, and abdominal targets, with consistent SOTA performance for a unified SFUDA framework in medical image segmentation.
AI.conclusion: Tell2Adapt demonstrates that leveraging VFMs with CAPR and VPR can achieve robust, generalizable SFUDA for multi-target medical image segmentation, providing reliable performance across diverse targets and domain shifts; code available at the authors’ repository.

title: Mixture of Universal Experts: Scaling Virtual Width via Depth-Width Transformation
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) decouples model capacity from per-token computation, yet their scalability remains limited by the physical dimensions of depth and width. To overcome this, we propose Mixture of Universal Experts (MOUE),a MoE generalization introducing a novel scaling dimension: Virtual Width. In general, MoUE aims to reuse a universal layer-agnostic expert pool across layers, converting depth into virtual width under a fixed per-token activation budget.
