However, two challenges remain: a routing path explosion from recursive expert reuse, and a mismatch between the exposure induced by reuse and the conventional load-balancing objectives. We address these with three core components: a Staggered Rotational Topology for structured expert sharing, a Universal Expert Load Balance for depth-aware exposure correction, and a Universal Router with lightweight trajectory state for coherent multi-step routing. Empirically, MoUE consistently outperforms matched MoE baselines by up to 1.3% across scaling regimes, enables progressive conversion of existing MoE checkpoints with up to 4.2% gains, and reveals a new scaling dimension for MoE architectures.

AI.tldr: MOUE introduces Virtual Width by reusing a universal pool of experts across layers to convert depth into width under a fixed per-token budget. It mitigates routing explosion and exposure mismatches with three components, yielding consistent gains and enabling checkpoint reuse, thus revealing a new MoE scaling dimension.
AI.motivation: MoE scales capacity but remains constrained by depth and width under fixed per-token compute. Introducing a scalable dimension beyond depth/width—virtual width via universal expert reuse—promises higher capacity without increasing per-token cost.
AI.method: Propose MOUE with a universal, layer-agnostic expert pool and fixed per-token activation budget. Address routing path explosion with Staggered Rotational Topology (structured expert sharing), exposure mismatch with Universal Expert Load Balance (depth-aware exposure correction), and coherent multi-step routing with Universal Router (lightweight trajectory state).
AI.result: Empirically, MOUE beats matched MoE baselines by up to 1.3% across scaling regimes, enables progressive conversion of existing MoE checkpoints with up to 4.2% gains, and reveals a new scaling dimension for MoE architectures.
AI.conclusion: MOUE demonstrates that introducing a virtual width dimension via universal expert reuse and coordinated routing yields consistent performance gains and practical checkpoint adaptation, advancing scalable MoE design.

title: X-RAY: Mapping LLM Reasoning Capability via Formalized and Calibrated Probes
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve promising performance, yet their ability to reason remains poorly understood. Existing evaluations largely emphasize task-level accuracy, often conflating pattern matching with reasoning capability. We present X-RAY, an explainable reasoning analysis system that maps the LLM reasoning capability using calibrated, formally verified probes. We model reasoning capability as a function of extractable \textit{structure}, operationalized through formal properties such as constraint interaction, reasoning depth, and solution-space geometry. X-Ray generates probes via formal tools with controlled structural variations, enabling precise isolation of incremental structural information through formal calibration and verification. We evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs on problems ranging from junior-level to advanced in mathematics, physics, and chemistry. Our analysis reveals a systematic asymmetry in LLM reasoning: models are relatively robust to constraint refinement, where additional conditions shrink an existing solution space, but degrade sharply under solution-space restructuring, where modifications alter the underlying structural form of the solution manifold. Moreover, calibrated formal probes differentiate models that appear indistinguishable on standard benchmarks and reveal failure modes that are structurally interpretable rather than opaque. Beyond evaluation, our framework is contamination-free and supports the training and testing of reasoning models.

AI.tldr: X-RAY provides a formal, calibration-based probe framework to quantify LLM reasoning by analyzing how extractable problem structure affects reasoning; reveals a systematic asymmetry in robustness to structural changes; differentiates models beyond standard benchmarks; contamination-free and suitable for training/testing.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation largely tracks task-level accuracy and pattern matching, conflating it with reasoning; there is a need for interpretable, formally verifiable measures of reasoning capability in LLMs.
AI.method: Define reasoning capability as a function of extractable structure (constraint interaction, reasoning depth, solution-space geometry). Generate probes via formal tools with controlled structural variations; calibrate and verify to isolate incremental structural information; apply to problems across math, physics, chemistry.
AI.result: LLMs exhibit asymmetry: robustness to constraint refinement (adding conditions that shrink the solution space) but degradation under solution-space restructuring (changes to the underlying solution manifold). Calibrated probes differentiate models that appear indistinguishable on standard benchmarks and reveal structurally interpretable failure modes.
AI.conclusion: X-RAY offers a contamination-free framework for evaluating and auditing reasoning in LLMs, with applicability to training and testing of reasoning capabilities and potential for systematic analysis beyond conventional benchmarks.

title: Generalizable Multiscale Segmentation of Heterogeneous Map Collections
summary: Historical map collections are highly diverse in style, scale, and geographic focus, often consisting of many single-sheet documents. Yet most work in map recognition focuses on specialist models tailored to homogeneous map series. In contrast, this article aims to develop generalizable semantic segmentation models and ontology. First, we introduce Semap, a new open benchmark dataset comprising 1,439 manually annotated patches designed to reflect the variety of historical map documents. Second, we present a segmentation framework that combines procedural data synthesis with multiscale integration to improve robustness and transferability. This framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on both the HCMSSD and Semap datasets, showing that a diversity-driven approach to map recognition is not only viable but also beneficial. The results indicate that segmentation performance remains largely stable across map collections, scales, geographic regions, and publication contexts. By proposing benchmark datasets and methods for the generic segmentation of historical maps, this work opens the way to integrating the long tail of cartographic archives to historical geographic studies.

AI.tldr: Proposes Semap benchmark and a robustness-driven segmentation framework for historical maps, combining procedural data synthesis with multiscale integration to achieve state-of-the-art generalization across diverse map collections.
AI.motivation: Historical map archives are highly heterogeneous (style, scale, region, context) and lack generalizable segmentation models. A diverse benchmark is needed to train and evaluate models that can transfer across collections and support long-tail cartographic archives.
AI.method: Introduce Semap, a dataset of 1,439 manually annotated patches reflecting historical map diversity. Develop a segmentation framework that fuses procedural data synthesis with multiscale feature integration to improve robustness and transferability.
AI.result: The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on both HCMSSD and Semap datasets and shows segmentation robustness across map collections, scales, geographic regions, and publication contexts.
AI.conclusion: A diversity-driven, generic segmentation approach is viable and beneficial for historical maps, enabling integration of broad cartographic archives into historical geographic studies.

title: Functionality-Oriented LLM Merging on the Fisher--Rao Manifold
summary:
Weight-space merging aims to combine multiple fine-tuned LLMs into a single model without retraining, yet most existing approaches remain fundamentally parameter-space heuristics. This creates three practical limitations. First, linear averaging, task vectors, and related rules operate on Euclidean coordinates, even though the desired goal is to merge functionality, i.e., predictive behaviors across tasks. Second, when the source checkpoints are farther apart or more heterogeneous, Euclidean blends often trigger representation collapse, manifested as activation variance shrinkage and effective-rank degradation, which sharply degrades accuracy. Third, many geometry-inspired methods are most natural for two-model interpolation and do not extend cleanly to merging N>2 experts with a principled objective.
  We address these issues by formulating model merging as computing a weighted Karcher mean on the Fisher--Rao manifold, which is locally equivalent to minimizing a KL-based function distance between predictive distributions. We derive a practical fixed-point algorithm using a lightweight spherical proxy that preserves norms and generalizes directly to multi-expert merging. Across various benchmarks and collapse diagnostics, our method remains stable as the number and heterogeneity of merged models increase, consistently outperforming prior baselines.

AI.tldr: Reframes weight-space model merging as a weighted Karcher mean on the Fisher–Rao manifold, addressing Euclidean-integration drawbacks, enabling stable, multi-expert merging with a fixed-point algorithm using a spherical proxy.
AI.motivation: To merge multiple fine-tuned LLMs into a single model without retraining while avoiding representation collapse and the limitations of Euclidean blends, especially as the number and heterogeneity of sources grow.
AI.method: Formulate merging as minimizing a KL-based distance between predictive distributions via the Fisher–Rao geometry, derive a practical fixed-point algorithm with a spherical proxy that preserves norms and extends to N>2 experts.
AI.result: Empirically stable across increasing model count and heterogeneity, with consistent performance gains over baselines and robust against collapse diagnostics across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware weight-space merging on the Fisher–Rao manifold offers a principled, scalable alternative to Euclidean blends, yielding stable, multi-expert fusion without retraining and with improved predictive fidelity.

title: STRUCTUREDAGENT: Planning with AND/OR Trees for Long-Horizon Web Tasks
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled agentic systems for sequential decision-making. Such agents must perceive their environment, reason across multiple time steps, and take actions that optimize long-term objectives. However, existing web agents struggle on complex, long-horizon tasks due to limited in-context memory for tracking history, weak planning abilities, and greedy behaviors that lead to premature termination. To address these challenges, we propose STRUCTUREDAGENT, a hierarchical planning framework with two core components: (1) an online hierarchical planner that uses dynamic AND/OR trees for efficient search and (2) a structured memory module that tracks and maintains candidate solutions to improve constraint satisfaction in information-seeking tasks. The framework also produces interpretable hierarchical plans, enabling easier debugging and facilitating human intervention when needed. Our results on WebVoyager, WebArena, and custom shopping benchmarks show that STRUCTUREDAGENT improves performance on long-horizon web-browsing tasks compared to standard LLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: Introduces STRUCTUREDAGENT, a hierarchical planning framework for LLM agents that combines an online hierarchical planner using dynamic AND/OR trees with a structured memory module to improve long-horizon web tasks and provide interpretable plans.
AI.motivation: Existing web agents suffer from limited in-context memory, weak multi-step planning, and greedy behavior, hindering long-horizon objective achievement and information-seeking accuracy.
AI.method: Two core components: (1) an online hierarchical planner utilizing dynamic AND/OR trees for efficient search; (2) a structured memory module that tracks and maintains candidate solutions to improve constraint satisfaction in information-seeking tasks; outputs interpretable hierarchical plans and supports human intervention.
AI.result: Empirical results on WebVoyager, WebArena, and custom shopping benchmarks show that STRUCTUREDAGENT improves performance on long-horizon web-browsing tasks compared to standard LLM-based agents.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical planning with structured memory yields interpretable plans, better long-horizon performance, and easier debugging/intervention, addressing core weaknesses of existing LLM-based web agents.

title: CoIn3D: Revisiting Configuration-Invariant Multi-Camera 3D Object Detection
summary: Multi-camera 3D object detection (MC3D) has attracted increasing attention with the growing deployment of multi-sensor physical agents, such as robots and autonomous vehicles. However, MC3D models still struggle to generalize to unseen platforms with new multi-camera configurations. Current solutions simply employ a meta-camera for unified representation but lack comprehensive consideration. In this paper, we revisit this issue and identify that the devil lies in spatial prior discrepancies across source and target configurations, including different intrinsics, extrinsics, and array layouts. To address this, we propose CoIn3D, a generalizable MC3D framework that enables strong transferability from source configurations to unseen target ones. CoIn3D explicitly incorporates all identified spatial priors into both feature embedding and image observation through spatial-aware feature modulation (SFM) and camera-aware data augmentation (CDA), respectively. SFM enriches feature space by integrating four spatial representations, such as focal length, ground depth, ground gradient, and Plücker coordinate. CDA improves observation diversity under various configurations via a training-free dynamic novel-view image synthesis scheme. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CoIn3D achieves strong cross-configuration performance on landmark datasets such as NuScenes, Waymo, and Lyft, under three dominant MC3D paradigms represented by BEVDepth, BEVFormer, and PETR.

AI.tldr: CoIn3D is a generalizable MC3D framework that tackles spatial-prior gaps across camera configurations by augmenting feature embeddings with spatial priors and applying camera-aware, training-free data augmentation, enabling strong cross-configuration transfer to unseen rigs.
AI.motivation: MC3D models struggle to generalize to unseen platforms with different camera intrinsics, extrinsics, and array layouts. The mismatch in spatial priors limits cross-configuration transfer and existing meta-camera approaches are insufficient.
AI.method: Introduce SFM (spatial-aware feature modulation) to inject four spatial representations (focal length, ground depth, ground gradient, Plücker coordinates) into the feature space, and CDA (camera-aware data augmentation) to diversify observations via a training-free dynamic novel-view image synthesis scheme. The framework is designed to work with major MC3D paradigms (BEVDepth, BEVFormer, PETR) and evaluated on NuScenes, Waymo, and Lyft.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show strong cross-configuration performance, demonstrating robust transferability from source camera configurations to unseen target configurations across diverse datasets and MC3D backbones.
AI.conclusion: CoIn3D effectively addresses spatial-prior discrepancies between source and target configurations, achieving generalization to unseen multi-camera setups by embedding spatial priors in features and observations through SFM and CDA. This yields improved cross-configuration robustness across standard MC3D paradigms and datasets.

title: Exploiting Intermediate Reconstructions in Optical Coherence Tomography for Test-Time Adaption of Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Primary health care frequently relies on low-cost imaging devices, which are commonly used for screening purposes. To ensure accurate diagnosis, these systems depend on advanced reconstruction algorithms designed to approximate the performance of high-quality counterparts. Such algorithms typically employ iterative reconstruction methods that incorporate domain-specific prior knowledge. However, downstream task performance is generally assessed using only the final reconstructed image, thereby disregarding the informative intermediate representations generated throughout the reconstruction process. In this work, we propose IRTTA to exploit these intermediate representations at test-time by adapting the normalization-layer parameters of a frozen downstream network via a modulator network that conditions on the current reconstruction timescale. The modulator network is learned during test-time using an averaged entropy loss across all individual timesteps. Variation among the timestep-wise segmentations additionally provides uncertainty estimates at no extra cost. This approach enhances segmentation performance and enables semantically meaningful uncertainty estimation, all without modifying either the reconstruction process or the downstream model.

AI.tldr: A test-time adaptation method (IRTTA) exploits intermediate iterative reconstructions to adapt a frozen segmentation model by modulating its normalization parameters via a timesteps-conditioned modulator, improving segmentation and yielding uncertainty estimates without changing reconstruction or the downstream model.
AI.motivation: Downstream task evaluation typically focuses on final reconstructions, ignoring informative intermediate representations; leveraging these can boost performance and enable uncertainty estimation in low-cost imaging contexts.
AI.method: A modulator network, conditioned on the current reconstruction timescale, adjusts the normalization-layer parameters of a frozen downstream network during test time. The modulator is trained with an averaged entropy loss across all timesteps. Uncertainty arises from variability across timestep-wise segmentations.
AI.result: Segmentation performance is enhanced; the approach provides semantically meaningful uncertainty estimates at no extra cost and without modifying the reconstruction process or the downstream model.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting intermediate representations in iterative reconstruction via test-time normalization modulation is effective for improving downstream segmentation and obtaining uncertainty estimates in low-cost imaging pipelines.

title: Lightweight and Scalable Transfer Learning Framework for Load Disaggregation
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Non-Intrusive Load Monitoring (NILM) aims to estimate appliance-level consumption from aggregate electrical signals recorded at a single measurement point. In recent years, the field has increasingly adopted deep learning approaches; however, cross-domain generalization remains a persistent challenge due to variations in appliance characteristics, usage patterns, and background loads across homes. Transfer learning provides a practical paradigm to adapt models with limited target data. However, existing methods often assume a fixed appliance set, lack flexibility for evolving real-world deployments, remain unsuitable for edge devices, or scale poorly for real-time operation. This paper proposes RefQuery, a scalable multi-appliance, multi-task NILM framework that conditions disaggregation on compact appliance fingerprints, allowing one shared model to serve many appliances without a fixed output set. RefQuery keeps a pretrained disaggregation network fully frozen and adapts to a target home by learning only a per-appliance embedding during a lightweight backpropagation stage. Experiments on three public datasets demonstrate that RefQuery delivers a strong accuracy-efficiency trade-off against single-appliance and multi-appliance baselines, including modern Transformer-based methods. These results support RefQuery as a practical path toward scalable, real-time NILM on resource-constrained edge devices.

AI.tldr: RefQuery is a scalable, multi-appliance NILM framework that uses per-appliance embeddings and a frozen base disaggregation network to adapt to new homes with limited data, enabling real-time edge deployment and avoiding fixed output sets.
AI.motivation: Cross-domain variability in NILM (appliance signatures, usage patterns, and background loads) and the need for flexible, scalable transfer learning that supports evolving appliance sets and edge constraints.
AI.method: Keep a pretrained disaggregation network frozen. Adapt to a target home by learning a lightweight per-appliance embedding and conditioning the shared model with compact appliance fingerprints. No fixed output set; supports multiple appliances with a single model. Evaluated on three public datasets, compared against single-appliance and multi-appliance baselines, including Transformer-based methods.
AI.result: RefQuery achieves a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off, outperforming baselines and matching or surpassing modern Transformer-based approaches, enabling practical real-time NILM on resource-constrained edge devices.
AI.conclusion: A practical, scalable NILM framework that decouples appliance-specific adaptation from the base model, enabling dynamic appliance support without retraining the core network and facilitating edge-friendly deployment.

title: WebChain: A Large-Scale Human-Annotated Dataset of Real-World Web Interaction Traces
summary: We introduce WebChain, the largest open-source dataset of human-annotated trajectories on real-world websites, designed to accelerate reproducible research in web agents. It contains 31,725 trajectories and 318k steps, featuring a core Triple Alignment of visual, structural, and action data to provide rich, multi-modal supervision. The data is collected via a scalable pipeline that ensures coverage of complex, high-value tasks often missed by synthetic methods. Leveraging this dataset, we propose a Dual Mid-Training recipe that decouples spatial grounding from planning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on our proposed WebChainBench and other public GUI benchmarks. Our work provides the data and insights necessary to build and rigorously evaluate the next generation of scalable web agents.

AI.tldr: WebChain introduces a large open-source dataset of real-world website trajectories (31,725 trajectories, 318k steps) with a core Triple Alignment of visual, structural, and action data. It also presents a Dual Mid-Training recipe to decouple spatial grounding from planning, achieving state-of-the-art results on WebChainBench and public GUI benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To enable reproducible research and robust evaluation of web agents by providing real-world, multi-modal trajectory data that is not well captured by synthetic datasets.
AI.method: Data collection via a scalable pipeline yielding real-user-like trajectories with a Triple Alignment: visual, structural, and action data; a Dual Mid-Training recipe that separates spatial grounding from planning during model training.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on WebChainBench and other public GUI benchmarks; provides a large, multi-modal dataset to facilitate development and rigorous evaluation of web agents.
AI.conclusion: WebChain offers both the data and methodological insights needed to build and rigorously evaluate scalable web agents, advancing reproducible research in the domain.

title: Decoupling Task and Behavior: A Two-Stage Reward Curriculum in Reinforcement Learning for Robotics
summary: Deep Reinforcement Learning is a promising tool for robotic control, yet practical application is often hindered by the difficulty of designing effective reward functions. Real-world tasks typically require optimizing multiple objectives simultaneously, necessitating precise tuning of their weights to learn a policy with the desired characteristics. To address this, we propose a two-stage reward curriculum where we decouple task-specific objectives from behavioral terms. In our method, we first train the agent on a simplified task-only reward function to ensure effective exploration before introducing the full reward that includes auxiliary behavior-related terms such as energy efficiency. Further, we analyze various transition strategies and demonstrate that reusing samples between phases is critical for training stability. We validate our approach on the DeepMind Control Suite, ManiSkill3, and a mobile robot environment, modified to include auxiliary behavioral objectives. Our method proves to be simple yet effective, substantially outperforming baselines trained directly on the full reward while exhibiting higher robustness to specific reward weightings.

AI.tldr: Two-stage reward curriculum decouples task rewards from auxiliary behavioral terms, enabling better exploration and more stable learning in multi-objective RL. Sample reuse across stages improves training stability. Validated on DM Control Suite, ManiSkill3, and a mobile robot environment with auxiliary objectives; outperforms full-reward baselines and shows robustness to reward weightings.
AI.motivation: Reward shaping for multi-objective deep reinforcement learning is challenging; balancing task performance with auxiliary behavioral terms (e.g., energy efficiency) requires careful reward weight tuning, which hampers practical deployment in robotics.
AI.method: 1) Train with a simplified, task-only reward to promote exploration. 2) Introduce the full reward including auxiliary behavioral terms. 3) Explore different transition strategies between phases, finding sample reuse to be critical for stability. 4) Validate on DM Control Suite, ManiSkill3, and a mobile-robot environment modified with auxiliary objectives.
AI.result: The two-stage curriculum substantially outperforms baselines trained directly on the full reward, and exhibits greater robustness to variations in reward weights.
AI.conclusion: A simple two-stage reward curriculum with sample reuse provides a practical and effective approach to multi-objective RL in robotic control, reducing sensitivity to reward weightings and improving stability and performance.

title: Competitive Multi-Operator Reinforcement Learning for Joint Pricing and Fleet Rebalancing in AMoD Systems
summary: Autonomous Mobility-on-Demand (AMoD) systems promise to revolutionize urban transportation by providing affordable on-demand services to meet growing travel demand. However, realistic AMoD markets will be competitive, with multiple operators competing for passengers through strategic pricing and fleet deployment. While reinforcement learning has shown promise in optimizing single-operator AMoD control, existing work fails to capture competitive market dynamics. We investigate the impact of competition on policy learning by introducing a multi-operator reinforcement learning framework where two operators simultaneously learn pricing and fleet rebalancing policies. By integrating discrete choice theory, we enable passenger allocation and demand competition to emerge endogenously from utility-maximizing decisions. Experiments using real-world data from multiple cities demonstrate that competition fundamentally alters learned behaviors, leading to lower prices and distinct fleet positioning patterns compared to monopolistic settings. Notably, we demonstrate that learning-based approaches are robust to the additional stochasticity of competition, with competitive agents successfully converging to effective policies while accounting for partially unobserved competitor strategies.

AI.tldr: A two-operator RL framework for AMoD with endogenous competition via discrete choice, showing competition lowers prices and reshapes fleet positioning; robust to stochastic and partially observed rival behavior.
AI.motivation: To capture market dynamics absent in single-operator RL by modeling competition and passenger choice; evaluate how competition affects learned policies and performance in AMoD.
AI.method: Simultaneous training of two operators for pricing and fleet rebalancing; integrate discrete choice theory to let passengers allocate demand based on utilities; use real-city data and test robustness to rival strategies.
AI.result: Competition changes learned policies: lower prices, different fleet positioning; RL agents converge to effective policies even with competitive stochasticity and partial observability.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end multi-operator RL with discrete choice can reveal competitive dynamics in AMoD, informing policy design and deployment; future work could explore more operators, market dynamics, and scalability.

title: UniSTOK: Uniform Inductive Spatio-Temporal Kriging
summary: Spatio-temporal kriging aims to infer signals at unobserved locations from observed sensors and is critical to applications such as transportation and environmental monitoring. In practice, however, observed sensors themselves often exhibit heterogeneous missingness, forcing inductive kriging models to rely on crudely imputed inputs. This setting brings three key challenges: (1) it is unclear whether an value is a true signal or a missingness-induced artifact; (2) missingness is highly heterogeneous across sensors and time; (3) missing observations distort the local spatio-temporal structure. To address these issues, we propose Uniform Inductive Spatio-Temporal Kriging (UniSTOK), a plug-and-play framework that enhances existing inductive kriging backbones under missing observation. Our framework forms a dual-branch input consisting of the original observations and a jigsaw-augmented counterpart that synthesizes proxy signals only at missing entries. The two branches are then processed in parallel by a shared spatio-temporal backbone with explicit missingness mask modulation. Their outputs are finally adaptively fused via dual-channel attention. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets under diverse missing patterns demonstrate consistent and significant improvements.

AI.tldr: UniSTOK is a plug-and-play framework that improves inductive spatio-temporal kriging under missing data by using a dual-branch input (original observations and jigsaw-augmented proxies for missing entries) processed by a shared spatio-temporal backbone with missingness mask modulation, and fused via dual-channel attention.
AI.motivation: Observed sensors often have heterogeneous missingness which biases kriging; there is a need to distinguish true signals from missingness artifacts, cope with variable missingness, and preserve spatio-temporal structure for accurate inference.
AI.method: Two-branch input: (1) original observations; (2) jigsaw-augmented proxies synthesized only at missing entries. A shared spatio-temporal backbone processes both with explicit missingness mask modulation. Outputs are adaptively fused via dual-channel attention.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multiple real-world datasets with diverse missing patterns shows consistent and significant improvements over baselines.
AI.conclusion: UniSTOK provides a practical, plug-and-play enhancement for inductive spatio-temporal kriging under missing data by leveraging dual inputs and attention-based fusion, improving robustness to irregular missingness.

title: Act, Think or Abstain: Complexity-Aware Adaptive Inference for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Current research on Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models predominantly focuses on enhancing generalization through established reasoning techniques. While effective, these improvements invariably increase computational complexity and inference latency. Furthermore, these mechanisms are typically applied indiscriminately, resulting in the inefficient allocation of resources for trivial tasks while simultaneously failing to provide the uncertainty estimation necessary to prevent catastrophic failure on out-of-distribution tasks. Inspired by human cognition, we propose an adaptive framework that dynamically routes VLA execution based on the complexity of the perceived state. Our approach transforms the VLA's vision-language backbone into an active detection tool by projecting latent embeddings into an ensemble of parametric and non-parametric estimators. This allows the system to execute known tasks immediately (Act), reason about ambiguous scenarios (Think), and preemptively halt execution when encountering significant physical or semantic anomalies (Abstain). In our empirical analysis, we observe a phenomenon where visual embeddings alone are superior for inferring task complexity due to the semantic invariance of language. Evaluated on the LIBERO and LIBERO-PRO benchmarks as well as on a real robot, our vision-only configuration achieves 80% F1-Score using as little as 5% of training data, establishing itself as a reliable and efficient task complexity detector.

AI.tldr: An adaptive Vision-Language-Action framework dynamically routes execution (Act/Think/Abstain) by projecting latent embeddings into an ensemble of estimators to detect task complexity; vision alone serves as a strong complexity detector, enabling efficient, uncertainty-aware VLA on LIBERO benchmarks and a real robot.
AI.motivation: Address the high computational cost, latency and lack of uncertainty estimation in current VLA models; inspired by human cognition to route processing based on perceived state.
AI.method: Convert the VLA backbone into an active detection tool by projecting latent embeddings into an ensemble of parametric and non-parametric estimators; implement dynamic routing to Act (execute known tasks), Think (reason about ambiguity), and Abstain (halt on anomalies); observe that visual embeddings outperform language for complexity detection; validate on LIBERO, LIBERO-PRO, and a real robot; vision-only setup achieves high performance with scarce data.
AI.result: Vision-only configuration achieves ~80% F1 on LIBERO/LIBERO-PRO with only ~5% of training data, indicating strong efficiency and reliability as a task complexity detector; supports resource-efficient, uncertainty-aware VLA execution.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, perception-driven routing enables efficient resource allocation and uncertainty estimation in VLA systems; visual embeddings suffice for task complexity detection and the approach shows promise for real-world robotic applications.

title: CLIP-driven Zero-shot Learning with Ambiguous Labels
comment: Accepted by ICASSP 2026 (IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing)
summary: Zero-shot learning (ZSL) aims to recognize unseen classes by leveraging semantic information from seen classes, but most existing methods assume accurate class labels for training instances. However, in real-world scenarios, noise and ambiguous labels can significantly reduce the performance of ZSL. To address this, we propose a new CLIP-driven partial label zero-shot learning (CLIP-PZSL) framework to handle label ambiguity. First, we use CLIP to extract instance and label features. Then, a semantic mining block fuses these features to extract discriminative label embeddings. We also introduce a partial zero-shot loss, which assigns weights to candidate labels based on their relevance to the instance and aligns instance and label embeddings to minimize semantic mismatch. As the training goes on, the ground-truth labels are progressively identified, and the refined labels and label embeddings in turn help improve the semantic alignment of instance and label features. Comprehensive experiments on several datasets demonstrate the advantage of CLIP-PZSL.

AI.tldr: A CLIP-driven partial label zero-shot learning framework (CLIP-PZSL) to handle noisy/ambiguous labels in ZSL via semantic mining, a partial zero-shot loss, and iterative label refinement that improves instance–label alignment; experiments show advantages across datasets.
AI.motivation: Real-world ZSL is hampered by noisy/ambiguous training labels; existing methods assume clean labels and may misclassify, necessitating robust alignment under partial labeling.
AI.method: Leverage CLIP to extract features for instances and candidate labels; a semantic mining block fuses these features to produce discriminative label embeddings; introduce a partial zero-shot loss that weights candidate labels by their relevance to the instance and aligns instance and label embeddings to reduce semantic mismatch; during training, ground-truth labels are progressively identified, and refined labels/embeddings further improve alignment.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple datasets show CLIP-PZSL outperforms baselines, validating the effectiveness of semantic mining, partial loss, and progressive label refinement.
AI.conclusion: CLIP-PZSL effectively mitigates label ambiguity in zero-shot learning via progressive label refinement and semantic alignment, achieving strong empirical performance.

title: Non-Euclidean Gradient Descent Operates at the Edge of Stability
summary: The Edge of Stability (EoS) is a phenomenon where the sharpness (largest eigenvalue) of the Hessian converges to $2/η$ during training with gradient descent (GD) with a step-size $η$. Despite (apparently) violating classical smoothness assumptions, EoS has been widely observed in deep learning, but its theoretical foundations remain incomplete. We provide an interpretation of EoS through the lens of Directional Smoothness Mishkin et al. [2024]. This interpretation naturally extends to non-Euclidean norms, which we use to define generalized sharpness under an arbitrary norm. Our generalized sharpness measure includes previously studied vanilla GD and preconditioned GD as special cases, as well as methods for which EoS has not been studied, such as $\ell_{\infty}$-descent, Block CD, Spectral GD, and Muon without momentum. Through experiments on neural networks, we show that non-Euclidean GD with our generalized sharpness also exhibits progressive sharpening followed by oscillations around or above the threshold $2/η$. Practically, our framework provides a single, geometry-aware spectral measure that works across optimizers.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the Edge of Stability (EoS) concept by introducing a generalized sharpness under arbitrary norms via Directional Smoothness, unifying GD and non-Euclidean optimizers, and showing progressive sharpening with oscillations around the threshold 2/η; it provides a geometry-aware spectral measure applicable across optimizers and demonstrates empirical evidence on neural networks.
AI.motivation: To understand and unify EoS across various optimization dynamics and norms, enabling a single interpretive framework for sharpness that is robust to normalization and preconditioning.
AI.method: Define generalized sharpness under any norm, interpret EoS through Directional Smoothness, relate to Mishkin et al. 2024, show that vanilla GD, preconditioned GD, and other methods (ℓ∞-descent, Block CD, Spectral GD, Muon without momentum) fit into the framework, and validate with experiments on neural networks.
AI.result: A single, geometry-aware spectral measure that captures EoS behavior across optimizers; non-Euclidean GD exhibits progressive sharpening and oscillations around/above 2/η; special cases recover known results.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework unifies sharpness notions across optimizers, extends to non-Euclidean geometries, and provides a practical diagnostic tool for monitoring stability and step-size effects in training.

title: Building AI Coding Agents for the Terminal: Scaffolding, Harness, Context Engineering, and Lessons Learned
comment: Work in progress, new versions will be updated continuously
summary: The landscape of AI coding assistance is undergoing a fundamental shift from complex IDE plugins to versatile, terminal-native agents. Operating directly where developers manage source control, execute builds, and deploy environments, CLI-based agents offer unprecedented autonomy for long-horizon development tasks. In this paper, we present OPENDEV, an open-source, command-line coding agent engineered specifically for this new paradigm. Effective autonomous assistance requires strict safety controls and highly efficient context management to prevent context bloat and reasoning degradation. OPENDEV overcomes these challenges through a compound AI system architecture with workload-specialized model routing, a dual-agent architecture separating planning from execution, lazy tool discovery, and adaptive context compaction that progressively reduces older observations. Furthermore, it employs an automated memory system to accumulate project-specific knowledge across sessions and counteracts instruction fade-out through event-driven system reminders. By enforcing explicit reasoning phases and prioritizing context efficiency, OPENDEV provides a secure, extensible foundation for terminal-first AI assistance, offering a blueprint for robust autonomous software engineering.

AI.tldr: OPENDEV is an open-source CLI-based autonomous coding agent designed for terminal-native development, introducing safety- and context-efficient architecture (dual-agent planning/execution, workload routing, lazy tool discovery, adaptive context compaction, and cross-session memory) to enable secure long-horizon automated software engineering.
AI.motivation: Shift AI coding assistance from IDE plugins to terminal-native agents to support long-horizon development tasks with strong safety and efficient context management.
AI.method: Compound AI system architecture featuring: (1) workload-specialized model routing; (2) dual-agent architecture separating planning from execution; (3) lazy tool discovery; (4) adaptive context compaction that progressively prunes older observations; (5) automated, project-specific memory across sessions; (6) event-driven system reminders to counter instruction fade-out; (7) explicit reasoning phases to enforce disciplined AI behavior.
AI.result: Conceptual design and open-source blueprint of OPENDEV; no reported empirical evaluation in the abstract, but the paper articulates architectural mechanisms and implementation guidance for terminal-first autonomous software engineering.
AI.conclusion: OPENDEV offers a secure, extensible foundation for terminal-first AI assistance and provides a blueprint for robust autonomous software engineering.

title: Digital Twin Driven Textile Classification and Foreign Object Recognition in Automated Sorting Systems
comment: 10 pages,single column, 5 figures, preprint for Photomet Edumet 2026 (Klagenfurt, Austria)
summary: The increasing demand for sustainable textile recycling requires robust automation solutions capable of handling deformable garments and detecting foreign objects in cluttered environments. This work presents a digital twin driven robotic sorting system that integrates grasp prediction, multi modal perception, and semantic reasoning for real world textile classification. A dual arm robotic cell equipped with RGBD sensing, capacitive tactile feedback, and collision-aware motion planning autonomously separates garments from an unsorted basket, transfers them to an inspection zone, and classifies them using state of the art Visual Language Models (VLMs). We benchmark nine VLM s from five model families on a dataset of 223 inspection scenarios comprising shirts, socks, trousers, underwear, foreign objects (including garments outside of the aforementioned classes), and empty scenes. The evaluation assesses per class accuracy, hallucination behavior, and computational performance under practical hardware constraints. Results show that the Qwen model family achieves the highest overall accuracy (up to 87.9 %), with strong foreign object detection performance, while lighter models such as Gemma3 offer competitive speed accuracy trade offs for edge deployment. A digital twin combined with MoveIt enables collision aware path planning and integrates segmented 3D point clouds of inspected garments into the virtual environment for improved manipulation reliability. The presented system demonstrates the feasibility of combining semantic VLM reasoning with conventional grasp detection and digital twin technology for scalable, autonomous textile sorting in realistic industrial settings.

AI.tldr: A digital twin–driven autonomous textile sorting system that uses grasp prediction, multimodal perception, and Visual Language Models (VLMs) to classify garments; benchmarks nine VLMs on 223 inspection scenarios; Qwen yields top accuracy (~87.9%) with strong foreign-object detection; MoveIt-based planning and digital twin integration enable collision-aware manipulation; lighter models like Gemma3 offer favorable speed-accuracy trade-offs for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: To enable sustainable textile recycling by automating handling of deformable garments and detecting foreign objects in cluttered environments.
AI.method: A dual-arm robot with RGBD sensing, capacitive tactile feedback, and collision-aware motion planning autonomously gathers garments from an unsorted basket, moves them to an inspection zone, and classifies them using VLMs. A digital twin with MoveIt integrates segmented 3D garment point clouds into the virtual environment for improved manipulation. Nine VLMs from five families are benchmarked on 223 inspection scenarios (shirts, socks, trousers, underwear, foreign objects, and empty scenes). Evaluation focuses on per-class accuracy, hallucination behavior, and computational performance under practical hardware constraints.
AI.result: Qwen family achieves the highest overall accuracy up to 87.9%, with strong foreign-object detection. Lighter models like Gemma3 offer competitive speed-accuracy trade-offs for edge deployment. The digital twin plus MoveIt enables collision-aware path planning and seamless integration of 3D garment data into the virtual environment to improve manipulation reliability.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the feasibility of combining semantic VLM reasoning with traditional grasp detection and digital twin technology to enable scalable, autonomous textile sorting in realistic industrial settings.

title: A 360-degree Multi-camera System for Blue Emergency Light Detection Using Color Attention RT-DETR and the ABLDataset
comment: 16 pages, 17 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Vehicles
summary: This study presents an advanced system for detecting blue lights on emergency vehicles, developed using ABLDataset, a curated dataset that includes images of European emergency vehicles under various climatic and geographic conditions. The system employs a configuration of four fisheye cameras, each with a 180-degree horizontal field of view, mounted on the sides of the vehicle. A calibration process enables the azimuthal localization of the detections. Additionally, a comparative analysis of major deep neural network algorithms was conducted, including YOLO (v5, v8, and v10), RetinaNet, Faster R-CNN, and RT-DETR. RT-DETR was selected as the base model and enhanced through the incorporation of a color attention block, achieving an accuracy of 94.7 percent and a recall of 94.1 percent on the test set, with field test detections reaching up to 70 meters. Furthermore, the system estimates the approach angle of the emergency vehicle relative to the center of the car using geometric transformations. Designed for integration into a multimodal system that combines visual and acoustic data, this system has demonstrated high efficiency, offering a promising approach to enhancing Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and road safety.

AI.tldr: RT-DETR with color attention on a 4-camera 180° fisheye setup detects blue emergency-vehicle lights using the ABLDataset, achieving ~94.7% accuracy and 94.1% recall, with azimuth localization and a 70 m field-test range, plus geometric approach-angle estimation for multimodal ADAS integration.
AI.motivation: Robust, real-time recognition and localization of emergency-vehicle blue lights across diverse climates/geographies to improve road safety; current methods struggle with wide FOV, occlusion, and varying illumination; leveraging a European emergency-vehicle dataset and advanced detectors.
AI.method: Four 180° fisheye cameras mounted on the vehicle sides; azimuth calibration for localization; comprehensive comparison of DETR/YOLO RetinaNet Faster R-CNN; RT-DETR enhanced with a color attention block; training/testing on ABLDataset; geometric transforms to estimate approach angle; integration with visual+acoustic multimodal data.
AI.result: RT-DETR with color attention achieved 94.7% accuracy and 94.1% recall on the test set; field detections up to 70 meters; azimuth localization enabled; approach angle estimation; fits into a multimodal ADAS framework.
AI.conclusion: The approach is effective and promising for robust blue-light detection and localization, with potential real-world impact on ADAS and road safety; further work needed on generalization, speed/latency, and cross-domain validation.

title: Poisoning the Inner Prediction Logic of Graph Neural Networks for Clean-Label Backdoor Attacks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have achieved remarkable results in various tasks. Recent studies reveal that graph backdoor attacks can poison the GNN model to predict test nodes with triggers attached as the target class. However, apart from injecting triggers to training nodes, these graph backdoor attacks generally require altering the labels of trigger-attached training nodes into the target class, which is impractical in real-world scenarios. In this work, we focus on the clean-label graph backdoor attack, a realistic but understudied topic where training labels are not modifiable. According to our preliminary analysis, existing graph backdoor attacks generally fail under the clean-label setting. Our further analysis identifies that the core failure of existing methods lies in their inability to poison the prediction logic of GNN models, leading to the triggers being deemed unimportant for prediction. Therefore, we study a novel problem of effective clean-label graph backdoor attacks by poisoning the inner prediction logic of GNN models. We propose BA-Logic to solve the problem by coordinating a poisoned node selector and a logic-poisoning trigger generator. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that our method effectively enhances the attack success rate and surpasses state-of-the-art graph backdoor attack competitors under clean-label settings. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/BA-Logic

AI.tldr: BA-Logic introduces a clean-label graph backdoor attack by poisoning the GNN's inner prediction logic via a poisoned-node selector and a logic-poisoning trigger generator, achieving higher attack success under clean-label constraints than prior methods.
AI.motivation: In real-world scenarios, training labels for trigger-attached nodes cannot be modified; existing graph backdoor attacks rely on label changes and fail under clean-label conditions. A method that corrupts the model's decision logic is needed to make triggers influential without altering labels.
AI.method: Propose a two-part framework: (1) a poisoned node selector to identify and select nodes for poisoning without label changes, and (2) a logic-poisoning trigger generator to craft triggers that manipulatively alter the GNN's internal prediction logic. The two components are trained jointly to maximize target misclassification while preserving clean-label integrity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets show BA-Logic achieves higher attack success rates and outperforms state-of-the-art graph backdoor attackers under clean-label settings; code is released.
AI.conclusion: Poisoning the inner prediction logic is an effective strategy for clean-label graph backdoors, and BA-Logic provides a practical, stronger approach than existing methods, underscoring security risks in settings where training labels cannot be modified.

title: Ailed: A Psyche-Driven Chess Engine with Dynamic Emotional Modulation
comment: 27 pages, 8 figures, 11 tables. Open source: https://github.com/chrnx-dev/ailed-chess
summary:
Chess engines passed human strength years ago, but they still don't play like humans. A grandmaster under clock pressure blunders in ways a club player on a hot streak never would. Conventional engines capture none of this.
  This paper proposes a personality x psyche decomposition to produce behavioral variability in chess play, drawing on patterns observed in human games. Personality is static -- a preset that pins down the engine's character. Psyche is dynamic -- a bounded scalar ψ_t \in [-100, +100], recomputed from five positional factors after every move. These two components feed into an audio-inspired signal chain (noise gate, compressor/expander, five-band equalizer, saturation limiter) that reshapes move probability distributions on the fly. The chain doesn't care what engine sits behind it: any system that outputs move probabilities will do. It needs no search and carries no state beyond ψ_t.
  I test the framework across 12,414 games against Maia2-1100, feeding it two probability sources that differ by ~2,800x in training data. Both show the same monotonic gradient in top-move agreement (~20-25 pp spread from stress to overconfidence), which tells us the behavioral variation comes from the signal chain, not from the model underneath. When the psyche runs overconfident, the chain mostly gets out of the way (66% agreement with vanilla Maia2). Under stress, the competitive score falls from 50.8% to 30.1%. The patterns are reminiscent of tilt and overconfidence as described in human play, but I should be upfront: this study includes no human-subject validation.

AI.tldr: A modular, audio-inspired signal chain modulates chess engines' move probabilities via a static personality and a dynamic psyche, producing human-like behavioral variability without changing the underlying model.
AI.motivation: To capture human-like variability such as tilt, overconfidence, and stress responses in chess play, which conventional engines underperform at modeling, by introducing an architecture-agnostic variability mechanism.
AI.method: Introduce two components: a static personality preset (fixed) and a dynamic psyche ψ_t ∈ [-100,100], updated after each move from five positional features. These feed a signal chain (noise gate, compressor/expander, five-band EQ, saturation limiter) that reshapes move probability distributions; the chain is model-agnostic and requires no search or persistent state beyond ψ_t.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 12,414 games against Maia2-1100 using two probability sources with ~2800x data difference shows a monotonic gradient in top-move agreement (~20-25 percentage points) from stress to overconfidence. When psyche is overconfident, chain largely defers to vanilla Maia2 (66% agreement). Under stress, competitive score drops from 50.8% to 30.1%. Findings align with tilt/overconfidence patterns, but no human-subject validation is included.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that behavioral variability akin to human play can be induced by a modular signal chain around any move-probability source, independent of the underlying model, though validation with human subjects is needed to confirm behavioral fidelity.

title: Planning in 8 Tokens: A Compact Discrete Tokenizer for Latent World Model
summary: World models provide a powerful framework for simulating environment dynamics conditioned on actions or instructions, enabling downstream tasks such as action planning or policy learning. Recent approaches leverage world models as learned simulators, but its application to decision-time planning remains computationally prohibitive for real-time control. A key bottleneck lies in latent representations: conventional tokenizers encode each observation into hundreds of tokens, making planning both slow and resource-intensive. To address this, we propose CompACT, a discrete tokenizer that compresses each observation into as few as 8 tokens, drastically reducing computational cost while preserving essential information for planning. An action-conditioned world model that occupies CompACT tokenizer achieves competitive planning performance with orders-of-magnitude faster planning, offering a practical step toward real-world deployment of world models.

AI.tldr: CompACT: a discrete tokenizer compresses observations to ~8 tokens for faster action-conditioned world-model planning, achieving competitive planning performance with orders of magnitude faster planning.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational bottleneck in world-model-based planning by compressing latent representations, enabling real-time decision-time planning.
AI.method: Introduce CompACT, a discrete tokenizer that encodes each observation into as few as 8 tokens and integrate it with an action-conditioned world model. Evaluate planning speed and planning quality against baselines.
AI.result: Planning with the CompACT-based world model is orders of magnitude faster while maintaining competitive planning performance compared with higher-token baselines.
AI.conclusion: Discrete, highly compact tokenization can enable real-time planning with world-models without substantial loss in planning effectiveness, advancing practical deployment.

title: MI-DETR: A Strong Baseline for Moving Infrared Small Target Detection with Bio-Inspired Motion Integration
summary: Infrared small target detection (ISTD) is challenging because tiny, low-contrast targets are easily obscured by complex and dynamic backgrounds. Conventional multi-frame approaches typically learn motion implicitly through deep neural networks, often requiring additional motion supervision or explicit alignment modules. We propose Motion Integration DETR (MI-DETR), a bio-inspired dual-pathway detector that processes one infrared frame per time step while explicitly modeling motion. First, a retina-inspired cellular automaton (RCA) converts raw frame sequences into a motion map defined on the same pixel grid as the appearance image, enabling parvocellular-like appearance and magnocellular-like motion pathways to be supervised by a single set of bounding boxes without extra motion labels or alignment operations. Second, a Parvocellular-Magnocellular Interconnection (PMI) Block facilitates bidirectional feature interaction between the two pathways, providing a biologically motivated intermediate interconnection mechanism. Finally, a RT-DETR decoder operates on features from the two pathways to produce detection results. Surprisingly, our proposed simple yet effective approach yields strong performance on three commonly used ISTD benchmarks. MI-DETR achieves 70.3% mAP@50 and 72.7% F1 on IRDST-H (+26.35 mAP@50 over the best multi-frame baseline), 98.0% mAP@50 on DAUB-R, and 88.3% mAP@50 on ITSDT-15K, demonstrating the effectiveness of biologically inspired motion-appearance integration. Code is available at https://github.com/nliu-25/MI-DETR.

AI.tldr: MI-DETR is a dual-pathway detector for infrared small target detection that jointly models appearance and motion via a retina-inspired cellular automaton motion map and a bidirectional Parvocellular-Magnocellular interconnection, decoded by an RT-DETR to yield state-of-the-art results without extra motion supervision.
AI.motivation: ISTD is challenging due to tiny, low-contrast targets embedded in complex, dynamic backgrounds. Conventional multi-frame approaches often learn motion implicitly and may require additional motion supervision or explicit alignment modules.
AI.method: Proposes two parallel pathways: a parvocellular-like appearance path and a magnocellular-like motion path. A retina-inspired cellular automaton (RCA) converts frame sequences into a motion map on the pixel grid, enabling supervision by a single set of bounding boxes without extra motion labels or alignment. A Parvocellular-Magnocellular Interconnection (PMI) Block enables bidirectional feature interaction between the two pathways. Features from both are fed to an RT-DETR decoder to produce detections, trained with standard bounding boxes only.
AI.result: On IRDST-H: 70.3% mAP@50 and 72.7% F1 (with +26.35 mAP@50 over the best multi-frame baseline). On DAUB-R: 98.0% mAP@50. On ITSDT-15K: 88.3% mAP@50.
AI.conclusion: Biologically inspired motion-appearance integration via RCA and PMI with an RT-DETR decoder yields strong ISTD performance across three benchmarks and eliminates the need for explicit motion supervision or alignment modules; code is available.

title: MCEL: Margin-Based Cross-Entropy Loss for Error-Tolerant Quantized Neural Networks
summary: Robustness to bit errors is a key requirement for the reliable use of neural networks (NNs) on emerging approximate computing platforms and error-prone memory technologies. A common approach to achieve bit error tolerance in NNs is injecting bit flips during training according to a predefined error model. While effective in certain scenarios, training-time bit flip injection introduces substantial computational overhead, often degrades inference accuracy at high error rates, and scales poorly for larger NN architectures. These limitations make error injection an increasingly impractical solution for ensuring robustness on future approximate computing platforms and error-prone memory technologies. In this work, we investigate the mechanisms that enable NNs to tolerate bit errors without relying on error-aware training. We establish a direct connection between bit error tolerance and classification margins at the output layer. Building on this insight, we propose a novel loss function, the Margin Cross-Entropy Loss (MCEL), which explicitly promotes logit-level margin separation while preserving the favorable optimization properties of the standard cross-entropy loss. Furthermore, MCEL introduces an interpretable margin parameter that allows robustness to be tuned in a principled manner. Extensive experimental evaluations across multiple datasets of varying complexity, diverse NN architectures, and a range of quantization schemes demonstrate that MCEL substantially improves bit error tolerance, up to 15 % in accuracy for an error rate of 1 %. Our proposed MCEL method is simple to implement, efficient, and can be integrated as a drop-in replacement for standard CEL. It provides a scalable and principled alternative to training-time bit flip injection, offering new insights into the origins of NN robustness and enabling more efficient deployment on approximate computing and memory systems.

AI.tldr: Introduces Margin Cross-Entropy Loss (MCEL) to boost neural network bit-error tolerance without error-aware training by promoting larger output margins; achieves up to 15% accuracy gains at 1% error and can replace standard cross-entropy.
AI.motivation: Bit errors in memory/approximate hardware degrade NN performance. Training-time error-injection is costly, scales poorly, and may hurt accuracy; a training-free, principled robustness mechanism is needed.
AI.method: Define MCEL to explicitly promote logit-level margin separation while preserving standard cross-entropy optimization properties. Include an interpretable, tunable margin parameter. Can be used as a drop-in replacement for CEL.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across datasets, architectures, and quantization schemes show substantial improvements in bit-error tolerance, up to 15% accuracy at 1% error rate. MCEL is simple, efficient, and scalable, offering a practical alternative to training-time bit-flip injection.
AI.conclusion: MCEL provides a principled, scalable mechanism for NN robustness to bit errors, enabling more efficient deployment on approximate computing platforms. It deepens understanding of margin-based robustness and invites further theoretical and empirical exploration.

title: PACE: A Personalized Adaptive Curriculum Engine for 9-1-1 Call-taker Training
summary: 9-1-1 call-taking training requires mastery of over a thousand interdependent skills, covering diverse incident types and protocol-specific nuances. A nationwide labor shortage is already straining training capacity, but effective instruction still demands that trainers tailor objectives to each trainee's evolving competencies. This personalization burden is one that current practice cannot scale. Partnering with Metro Nashville Department of Emergency Communications (MNDEC), we propose PACE (Personalized Adaptive Curriculum Engine), a co-pilot system that augments trainer decision-making by (1) maintaining probabilistic beliefs over trainee skill states, (2) modeling individual learning and forgetting dynamics, and (3) recommending training scenarios that balance acquisition of new competencies with retention of existing ones. PACE propagates evidence over a structured skill graph to accelerate diagnostic coverage and applies contextual bandits to select scenarios that target gaps the trainee is prepared to address. Empirical results show that PACE achieves 19.50% faster time-to-competence and 10.95% higher terminal mastery compared to state-of-the-art frameworks. Co-pilot studies with practicing training officers further demonstrate a 95.45% alignment rate between PACE's and experts' pedagogical judgments on real-world cases. Under estimation, PACE cuts turnaround time to merely 34 seconds from 11.58 minutes, up to 95.08% reduction.

AI.tldr: PACE is a co-pilot system that personalizes 9-1-1 call-taking training by modeling trainee skill states, learning/forgetting dynamics, and recommending targeted scenarios via contextual bandits.
AI.motivation: To address a nationwide labor shortage and training bottlenecks by enabling scalable, personalized training that aligns with evolving trainee competencies.
AI.method: Maintain probabilistic beliefs over trainee skills; model learning/forgetting dynamics; use a structured skill graph to propagate evidence; apply contextual bandits to select training scenarios that balance acquisition and retention.
AI.result: PACE yields 19.50% faster time-to-competence, 10.95% higher terminal mastery versus state-of-the-art; 95.45% alignment with experts on real cases; under estimation, reduces turnaround from 11.58 minutes to 34 seconds (up to 95.08% reduction).
AI.conclusion: PACE demonstrates substantial efficiency and efficacy gains in personalized emergency communications training, suggesting scalable impact for workforce development and improved diagnostic coverage.

title: UniM: A Unified Any-to-Any Interleaved Multimodal Benchmark
summary: In real-world multimodal applications, systems usually need to comprehend arbitrarily combined and interleaved multimodal inputs from users, while also generating outputs in any interleaved multimedia form. This capability defines the goal of any-to-any interleaved multimodal learning under a unified paradigm of understanding and generation, posing new challenges and opportunities for advancing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). To foster and benchmark this capability, this paper introduces the UniM benchmark, the first Unified Any-to-Any Interleaved Multimodal dataset. UniM contains 31K high-quality instances across 30 domains and 7 representative modalities: text, image, audio, video, document, code, and 3D, each requiring multiple intertwined reasoning and generation capabilities. We further introduce the UniM Evaluation Suite, which assesses models along three dimensions: Semantic Correctness & Generation Quality, Response Structure Integrity, and Interleaved Coherence. In addition, we propose UniMA, an agentic baseline model equipped with traceable reasoning for structured interleaved generation. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the difficulty of UniM and highlight key challenges and directions for advancing unified any-to-any multimodal intelligence. The project page is https://any2any-mllm.github.io/unim.

AI.tldr: Introduces UniM benchmark and UniMA baseline for unified any-to-any interleaved multimodal learning, featuring a 31K-instance dataset across 7 modalities and a three-dimension evaluation suite; demonstrates the task's difficulty and outlines directions for future research.
AI.motivation: To address real-world needs where multimodal inputs/outputs are arbitrarily interleaved, requiring unified understanding and generation across diverse modalities; existing benchmarks/models are insufficient for this broad capability.
AI.method: Construct UniM dataset (31K samples, 30 domains, 7 modalities: text, image, audio, video, document, code, 3D); develop UniM Evaluation Suite with three axes—Semantic Correctness & Generation Quality, Response Structure Integrity, Interleaved Coherence; propose UniMA, an agentic baseline with traceable reasoning for structured interleaved generation.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate UniM is challenging; models exhibit substantial gaps in semantic accuracy, structure maintenance, and coherence across interleaved streams; UniMA provides a plausible baseline with directive traceability and highlights actionable directions for improving unified multimodal models.
AI.conclusion: UniM establishes a unified framework and dataset for evaluating any-to-any interleaved multimodal intelligence, revealing key challenges and guiding future research toward more capable, traceable, and coherent multimodal LLMs.

title: Asymptotic Behavior of Multi--Task Learning: Implicit Regularization and Double Descent Effects
summary: Multi--task learning seeks to improve the generalization error by leveraging the common information shared by multiple related tasks. One challenge in multi--task learning is identifying formulations capable of uncovering the common information shared between different but related tasks. This paper provides a precise asymptotic analysis of a popular multi--task formulation associated with misspecified perceptron learning models. The main contribution of this paper is to precisely determine the reasons behind the benefits gained from combining multiple related tasks. Specifically, we show that combining multiple tasks is asymptotically equivalent to a traditional formulation with additional regularization terms that help improve the generalization performance. Another contribution is to empirically study the impact of combining tasks on the generalization error. In particular, we empirically show that the combination of multiple tasks postpones the double descent phenomenon and can mitigate it asymptotically.

AI.tldr: In multi-task learning, combining related tasks yields improvements that are asymptotically equivalent to adding regularization in a single-task formulation; this explains the generalization gains and indicates the method can delay or mitigate double descent.
AI.motivation: To uncover why multi-task learning helps generalization, by analyzing misspecified perceptron-based formulations and deriving asymptotic equivalence to regularized single-task learning.
AI.method: Perform precise asymptotic analysis of a popular multi-task formulation under misspecification; derive equivalence to a traditional formulation with extra regularization terms; conduct empirical studies on generalization and double descent.
AI.result: Asymptotically, the multi-task formulation behaves like a regularized single-task model; the added regularization terms explain the generalization gains. Empirical results show that task combination postpones and can mitigate double descent.
AI.conclusion: The benefits of multitask learning originate from implicit regularization; appropriate task sharing acts as a regularizer, and combining tasks improves generalization and can address double descent.

title: Legal interpretation and AI: from expert systems to argumentation and LLMs
summary: AI and Law research has encountered legal interpretation in different ways, in the context of its evolving approaches and methodologies. Research on expert system has focused on legal knowledge engineering, with the goal of ensuring that human-generated interpretations can be precisely transferred into knowledge-bases, to be consistently applied. Research on argumentation has aimed at representing the structure of interpretive arguments, as well as their dialectical interactions, to assess of the acceptability of interpretive claims within argumentation frameworks. Research on machine learning has focused on the automated generation of interpretive suggestions and arguments, through general and specialised language models, now being increasingly deployed in legal practice.

AI.tldr: AI & Law studies legal interpretation through three strands: expert systems for knowledge transfer, argumentation for structuring/evaluating interpretive claims, and ML for generating interpretive suggestions.
AI.motivation: To address the complexity of legal interpretation across evolving approaches, aiming for consistency, transparency, and practical deployment of interpretive tools in legal practice.
AI.method: 1) Expert systems and legal knowledge engineering to codify human interpretations into knowledge bases; 2) Formal argumentation to model interpretive argument structures and dialectical interactions within argumentation frameworks; 3) Machine learning, via general and specialized language models, to automate generation of interpretive suggestions and arguments.
AI.result: No empirical results are reported; the abstract surveys the landscape, identifying three modalities (knowledge engineering, argumentation, ML) and their emerging roles in practice.
AI.conclusion: AI & Law interpretation is a multi-faceted field combining knowledge engineering, argumentation theory, and ML, with ML-driven practice becoming increasingly prevalent in legal practice.

title: MoRe: Motion-aware Feed-forward 4D Reconstruction Transformer
summary: Reconstructing dynamic 4D scenes remains challenging due to the presence of moving objects that corrupt camera pose estimation. Existing optimization methods alleviate this issue with additional supervision, but they are mostly computationally expensive and impractical in real-time applications. To address these limitations, we propose MoRe, a feedforward 4D reconstruction network that efficiently recovers dynamic 3D scenes from monocular videos. Built upon a strong static reconstruction backbone, MoRe employs an attention-forcing strategy to disentangle dynamic motion from static structure. To further enhance robustness, we fine-tune the model on large-scale, diverse datasets encompassing both dynamic and static scenes. Moreover, our grouped causal attention captures temporal dependencies and adapts to varying token lengths across frames, ensuring temporally coherent geometry reconstruction. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that MoRe achieves high-quality dynamic reconstructions with exceptional efficiency.

AI.tldr: MoRe is a fast, feedforward 4D reconstruction network that reconstructs dynamic 3D scenes from monocular video by disentangling motion from static structure with attention, fine-tuning on diverse data, and using grouped causal attention for temporal coherence.
AI.motivation: Dynamic objects contaminate pose estimation in 4D reconstruction, and optimization-based solutions are computationally expensive and often impractical for real-time use, creating a need for a fast, robust alternative.
AI.method: MoRe builds on a strong static-reconstruction backbone and introduces an attention-forcing strategy to separate dynamic motion from static structure. It is fine-tuned on large-scale, diverse datasets that include dynamic and static scenes. Grouped causal attention captures temporal dependencies and adapts to varying token lengths across frames to ensure temporally coherent geometry.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations across multiple benchmarks show that MoRe achieves high-quality dynamic reconstructions with exceptional efficiency, outperforming or matching state-of-the-art methods in both quality and speed.
AI.conclusion: MoRe enables real-time or near-real-time dynamic 4D reconstruction by leveraging attention-based motion disentanglement, large-scale fine-tuning, and temporally coherent grouped causal attention.

title: Deep Learning-Driven Friendly Jamming for Secure Multicarrier ISAC Under Channel Uncertainty
summary: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems promise efficient spectrum utilization by jointly supporting radar sensing and wireless communication. This paper presents a deep learning-driven framework for enhancing physical-layer security in multicarrier ISAC systems under imperfect channel state information (CSI) and in the presence of unknown eavesdropper (Eve) locations. Unlike conventional ISAC-based friendly jamming (FJ) approaches that require Eve's CSI or precise angle-of-arrival (AoA) estimates, our method exploits radar echo feedback to guide directional jamming without explicit Eve's information. To enhance robustness to radar sensing uncertainty, we propose a radar-aware neural network that jointly optimizes beamforming and jamming by integrating a novel nonparametric Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) estimator based on f-divergence. The jamming design satisfies the Cramer-Rao lower bound (CRLB) constraints even in the presence of noisy AoA. For efficient implementation, we introduce a quantized tensor train-based encoder that reduces the model size by more than 100 times with negligible performance loss. We also integrate a non-overlapping secure scheme into the proposed framework, in which specific sub-bands can be dedicated solely to communication. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the proposed solution achieves significant improvements in secrecy rate, reduced block error rate (BLER), and strong robustness against CSI uncertainty and angular estimation errors, underscoring the effectiveness of the proposed deep learning-driven friendly jamming framework under practical ISAC impairments.

AI.tldr: A DL-driven ISAC framework enhances physical-layer security under imperfect CSI and unknown Eve, using radar echo feedback for directional jamming, a radar-aware NN with a novel FIM estimator from f-divergence, CRLB-consistent robust design, and a quantized tensor-train encoder for >100x compression; plus a non-overlapping secure sub-band scheme. Results show improved secrecy rate, lower BLER, and robustness.
AI.motivation: ISAC systems require security without Eve's CSI/AoA and must be robust to radar sensing uncertainty; there is a need for efficient, compressed DL models that jointly optimize sensing, communication, and jamming under practical impairments.
AI.method: A radar-aware neural network jointly optimizes beamforming and jamming; introduces a nonparametric Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) estimator based on f-divergence; enforces CRLB constraints under noisy AoA; leverages radar echo feedback to guide jamming without explicit Eve information; uses a quantized tensor train encoder to dramatically reduce model size; integrates a non-overlapping secure scheme with dedicated sub-bands for communication.
AI.result: Simulations indicate significant improvements in secrecy rate and reduction of block error rate (BLER); enhanced robustness to CSI uncertainty and angular estimation errors; effective under practical ISAC impairments; model compression achieves >100x size reduction with negligible performance loss.
