AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates the viability of deep learning–driven friendly jamming in multicarrier ISAC with imperfect CSI and unknown Eve locations, offering robust performance and scalable implementation; future work could address real-world validation, complexity analysis, and extension to broader ISAC scenarios.

title: Judge Reliability Harness: Stress Testing the Reliability of LLM Judges
comment: Accepted at Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond Workshop at ICLR 2026 - April 26, 2026, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
summary: We present the Judge Reliability Harness, an open source library for constructing validation suites that test the reliability of LLM judges. As LLM based scoring is widely deployed in AI benchmarks, more tooling is needed to efficiently assess the reliability of these methods. Given a benchmark dataset and an LLM judge configuration, the harness generates reliability tests that evaluate both binary judgment accuracy and ordinal grading performance for free-response and agentic task formats. We evaluate four state-of-the-art judges across four benchmarks spanning safety, persuasion, misuse, and agentic behavior, and find meaningful variation in performance across models and perturbation types, highlighting opportunities to improve the robustness of LLM judges. No judge that we evaluated is uniformly reliable across benchmarks using our harness. For example, our preliminary experiments on judges revealed consistency issues as measured by accuracy in judging another LLM's ability to complete a task due to simple text formatting changes, paraphrasing, changes in verbosity, and flipping the ground truth label in LLM-produced responses. The code for this tool is available at: https://github.com/RANDCorporation/judge-reliability-harness

AI.tldr: An open-source harness (Judge Reliability Harness) to test LLM judges' reliability by generating reliability tests for binary judgments and ordinal grading across free-response and agentic tasks; evaluation across four judges and four benchmarks shows significant variation and lack of uniform reliability; formatting and paraphrasing changes can flip ground-truth labels; code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: As LLM-based scoring is widely deployed in AI benchmarks, there is a need to systematically assess and improve the robustness of judgments produced by LLM judges.
AI.method: Given a benchmark dataset and an LLM judge configuration, the harness creates reliability tests for binary judgments and ordinal grading across task formats; evaluate four state-of-the-art judges on four benchmarks spanning safety, persuasion, misuse, and agentic behavior; analyze perturbations and consistency.
AI.result: Found meaningful variation in performance across models and perturbation types; no judge uniformly reliable across benchmarks; preliminary experiments show consistency issues due to simple changes in text formatting, paraphrasing, verbosity, and ground-truth label flips.
AI.conclusion: The robustness of LLM judges remains limited; the harness can reveal reliability gaps and guide improvements; code is publicly available.

title: Orthogonal Spatial-temporal Distributional Transfer for 4D Generation
summary: In the AIGC era, generating high-quality 4D content has garnered increasing research attention. Unfortunately, current 4D synthesis research is severely constrained by the lack of large-scale 4D datasets, preventing models from adequately learning the critical spatial-temporal features necessary for high-quality 4D generation, thus hindering progress in this domain. To combat this, we propose a novel framework that transfers rich spatial priors from existing 3D diffusion models and temporal priors from video diffusion models to enhance 4D synthesis. We develop a spatial-temporal-disentangled 4D (STD-4D) Diffusion model, which synthesizes 4D-aware videos through disentangled spatial and temporal latents. To facilitate the best feature transfer, we design a novel Orthogonal Spatial-temporal Distributional Transfer (Orster) mechanism, where the spatiotemporal feature distributions are carefully modeled and injected into the STD-4D Diffusion. Furthermore, during the 4D construction, we devise a spatial-temporal-aware HexPlane (ST-HexPlane) to integrate the transferred spatiotemporal features, thereby improving 4D deformation and 4D Gaussian feature modeling. Experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing approaches, achieving superior spatial-temporal consistency and higher-quality 4D synthesis.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based 4D synthesis framework (STD-4D) that transfers spatial priors from 3D diffusion models and temporal priors from video diffusion models via Orster distributional transfer and ST-HexPlane integration to achieve higher-quality, spatial-temporally consistent 4D content.
AI.motivation: 4D synthesis is hampered by a lack of large-scale 4D datasets. The work leverages abundant 3D and video priors to compensate for data scarcity, enabling learning of essential spatial-temporal features through disentangled latents.
AI.method: Introduce STD-4D diffusion model with spatial–temporal latent disentanglement. Propose Orster (Orthogonal Spatial-temporal Distributional Transfer) to model/inject spatiotemporal feature distributions. Develop ST-HexPlane to integrate transferred features into 4D representations for improved deformation and Gaussian feature modeling.
AI.result: Experiments show superior spatial-temporal consistency and higher-quality 4D synthesis compared with existing methods. Ablation studies indicate that both Orster and ST-HexPlane contribute significantly to performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively transfers priors from 3D and video diffusion to 4D synthesis, delivering high-quality, temporally coherent 4D content and enabling more data-efficient learning in 4D domains.

title: Reward-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning
summary: RL agents are typically trained under a single, fixed reward function, which makes them brittle to reward misspecification and limits their ability to adapt to changing task preferences. We introduce Reward-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (RCRL), a framework that trains a single agent to optimize a family of reward specifications while collecting experience under only one nominal objective. RCRL conditions the agent on reward parameterizations and learns multiple reward objectives from a shared replay data entirely off-policy, enabling a single policy to represent reward-specific behaviors. Across single-task, multi-task, and vision-based benchmarks, we show that RCRL not only improves performance under the nominal reward parameterization, but also enables efficient adaptation to new parameterizations. Our results demonstrate that RCRL provides a scalable mechanism for learning robust, steerable policies without sacrificing the simplicity of single-task training.

AI.tldr: RCRL trains one policy conditioned on reward parameters, learning multiple reward objectives from shared off-policy data collected under a nominal objective, enabling robust, steerable behavior across reward specifications.
AI.motivation: Reward misspecification makes RL policies brittle and hard to adapt; a single policy capable of handling multiple reward preferences would improve robustness and adaptability without retraining for each reward function.
AI.method: Propose Reward-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (RCRL): train a single agent conditioned on reward parameterizations; collect experience under a nominal objective; learn multiple reward objectives from a shared replay data entirely off-policy, yielding a single policy that can realize reward-specific behaviors.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across single-task, multi-task, and vision-based benchmarks shows that RCRL improves performance under the nominal reward and enables efficient adaptation to new reward parameterizations, without sacrificing the simplicity of single-task training.
AI.conclusion: RCRL offers a scalable framework for creating robust, steerable policies by conditioning on reward parameters and leveraging off-policy learning on shared data, maintaining the simplicity of single-task training.

title: Dissociating Direct Access from Inference in AI Introspection
summary: Introspection is a foundational cognitive ability, but its mechanism is not well understood. Recent work has shown that AI models can introspect. We study their mechanism of introspection, first extensively replicating Lindsey et al. (2025)'s thought injection detection paradigm in large open-source models. We show that these models detect injected representations via two separable mechanisms: (i) probability-matching (inferring from perceived anomaly of the prompt) and (ii) direct access to internal states. The direct access mechanism is content-agnostic: models detect that an anomaly occurred but cannot reliably identify its semantic content. The two model classes we study confabulate injected concepts that are high-frequency and concrete (e.g., "apple'"); for them correct concept guesses typically require significantly more tokens. This content-agnostic introspective mechanism is consistent with leading theories in philosophy and psychology.

AI.tldr: AI models exhibit two separable introspection mechanisms during thought-injection detection: probability-matching (inferring prompt anomaly) and direct access to internal states; the latter is content-agnostic. Models confabulate high-frequency, concrete injected concepts, and correct guesses require more tokens. This supports content-agnostic introspection theories in cognitive science.
AI.motivation: Clarify the mechanism of machine introspection by replicating an established thought-injection paradigm in large open-source models and disentangling content-agnostic versus content-based detection.
AI.method: Replicate Lindsey et al. (2025) thought-injection detection paradigm in large open-source models; analyze signals to distinguish probability-matching from direct internal-state access; categorize model behavior, examine injected concepts, and measure token thresholds for correct inference.
AI.result: Two separable mechanisms observed: (i) probability-matching to perceived prompt anomaly; (ii) direct access to internal states. Direct access is content-agnostic and cannot reliably identify semantic content. Two model classes confabulate injected concepts that are high-frequency and concrete; correct concept guesses require significantly more tokens.
AI.conclusion: Supports a dual-mechanism view of introspection: a content-agnostic internal-state monitoring process alongside a content-based inference path. Aligns with philosophy/psychology theories and has implications for interpretability and AI alignment.

title: GEM-TFL: Bridging Weak and Full Supervision for Forgery Localization through EM-Guided Decomposition and Temporal Refinement
summary: Temporal Forgery Localization (TFL) aims to precisely identify manipulated segments within videos or audio streams, providing interpretable evidence for multimedia forensics and security. While most existing TFL methods rely on dense frame-level labels in a fully supervised manner, Weakly Supervised TFL (WS-TFL) reduces labeling cost by learning only from binary video-level labels. However, current WS-TFL approaches suffer from mismatched training and inference objectives, limited supervision from binary labels, gradient blockage caused by non-differentiable top-k aggregation, and the absence of explicit modeling of inter-proposal relationships. To address these issues, we propose GEM-TFL (Graph-based EM-powered Temporal Forgery Localization), a two-phase classification-regression framework that effectively bridges the supervision gap between training and inference. Built upon this foundation, (1) we enhance weak supervision by reformulating binary labels into multi-dimensional latent attributes through an EM-based optimization process; (2) we introduce a training-free temporal consistency refinement that realigns frame-level predictions for smoother temporal dynamics; and (3) we design a graph-based proposal refinement module that models temporal-semantic relationships among proposals for globally consistent confidence estimation. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that GEM-TFL achieves more accurate and robust temporal forgery localization, substantially narrowing the gap with fully supervised methods.

AI.tldr: GEM-TFL is a weakly supervised temporal forgery localization approach that uses an EM-based latent attribute reformulation of video-level labels, a training-free temporal consistency refinement, and a graph-based proposal refinement to bridge training/inference disparities and model inter-proposal relations, achieving closer performance to fully supervised methods.
AI.motivation: Weakly supervised temporal forgery localization suffers from misaligned training/inference objectives, limited signal from binary labels, non-differentiable top-k aggregation, and no explicit modeling of inter-proposal relationships. The work aims to bridge these gaps.
AI.method: A two-phase classification-regression framework. (1) EM-based optimization converts binary video-level labels into multi-dimensional latent attributes to enhance weak supervision. (2) A training-free temporal consistency refinement realigns frame-level predictions for smoother dynamics. (3) A graph-based proposal refinement module models temporal-semantic relationships among proposals for globally consistent confidence estimation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets show that GEM-TFL achieves more accurate and robust temporal forgery localization and narrows the gap with fully supervised methods.
AI.conclusion: GEM-TFL effectively bridges supervision gaps in weakly supervised TFL, improves robustness, and provides a viable path toward performance close to fully supervised approaches.

title: Synchronization-based clustering on the unit hypersphere
summary: Clustering on the unit hypersphere is a fundamental problem in various fields, with applications ranging from gene expression analysis to text and image classification. Traditional clustering methods are not always suitable for unit sphere data, as they do not account for the geometric structure of the sphere. We introduce a novel algorithm for clustering data represented as points on the unit sphere $\mathbf{S}^{d-1}$. Our method is based on the $d$-dimensional generalized Kuramoto model. The effectiveness of the introduced method is demonstrated on synthetic and real-world datasets. Results are compared with some of the traditional clustering methods, showing that our method achieves similar or better results in terms of clustering accuracy.

AI.tldr: A geometry-aware clustering method for unit-sphere data using a d-dimensional generalized Kuramoto model, showing competitive accuracy on synthetic and real datasets.
AI.motivation: Unit-sphere data lies on a curved manifold; traditional Euclidean clustering methods ignore spherical geometry, potentially degrading performance for angular data. A geometry-consistent approach may yield improved clustering by leveraging manifold structure.
AI.method: Extend the Kuramoto model to the d-dimensional unit-sphere S^{d-1}; formulate a clustering algorithm based on phase synchronization dynamics on the sphere and derive an iterative procedure to assign points to clusters while respecting spherical geometry.
AI.result: Empirical validation on synthetic and real-world datasets indicates the method achieves similar or better clustering accuracy compared to traditional clustering methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed generalized Kuramoto-based clustering provides a geometry-aware alternative for unit-sphere data, with competitive performance; further work would clarify theoretical guarantees, scalability, and comparisons to spherical clustering models.

title: Distributed Partial Information Puzzles: Examining Common Ground Construction Under Epistemic Asymmetry
summary: Establishing common ground, a shared set of beliefs and mutually recognized facts, is fundamental to collaboration, yet remains a challenge for current AI systems, especially in multimodal, multiparty settings, where the collaborators bring different information to the table. We introduce the Distributed Partial Information Puzzle (DPIP), a collaborative construction task that elicits rich multimodal communication under epistemic asymmetry. We present a multimodal dataset of these interactions, annotated and temporally aligned across speech, gesture, and action modalities to support reasoning over propositional content and belief dynamics. We then evaluate two paradigms for modeling common ground (CG): (1) state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs), prompted to infer shared beliefs from multimodal updates, and (2) an axiomatic pipeline grounded in Dynamic Epistemic Logic (DEL) that incrementally performs the same task. Results on the annotated DPIP data indicate that it poses a challenge to modern LLMs' abilities to track both task progression and belief state.

AI.tldr: Introduces DPIP: a multimodal, epistimic-asymmetric collaborative task with a new dataset (speech, gesture, action) to study common ground; contrasts LLM-based inference of shared beliefs with a DEL-based axiomatic approach; finds LLMs struggle with tracking task progression and belief state.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of establishing common ground in AI systems, especially in multimodal, multiparty settings where collaborators hold different information.
AI.method: 1) Propose DPIP collaborative task; 2) Compile and annotate a multimodal dataset aligned across speech, gesture, and action; 3) evaluate two modeling paradigms: (a) prompting LLMs to infer shared beliefs from multimodal updates, (b) an axiomatic DEL-based pipeline that incrementally reasons about belief dynamics.
AI.result: DPIP poses challenges for modern LLMs in maintaining and updating shared beliefs alongside task progression; DEL-based reasoning provides a principled alternative for tracking belief dynamics, highlighting a gap in current LLM capabilities.
AI.conclusion: DPIP provides a valuable benchmark for common-ground reasoning in AI and multimodal collaboration, and combining multimodal datasets with epistemic-logic frameworks can guide development of more robust, interoperable collaborative AI systems.

title: Diff-ES: Stage-wise Structural Diffusion Pruning via Evolutionary Search
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in high-fidelity image generation but remain computationally demanding due to their multi-step denoising process and large model sizes. Although prior work improves efficiency either by reducing sampling steps or by compressing model parameters, existing structured pruning approaches still struggle to balance real acceleration and image quality preservation. In particular, prior methods such as MosaicDiff rely on heuristic, manually tuned stage-wise sparsity schedules and stitch multiple independently pruned models during inference, which increases memory overhead. However, the importance of diffusion steps is highly non-uniform and model-dependent. As a result, schedules derived from simple heuristics or empirical observations often fail to generalize and may lead to suboptimal performance. To this end, we introduce \textbf{Diff-ES}, a stage-wise structural \textbf{Diff}usion pruning framework via \textbf{E}volutionary \textbf{S}earch, which optimizes the stage-wise sparsity schedule and executes it through memory-efficient weight routing without model duplication. Diff-ES divides the diffusion trajectory into multiple stages, automatically discovers an optimal stage-wise sparsity schedule via evolutionary search, and activates stage-conditioned weights dynamically without duplicating model parameters. Our framework naturally integrates with existing structured pruning methods for diffusion models including depth and width pruning. Extensive experiments on DiT and SDXL demonstrate that Diff-ES consistently achieves wall-clock speedups while incurring minimal degradation in generation quality, establishing state-of-the-art performance for structured diffusion model pruning.

AI.tldr: Diff-ES introduces evolutionary search-driven stage-wise structured pruning for diffusion models, enabling memory-efficient weight routing without model duplication and achieving wall-clock speedups with minimal quality loss.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models are powerful but computationally expensive due to multi-step denoising and large parameter counts. Existing pruning methods (e.g., MosaicDiff) rely on heuristic schedules and require duplicating pruned models, which increases memory overhead and hurts generalization. There is non-uniform importance across diffusion steps and model-dependent behavior that simple heuristics fail to capture.
AI.method: Diff-ES partitions the diffusion trajectory into multiple stages and uses evolutionary search to automatically discover an optimal stage-wise sparsity schedule. It then activates stage-conditioned weights dynamically during inference without duplicating parameters, enabling memory-efficient pruning. The method integrates with existing structured pruning (depth/width) and uses memory-efficient weight routing.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on DiT and SDXL shows consistent wall-clock speedups with minimal degradation in generation quality, achieving state-of-the-art performance for structured diffusion model pruning.
AI.conclusion: Diff-ES effectively balances speed and quality by learning stage-wise sparsity schedules in a memory-efficient framework, generalizing across diffusion models and pruning types without model duplication.

title: Aura: Universal Multi-dimensional Exogenous Integration for Aviation Time Series
summary: Time series forecasting has witnessed an increasing demand across diverse industrial applications, where accurate predictions are pivotal for informed decision-making. Beyond numerical time series data, reliable forecasting in practical scenarios requires integrating diverse exogenous factors. Such exogenous information is often multi-dimensional or even multimodal, introducing heterogeneous interactions that unimodal time series models struggle to capture. In this paper, we delve into an aviation maintenance scenario and identify three distinct types of exogenous factors that influence temporal dynamics through distinct interaction modes. Based on this empirical insight, we propose Aura, a universal framework that explicitly organizes and encodes heterogeneous external information according to its interaction mode with the target time series. Specifically, Aura utilizes a tailored tripartite encoding mechanism to embed heterogeneous features into well-established time series models, ensuring seamless integration of non-sequential context. Extensive experiments on a large-scale, three-year industrial dataset from China Southern Airlines, covering the Boeing 777 and Airbus A320 fleets, demonstrate that Aura consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across all baselines and exhibits superior adaptability. Our findings highlight Aura's potential as a general-purpose enhancement for aviation safety and reliability.

AI.tldr: Aura: a universal tripartite encoding framework to integrate heterogeneous exogenous factors into time series forecasting, achieving state-of-the-art results on aviation maintenance data.
AI.motivation: Forecasting with exogenous factors that are multi-dimensional or multimodal; conventional unimodal time series models struggle to capture diverse interaction modes. Empirical aviation maintenance data reveals three distinct exogenous interaction types.
AI.method: Aura introduces a tailored tripartite encoding mechanism that organizes and encodes heterogeneous external information according to its interaction mode with the target time series and embeds these features into established time series models, including non-sequential context.
AI.result: Aura achieves state-of-the-art performance across all baselines on a large-scale three-year industrial dataset from China Southern Airlines, covering Boeing 777 and Airbus A320 fleets, and shows superior adaptability.
AI.conclusion: Aura has potential as a general-purpose enhancement for aviation safety and reliability and could generalize to broader time series forecasting tasks involving heterogeneous exogenous information.

title: Towards Provably Unbiased LLM Judges via Bias-Bounded Evaluation
summary: As AI models progress beyond simple chatbots into more complex workflows, we draw ever closer to the event horizon beyond which AI systems will be utilized in autonomous, self-maintaining feedback loops. Any autonomous AI system will depend on automated, verifiable rewards and feedback; in settings where ground truth is sparse or non-deterministic, one practical source of such rewards is an LLM-as-a-Judge. Although LLM judges continue to improve, the literature has yet to introduce systems capable of enforcing standards with strong guarantees, particularly when bias vectors are unknown or adversarially discovered. To remedy this issue, we propose average bias-boundedness (A-BB), an algorithmic framework which formally guarantees reductions of harm/impact as a result of any measurable bias in an LLM judge. Evaluating on Arena-Hard-Auto with four LLM judges, we achieve (tau=0.5, delta=0.01) bias-bounded guarantees while retaining 61-99% correlation with original rankings across formatting and schematic bias settings, with most judge-bias combinations exceeding 80%. The code to reproduce our findings is available at https://github.com/penfever/bias-bounded-evaluation.

AI.tldr: Proposes A-BB, a formal framework to bound harm from bias in LLM-based judges; demonstrates bias-bounded guarantees with notable correlation retention across adversarial bias settings; reproducible code provided.
AI.motivation: AI systems are moving into autonomous feedback loops that rely on automated, verifiable rewards. LLM-based judges, while improving, can introduce harmful bias, especially when bias vectors are unknown or adversarial. A formal guarantee is needed to constrain harm.
AI.method: Introduce average bias-boundedness (A-BB) as an algorithmic framework that guarantees reductions in harm/impact from measurable bias in an LLM judge. Evaluate on Arena-Hard-Auto with four LLM judges, aiming for (tau=0.5, delta=0.01) bias-bounded guarantees and preserve correlation with original rankings across various bias settings.
AI.result: Achieves tau=0.5, delta=0.01 bias-bounded guarantees while retaining 61-99% correlation with original rankings across formatting and schematic bias settings; most judge-bias combinations exceed 80% correlation.
AI.conclusion: A-BB provides formal guarantees to mitigate bias in LLM judges within autonomous systems, showing viability across bias settings. Reproducible via code at the provided GitHub link.

title: BLINK: Behavioral Latent Modeling of NK Cell Cytotoxicity
summary: Machine learning models of cellular interaction dynamics hold promise for understanding cell behavior. Natural killer (NK) cell cytotoxicity is a prominent example of such interaction dynamics and is commonly studied using time-resolved multi-channel fluorescence microscopy. Although tumor cell death events can be annotated at single frames, NK cytotoxic outcome emerges over time from cellular interactions and cannot be reliably inferred from frame-wise classification alone. We introduce BLINK, a trajectory-based recurrent state-space model that serves as a cell world model for NK-tumor interactions. BLINK learns latent interaction dynamics from partially observed NK-tumor interaction sequences and predicts apoptosis increments that accumulate into cytotoxic outcomes. Experiments on long-term time-lapse NK-tumor recordings show improved cytotoxic outcome detection and enable forecasting of future outcomes, together with an interpretable latent representation that organizes NK trajectories into coherent behavioral modes and temporally structured interaction phases. BLINK provides a unified framework for quantitative evaluation and structured modeling of NK cytotoxic behavior at the single-cell level.

AI.tldr: BLINK is a trajectory-based recurrent state-space model that learns latent NK-tumor interaction dynamics to predict cytotoxic outcomes from partial observations, enabling future outcome forecasting and interpretable behavioral modes.
AI.motivation: NK cytotoxicity evolves over time and frame-wise classifications fail to capture temporal structure; a trajectory-aware model is needed to leverage partial, long-term single-cell imaging data.
AI.method: BLINK introduces a trajectory-based recurrent state-space framework that infers latent interaction dynamics from partially observed NK-tumor sequences, predicts apoptosis increments accumulating into cytotoxic outcomes, and yields an interpretable latent space organized into behavioral modes and interaction phases.
AI.result: Applied to long-term time-lapse NK-tumor data, BLINK improves cytotoxic outcome detection, enables forecasting of future outcomes, and provides an interpretable latent representation that clusters NK trajectories into coherent modes and temporally structured phases of interaction.
AI.conclusion: BLINK offers a unified framework for quantitative evaluation and structured modeling of NK cytotoxic behavior at the single-cell level.

title: Axiomatic On-Manifold Shapley via Optimal Generative Flows
summary: Shapley-based attribution is critical for post-hoc XAI but suffers from off-manifold artifacts due to heuristic baselines. While generative methods attempt to address this, they often introduce geometric inefficiency and discretization drift. We propose a formal theory of on-manifold Aumann-Shapley attributions driven by optimal generative flows. We prove a representation theorem establishing the gradient line integral as the unique functional satisfying efficiency and geometric axioms, notably reparameterization invariance. To resolve path ambiguity, we select the kinetic-energy-minimizing Wasserstein-2 geodesic transporting a prior to the data distribution. This yields a canonical attribution family that recovers classical Shapley for additive models and admits provable stability bounds against flow approximation errors. By reframing baseline selection as a variational problem, our method experimentally outperforms baselines, achieving strict manifold adherence via vanishing Flow Consistency Error and superior semantic alignment characterized by Structure-Aware Total Variation. Our code is on https://github.com/cenweizhang/OTFlowSHAP.

AI.tldr: A theory for on-manifold Shapley attributions via optimal generative flows; characterizes a unique gradient-line integral functional under efficiency and geometric axioms, fixes path via Weyl? actually Wasserstein-2 geodesic; canonical attribution recovers Shapley for additive models, with stability bounds; empirical gains and vanishing Flow Consistency Error; code available.
AI.motivation: Shapley attribution in post-hoc XAI suffers from off-manifold artifacts due to heuristic baselines and discretization/geometry issues in generative approaches; need a principled on-manifold formulation with invariant, stable attributions.
AI.method: Develop a formal theory of on-manifold Aumann-Shapley attributions using optimal generative flows. Prove a representation theorem that the gradient line integral is the unique functional satisfying efficiency and geometric axioms (including reparameterization invariance). Resolve path ambiguity by selecting the kinetic-energy-minimizing Wasserstein-2 geodesic transporting a prior to the data distribution. Define a canonical attribution family, recover classical Shapley for additive models, and derive stability bounds against flow approximation errors. Reframe baseline selection as a variational problem.
AI.result: The approach yields a canonical attribution family on the data manifold; it recovers classical Shapley in additive-model cases; provides provable stability bounds against flow approximation; empirically outperforms baselines with strict manifold adherence (Flow Consistency Error vanishes) and improved semantic alignment (Structure-Aware Total Variation). Code released at the given GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: The proposed OTFlowSHAP framework delivers on-manifold Shapley attributions with theoretical guarantees (representation theorem, invariance, stability) and practical empirical gains, unifying Shapley with optimal transport-based geodesics and enabling principled baseline design via variational baselines.

title: The Spike, the Sparse and the Sink: Anatomy of Massive Activations and Attention Sinks
summary: We study two recurring phenomena in Transformer language models: massive activations, in which a small number of tokens exhibit extreme outliers in a few channels, and attention sinks, in which certain tokens attract disproportionate attention mass regardless of semantic relevance. Prior work observes that these phenomena frequently co-occur and often involve the same tokens, but their functional roles and causal relationship remain unclear. Through systematic experiments, we show that the co-occurrence is largely an architectural artifact of modern Transformer design, and that the two phenomena serve related but distinct functions. Massive activations operate globally: they induce near-constant hidden representations that persist across layers, effectively functioning as implicit parameters of the model. Attention sinks operate locally: they modulate attention outputs across heads and bias individual heads toward short-range dependencies. We identify the pre-norm configuration as the key choice that enables the co-occurrence, and show that ablating it causes the two phenomena to decouple.

AI.tldr: In Transformer LMs, massive activations and attention sinks co-occur due to architecture; massive activations act as global implicit parameters, attention sinks bias local heads; pre-norm enables co-occurrence; removing pre-norm decouples the phenomena.
AI.motivation: Clarify the causal relationship and functional roles of two observed phenomena (massive activations and attention sinks) that frequently co-occur in Transformers.
AI.method: Systematic experiments on modern Transformer architectures; analyze activation outliers and attention mass; assess persistence of representations across layers; compare pre-norm vs post-norm configurations; ablation of pre-norm to test decoupling; examine cross-head effects.
AI.result: Co-occurrence largely arises from architecture rather than semantics; massive activations produce near-constant hidden representations across layers, acting as implicit parameters; attention sinks affect attention outputs across heads, biasing them toward short-range dependencies; ablation of pre-norm decouples the two phenomena, confirming their distinct roles.
AI.conclusion: Massive activations and attention sinks are related but distinct consequences of Transformer design; recognizing them as separate functions improves understanding of model behavior and informs architectural choices, particularly the role of pre-norm.

title: UniPAR: A Unified Framework for Pedestrian Attribute Recognition
summary: Pedestrian Attribute Recognition is a foundational computer vision task that provides essential support for downstream applications, including person retrieval in video surveillance and intelligent retail analytics. However, existing research is frequently constrained by the ``one-model-per-dataset" paradigm and struggles to handle significant discrepancies across domains in terms of modalities, attribute definitions, and environmental scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose UniPAR, a unified Transformer-based framework for PAR. By incorporating a unified data scheduling strategy and a dynamic classification head, UniPAR enables a single model to simultaneously process diverse datasets from heterogeneous modalities, including RGB images, video sequences, and event streams. We also introduce an innovative phased fusion encoder that explicitly aligns visual features with textual attribute queries through a late deep fusion strategy. Experimental results on the widely used benchmark datasets, including MSP60K, DukeMTMC, and EventPAR, demonstrate that UniPAR achieves performance comparable to specialized SOTA methods. Furthermore, multi-dataset joint training significantly enhances the model's cross-domain generalization and recognition robustness in extreme environments characterized by low light and motion blur. The source code of this paper will be released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/OpenPAR

AI.tldr: Summary generation failed
AI.motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable
AI.method: Method extraction failed
AI.result: Result analysis unavailable
AI.conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

title: SRasP: Self-Reorientation Adversarial Style Perturbation for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning
summary: Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (CD-FSL) aims to transfer knowledge from a seen source domain to unseen target domains, serving as a key benchmark for evaluating the robustness and transferability of models. Existing style-based perturbation methods mitigate domain shift but often suffer from gradient instability and convergence to sharp minima.To address these limitations, we propose a novel crop-global style perturbation network, termed Self-Reorientation Adversarial \underline{S}tyle \underline{P}erturbation (SRasP). Specifically, SRasP leverages global semantic guidance to identify incoherent crops, followed by reorienting and aggregating the style gradients of these crops with the global style gradients within one image. Furthermore, we propose a novel multi-objective optimization function to maximize visual discrepancy while enforcing semantic consistency among global, crop, and adversarial features. Applying the stabilized perturbations during training encourages convergence toward flatter and more transferable solutions, improving generalization to unseen domains. Extensive experiments are conducted on multiple CD-FSL benchmarks, demonstrating consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: We propose SRasP: a crop-global style perturbation framework for cross-domain few-shot learning that reorients and aggregates style gradients from incoherent crops guided by global semantics, coupled with a multi-objective loss. This yields more stable training, flatter minima, and improved unseen-domain generalization, achieving state-of-the-art results on CD-FSL benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Style-based perturbations used to mitigate domain shift in CD-FSL often suffer from gradient instability and convergence to sharp minima, hindering generalization to unseen domains.
AI.method: Introduce Self-Reorientation Adversarial Style Perturbation (SRasP). It uses global semantic guidance to identify incoherent crops, reorients and aggregates their style gradients with the global style gradients within a single image, and optimizes with a multi-objective loss that (i) maximizes visual discrepancy and (ii) enforces semantic consistency among global, crop, and adversarial features.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple CD-FSL benchmarks show consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating better stability and transferability.
AI.conclusion: SRasP stabilizes training by producing perturbations that lead to flatter minima and more transferable representations, improving generalization to unseen domains in CD-FSL.

title: FedBCD:Communication-Efficient Accelerated Block Coordinate Gradient Descent for Federated Learning
summary:
Although Federated Learning has been widely studied in recent years, there are still high overhead expenses in each communication round for large-scale models such as Vision Transformer. To lower the communication complexity, we propose a novel Federated Block Coordinate Gradient Descent (FedBCGD) method for communication efficiency. The proposed method splits model parameters into several blocks, including a shared block and enables uploading a specific parameter block by each client, which can significantly reduce communication overhead. Moreover, we also develop an accelerated FedBCGD algorithm (called FedBCGD+) with client drift control and stochastic variance reduction. To the best of our knowledge, this paper is the first work on parameter block communication for training large-scale deep models. We also provide the convergence analysis for the proposed algorithms. Our theoretical results show that the communication complexities of our algorithms are a factor $1/N$ lower than those of existing methods, where $N$ is the number of parameter blocks, and they enjoy much faster convergence than their counterparts. Empirical results indicate the superiority of the proposed algorithms compared to state-of-the-art algorithms.
  The code is available at https://github.com/junkangLiu0/FedBCGD.

AI.tldr: FedBCGD introduces block-coordinate gradient descent in federated learning to reduce communication by partitioning model parameters into multiple blocks (including a shared block) and uploading only a specific block per client; FedBCGD+ adds drift control and stochastic variance reduction for faster convergence; theoretical results show 1/N reduction in communication complexity; empirical results show superiority; first to propose parameter block communication for large-scale models; code available at GitHub.
AI.motivation: High communication overhead in FL for large models (e.g., Vision Transformers) motivates reducing per-round data exchange; block-wise parameter updates offer a path to scalable distributed learning.
AI.method: Partition model parameters into blocks, designate a shared block; clients upload one block per round; FedBCGD algorithm; accelerated FedBCGD+ with drift control and SVRG-like variance reduction; convergence analysis provided.
AI.result: Theoretical: communication complexity reduced by factor 1/N relative to existing methods; faster convergence than counterparts. Empirical: results show superiority over SOTA methods.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a novel parameter-block communication approach (FedBCGD) and its accelerated version (FedBCGD+) for large-scale models; first to address parameter-block communication; code released; practical impact for FL with ViT.

title: Federated Causal Discovery Across Heterogeneous Datasets under Latent Confounding
summary: Causal discovery across multiple datasets is often constrained by data privacy regulations and cross-site heterogeneity, limiting the use of conventional methods that require a single, centralized dataset. To address these challenges, we introduce fedCI, a federated conditional independence test that rigorously handles heterogeneous datasets with non-identical sets of variables, site-specific effects, and mixed variable types, including continuous, ordinal, binary, and categorical variables. At its core, fedCI uses a federated Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) procedure to estimate the parameters of generalized linear models underlying likelihood-ratio tests for conditional independence. Building on this, we develop fedCI-IOD, a federated extension of the Integration of Overlapping Datasets (IOD) algorithm, that replaces its meta-analysis strategy and enables, for the fist time, federated causal discovery under latent confounding across distributed and heterogeneous datasets. By aggregating evidence federatively, fedCI-IOD not only preserves privacy but also substantially enhances statistical power, achieving performance comparable to fully pooled analyses and mitigating artifacts from low local sample sizes. Our tools are publicly available as the fedCI Python package, a privacy-preserving R implementation of IOD, and a web application for the fedCI-IOD pipeline, providing versatile, user-friendly solutions for federated conditional independence testing and causal discovery.

AI.tldr: fedCI introduces a privacy-preserving federated conditional independence test for heterogeneous datasets with mixed variable types; it extends to fedCI-IOD for federated causal discovery under latent confounding, with strong privacy guarantees and performance close to pooled analyses, plus available software tools.
AI.motivation: Data privacy regulations and cross-site heterogeneity hinder centralized causal discovery. There is a need for methods that (i) handle non-identical variable sets, site-specific effects, and mixed data types, (ii) operate on distributed data without sharing raw data, and (iii) enable causal discovery under latent confounding.
AI.method: fedCI employs a federated Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) procedure to estimate GLM parameters underlying likelihood-ratio tests for conditional independence. fedCI-IOD extends the Integration of Overlapping Datasets (IOD) to a federated setting by replacing meta-analysis with a privacy-preserving aggregation, enabling causal discovery under latent confounding across distributed, heterogeneous datasets.
AI.result: Federated approach preserves privacy, increases statistical power, and achieves performance close to fully pooled analyses, mitigating artifacts from small local sample sizes. It handles heterogeneous datasets with non-identical variable sets and mixed variable types (continuous, ordinal, binary, categorical).
AI.conclusion: The work provides practical tools for federated conditional independence testing and causal discovery: the fedCI Python package, a privacy-preserving R implementation of IOD, and a web app for the fedCI-IOD pipeline, facilitating privacy-preserving, scalable causal inference across distributed datasets.

title: SSR-GS: Separating Specular Reflection in Gaussian Splatting for Glossy Surface Reconstruction
summary:
In recent years, 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) has achieved remarkable progress in novel view synthesis. However, accurately reconstructing glossy surfaces under complex illumination remains challenging, particularly in scenes with strong specular reflections and multi-surface interreflections. To address this issue, we propose SSR-GS, a specular reflection modeling framework for glossy surface reconstruction. Specifically, we introduce a prefiltered Mip-Cubemap to model direct specular reflections efficiently, and propose an IndiASG module to capture indirect specular reflections.
  Furthermore, we design Visual Geometry Priors (VGP) that couple a reflection-aware visual prior via a reflection score (RS) to downweight the photometric loss contribution of reflection-dominated regions, with geometry priors derived from VGGT, including progressively decayed depth supervision and transformed normal constraints. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that SSR-GS achieves state-of-the-art performance in glossy surface reconstruction.

AI.tldr: SSR-GS proposes a specular reflection modeling framework for glossy surface reconstruction in 3D Gaussian splatting, combining a prefiltered Mip-Cubemap for direct reflections, IndiASG for indirect reflections, and Visual Geometry Priors with a reflection score to downweight reflective regions, plus VGGT-based depth/normal priors; achieves state-of-the-art results on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Glossy surfaces under complex illumination (strong speculars and interreflections) pose a major challenge for 3D Gaussian splatting; existing methods struggle to disentangle and reconstruct shinier materials.
AI.method: Introduce a prefiltered Mip-Cubemap to efficiently model direct specular reflections; propose IndiASG module to capture indirect specular reflections; design Visual Geometry Priors (VGP) with a reflection-aware prior via a reflection score to downweight reflection-dominated photometric loss; derive geometry priors from VGGT, including progressively decayed depth supervision and transformed normal constraints.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show SSR-GS achieves state-of-the-art performance in glossy surface reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: SSR-GS effectively models both direct and indirect specular reflections and leverages geometry priors to improve glossy surface reconstruction in 3D Gaussian splatting, with strong empirical results; provides a comprehensive framework for handling challenging illumination in glossy materials.

title: Balancing Privacy-Quality-Efficiency in Federated Learning through Round-Based Interleaving of Protection Techniques
summary:
In federated learning (FL), balancing privacy protection, learning quality, and efficiency remains a challenge. Privacy protection mechanisms, such as Differential Privacy (DP), degrade learning quality, or, as in the case of Homomorphic Encryption (HE), incur substantial system overhead. To address this, we propose Alt-FL, a privacy-preserving FL framework that combines DP, HE, and synthetic data via a novel round-based interleaving strategy. Alt-FL introduces three new methods, Privacy Interleaving (PI), Synthetic Interleaving with DP (SI/DP), and Synthetic Interleaving with HE (SI/HE), that enable flexible quality-efficiency trade-offs while providing privacy protection.
  We systematically evaluate Alt-FL against representative reconstruction attacks, including Deep Leakage from Gradients, Inverting Gradients, When the Curious Abandon Honesty, and Robbing the Fed, using a LeNet-5 model on CIFAR-10 and Fashion-MNIST. To enable fair comparison between DP- and HE-based defenses, we introduce a new attacker-centric framework that compares empirical attack success rates across the three proposed interleaving methods. Our results show that, for the studied attacker model and dataset, PI achieves the most balanced trade-offs at high privacy protection levels, while DP-based methods are preferable at intermediate privacy requirements. We also discuss how such results can be the basis for selecting privacy-preserving FL methods under varying privacy and resource constraints.

AI.tldr: Alt-FL introduces a round-based interleaving framework that combines differential privacy, homomorphic encryption, and synthetic data to offer flexible privacy-utility-efficiency trade-offs in federated learning. An attacker-centric evaluation shows Privacy Interleaving (PI) is most balanced at high privacy, while DP-based methods excel at intermediate privacy levels; a framework is proposed to help select privacy-preserving FL methods under varying constraints.
AI.motivation: Address the core trade-off in federated learning among privacy protection, learning quality, and system efficiency. DP can degrade model utility; HE incurs overhead; synthetic data may mitigate costs. A flexible, interleaved approach aims to balance these aspects while preserving privacy.
AI.method: Introduce Alt-FL with three interleaving strategies: Privacy Interleaving (PI), Synthetic Interleaving with DP (SI/DP), and Synthetic Interleaving with HE (SI/HE). Implement a round-based interleaving scheme to blend DP, HE, and synthetic data. Evaluate using an attacker-centric framework against reconstruction attacks (Deep Leakage from Gradients, Inverting Gradients, When the Curious Abandon Honesty, Robbing the Fed) on LeNet-5 with CIFAR-10 and Fashion-MNIST. Compare attack success rates across interleaving methods; provide guidance for method selection under privacy/resource constraints.
AI.result: PI achieves the most balanced privacy-utility-efficiency trade-off at high privacy levels; DP-based methods are preferable at intermediate privacy requirements. The attacker-centric framework enables fair comparison between DP- and HE-based defenses. The study demonstrates how privacy-preserving FL method choices can be guided by privacy and resource constraints.
AI.conclusion: Alt-FL enables flexible privacy-preserving FL by interleaving DP, HE, and synthetic data. PI is generally strongest at high privacy, while DP-based methods perform better at intermediate privacy; the framework supports method selection under varying privacy and resource constraints, with implications for deploying privacy-preserving FL in practice.

title: The Impact of Preprocessing Methods on Racial Encoding and Model Robustness in CXR Diagnosis
comment: Preprint accepted for publication at BVM 2026 (https://www.bvm-conf.org/)
summary: Deep learning models can identify racial identity with high accuracy from chest X-ray (CXR) recordings. Thus, there is widespread concern about the potential for racial shortcut learning, where a model inadvertently learns to systematically bias its diagnostic predictions as a function of racial identity. Such racial biases threaten healthcare equity and model reliability, as models may systematically misdiagnose certain demographic groups. Since racial shortcuts are diffuse - non-localized and distributed throughout the whole CXR recording - image preprocessing methods may influence racial shortcut learning, yet the potential of such methods for reducing biases remains underexplored. Here, we investigate the effects of image preprocessing methods including lung masking, lung cropping, and Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE). These approaches aim to suppress spurious cues encoding racial information while preserving diagnostic accuracy. Our experiments reveal that simple bounding box-based lung cropping can be an effective strategy for reducing racial shortcut learning while maintaining diagnostic model performance, bypassing frequently postulated fairness-accuracy trade-offs.

AI.tldr: Bounding-box lung cropping reduces racial shortcut learning in chest X-ray models while preserving diagnostic performance.
AI.motivation: Racial identity cues can be diffusely encoded in CXRs; exploring preprocessing to suppress racial cues aims to improve fairness and reliability without harming accuracy.
AI.method: Compare image preprocessing methods (lung masking, lung cropping via bounding boxes, CLAHE) on CXR classifiers to assess impact on bias and diagnostic accuracy.
AI.result: Lung cropping by simple bounding boxes effectively reduces racial shortcut learning and maintains diagnostic accuracy; CLAHE and masking show mixed or lesser effects on bias.
AI.conclusion: Simple, bounding-box lung cropping can mitigate racial bias in CXR models without sacrificing performance, suggesting preprocessing choices can influence fairness beyond the model architecture.

title: Trainable Bitwise Soft Quantization for Input Feature Compression
summary: The growing demand for machine learning applications in the context of the Internet of Things calls for new approaches to optimize the use of limited compute and memory resources. Despite significant progress that has been made w.r.t. reducing model sizes and improving efficiency, many applications still require remote servers to provide the required resources. However, such approaches rely on transmitting data from edge devices to remote servers, which may not always be feasible due to bandwidth, latency, or energy constraints. We propose a task-specific, trainable feature quantization layer that compresses the input features of a neural network. This can significantly reduce the amount of data that needs to be transferred from the device to a remote server. In particular, the layer allows each input feature to be quantized to a user-defined number of bits, enabling a simple on-device compression at the time of data collection. The layer is designed to approximate step functions with sigmoids, enabling trainable quantization thresholds. By concatenating outputs from multiple sigmoids, introduced as bitwise soft quantization, it achieves trainable quantized values when integrated with a neural network. We compare our method to full-precision inference as well as to several quantization baselines. Experiments show that our approach outperforms standard quantization methods, while maintaining accuracy levels close to those of full-precision models. In particular, depending on the dataset, compression factors of $5\times$ to $16\times$ can be achieved compared to $32$-bit input without significant performance loss.

AI.tldr: A trainable, task-specific feature quantization layer enables on-device data compression for IoT ML tasks, using bitwise soft quantization via concatenated sigmoids to approximate quantization thresholds; achieves 5x–16x compression with minimal accuracy loss versus full-precision.
AI.motivation: Edge IoT applications face bandwidth, latency, and energy constraints; transmitting raw or high-precision data is often infeasible. There is a need for trainable, task-aware quantization to reduce data size without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: Proposes a trainable feature quantization layer that quantizes each input feature to a user-defined bit-width. It uses multiple sigmoid-based components to approximate step quantization; outputs from these sigmoids are concatenated to form bitwise soft quantized values, enabling differentiable, trainable thresholds that can be integrated into neural networks for on-device compression.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows the proposed method outperforms standard quantization baselines and achieves compression factors of 5× to 16× (versus 32-bit inputs) with accuracy close to full-precision models across datasets.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides effective on-device quantization for IoT-related ML tasks, reducing data transmission requirements while maintaining competitive accuracy. It demonstrates the practicality of trainable, bitwise soft quantization for edge computing scenarios.

title: Generic Camera Calibration using Blurry Images
summary: Camera calibration is the foundation of 3D vision. Generic camera calibration can yield more accurate results than parametric cam era calibration. However, calibrating a generic camera model using printed calibration boards requires far more images than parametric calibration, making motion blur practically unavoidable for individual users. As a f irst attempt to address this problem, we draw on geometric constraints and a local parametric illumination model to simultaneously estimate feature locations and spatially varying point spread functions, while re solving the translational ambiguity that need not be considered in con ventional image deblurring tasks. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of our approach.

AI.tldr: A method to calibrate generic camera models under motion blur by jointly estimating feature locations and spatially varying PSFs using geometric constraints and a local illumination model, addressing translational ambiguity; experimental results validate effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Generic camera calibration can yield higher accuracy than parametric calibration, but requires many images and is vulnerable to motion blur when using printed calibration boards, hindering practical use for individuals.
AI.method: Proposes a joint estimation framework that combines geometric constraints with a local parametric illumination model to simultaneously estimate feature locations and spatially varying point spread functions (PSFs). It also resolves translational ambiguity that is not typically addressed in conventional image deblurring.
AI.result: Experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a viable solution to calibrating generic camera models under blur by jointly handling feature localization and blur modeling, with translational ambiguity resolved during optimization.

title: Incentive Aware AI Regulations: A Credal Characterisation
summary: While high-stakes ML applications demand strict regulations, strategic ML providers often evade them to lower development costs. To address this challenge, we cast AI regulation as a mechanism design problem under uncertainty and introduce regulation mechanisms: a framework that maps empirical evidence from models to a license for some market share. The providers can select from a set of licenses, effectively forcing them to bet on their model's ability to fulfil regulation. We aim at regulation mechanisms that achieve perfect market outcome, i.e. (a) drive non-compliant providers to self-exclude, and (b) ensure participation from compliant providers. We prove that a mechanism has perfect market outcome if and only if the set of non-compliant distributions forms a credal set, i.e., a closed, convex set of probability measures. This result connects mechanism design and imprecise probability by establishing a duality between regulation mechanisms and the set of non-compliant distributions. We also demonstrate these mechanisms in practice via experiments on regulating use of spurious features for prediction and fairness. Our framework provides new insights at the intersection of mechanism design and imprecise probability, offering a foundation for development of enforceable AI regulations.

AI.tldr: Proposes regulation mechanisms that treat AI regulation as a mechanism-design problem under uncertainty; proves perfect market outcome iff the set of non-compliant distributions forms a credal set; demonstrates empirically on regulating spurious features and fairness; links mechanism design with imprecise probability for enforceable AI regulation.
AI.motivation: Address the tendency of strategic ML providers to evade strict, high-stakes regulations by offering a principled, enforceable regulatory framework that aligns incentives with compliance.
AI.method: Introduce regulation mechanisms that map empirical evidence from models to a license for market share. Providers choose among licenses, effectively betting on their ability to meet regulation. Characterize perfect market outcome via a duality: the non-compliant distributions must form a credal set (closed, convex set of probability measures). Provide theoretical results and validate via experiments regulating spurious features and fairness.
AI.result: Characterization: perfect market outcome occurs iff the non-compliant distributions form a credal set, establishing a duality between regulation mechanisms and imprecise probability. Empirical demonstrations show regulation of spurious features and fairness in ML models.
AI.conclusion: Frames AI regulation as a bridge between mechanism design and imprecise probability, offering a foundational, enforceable approach for regulating AI. Further work needed on practical deployment, measurement of evidence, and computational aspects.

title: Mario: Multimodal Graph Reasoning with Large Language Models
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have opened new avenues for multimodal reasoning. Yet, most existing methods still rely on pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) to encode image-text pairs in isolation, ignoring the relational structure that real-world multimodal data naturally form. This motivates reasoning on multimodal graphs (MMGs), where each node has textual and visual attributes and edges provide structural cues. Enabling LLM-based reasoning on such heterogeneous multimodal signals while preserving graph topology introduces two key challenges: resolving weak cross-modal consistency and handling heterogeneous modality preference. To address this, we propose Mario, a unified framework that simultaneously resolves the two above challenges and enables effective LLM-based reasoning over MMGs. Mario consists of two innovative stages. Firstly, a graph-conditioned VLM design that jointly refines textual and visual features through fine-grained cross-modal contrastive learning guided by graph topology. Secondly, a modality-adaptive graph instruction tuning mechanism that organizes aligned multimodal features into graph-aware instruction views and employs a learnable router to surface, for each node and its neighborhood, the most informative modality configuration to the LLM. Extensive experiments across diverse MMG benchmarks demonstrate that Mario consistently outperforms state-of-the-art graph models in both supervised and zero-shot scenarios for node classification and link prediction. The code will be made available at https://github.com/sunyuanfu/Mario.

AI.tldr: Mario provides a unified framework for LLM-based reasoning on multimodal graphs, achieving superior performance on node classification and link prediction via graph-conditioned VLMs and modality-adaptive graph instruction tuning.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal reasoning methods rely on pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) that encode image-text pairs in isolation, ignoring the relational structure of multimodal data. There is a need to reason over multimodal graphs (MMGs) with preserved topology, addressing weak cross-modal consistency and heterogeneous modality preference.
AI.method: Mario comprises two stages: (1) a graph-conditioned VLM design that jointly refines textual and visual features via fine-grained cross-modal contrastive learning guided by graph topology; (2) a modality-adaptive graph instruction tuning mechanism that creates graph-aware instruction views for aligned multimodal features and uses a learnable router to surface, for each node and its neighborhood, the most informative modality configuration to the LLM.
AI.result: Across diverse MMG benchmarks, Mario consistently outperforms state-of-the-art graph models in both supervised and zero-shot settings for node classification and link prediction.
AI.conclusion: Mario effectively resolves weak cross-modal consistency and heterogeneous modality preference, enabling effective LLM-based reasoning on MMGs; code will be released at the provided GitHub link.

title: Towards a data-scale independent regulariser for robust sparse identification of non-linear dynamics
summary: Data normalisation, a common and often necessary preprocessing step in engineering and scientific applications, can severely distort the discovery of governing equations by magnitudebased sparse regression methods. This issue is particularly acute for the Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (SINDy) framework, where the core assumption of sparsity is undermined by the interaction between data scaling and measurement noise. The resulting discovered models can be dense, uninterpretable, and physically incorrect. To address this critical vulnerability, we introduce the Sequential Thresholding of Coefficient of Variation (STCV), a novel, computationally efficient sparse regression algorithm that is inherently robust to data scaling. STCV replaces conventional magnitude-based thresholding with a dimensionless statistical metric, the Coefficient Presence (CP), which assesses the statistical validity and consistency of candidate terms in the model library. This shift from magnitude to statistical significance makes the discovery process invariant to arbitrary data scaling. Through comprehensive benchmarking on canonical dynamical systems and practical engineering problems, including a physical mass-spring-damper experiment, we demonstrate that STCV consistently and significantly outperforms standard Sequential Thresholding Least Squares (STLSQ) and Ensemble-SINDy (E-SINDy) on normalised, noisy datasets. The results show that STCV-based methods can successfully identify the correct, sparse physical laws even when other methods fail. By mitigating the distorting effects of normalisation, STCV makes sparse system identification a more reliable and automated tool for real-world applications, thereby enhancing model interpretability and trustworthiness.

AI.tldr: STCV is a scale-invariant sparse regression method for SINDy that uses a coefficient-of-variation-based statistic (Coefficient Presence) instead of magnitude-based thresholding, improving robustness to data scaling and noise; it outperforms STLSQ and E-SINDy on normalised datasets and canonical/engineering problems.
AI.motivation: Data normalization can distort the discovery of governing equations in magnitude-based sparse regression (e.g., SINDy) by coupling scaling with measurement noise, leading to dense, unintelligible models.
AI.method: Introduce Sequential Thresholding of Coefficient of Variation (STCV). Replace magnitude thresholding with a dimensionless metric, Coefficient Presence (CP), to assess statistical validity and consistency of candidate terms. STCV is computationally efficient and invariant to data scaling.
AI.result: Benchmarking on canonical dynamical systems and a mass-spring-damper experiment shows STCV-based methods consistently outperform STLSQ and E-SINDy on normalised, noisy data, recovering correct sparse laws where others fail.
AI.conclusion: STCV mitigates normalization distortion, yielding more reliable, automated sparse system identification with improved interpretability and trustworthiness.

title: Logi-PAR: Logic-Infused Patient Activity Recognition via Differentiable Rule
summary: Patient Activity Recognition (PAR) in clinical settings uses activity data to improve safety and quality of care. Although significant progress has been made, current models mainly identify which activity is occurring. They often spatially compose sub-sparse visual cues using global and local attention mechanisms, yet only learn logically implicit patterns due to their neural-pipeline. Advancing clinical safety requires methods that can infer why a set of visual cues implies a risk, and how these can be compositionally reasoned through explicit logic beyond mere classification. To address this, we proposed Logi-PAR, the first Logic-Infused Patient Activity Recognition Framework that integrates contextual fact fusion as a multi-view primitive extractor and injects neural-guided differentiable rules. Our method automatically learns rules from visual cues, optimizing them end-to-end while enabling the implicit emergence patterns to be explicitly labelled during training. To the best of our knowledge, Logi-PAR is the first framework to recognize patient activity by applying learnable logic rules to symbolic mappings. It produces auditable why explanations as rule traces and supports counterfactual interventions (e.g., risk would decrease by 65% if assistance were present). Extensive evaluation on clinical benchmarks (VAST and OmniFall) demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming Vision-Language Models and transformer baselines. The code is available via: https://github.com/zararkhan985/Logi-PAR.git}

AI.tldr: Logi-PAR: a logic-infused patient activity recognition framework that learns differentiable rules from visual cues, producing auditable rule traces and counterfactuals, achieving state-of-the-art on VAST/OmniFall.
AI.motivation: Current PAR models largely perform black-box classification and rely on implicit neural patterns, lacking explicit, auditable logical reasoning and compositional explanations necessary for clinical safety.
AI.method: Logi-PAR integrates contextual fact fusion as a multi-view primitive extractor and injects neural-guided differentiable rules. It automatically learns rules from visual cues, end-to-end optimization, with emergent patterns explicitly labeled during training. It yields auditable rule traces and supports counterfactual interventions.
AI.result: On clinical benchmarks VAST and OmniFall, Logi-PAR achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming Vision-Language Models and transformer baselines.
AI.conclusion: Logi-PAR is the first framework to recognize patient activity by applying learnable logic rules to symbolic mappings, enabling auditable explanations and counterfactual reasoning for safer clinical PAR; code is available online.

title: Stable-LoRA: Stabilizing Feature Learning of Low-Rank Adaptation
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is a widely adopted parameter-efficient method for fine-tuning Large Langauge Models. It updates the weight matrix as $W=W_0+sBA$, where $W_0$ is the original frozen weight, $s$ is a scaling factor and $A$,$B$ are trainable low-rank matrices. Despite its robust empirical effectiveness, the theoretical foundations of LoRA remain insufficiently understood, particularly with respect to feature learning stability. In this paper, we first establish that, LoRA can, in principle, naturally achieve and sustain stable feature learning (i.e., be self-stabilized) under appropriate hyper-parameters and initializations of $A$ and $B$. However, we also uncover a fundamental limitation that the necessary non-zero initialization of $A$ compromises self-stability, leading to suboptimal performances. To address this challenge, we propose Stable-LoRA, a weight-shrinkage optimization strategy that dynamically enhances stability of LoRA feature learning. By progressively shrinking $A$ during the earliest training steps, Stable-LoRA is both theoretically and empirically validated to effectively eliminate instability of LoRA feature learning while preserving the benefits of the non-zero start. Experiments show that Stable-LoRA consistently outperforms other baselines across diverse models and tasks, with no additional memory usage and only negligible computation overheads. The code is available at https://github.com/Yize-Wu/Stable-LoRA.

AI.tldr: LoRA can, in principle, support stable feature learning, but a non-zero initialization of A undermines self-stability. Stable-LoRA introduces a gradual shrinkage of A during early training to restore stability while preserving LoRA benefits, achieving better performance with negligible overhead and no extra memory.
AI.motivation: Address the theoretical gap in LoRA regarding feature-learning stability and resolve instability caused by non-zero A initialization while maintaining efficiency.
AI.method: The paper provides a theoretical analysis showing when LoRA can self-stabilize under certain hyper-parameter and initialization conditions, identifies a fundamental limitation due to non-zero A initialization, and proposes Stable-LoRA—a dynamic A-shrinkage schedule applied in early training. It validates stability both theoretically and empirically and compares against baselines across models/tasks.
AI.result: Stable-LoRA effectively eliminates instability in LoRA feature learning, preserves the advantages of a non-zero starting point, and outperforms baselines with negligible computation overhead and no additional memory; code is released.
AI.conclusion: Stable-LoRA offers a principled, memory-efficient solution to stabilize LoRA feature learning, reconciling theoretical stability with practical gains and broad applicability.

title: Semantic Class Distribution Learning for Debiasing Semi-Supervised Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Medical image segmentation is critical for computer-aided diagnosis. However, dense pixel-level annotation is time-consuming and expensive, and medical datasets often exhibit severe class imbalance. Such imbalance causes minority structures to be overwhelmed by dominant classes in feature representations, hindering the learning of discriminative features and making reliable segmentation particularly challenging. To address this, we propose the Semantic Class Distribution Learning (SCDL) framework, a plug-and-play module that mitigates supervision and representation biases by learning structured class-conditional feature distributions. SCDL integrates Class Distribution Bidirectional Alignment (CDBA) to align embeddings with learnable class proxies and leverages Semantic Anchor Constraints (SAC) to guide proxies using labeled data. Experiments on the Synapse and AMOS datasets demonstrate that SCDL significantly improves segmentation performance across both overall and class-level metrics, with particularly strong gains on minority classes, achieving state-of-the-art results. Our code is released at https://github.com/Zyh55555/SCDL.

AI.tldr: SCDL is a plug-and-play framework for medical image segmentation that mitigates class imbalance and annotation burden by learning structured class-conditional feature distributions using CDBA and SAC, achieving state-of-the-art results on Synapse and AMOS.
AI.motivation: Dense pixel-level annotation is expensive, and medical datasets exhibit severe class imbalance, causing minority structures to be overwhelmed in feature learning. A method is needed to align representations with class-level structure and proxies.
AI.method: Semantic Class Distribution Learning (SCDL) with two components: Class Distribution Bidirectional Alignment (CDBA) that aligns embeddings with learnable class proxies, and Semantic Anchor Constraints (SAC) that guide proxies using labeled data. It is a plug-and-play module that can be integrated into segmentation models.
AI.result: Empirical results on the Synapse and AMOS datasets show significant improvements in both overall and per-class metrics, with particularly strong gains on minority classes and state-of-the-art performance. Code is released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: SCDL mitigates supervision and representation biases by learning structured class-conditional distributions, improving segmentation performance, especially for minority classes.

title: Early Warning of Intraoperative Adverse Events via Transformer-Driven Multi-Label Learning
summary: Early warning of intraoperative adverse events plays a vital role in reducing surgical risk and improving patient safety. While deep learning has shown promise in predicting the single adverse event, several key challenges remain: overlooking adverse event dependencies, underutilizing heterogeneous clinical data, and suffering from the class imbalance inherent in medical datasets. To address these issues, we construct the first Multi-label Adverse Events dataset (MuAE) for intraoperative adverse events prediction, covering six critical events. Next, we propose a novel Transformerbased multi-label learning framework (IAENet) that combines an improved Time-Aware Feature-wise Linear Modulation (TAFiLM) module for static covariates and dynamic variables robust fusion and complex temporal dependencies modeling. Furthermore, we introduce a Label-Constrained Reweighting Loss (LCRLoss) with co-occurrence regularization to effectively mitigate intra-event imbalance and enforce structured consistency among frequently co-occurring events. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IAENet consistently outperforms strong baselines on 5, 10, and 15-minute early warning tasks, achieving improvements of +5.05%, +2.82%, and +7.57% on average F1 score. These results highlight the potential of IAENet for supporting intelligent intraoperative decision-making in clinical practice.

AI.tldr: MuAE introduces a multi-label intraoperative adverse events dataset and IAENet, a Transformer-based framework with TAFiLM and LCRLoss, yielding multi-label early warning improvements across 5/10/15 minutes.
AI.motivation: Current single-event predictions ignore inter-event dependencies, underutilize heterogeneous clinical data, and suffer from severe class imbalance in intraoperative data. A multi-label, data-fusion, co-occurrence-aware approach is needed.
AI.method: Construct MuAE dataset covering six intraoperative adverse events. Propose IAENet, a Transformer-based multi-label model with an enhanced Time-Aware Feature-wise Linear Modulation (TAFiLM) module for static covariates and dynamic variables, enabling robust fusion and modeling of temporal dependencies.
