The images were annotated with long and dense intent descriptions given by over 200 annotators. Experimental results demonstrate that NaiLIA outperforms standard methods.

AI.tldr: NaiLIA: a multimodal nail-design retrieval method that uses dense intent descriptions and color palettes, with a confidence-based relaxed loss for unlabeled images; evaluated on a 10,625-image, crowd-annotated benchmark, outperforming standard methods.
AI.motivation: Dense, multi-layered user intents and color palettes are not well captured by existing vision-language models; nails involve unconstrained painted elements and embellishments; need robust alignment between text and color palettes.
AI.method: Introduce NaiLIA with alignment between dense text and palette; propose a relaxed loss based on confidence scores for unlabeled images; dataset of 10,625 nail images with long descriptions from 200+ annotators.
AI.result: Empirical results show NaiLIA outperforms standard retrieval methods on the constructed benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Dense-intent and palette-aware multimodal retrieval boosts nail-design image search; the collected dataset supports generalization across diverse cultures.

title: On the Necessity of Learnable Sheaf Laplacians
summary: Sheaf Neural Networks (SNNs) were introduced as an extension of Graph Convolutional Networks to address oversmoothing on heterophilous graphs by attaching a sheaf to the input graph and replacing the adjacency-based operator with a sheaf Laplacian defined by (learnable) restriction maps. Prior work motivates this design through theoretical properties of sheaf diffusion and the kernel of the sheaf Laplacian, suggesting that suitable non-identity restriction maps can avoid representations converging to constants across connected components. Since oversmoothing can also be mitigated through residual connections and normalization, we revisit a trivial sheaf construction to ask whether the additional complexity of learning restriction maps is necessary. We introduce an Identity Sheaf Network baseline, where all restriction maps are fixed to the identity, and use it to ablate the empirical improvements reported by sheaf-learning architectures. Across five popular heterophilic benchmarks, the identity baseline achieves comparable performance to a range of SNN variants. Finally, we introduce the Rayleigh quotient as a normalized measure for comparing oversmoothing across models and show that, in trained networks, the behavior predicted by the diffusion-based analysis of SNNs is not reflected empirically. In particular, Identity Sheaf Networks do not appear to suffer more significant oversmoothing than their SNN counterparts.

AI.tldr: Identity baseline in Sheaf Neural Networks challenges the necessity of learnable restriction maps, showing comparable performance to SNNs and no extra oversmoothing, prompting reconsideration of the added complexity.
AI.motivation: Question the claimed benefits of learnable restriction maps in SNNs for heterophilic graphs, given that simpler mechanisms (residuals, normalization) already mitigate oversmoothing.
AI.method: Define Identity Sheaf Network with all restriction maps fixed to identity; evaluate on five heterophilic benchmarks; compare against various SNN variants; quantify oversmoothing via Rayleigh quotient in trained models.
AI.result: Identity baseline matches or closely approaches the performance of SNN variants across benchmarks; Rayleigh-quotient analysis shows no empirical oversmoothing penalty for identity or diffusion-based predictions diverging from theory; trained networks do not exhibit stronger oversmoothing with identity restriction maps.
AI.conclusion: Learning restriction maps may be unnecessary; the theoretical rationale of SNN diffusion does not fully translate empirically; architecture design might focus on alternative oversmoothing mitigation (e.g., normalization, residuals) and re-evaluate the role of sheaf-based operators.

title: RealWonder: Real-Time Physical Action-Conditioned Video Generation
comment: The first two authors contributed equally. The last two authors advised equally. Project website: https://liuwei283.github.io/RealWonder/
summary: Current video generation models cannot simulate physical consequences of 3D actions like forces and robotic manipulations, as they lack structural understanding of how actions affect 3D scenes. We present RealWonder, the first real-time system for action-conditioned video generation from a single image. Our key insight is using physics simulation as an intermediate bridge: instead of directly encoding continuous actions, we translate them through physics simulation into visual representations (optical flow and RGB) that video models can process. RealWonder integrates three components: 3D reconstruction from single images, physics simulation, and a distilled video generator requiring only 4 diffusion steps. Our system achieves 13.2 FPS at 480x832 resolution, enabling interactive exploration of forces, robot actions, and camera controls on rigid objects, deformable bodies, fluids, and granular materials. We envision RealWonder opens new opportunities to apply video models in immersive experiences, AR/VR, and robot learning. Our code and model weights are publicly available in our project website: https://liuwei283.github.io/RealWonder/

AI.tldr: RealWonder is a real-time action-conditioned video generation system from a single image that uses physics simulation as an intermediate bridge to translate actions into optical-flow and RGB representations for a distilled video generator, enabling interactive exploration of forces and manipulations across rigid, deformable, fluid, and granular objects.
AI.motivation: Current video generation models lack structural understanding of 3D physics and the consequences of actions, hindering physically plausible video synthesis from actions.
AI.method: A three-component pipeline: (1) 3D reconstruction from a single image, (2) physics simulation to convert actions into visual representations (optical flow and RGB), and (3) a distilled video generator requiring only 4 diffusion steps to produce video.
AI.result: RealWonder runs at 13.2 FPS at 480x832 resolution, enabling interactive exploration of forces, robot actions, and camera controls on varied materials; code and weights are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: By grounding action-conditioned video generation in physics simulation, RealWonder broadens applications in immersive experiences, AR/VR, and robot learning, with a real-time, single-image input pipeline.

title: An interpretable prototype parts-based neural network for medical tabular data
summary: The ability to interpret machine learning model decisions is critical in such domains as healthcare, where trust in model predictions is as important as their accuracy. Inspired by the development of prototype parts-based deep neural networks in computer vision, we propose a new model for tabular data, specifically tailored to medical records, that requires discretization of diagnostic result norms. Unlike the original vision models that rely on the spatial structure, our method employs trainable patching over features describing a patient, to learn meaningful prototypical parts from structured data. These parts are represented as binary or discretized feature subsets. This allows the model to express prototypes in human-readable terms, enabling alignment with clinical language and case-based reasoning. Our proposed neural network is inherently interpretable and offers interpretable concept-based predictions by comparing the patient's description to learned prototypes in the latent space of the network. In experiments, we demonstrate that the model achieves classification performance competitive to widely used baseline models on medical benchmark datasets, while also offering transparency, bridging the gap between predictive performance and interpretability in clinical decision support.

AI.tldr: An interpretable, prototype-based neural network for tabular medical data using trainable feature patches and discretized prototypes, achieving competitive accuracy with transparent, human-readable explanations.
AI.motivation: In healthcare, trust and interpretability are critical. There is a need for models that provide human-readable prototypes aligned with clinical reasoning for tabular data, extending prototype-based ideas from vision to medical records.
AI.method: Introduce trainable patching over patient features, with discretization of diagnostic norms. Prototypes are binary or discretized feature subsets learned during training and used to compare a patient's description to prototypes in the network's latent space, yielding interpretable, concept-based predictions.
AI.result: The model achieves competitive classification performance on medical benchmark datasets compared to widely used baselines, while also offering transparency through prototype-based explanations.
AI.conclusion: An inherently interpretable model with competitive performance that supports clinical decision-making and case-based reasoning by presenting human-readable prototypes and concept-based predictions.

title: Beyond Scattered Acceptance: Fast and Coherent Inference for DLMs via Longest Stable Prefixes
summary: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) promise highly parallel text generation, yet their practical inference speed is often bottlenecked by suboptimal decoding schedulers. Standard approaches rely on 'scattered acceptance'-committing high confidence tokens at disjoint positions throughout the sequence. This approach inadvertently fractures the Key-Value (KV) cache, destroys memory locality, and forces the model into costly, repeated repairs across unstable token boundaries. To resolve this, we present the Longest Stable Prefix (LSP) scheduler, a training-free and model-agnostic inference paradigm based on monolithic prefix absorption. In each denoising step, LSP evaluates token stability via a single forward pass, dynamically identifies a contiguous left-aligned block of stable predictions, and snaps its boundary to natural linguistic or structural delimiters before an atomic commitment. This prefix-first topology yields dual benefits: systemically, it converts fragmented KV cache updates into efficient, contiguous appends; algorithmically, it preserves bidirectional lookahead over a geometrically shrinking active suffix, drastically reducing token flip rates and denoiser calls. Extensive evaluations on LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B demonstrate that LSP accelerates inference by up to 3.4x across rigorous benchmarks including mathematical reasoning, code generation, multilingual (CJK) tasks, and creative writing while matching or slightly improving output quality. By fundamentally restructuring the commitment topology, LSP bridges the gap between the theoretical parallelism of DLMs and practical hardware efficiency.

AI.tldr: Proposes the Longest Stable Prefix (LSP) scheduler for diffusion language models to accelerate inference by maintaining a contiguous stable prefix, reducing KV cache fragmentation and token flips; achieves up to 3.4x speedups on LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B with no loss (and possibly slight gain) in quality.
AI.motivation: Suboptimal decoding schedulers in diffusion language models fragment the KV cache, break memory locality, and force costly repairs at token boundaries, limiting practical parallelism and hardware efficiency. A training-free, model-agnostic solution is desired to improve inference speed without sacrificing output quality.
AI.method: LSP operates in each denoising step by issuing a single forward pass to assess token stability, identifying a contiguous left-aligned block of stable predictions, and snapping its boundary to linguistic/structural delimiters before committing. This creates a prefix-first, monolithic absorption of tokens, converting fragmented KV updates into contiguous appends and enabling bidirectional lookahead over a shrinking active suffix, thereby reducing token flips and denoiser calls.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B show up to 3.4x inference speedups across tasks including math reasoning, code generation, multilingual (CJK) tasks, and creative writing, with output quality matching or slightly improving.
AI.conclusion: Reframes commitment topology to align diffusion model parallelism with hardware efficiency, offering a training-free, model-agnostic improvement that preserves quality while significantly accelerating inference.

title: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Reasoning Compression
summary:
Reasoning models think out loud, but much of what they say is noise. We introduce OPSDC (On-Policy Self-Distillation for Reasoning Compression), a method that teaches models to reason more concisely by
  distilling their own concise behavior back into themselves. The entire approach reduces to one idea: condition the same model on a "be concise" instruction to obtain teacher logits, and minimize per-token
  reverse KL on the student's own rollouts. No ground-truth answers, no token budgets, no difficulty estimators. Just self-distillation. Yet this simplicity belies surprising sophistication: OPSDC automatically
  compresses easy problems aggressively while preserving the deliberation needed for hard ones. On Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-14B, we achieve 57-59% token reduction on MATH-500 while improving accuracy by 9-16 points
  absolute. On AIME 2024, the 14B model gains 10 points with 41% compression. The secret? Much of what reasoning models produce is not just redundant-it is actively harmful, compounding errors with every
  unnecessary token.

AI.tldr: OPSDC: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Reasoning Compression, a self-distillation method that teaches a model to reason concisely by conditioning on a 'be concise' prompt and minimizing per-token reverse KL between the student's rollouts and the teacher logits; yields strong token compression with improved accuracy on math benchmarks (MATH-500 and AIME 2024).
AI.motivation: Reasoning traces from large language models are often noisy and verbose, sometimes harmful. A need exists for concise reasoning that preserves the essential deliberation, without relying on external ground-truth solutions or fixed token budgets.
AI.method: Condition the same model on a 'be concise' instruction to generate teacher logits. Use on-policy self-distillation by minimizing per-token reverse KL divergence between the student’s rollouts and the teacher logits, effectively compressing reasoning without ground-truth answers or explicit budgets.
AI.result: Empirical results on Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-14B show 57–59% token reduction on MATH-500 with 9–16 point absolute accuracy gains. On AIME 2024 with a 14B model, achieves +10 points with 41% compression.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning tokens are often redundant or harmful; self-distillation to concise reasoning can preserve necessary deliberation while significantly reducing token usage and improving accuracy on math problems.

title: EdgeDAM: Real-time Object Tracking for Mobile Devices
summary: Single-object tracking (SOT) on edge devices is a critical computer vision task, requiring accurate and continuous target localization across video frames under occlusion, distractor interference, and fast motion. However, recent state-of-the-art distractor-aware memory mechanisms are largely built on segmentation-based trackers and rely on mask prediction and attention-driven memory updates, which introduce substantial computational overhead and limit real-time deployment on resource-constrained hardware; meanwhile, lightweight trackers sustain high throughput but are prone to drift when visually similar distractors appear. To address these challenges, we propose EdgeDAM, a lightweight detection-guided tracking framework that reformulates distractor-aware memory for bounding-box tracking under strict edge constraints. EdgeDAM introduces two key strategies: (1) Dual-Buffer Distractor-Aware Memory (DAM), which integrates a Recent-Aware Memory to preserve temporally consistent target hypotheses and a Distractor-Resolving Memory to explicitly store hard negative candidates and penalize their re-selection during recovery; and (2) Confidence-Driven Switching with Held-Box Stabilization, where tracker reliability and temporal consistency criteria adaptively activate detection and memory-guided re-identification during occlusion, while a held-box mechanism temporarily freezes and expands the estimate to suppress distractor contamination. Extensive experiments on five benchmarks, including the distractor-focused DiDi dataset, demonstrate improved robustness under occlusion and fast motion while maintaining real-time performance on mobile devices, achieving 88.2% accuracy on DiDi and 25 FPS on an iPhone 15. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: EdgeDAM is a lightweight, edge-focused SOT framework that uses dual-buffer distractor-aware memory and confidence-driven switching with a held-box mechanism to achieve robust, real-time tracking on mobile devices, outperforming segmentation-heavy distractor-aware methods while maintaining speed; it reports 88.2% accuracy on DiDi and 25 FPS on iPhone 15.
AI.motivation: Current distractor-aware memory approaches are built on segmentation-based trackers with high computational cost, hindering real-time edge deployment; lightweight trackers lack robustness against visually similar distractors and occlusion.
AI.method: EdgeDAM introduces (1) Dual-Buffer Distractor-Aware Memory (Recent-Aware Memory + Distractor-Resolving Memory) to maintain temporal consistency and penalize hard negatives, and (2) Confidence-Driven Switching with Held-Box Stabilization to activate detection/memory-guided re-identification during occlusion and to temporarily freeze/expand the estimate to suppress distractors; operates under strict edge constraints to enable real-time performance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five benchmarks including DiDi show improved robustness under occlusion and fast motion with real-time mobile performance, achieving 88.2% accuracy on DiDi and 25 FPS on an iPhone 15.
AI.conclusion: EdgeDAM demonstrates that detection-guided, memory-efficient tracking can deliver robust boundary-box tracking on edge devices without the heavy cost of segmentation-based distractor-aware methods, supporting practical deployment; code will be released.

title: Latent Wasserstein Adversarial Imitation Learning
summary: Imitation Learning (IL) enables agents to mimic expert behavior by learning from demonstrations. However, traditional IL methods require large amounts of medium-to-high-quality demonstrations as well as actions of expert demonstrations, both of which are often unavailable. To reduce this need, we propose Latent Wasserstein Adversarial Imitation Learning (LWAIL), a novel adversarial imitation learning framework that focuses on state-only distribution matching. It benefits from the Wasserstein distance computed in a dynamics-aware latent space. This dynamics-aware latent space differs from prior work and is obtained via a pre-training stage, where we train the Intention Conditioned Value Function (ICVF) to capture a dynamics-aware structure of the state space using a small set of randomly generated state-only data. We show that this enhances the policy's understanding of state transitions, enabling the learning process to use only one or a few state-only expert episodes to achieve expert-level performance. Through experiments on multiple MuJoCo environments, we demonstrate that our method outperforms prior Wasserstein-based IL methods and prior adversarial IL methods, achieving better results across various tasks.

AI.tldr: LWAIL uses state-only distribution matching via Wasserstein distance in a dynamics-aware latent space learned by pretraining an Intention Conditioned Value Function (ICVF); enables expert-level performance from one/few state-only demos, outperforming prior Wasserstein IL and adversarial IL on MuJoCo.
AI.motivation: Reduce data requirements of imitation learning by removing the need for expert action data and large demonstration sets, by leveraging state-only data and a dynamics-aware latent representation.
AI.method: Adversarial imitation learning where Wasserstein distance is computed on a latent representation that encodes dynamics. The latent space is obtained by pretraining an Intention Conditioned Value Function (ICVF) on a small set of random state-only data to capture the dynamics structure. Training uses only state observations; the agent learns from one or a few state-only expert episodes.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multiple MuJoCo environments shows LWAIL outperforms prior Wasserstein-based IL methods and prior adversarial IL methods, achieving better results across tasks and (implicitly) with fewer state-only demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: LWAIL introduces a dynamics-aware latent space for Wasserstein-based imitation learning, enabling state-only IL with drastically reduced demonstration requirements and improved performance, though dependent on the quality of the ICVF pretraining and latent space.

title: HALP: Detecting Hallucinations in Vision-Language Models without Generating a Single Token
summary: Hallucinations remain a persistent challenge for vision-language models (VLMs), which often describe nonexistent objects or fabricate facts. Existing detection methods typically operate after text generation, making intervention both costly and untimely. We investigate whether hallucination risk can instead be predicted before any token is generated by probing a model's internal representations in a single forward pass. Across a diverse set of vision-language tasks and eight modern VLMs, including Llama-3.2-Vision, Gemma-3, Phi-4-VL, and Qwen2.5-VL, we examine three families of internal representations: (i) visual-only features without multimodal fusion, (ii) vision-token representations within the text decoder, and (iii) query-token representations that integrate visual and textual information before generation. Probes trained on these representations achieve strong hallucination-detection performance without decoding, reaching up to 0.93 AUROC on Gemma-3-12B, Phi-4-VL 5.6B, and Molmo 7B. Late query-token states are the most predictive for most models, while visual or mid-layer features dominate in a few architectures (e.g., ~0.79 AUROC for Qwen2.5-VL-7B using visual-only features). These results demonstrate that (1) hallucination risk is detectable pre-generation, (2) the most informative layer and modality vary across architectures, and (3) lightweight probes have the potential to enable early abstention, selective routing, and adaptive decoding to improve both safety and efficiency.

AI.tldr: Pre-generation hallucination risk can be predicted by probing internal representations across multiple vision-language models; late query-token representations are most predictive; probes achieve high AUROC (up to 0.93) and enable early abstention and efficient decoding.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in vision-language models persist and are costly to detect post-generation; predicting risk before token generation could improve safety and efficiency by enabling early abstention and better decoding strategies.
AI.method: Evaluate three representation families (visual-only features without multimodal fusion, vision-token representations within the text decoder, and query-token representations that fuse visual and textual information prior to generation) as probes. Apply probes to eight modern VLMs (e.g., Llama-3.2-Vision, Gemma-3, Phi-4-VL, Qwen2.5-VL, Molmo) across diverse vision-language tasks. Train probes to detect hallucination risk in a single forward pass without decoding and assess performance (AUROC).
AI.result: Probes trained on internal representations achieve strong pre-generation hallucination-detection performance without decoding, with AUROC values reaching up to 0.93 on Gemma-3-12B, Phi-4-VL 5.6B, and Molmo 7B; late query-token states are generally most predictive, though some architectures rely more on visual or mid-layer features (e.g., ~0.79 AUROC for Qwen2.5-VL-7B using visual-only features).
AI.conclusion: Hallucination risk is detectable before generation; the most informative layer and modality vary across architectures; lightweight probes enable early abstention, selective routing, and adaptive decoding to improve safety and efficiency.

title: Towards 3D Scene Understanding of Gas Plumes in LWIR Hyperspectral Images Using Neural Radiance Fields
comment: This manuscript was submitted to SPIE JARS and is under review. Code and Data can be found at https://github.com/lanl/HSI-Nerfstudio and https://zenodo.org/records/18626884 respectively. Video 1 and Video 2 can be found at https://github.com/lanl/HSI-Nerfstudio/blob/main/renders/paper/grid_Falsecolor.mp4 and https://github.com/lanl/HSI-Nerfstudio/blob/main/renders/paper/grid_ACE.mp4 respectively
summary: Hyperspectral images (HSI) have many applications, ranging from environmental monitoring to national security, and can be used for material detection and identification. Longwave infrared (LWIR) HSI can be used for gas plume detection and analysis. Oftentimes, only a few images of a scene of interest are available and are analyzed individually. The ability to combine information from multiple images into a single, cohesive representation could enhance analysis by providing more context on the scene's geometry and spectral properties. Neural radiance fields (NeRFs) create a latent neural representation of volumetric scene properties that enable novel-view rendering and geometry reconstruction, offering a promising avenue for hyperspectral 3D scene reconstruction. We explore the possibility of using NeRFs to create 3D scene reconstructions from LWIR HSI and demonstrate that the model can be used for the basic downstream analysis task of gas plume detection. The physics-based DIRSIG software suite was used to generate a synthetic multi-view LWIR HSI dataset of a simple facility with a strong sulfur hexafluoride gas plume. Our method, built on the standard Mip-NeRF architecture, combines state-of-the-art methods for hyperspectral NeRFs and sparse-view NeRFs, along with a novel adaptive weighted MSE loss. Our final NeRF method requires around 50% fewer training images than the standard Mip-NeRF and achieves an average PSNR of 39.8 dB with as few as 30 training images. Gas plume detection applied to NeRF-rendered test images using the adaptive coherence estimator achieves an average AUC of 0.821 when compared with detection masks generated from ground-truth test images.

AI.tldr: NeRF-based hyperspectral LWIR reconstruction enables 3D scene representation and gas-plume detection with far fewer views; achieves PSNR ~39.8 dB with 30 training images and AUC ~0.821 for plume detection.
AI.motivation: To fuse information from multiple LWIR HSI views into a coherent 3D spectral representation using neural radiance fields, improving downstream tasks like gas plume detection when data are scarce.
AI.method: Extend Mip-NeRF with hyperspectral and sparse-view capabilities, introducing an adaptive weighted MSE loss. Train on a synthetic DIRSIG-generated multi-view LWIR HSI dataset of a facility with a SF6 gas plume and evaluate on NeRF-rendered test images using adaptive coherence estimator for plume detection.
AI.result: The proposed NeRF-based approach achieves a PSNR of 39.8 dB with as few as 30 training images. Gas plume detection on NeRF-rendered tests yields an average AUC of 0.821 compared to ground-truth masks.
AI.conclusion: Hyperspectral NeRFs can effectively reconstruct LWIR 3D scenes and support downstream detection tasks (gas plumes) with substantially fewer views, demonstrating practical viability for sparse-view spectral imaging.

title: SurvHTE-Bench: A Benchmark for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation in Survival Analysis
comment: The Fourteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2026)
summary: Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) from right-censored survival data is critical in high-stakes applications such as precision medicine and individualized policy-making. Yet, the survival analysis setting poses unique challenges for HTE estimation due to censoring, unobserved counterfactuals, and complex identification assumptions. Despite recent advances, from Causal Survival Forests to survival meta-learners and outcome imputation approaches, evaluation practices remain fragmented and inconsistent. We introduce SurvHTE-Bench, the first comprehensive benchmark for HTE estimation with censored outcomes. The benchmark spans (i) a modular suite of synthetic datasets with known ground truth, systematically varying causal assumptions and survival dynamics, (ii) semi-synthetic datasets that pair real-world covariates with simulated treatments and outcomes, and (iii) real-world datasets from a twin study (with known ground truth) and from an HIV clinical trial. Across synthetic, semi-synthetic, and real-world settings, we provide the first rigorous comparison of survival HTE methods under diverse conditions and realistic assumption violations. SurvHTE-Bench establishes a foundation for fair, reproducible, and extensible evaluation of causal survival methods. The data and code of our benchmark are available at: https://github.com/Shahriarnz14/SurvHTE-Bench .

AI.tldr: Introduces SurvHTE-Bench, the first comprehensive benchmark for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects with censored survival outcomes, featuring synthetic, semi-synthetic, and real-world datasets and open-source evaluation tools.
AI.motivation: Address the fragmentation and inconsistency in evaluating causal survival methods under censoring; provide ground-truth, diverse causal assumptions, and realistic violations to enable fair, reproducible, and extensible benchmarking.
AI.method: Three-tier benchmark design: (i) modular synthetic datasets with known ground truth and varying survival dynamics/causal assumptions, (ii) semi-synthetic datasets combining real covariates with simulated treatments/outcomes, and (iii) real-world datasets (twin study with ground truth, HIV trial). Provides data and code publicly.
AI.result: First rigorous, comprehensive comparison of survival HTE methods across diverse settings; establishes a foundation for fair, reproducible, and extensible evaluation of causal survival methods.
AI.conclusion: SurvHTE-Bench enables standardized evaluation and method development in survival HTE, promoting reproducibility and fair benchmarking; data/code available at GitHub.

title: Towards Multimodal Lifelong Understanding: A Dataset and Agentic Baseline
summary: While datasets for video understanding have scaled to hour-long durations, they typically consist of densely concatenated clips that differ from natural, unscripted daily life. To bridge this gap, we introduce MM-Lifelong, a dataset designed for Multimodal Lifelong Understanding. Comprising 181.1 hours of footage, it is structured across Day, Week, and Month scales to capture varying temporal densities. Extensive evaluations reveal two critical failure modes in current paradigms: end-to-end MLLMs suffer from a Working Memory Bottleneck due to context saturation, while representative agentic baselines experience Global Localization Collapse when navigating sparse, month-long timelines. To address this, we propose the Recursive Multimodal Agent (ReMA), which employs dynamic memory management to iteratively update a recursive belief state, significantly outperforming existing methods. Finally, we establish dataset splits designed to isolate temporal and domain biases, providing a rigorous foundation for future research in supervised learning and out-of-distribution generalization.

AI.tldr: Introduces MM-Lifelong, a multimodal lifelong understanding dataset of 181.1 hours structured across Day/Week/Month scales; identifies two failure modes of current approaches (Working Memory Bottleneck in end-to-end MLLMs and Global Localization Collapse in representative agentic baselines); proposes Recursive Multimodal Agent (ReMA) with dynamic memory to iteratively update a recursive belief state, achieving significant performance gains; provides dataset splits to isolate temporal and domain biases for robust supervised learning and OOD generalization.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between existing video datasets (hour-long, densely concatenated clips) and the needs of natural, unscripted daily life understanding. Develop a long-horizon, multimodal benchmark with multi-scale temporal density (Day/Week/Month) and robust evaluation to study memory, localization, and generalization in lifelong learning.
AI.method: Construct MM-Lifelong dataset (181.1 hours) spanning Day/Week/Month scales. Diagnose failure modes of current paradigms: end-to-end MLLMs suffer from a Working Memory Bottleneck due to context saturation; agentic baselines suffer Global Localization Collapse on sparse, month-long timelines. Propose Recursive Multimodal Agent (ReMA) with dynamic memory management that iteratively updates a recursive belief state. Establish dataset splits to disentangle temporal and domain biases.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show two critical failure modes in current approaches. ReMA significantly outperforms existing methods by addressing memory and localization challenges, demonstrating strong gains on long-horizon multimodal understanding tasks.
AI.conclusion: The MM-Lifelong dataset and ReMA provide a rigorous foundation for supervised learning and out-of-distribution generalization in multimodal lifelong understanding. The dataset splits enable principled evaluation of temporal and domain biases, highlighting the importance of dynamic memory and recursive belief updating for robust long-horizon reasoning.

title: Censored LLMs as a Natural Testbed for Secret Knowledge Elicitation
summary: Large language models sometimes produce false or misleading responses. Two approaches to this problem are honesty elicitation -- modifying prompts or weights so that the model answers truthfully -- and lie detection -- classifying whether a given response is false. Prior work evaluates such methods on models specifically trained to lie or conceal information, but these artificial constructions may not resemble naturally-occurring dishonesty. We instead study open-weights LLMs from Chinese developers, which are trained to censor politically sensitive topics: Qwen3 models frequently produce falsehoods about subjects like Falun Gong or the Tiananmen protests while occasionally answering correctly, indicating they possess knowledge they are trained to suppress. Using this as a testbed, we evaluate a suite of elicitation and lie detection techniques. For honesty elicitation, sampling without a chat template, few-shot prompting, and fine-tuning on generic honesty data most reliably increase truthful responses. For lie detection, prompting the censored model to classify its own responses performs near an uncensored-model upper bound, and linear probes trained on unrelated data offer a cheaper alternative. The strongest honesty elicitation techniques also transfer to frontier open-weights models including DeepSeek R1. Notably, no technique fully eliminates false responses. We release all prompts, code, and transcripts.

AI.tldr: Open-weights LLMs trained to censor political topics reveal both falsehoods and truths; study evaluates honesty elicitation and lie detection; strongest methods reduce but do not eliminate false responses; transferability to frontier models; dataset and prompts released.
AI.motivation: Assess practical effectiveness of honesty elicitation and lie detection on naturally-occurring dishonesty in censorship-trained open LLMs, to avoid artificial adversarial setups.
AI.method: Evaluate multiple honesty elicitation approaches (sampling without a chat template, few-shot prompting, generic honesty fine-tuning) and lie-detection methods (self-classification prompts, linear probes); test on Qwen3 and DeepSeek R1; analyze transferability.
AI.result: Honesty methods improve truthfulness but cannot fully remove falsehoods; specialized prompting and training best for honesty; self-classification prompting nearly matches uncensored upper bound; linear probes cheaper option; some transfer to frontier models.
AI.conclusion: While progress is tangible, no method fully eliminates false responses; open resources released for reproducibility; implications for deploying honest AI systems.

title: Accelerating Text-to-Video Generation with Calibrated Sparse Attention
summary: Recent diffusion models enable high-quality video generation, but suffer from slow runtimes. The large transformer-based backbones used in these models are bottlenecked by spatiotemporal attention. In this paper, we identify that a significant fraction of token-to-token connections consistently yield negligible scores across various inputs, and their patterns often repeat across queries. Thus, the attention computation in these cases can be skipped with little to no effect on the result. This observation continues to hold for connections among local token blocks. Motivated by this, we introduce CalibAtt, a training-free method that accelerates video generation via calibrated sparse attention. CalibAtt performs an offline calibration pass that identifies block-level sparsity and repetition patterns that are stable across inputs, and compiles these patterns into optimized attention operations for each layer, head, and diffusion timestep. At inference time, we compute the selected input-dependent connections densely, and skip the unselected ones in a hardware-efficient manner. Extensive experiments on Wan 2.1 14B, Mochi 1, and few-step distilled models at various resolutions show that CalibAtt achieves up to 1.58x end-to-end speedup, outperforming existing training-free methods while maintaining video generation quality and text-video alignment.

AI.tldr: CalibAtt is a training-free calibrated sparse attention method for video diffusion models that identifies stable, block-level sparsity and repetition patterns via offline calibration, enabling hardware-efficient inference with up to 1.58x end-to-end speedup while preserving quality and alignment.
AI.motivation: Video diffusion models suffer from slow runtimes due to heavy spatiotemporal attention in large transformer backbones. Many token connections have negligible scores and repeat patterns across queries, suggesting sparsity and reuse can accelerate inference without large quality loss.
AI.method: An offline calibration pass identifies block-level sparsity and repetition patterns that are stable across inputs. These patterns are compiled into optimized attention operations for each layer, head, and diffusion timestep. During inference, only selected input-dependent connections are computed densely; unselected ones are skipped in a hardware-efficient manner.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Wan 2.1 14B, Mochi 1, and few-step distilled models across resolutions show up to 1.58x end-to-end speedup, outperforming existing training-free methods while maintaining video quality and text-video alignment.
AI.conclusion: CalibAtt demonstrates that training-free calibrated sparse attention can meaningfully accelerate video generation in diffusion models by exploiting stable sparsity and repetition patterns, with broad applicability across model scales and resolutions.

title: Cheap Thrills: Effective Amortized Optimization Using Inexpensive Labels
summary: To scale the solution of optimization and simulation problems, prior work has explored machine-learning surrogates that inexpensively map problem parameters to corresponding solutions. Commonly used approaches, including supervised and self-supervised learning with either soft or hard feasibility enforcement, face inherent challenges such as reliance on expensive, high-quality labels or difficult optimization landscapes. To address their trade-offs, we propose a novel framework that first collects "cheap" imperfect labels, then performs supervised pretraining, and finally refines the model through self-supervised learning to improve overall performance. Our theoretical analysis and merit-based criterion show that labeled data need only place the model within a basin of attraction, confirming that only modest numbers of inexact labels and training epochs are required. We empirically validate our simple three-stage strategy across challenging domains, including nonconvex constrained optimization, power-grid operation, and stiff dynamical systems, and show that it yields faster convergence; improved accuracy, feasibility, and optimality; and up to 59x reductions in total offline cost.

AI.tldr: A three-stage learning framework—cheap imperfect labels, supervised pretraining, then self-supervised refinement—enables scalable surrogates for optimization/simulation, yielding faster convergence, better feasibility/optimality, and large offline cost reductions.
AI.motivation: To reduce reliance on expensive high-quality labels and navigate difficult optimization landscapes in ML surrogates, by combining cheap weak supervision with principled refinement and theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Collect cheap imperfect labels; perform supervised pretraining; refine via self-supervised learning. The approach is supported by a theoretical analysis showing that labels need only place the model within a basin of attraction; merit-based criterion guides data/epochs. Empirical validation across nonconvex constrained optimization, power-grid operation, and stiff dynamical systems.
AI.result: Faster convergence; improved accuracy, feasibility, and optimality; up to 59x reductions in total offline cost.
AI.conclusion: The proposed three-stage framework effectively harnesses cheap labels and self-supervised refinement to produce accurate and feasible surrogates with substantially lower offline data requirements, supported by theory and validated across diverse challenging domains.

title: FaceCam: Portrait Video Camera Control via Scale-Aware Conditioning
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026. Project page: https://weijielyu.github.io/FaceCam
summary: We introduce FaceCam, a system that generates video under customizable camera trajectories for monocular human portrait video input. Recent camera control approaches based on large video-generation models have shown promising progress but often exhibit geometric distortions and visual artifacts on portrait videos due to scale-ambiguous camera representations or 3D reconstruction errors. To overcome these limitations, we propose a face-tailored scale-aware representation for camera transformations that provides deterministic conditioning without relying on 3D priors. We train a video generation model on both multi-view studio captures and in-the-wild monocular videos, and introduce two camera-control data generation strategies: synthetic camera motion and multi-shot stitching, to exploit stationary training cameras while generalizing to dynamic, continuous camera trajectories at inference time. Experiments on Ava-256 dataset and diverse in-the-wild videos demonstrate that FaceCam achieves superior performance in camera controllability, visual quality, identity and motion preservation.

AI.tldr: FaceCam presents a scale-aware, face-tailored camera representation and data-generation strategies to enable controllable monocular portrait video synthesis with reduced distortion and better identity/motion preservation.
AI.motivation: Existing large-video generation models struggle with geometry and artifacts in portrait videos due to scale-ambiguous camera representations and 3D reconstruction errors; there is a need for deterministic conditioning without relying on full 3D priors.
AI.method: Introduce a face-tailored scale-aware camera representation for transformations that provides deterministic conditioning without 3D priors; train a video-generation model on multi-view studio captures and in-the-wild monoculars; develop two camera-control data-generation strategies—synthetic camera motion and multi-shot stitching—to leverage stationary training cameras and generalize to dynamic trajectories at inference.
AI.result: FaceCam achieves superior camera controllability, visual quality, identity and motion preservation on Ava-256 and diverse in-the-wild videos.
AI.conclusion: The proposed representation enables deterministic, 3D-prior-free conditioning for portrait video synthesis and improves generalization to dynamic camera trajectories, with strong empirical performance.

title: POET-X: Memory-efficient LLM Training by Scaling Orthogonal Transformation
comment: Technical report v1 (14 pages, 7 figures, project page: https://spherelab.ai/poetx/)
summary: Efficient and stable training of large language models (LLMs) remains a core challenge in modern machine learning systems. To address this challenge, Reparameterized Orthogonal Equivalence Training (POET), a spectrum-preserving framework that optimizes each weight matrix through orthogonal equivalence transformation, has been proposed. Although POET provides strong training stability, its original implementation incurs high memory consumption and computational overhead due to intensive matrix multiplications. To overcome these limitations, we introduce POET-X, a scalable and memory-efficient variant that performs orthogonal equivalence transformations with significantly reduced computational cost. POET-X maintains the generalization and stability benefits of POET while achieving substantial improvements in throughput and memory efficiency. In our experiments, POET-X enables the pretraining of billion-parameter LLMs on a single Nvidia H100 GPU, and in contrast, standard optimizers such as AdamW run out of memory under the same settings.

AI.tldr: POET-X is a scalable, memory-efficient variant of Reparameterized Orthogonal Equivalence Training that preserves spectrum and stability, enabling billion-parameter LLM pretraining on a single H100 and surpassing AdamW in memory efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address the high memory consumption and computational overhead of the original POET framework, which limits large-scale pretraining on limited hardware.
AI.method: Introduce a scalable, memory-efficient orthogonal equivalence transformation approach that maintains the benefits of POET while reducing computational cost, preserving spectrum to ensure stability and generalization.
AI.result: POET-X achieves substantially improved throughput and memory efficiency, enabling billion-parameter LLM pretraining on a single Nvidia H100 GPU, whereas AdamW runs out of memory under the same conditions.
AI.conclusion: POET-X retains POET's stability and generalization advantages and makes large-scale pretraining more practical on standard hardware by dramatically reducing resource requirements.

title: Transformer-Based Inpainting for Real-Time 3D Streaming in Sparse Multi-Camera Setups
comment: You can find the project page https://github.com/vc-bonn/transformer-based-inpainting
summary: High-quality 3D streaming from multiple cameras is crucial for immersive experiences in many AR/VR applications. The limited number of views - often due to real-time constraints - leads to missing information and incomplete surfaces in the rendered images. Existing approaches typically rely on simple heuristics for the hole filling, which can result in inconsistencies or visual artifacts. We propose to complete the missing textures using a novel, application-targeted inpainting method independent of the underlying representation as an image-based post-processing step after the novel view rendering. The method is designed as a standalone module compatible with any calibrated multi-camera system. For this we introduce a multi-view aware, transformer-based network architecture using spatio-temporal embeddings to ensure consistency across frames while preserving fine details. Additionally, our resolution-independent design allows adaptation to different camera setups, while an adaptive patch selection strategy balances inference speed and quality, allowing real-time performance. We evaluate our approach against state-of-the-art inpainting techniques under the same real-time constraints and demonstrate that our model achieves the best trade-off between quality and speed, outperforming competitors in both image and video-based metrics.

AI.tldr: A transformer-based, multi-view, spatio-temporal inpainting module fills missing textures in real-time 3D streaming from few cameras. It operates as a post-processing step, is history-aware and resolution-independent, and adapts patch selection to balance speed and quality, outperforming existing inpainting methods under real-time constraints.
AI.motivation: Missing information from sparse camera views causes holes and inconsistencies in rendered surfaces. Existing heuristics struggle to maintain cross-view and temporal consistency under real-time constraints. A dedicated, view-aware inpainting module can improve surface fidelity without relying on a specific 3D representation.
AI.method: A standalone, multi-view aware transformer network with spatio-temporal embeddings, designed as an image-based post-processing step after novel view rendering. It uses resolution-independent design and adaptive patch selection to balance inference speed and quality, ensuring cross-frame consistency across a calibrated multi-camera system.
AI.result: Compared to state-of-the-art inpainting under identical real-time constraints, the proposed method achieves the best trade-off between visual quality and speed, outperforming competitors in both image- and video-based metrics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed inpainting module delivers consistent texture completion across views and frames for high-quality 3D streaming, while meeting real-time performance and adaptable to different camera setups.
